topics.title: Introduction to Energy Conversion - Energy Resources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT614 - ENERGY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Energy Conversion - Energy Resources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Energy?

From the rigorous perspective of physics and engineering thermodynamics, **energy** is defined as the capacity of a system to perform *work* or to transfer *heat*. Energy is a **scalar quantity**, always conserved in any isolated system as stated by the **First Law of Thermodynamics**.

Mathematically, energy exists in multiple interchangeable forms:
$$ E = \{ \text{kinetic},\ \text{potential},\ \text{thermal},\ \text{chemical},\ \text{electrical},\ \text{nuclear},\ \text{radiant} \} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Energy is the primary driver of all economic, biological, and physical processes. For an engineering student, energy is *the fundamental commodity* that powers machines, industries, transportation, and modern civilization.

### 1.2 What is Energy Conversion?

**Energy Conversion** (also called energy transformation) is the process of changing energy from one form to another. Since energy can never be created or destroyed (only transformed), every physical device in engineering — from a steam turbine to a solar panel — is essentially an *energy converter*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Water Bucket"**
> Imagine energy is water in a bucket.
> - The **water level** = the *quantity* of energy (Joules, kWh).
> - The **shape of the bucket** = the *form* of energy (kinetic, thermal, electrical).
> - **Pouring water from one bucket into another** = Energy Conversion.
> - **The water lost as a small spill (friction, heat, noise)** = Energy Loss (entropy increase, per the Second Law).
>
> Just as you cannot pour water without spilling a few drops, you cannot convert energy without losing some as low-grade heat.

### 1.3 What are Energy Resources?

**Energy Resources** are the naturally occurring or cultivated reserves from which usable energy can be extracted, converted, and harnessed to perform useful work. They form the **upstream supply chain** of every power plant, factory, and vehicle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constant to Remember:**
> **1 Joule (J)** = 1 N·m = **1 kg·m²/s²** — The SI unit of energy.
> **1 calorie (cal)** = **4.184 J** exactly — The heat required to raise 1 gram of water by 1 °C.

### 1.4 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Primary Energy** | Energy in its raw form as found in nature (coal, sunlight, uranium). |
| **Secondary Energy** | Energy derived after conversion from a primary source (electricity, gasoline). |
| **Commercial Energy** | Energy bought and sold in the market (coal, oil, electricity, LPG). |
| **Non-Commercial Energy** | Energy collected for free, locally (firewood, animal dung, straw). |
| **Energy Chain** | Sequential stages: **Resource → Extraction → Conversion → Distribution → End Use**. |
| **Energy Intensity** | Energy consumed per unit GDP (MJ per USD or kgoe per \$). |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Energy Resources

Energy resources are classified along multiple axes. The **KTU 2024 Scheme** emphasizes the following taxonomy:

#### A. By Regeneration Capability
- **Renewable Energy Resources** — Replenished naturally at a rate faster than or equal to human consumption.
  - Examples: **Solar, Wind, Hydro, Biomass, Geothermal, Tidal, Wave, Ocean Thermal.**
- **Non-Renventional (Non-Renewable) Energy Resources** — Exist in finite quantities; consume faster than nature regenerates.
  - Examples: **Coal, Petroleum, Natural Gas, Nuclear (Uranium, Thorium).**

#### B. By Conventionality
- **Conventional Energy** — Mature, widely-deployed technologies (fossil fuels + large hydro + nuclear).
- **Non-Conventional Energy** — Emerging or alternative sources (solar PV, wind turbines, tidal, fuel cells).

#### C. By End-Use Form
- **Primary, Secondary, Final, and Useful Energy** — Following the cascade logic of the energy chain.

### 2.2 The Energy Conversion Principle

Every conversion device obeys the **energy balance equation**:
$$ E_{\text{in}} = E_{\text{out,useful}} + E_{\text{out,lost}} $$

Equivalently, conversion efficiency is defined as:
$$ \eta_{\text{conversion}} = \frac{E_{\text{useful output}}}{E_{\text{input}}} \times 100\% $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Efficiency is Never 100 %:**
> The **Second Law of Thermodynamics** forces every real conversion to produce entropy. Heat dissipates to surroundings, friction consumes mechanical energy, and electrical resistance generates Joule heating. Therefore, $\eta < 1$ for every practical device.

### 2.3 Global vs. Indian Energy Scenario (High-Yield for KTU)

| Metric | Global (approx.) | India (approx.) |
|---|---|---|
| Primary energy mix dominated by | Fossil fuels (~**80 %**) | Coal-heavy (~**50 %+**) |
| Per-capita electricity consumption | ~**3,300 kWh/yr** | ~**1,200 kWh/yr** |
| Renewable share (installed capacity) | ~**30 %** | ~**43 %** (as of 2024, non-fossil) |
| Energy intensity | Declining globally | Among the highest globally |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** India imports over **85 %** of its crude oil and around **50 %** of its natural gas. Memorize this — examiners love asking about energy security.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Work done by a force | $W = F \cdot d \cdot \cos\theta$ | Joule (J) |
| 2 | Kinetic energy | $KE = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$ | J |
| 3 | Potential energy (gravity) | $PE = m g h$ | J |
| 4 | Electrical energy | $E = V I t = P t$ | J (or kWh) |
| 5 | Heat energy | $Q = m c \Delta T$ | J |
| 6 | Calorific value (energy density) | $CV = \dfrac{E_{\text{released}}}{m}$ | kJ/kg or kCal/kg |
| 7 | Conversion efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{E_{\text{out,useful}}}{E_{\text{in}}} \times 100$ | dimensionless (%) |
| 8 | Power | $P = \dfrac{dE}{dt}$ | Watt (W = J/s) |
| 9 | kWh to Joule | $1\ \text{kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^{6}\ \text{J}$ | J |
| 10 | 1 toe (tonne of oil equivalent) | $1\ \text{toe} = 41.868\ \text{GJ}$ | GJ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do not confuse Calorific Value units.** Use `kJ/kg` for solids/liquids, `kJ/m³` (or `MJ/Nm³`) for gases. KTU examiners often deduct marks for unit mismatch.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Plant Engineers** design Rankine/Brayton cycles to maximize $\eta$.
- **Automotive Engineers** select fuels based on calorific value and octane rating.
- **Renewable Energy Engineers** compute capacity factor and plant load factor for solar/wind farms.
- **Energy Managers** compute Energy Performance Indicators (EnPIs) to audit industrial plants.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Energy Unit Conversion from kWh to Joules

**Problem:** Convert 5 kWh of electrical energy into Joules (SI base unit).

**Step 1 — Recall definitions.**
Power in Watts equals energy in Joules divided by time in seconds.
$$ P = \frac{E}{t} \implies E = P \cdot t $$

**Step 2 — Substitute the unit Watts.**
By definition, **1 Watt = 1 Joule per second**.
$$ 1\ \text{W} = 1\ \frac{\text{J}}{\text{s}} $$

**Step 3 — Express 1 kWh in base units.**
$$ 1\ \text{kWh} = 1000\ \text{W} \times 3600\ \text{s} $$

**Step 4 — Substitute Watt = J/s.**
$$ 1\ \text{kWh} = 1000 \times \frac{\text{J}}{\text{s}} \times 3600\ \text{s} $$

**Step 5 — Cancel seconds.**
$$ 1\ \text{kWh} = 3{,}600{,}000\ \text{J} = 3.6 \times 10^{6}\ \text{J} $$

**Step 6 — Apply to the numerical value 5 kWh.**
$$ E = 5 \times 3.6 \times 10^{6}\ \text{J} = 1.8 \times 10^{7}\ \text{J} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** **5 kWh = 18 MJ = 1.8 × 10⁷ J.** This exact conversion is the most frequently tested numerical in KTU Module 1.

### 3.2 Derivation: Gross Calorific Value (GCV) vs Net Calorific Value (NCV)

The **Gross Calorific Value (GCV)** is the total heat released when 1 kg of fuel is burned, and the combustion products are cooled back to the original temperature (allowing water vapor to condense).

The **Net Calorific Value (NCV)** is the useful heat available when the water vapor escapes as steam (which is what happens in real boilers).

**Step 1 — Write the mass balance of water produced.**
For a hydrocarbon fuel containing hydrogen mass fraction $H$ (kg H per kg fuel), the mass of water formed per kg fuel is:
$$ m_{w} = 9 \cdot H $$

**Step 2 — Write the latent heat lost.**
Latent heat of vaporization of water = **2.26 MJ/kg** (at 100 °C).
$$ L_{\text{latent}} = m_{w} \cdot h_{fg} = 9H \times 2.26 = 20.34\,H \ \text{MJ/kg} $$

**Step 3 — Relate GCV and NCV.**
$$ \boxed{\ NCV = GCV - 9H \times 2.26\ \text{MJ/kg}\ } $$

Or, using **H₂ mass fraction expressed as percentage** (the standard KTU textbook form):
$$ NCV = GCV - 0.09 \cdot H\% \times 2242\ \text{kJ/kg} $$

**Worked Example:**
A coal has GCV = 25 MJ/kg and Hydrogen content = 5 %.
Find NCV.

**Step 1:**
$$ 9 \times H = 9 \times 0.05 = 0.45\ \text{kg water per kg fuel} $$

**Step 2:**
$$ \text{Latent heat lost} = 0.45 \times 2.26 = 1.017\ \text{MJ/kg} $$

**Step 3:**
$$ NCV = 25 - 1.017 = 23.983\ \text{MJ/kg} \approx 24\ \text{MJ/kg} $$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: First-Law Efficiency of Common Converters

| Conversion Device | Input Energy | Output (Useful) Energy | Typical $\eta$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Electric heater | Electrical | Heat | **~100 %** |
| Electric motor | Electrical | Mechanical | **~85–95 %** |
| Petrol engine | Chemical (fuel) | Mechanical | **~25–35 %** |
| Diesel engine | Chemical (fuel) | Mechanical | **~35–45 %** |
| Coal-fired boiler | Chemical | Thermal steam | **~80–90 %** |
| Thermal power plant | Chemical | Electrical | **~33–45 %** |
| Incandescent bulb | Electrical | Light | **~5 %** |
| LED bulb | Electrical | Light | **~40–50 %** |
| Solar PV cell | Solar radiation | Electrical | **~15–22 %** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** A common 3-mark question asks, *"Why is the efficiency of a thermal power plant only ~35 % even with the best engineering?"* — Answer: **Carnot limit + irreversibilities + condenser losses**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: The Energy Conversion Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R["ENERGY RESOURCE"] --> E["EXTRACTION & PROCESSING"]
    E --> C["CONVERSION PLANT"]
    C --> D["DISTRIBUTION NETWORK"]
    D --> U["END USER APPLICATION"]
    U -. Waste heat / losses .-> W["ENVIRONMENT SINK"]
    C -. Stack / exhaust losses .-> W
    D -. Line losses .-> W

    style R fill:#FFD580,stroke:#A8742A,color:#000000
    style E fill:#FFE0A8,stroke:#A8742A,color:#000000
    style C fill:#FFB570,stroke:#A8742A,color:#000000
    style D fill:#FF8A4A,stroke:#A8742A,color:#000000
    style U fill:#E0552A,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF
    style W fill:#777777,stroke:#222222,color:#FFFFFF
```

**Reading the diagram:**
The chain is **Resource → Extraction → Conversion → Distribution → End Use**, with loss streams bleeding into the environment at every stage. The cumulative efficiency $\eta_{\text{overall}} = \eta_{1}\cdot \eta_{2}\cdot \eta_{3}\cdot \eta_{4}$ — which is why overall efficiency is far lower than the conversion plant efficiency.

### 4.2 Mermaid Taxonomy: Classification of Energy Resources

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["ENERGY RESOURCES"] --> B["NON RENEWABLE"]
    A --> C["RENEWABLE"]

    B --> B1["FOSSIL FUELS"]
    B --> B2["NUCLEAR FUELS"]

    B1 --> B1a["COAL"]
    B1 --> B1b["CRUDE OIL"]
    B1 --> B1c["NATURAL GAS"]

    B2 --> B2a["URANIUM 235"]
    B2 --> B2b["THORIUM 232"]

    C --> C1["SOLAR"]
    C --> C2["WIND"]
    C --> C3["HYDRO"]
    C --> C4["BIOMASS"]
    C --> C5["GEOTHERMAL"]
    C --> C6["TIDAL WAVE OCEAN"]

    C1 --> C1a["PV CELL"]
    C1 --> C1b["SOLAR THERMAL"]

    style A fill:#2A6F97,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF
    style B fill:#8B2C2C,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF
    style C fill:#2E8B57,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF
```

### 4.3 Energy Form Transformation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CHEM["CHEMICAL ENERGY"] -->|"BOILER"| TH["THERMAL ENERGY"]
    TH -->|"TURBINE"| MECH["MECHANICAL ENERGY"]
    MECH -->|"GENERATOR"| ELE["ELECTRICAL ENERGY"]
    ELE -->|"MOTOR"| MECH2["MECHANICAL ENERGY"]
    ELE -->|"HEATER"| TH2["THERMAL ENERGY"]
    ELE -->|"LAMP"| RAD["RADIANT LIGHT ENERGY"]

    style CHEM fill:#E8B100,stroke:#000000,color:#000000
    style TH fill:#FF6B35,stroke:#000000,color:#000000
    style MECH fill:#6B7CFF,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF
    style ELE fill:#00B4A6,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF
    style RAD fill:#FFD23F,stroke:#000000,color:#000000
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define the term "Calorific Value" of a fuel. Differentiate between GCV and NCV.*

**Model Answer:**
- **Calorific Value** is the amount of heat energy released per unit mass (or volume) of a fuel when it undergoes complete combustion with oxygen.
- Units: **kJ/kg** for solid/liquid fuels; **kJ/m³** for gaseous fuels.
- **GCV (Gross Calorific Value):** Total heat released including the latent heat of condensation of water vapor formed during combustion.
- **NCV (Net Calorific Value):** Useful heat available when the water vapor escapes without condensing (real boiler condition).
- Relation: $\boxed{NCV = GCV - 9H \cdot 2.26\ \text{MJ/kg}}$ where $H$ is the mass fraction of hydrogen.

*(3 marks — precise definition + GCV/NCV differentiation + formula = full credit.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *List any six examples of renewable energy resources with one application of each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Renewable Resource | One Application |
|---|---|
| Solar (radiant) | Solar PV panels for rooftop electricity |
| Wind (kinetic) | Wind turbines feeding the grid |
| Hydro (gravitational potential) | Large hydroelectric dams (e.g., Idukki) |
| Biomass (chemical) | Biogas plants from agri-waste |
| Geothermal (thermal) | Direct space heating in Iceland |
| Tidal (kinetic) | Tidal barrage power plants |

*(3 marks — 6 examples × 0.5 each, neatly tabulated.)*

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Adapted]*

**(a)** Explain the various classifications of energy resources in detail with examples. *(7 marks)*
**(b)** A coal sample has the following composition by mass:
- C = 70 %, H = 5 %, O = 10 %, S = 2 %, N = 1 %, Ash = 12 %.
Calculate the **GCV** using **Dulong's formula** and hence find the **NCV**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

Energy resources are classified into four major categories:

1. **Primary vs Secondary** — *Primary* exists naturally (coal, crude oil, sunlight); *Secondary* is derived (electricity, gasoline, hydrogen).

2. **Renewable vs Non-Renewable** — *Renewable* regenerates naturally (solar, wind, hydro, biomass, geothermal, tidal); *Non-Renewable* is finite (coal, oil, gas, uranium).

3. **Commercial vs Non-Commercial** — *Commercial* is traded in markets (electricity, LPG); *Non-Commercial* is collected locally for free (firewood, cow-dung cakes — still used in rural India).

4. **Conventional vs Non-Conventional** — *Conventional* technologies are mature (thermal, hydro, nuclear); *Non-Conventional* are emerging alternatives (solar PV, wind, fuel cells, hydrogen).

*(Each category carries ~1.5 marks; examples are mandatory.)*

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — State Dulong's Formula for GCV (in kCal/kg):**
$$ GCV = \frac{8080\,C}{100} + \frac{34500\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right)}{100} + \frac{2240\,S}{100} $$

**Step 2 — Substitute values** (C=70, H=5, O=10, S=2):
$$ GCV = \frac{8080 \times 70}{100} + \frac{34500 \times \left(5 - \dfrac{10}{8}\right)}{100} + \frac{2240 \times 2}{100} $$

**Step 3 — Evaluate term-by-term:**
$$ \text{Term 1} = 80.8 \times 70 = 5656\ \text{kCal/kg} $$

$$ 5 - \frac{10}{8} = 5 - 1.25 = 3.75 $$

$$ \text{Term 2} = 345 \times 3.75 = 1293.75\ \text{kCal/kg} $$

$$ \text{Term 3} = 22.4 \times 2 = 44.8\ \text{kCal/kg} $$

**Step 4 — Sum the three terms:**
$$ GCV = 5656 + 1293.75 + 44.8 = 6994.55\ \text{kCal/kg} $$

**Step 5 — Convert kCal/kg to MJ/kg** (1 kCal = 4.184 kJ):
$$ GCV = 6994.55 \times 4.184 \approx 29{,}265\ \text{kJ/kg} \approx 29.27\ \text{MJ/kg} $$

**Step 6 — Calculate NCV:**
$$ NCV = GCV - 0.09 \times H\% \times 2242\ \text{kJ/kg} $$

$$ NCV = 6994.55 - 0.09 \times 5 \times 2242 = 6994.55 - 1008.9 $$

$$ \boxed{\ NCV \approx 5985.65\ \text{kCal/kg} \approx 25.04\ \text{MJ/kg}\ } $$

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Discuss the **global and Indian energy scenario** with reference to the share of fossil fuels, renewables, and the challenges of energy security. *(7 marks)*
**(b)** A thermal power plant consumes **1500 tonnes of coal per day** with a GCV of **25 MJ/kg**. The plant generates **150 MW** of electrical power continuously. Calculate:
1. The **daily energy input** in MJ and kWh.
2. The **overall efficiency** of the plant. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

- **Global Mix:** Fossil fuels contribute ~80 % of primary energy; coal ≈ 27 %, oil ≈ 31 %, gas ≈ 22 %. Renewables ≈ 13 %, hydro ≈ 7 %, nuclear ≈ 4 %.
- **Indian Mix:** Coal-heavy; over 50 % of primary energy is coal-based. India is the world's second-largest consumer of coal and third-largest emitter of CO₂.
- **Renewables Boom:** India targets **500 GW** of non-fossil installed capacity by 2030; already crossed ~190 GW (2024).
- **Challenges of Energy Security:**
  1. **Import dependence** (>85 % crude oil, ~50 % gas).
  2. **Per-capita consumption gap** vs developed world.
  3. **Aging thermal fleet** with poor efficiency (~33 %).
  4. **Grid integration** of variable renewables.
  5. **Financing & land-acquisition** delays for new projects.

*(Distribute ~1.5 marks per sub-point. Use bullet/numbered lists.)*

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Daily mass of coal consumed:**
$$ m = 1500\ \text{tonnes/day} = 1500 \times 10^{3}\ \text{kg/day} = 1.5 \times 10^{6}\ \text{kg/day} $$

**Step 2 — Daily energy INPUT:**
$$ E_{\text{in}} = m \times GCV = 1.5 \times 10^{6} \times 25\ \text{MJ/day} $$

$$ E_{\text{in}} = 37.5 \times 10^{6}\ \text{MJ/day} = 3.75 \times 10^{7}\ \text{MJ/day} $$

**Step 3 — Convert MJ/day to kWh/day:**
$$ E_{\text{in}} = \frac{3.75 \times 10^{7}}{3.6} \approx 1.0417 \times 10^{7}\ \text{kWh/day} $$

$$ \boxed{\ E_{\text{in}} \approx 1.042 \times 10^{7}\ \text{kWh/day}\ } $$

**Step 4 — Daily energy OUTPUT:**
$$ E_{\text{out}} = P \times t = 150\ \text{MW} \times 24\ \text{h} = 3600\ \text{MWh/day} $$

Convert to kWh:
$$ E_{\text{out}} = 3.6 \times 10^{6}\ \text{kWh/day} $$

**Step 5 — Overall efficiency:**
$$ \eta = \frac{E_{\text{out}}}{E_{\text{in}}} \times 100 = \frac{3.6 \times 10^{6}}{1.0417 \times 10^{7}} \times 100 $$

$$ \boxed{\ \eta \approx 34.56\ \%\ } $$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - Students often forget to **convert tonnes → kg** (lose 1 mark).
> - Mixing up **MJ with kWh** without the 3.6 × 10⁶ factor (lose 1 mark).
> - Reporting efficiency as a decimal (0.345) instead of % (34.5 %) — **mandatory to include the % unit** (lose 0.5 mark).
> - Forgetting to write **units at every step** — KTU strictly enforces units in numerical answers.

---

### 📝 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ **Energy** = capacity to do work; SI unit = **Joule (J)**; Power = J/s = **Watt (W)**.
- ✅ **1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J** *(most-tested conversion)*; **1 toe = 41.868 GJ**.
- ✅ **Energy resources** are classified as **Renewable vs Non-Renewable** AND **Conventional vs Non-Conventional**.
- ✅ **Primary energy** exists naturally; **Secondary energy** is derived (electricity, gasoline).
- ✅ **Renewable sources** = Solar, Wind, Hydro, Biomass, Geothermal, Tidal, Wave, OTEC.
- ✅ **Non-Renewable sources** = Coal, Crude Oil, Natural Gas, Uranium, Thorium.
- ✅ **Dulong's Formula** for GCV uses carbon, hydrogen (corrected for oxygen), and sulphur mass %.
- ✅ **NCV = GCV − 9H × 2.26 MJ/kg** (or in kCal/kg, GCV − 0.09·H%·2242).
- ✅ **Conversion efficiency** $\eta = E_{\text{useful}} / E_{\text{input}} \times 100\,\%$, always < 100 % due to **Second Law**.
- ✅ **Energy Chain:** Resource → Extraction → Conversion → Distribution → End-use → Waste Heat.
- ✅ **India's energy security challenge:** >85 % oil import dependence; coal-dominated grid.
- ✅ **Thermal power plant efficiency** is typically only **~33–45 %** due to Carnot and irreversibility limits.
- ✅ **Calorific value units:** kJ/kg (solids/liquids); MJ/Nm³ (gases) — **never mix them**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Mantra for this Topic:**
> "Memorize the **kWh ↔ J** conversion, **Dulong's formula**, and the **GCV → NCV** correction. These three items alone can fetch **8–10 marks** in any KTU Module 1 question paper."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Global and Indian Scenario of Energy Resources and Consumption
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT614 - ENERGY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Global and Indian Scenario of Energy Resources and Consumption

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Resources** are the naturally occurring substances or phenomena that can be converted into usable forms of mechanical work, heat, or electrical power to satisfy human and industrial demands. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme, the *Global and Indian Scenario* studies the **availability, distribution, consumption pattern, and security** of these resources at the worldwide and national level, anchoring policy decisions in measurable engineering and economic metrics.

According to the IEA (International Energy Agency) framework adopted globally and reflected in MNRE (Ministry of New and Renewable Energy, Government of India) reports:

- **Primary Energy**: Energy found in nature *before* any human-conversion process (coal, crude oil, sunlight, wind, uranium, falling water).
- **Secondary Energy**: Energy derived from primary sources after conversion (electricity, gasoline, hydrogen, diesel).
- **Commercial Energy**: Sources sold commercially (coal, oil, gas, electricity, nuclear).
- **Non-Commercial Energy**: Traditional fuels collected free of cost (firewood, agricultural residue, cow-dung, charcoal).

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy Reserves** refer to deposits that are *known, economically recoverable, and technologically accessible* under current conditions. **Energy Resources** is a broader term that includes all deposits, even those not yet economically or technically viable.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for Students

Imagine the world's energy supply as a giant **kitchen pantry** for planet Earth.

- **The shelves (Reserves)** are items you can see and use *right now* — a bag of rice (coal), a bottle of cooking oil (petroleum), a gas cylinder (natural gas), a solar-lit window (sunlight), and a wind-ventilated roof (wind).
- **The cellar (Resources)** is where future potential is stored — shale gas, tidal energy, hydrogen — not yet fully harvested.
- **The family's hunger (Global Energy Demand)** keeps growing every year, especially as India and China add millions of new "mouths" (industries, vehicles, air-conditioners).
- **India's kitchen**, in particular, is **large, crowded, and under-stocked** relative to the size of its family. It has abundant *sunshine* and *coal*, but *very little oil* and *gas*. So it must import a lot from neighbours (Middle East, Russia) to keep cooking.

This is precisely what the **Global and Indian Energy Scenario** maps out — *what is in the pantry, how much is being consumed, and whether it will last for the next generation.*

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used Worldwide (and in KTU Examinations)

The following bolded metrics appear repeatedly in KTU Energy Engineering papers, MNRE reports, and IEA reviews:

- **toe (Tonne of Oil Equivalent)**: 1 toe = 41.868 GJ = 11.63 MWh. Used for cross-comparing energy forms.
- **Mtoe**: Million tonnes of oil equivalent (used at national scale).
- **Per Capita Energy Consumption (PCEC)**: Total energy consumed ÷ Total population. Units: **toe/person/year**.
- **Energy Intensity (EI)**: Energy consumed per unit of GDP. Units: **toe per million USD (or kgoe per $ of GDP)**. Lower EI = more efficient economy.
- **Reserve-to-Production Ratio (R/P)**: Number of years the reserve will last at current production rate.
- **Installed Capacity**: Total nameplate power-generation capacity in **GW (GigaWatts)**.
- **Plant Load Factor (PLF)**: Actual energy output ÷ Maximum possible output.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Conversion Reference**:
> 1 Quad = 1.055 × 10¹⁸ J = 25.2 Mtoe
> 1 BTU = 1055 J
> 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ
> 1 toe = 41.868 GJ
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Energy Resources — Structured Logic

The taxonomy used by KTU, IEA, and MNRE follows a **two-tier classification**:

**Tier 1 — By Regeneration Capability:**
- **Non-Renewable (Exhaustible)**: Coal, Crude Oil, Natural Gas, Nuclear (Uranium, Thorium). Formed over millions of years; cannot be replenished on a human timescale.
- **Renewable (Inexhaustible)**: Solar, Wind, Hydro, Biomass, Geothermal, Tidal, Wave, Ocean Thermal.

**Tier 2 — By Form / Source:**
- **Fossil Fuels**: Coal, Oil, Gas.
- **Nuclear Fuels**: U-235, U-238, Th-232, Pu-239.
- **Solar Direct**: PV, Concentrated Solar Power (CSP).
- **Solar Indirect**: Wind, Hydro, Wave, Biomass (photosynthetic origin).
- **Geothermal**: Earth's internal heat from radioactive decay.
- **Gravity-based**: Tidal (moon-sun gravity), Ocean Thermal (temperature gradient).

### 2.2 Why This Classification Matters

- **Why classify by regeneration?** Because reserves of non-renewables are *depleting* every minute, and policy must be built around transition timelines.
- **Why distinguish primary vs secondary?** Because most usable end-energy (electricity, transport fuel) is a *converted* form, and **conversion losses** (≈ 60-70 % in thermal plants) must be accounted for in efficiency studies.
- **Why commercial vs non-commercial?** Because India's rural sector still depends heavily on non-commercial biomass, which is often *unrecorded* in GDP but *immense* in quantity (≈ 25 % of total rural energy use).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tonne of Oil Equivalent | 1 toe = 41.868 × 10⁹ J | toe | Standard energy unit |
| Primary Energy → Electricity | Eₑ = Eₜ × η | kWh | η = thermal efficiency |
| Per Capita Energy Consumption | PCEC = E_total / N_pop | toe / person / year | E_total in Mtoe |
| Energy Intensity | EI = E_total / GDP | kgoe / USD (PPP) | Lower = efficient |
| Reserve-to-Production Ratio | R/P = R_reserve / P_annual | years | Life expectancy of resource |
| Annual Growth Rate of Consumption | C_t = C₀ × (1 + g)ᵗ | Mtoe | Compound growth |
| Energy Demand Projection | D_n = D₀ × (1 + r)ⁿ | Mtoe | r = demand growth rate |
| Energy from Fuel Combustion | Q = m × CV | kJ or MJ | m = mass, CV = calorific value |
| Installed Capacity to Energy | E_gen = C_inst × PLF × 8760 | kWh / year | 8760 = hours in a year |
| Capacity Utilisation Factor | CUF = E_actual / (C_inst × 8760) | dimensionless | For renewables |
| Power from Solar Irradiance | P = A × G × η | W | A = area, G = irradiance (kW/m²) |
| Wind Power Extracted | P = 0.5 × ρ × A × v³ × C_p | W | ρ = air density, v = wind speed, C_p = power coefficient (Betz max = 0.593) |
| Calorific Value Conversion (Coal) | Q_total = Σ mᵢ × CVᵢ | MJ or kcal | Weighted by mass fraction |
| Specific Fuel Consumption | SFC = m_fuel / P_output | kg / kWh | Reciprocal of efficiency |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on R/P Ratio (R/P)**: KTU frequently tests this. If India's proven coal reserve is **R = 120 billion tonnes** and annual production is **P = 0.9 billion tonnes/year**, then R/P = 120 / 0.9 = **133 years**. This static measure, however, ignores rising demand and economic shifts.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Energy Planning**: Helps governments (MNRE, CEA) decide capacity additions for the next 5-25 years.
- **Policy Design**: India targets **500 GW of non-fossil capacity by 2030** (Panchamrit commitment at COP26, Glasgow, 2021).
- **Industrial Site Selection**: PLF data influences choice of coal vs gas vs solar plants in a region.
- **Climate Modeling**: EI and PCEC are the *two principal variables* in IPCC scenarios (SSP1–SSP5).
- **Trade & Geopolitics**: Countries with low oil R/P (India R/P ≈ 14 years for crude) depend on imports, making them vulnerable to price shocks (e.g., 2022 Russia-Ukraine impact on India).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Per Capita Energy Consumption

**Problem Context (KTU-Style Numerical):**
India's primary energy consumption in 2023 was approximately **36 EJ**. India's population in the same year was approximately **1.43 × 10⁹**. Compute the per capita energy consumption in **toe/person/year**.

**Step 1**: Write the formula.

$$
PCEC = \frac{E_{\text{total}}}{N_{\text{pop}}}
$$

**Step 2**: Convert total energy from Exajoules (EJ) to toe. Recall the standard:
1 EJ = 10¹⁸ J. And 1 toe = 41.868 × 10⁹ J.

$$
1 \text{ EJ} = \frac{10^{18} \text{ J}}{41.868 \times 10^{9} \text{ J/toe}} = 23.884 \times 10^{6} \text{ toe} = 23.884 \text{ Mtoe}
$$

**Step 3**: Convert India's 36 EJ into Mtoe.

$$
E_{\text{India}} = 36 \text{ EJ} \times 23.884 \text{ Mtoe/EJ} = 859.82 \text{ Mtoe}
$$

**Step 4**: Substitute values into the PCEC formula.

$$
PCEC = \frac{859.82 \times 10^{6} \text{ toe}}{1.43 \times 10^{9} \text{ people}} = 0.6012 \text{ toe/person/year}
$$

**Step 5**: Interpret the result.

$$
\boxed{PCEC_{\text{India}} \approx 0.60 \text{ toe/person/year}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: India's per capita energy consumption is **less than 0.6 toe**, which is roughly **one-third of the world average (~1.7 toe)** and **one-tenth of the USA (~7 toe)**. This indicates significant headroom for industrial growth but also highlights the *energy poverty* challenge.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — R/P Ratio and Years of Coal Left in India

**Problem Context:**
Given:
- India's proven coal reserves ≈ **R = 120 billion tonnes (Bt)**.
- India's annual coal production ≈ **P = 0.9 Bt/year**.
- India's annual coal *consumption* (including imports) is rising at a growth rate of **g = 4 % per year**.

Find: (a) Static R/P, (b) Dynamic R/P accounting for 4 % growth.

**Step 1**: Compute static R/P (the standard KTU formula).

$$
(R/P)_{\text{static}} = \frac{R}{P} = \frac{120 \text{ Bt}}{0.9 \text{ Bt/yr}} = 133.33 \text{ years}
$$

**Step 2**: For dynamic R/P, we model consumption as a *growing* geometric series.

Cumulative consumption over *n* years:

$$
\text{Consumed}(n) = P \times \frac{(1+g)^{n} - 1}{g}
$$

**Step 3**: Set Cumulative consumption = Total reserve R, solve for n.

$$
R = P \times \frac{(1+g)^{n} - 1}{g}
$$

$$
\frac{R \cdot g}{P} + 1 = (1+g)^{n}
$$

$$
n = \frac{\ln\!\left(1 + \dfrac{R \cdot g}{P}\right)}{\ln(1+g)}
$$

**Step 4**: Substitute numbers.

$$
n = \frac{\ln\!\left(1 + \dfrac{120 \times 0.04}{0.9}\right)}{\ln(1.04)} = \frac{\ln(1 + 5.333)}{\ln(1.04)}
$$

$$
n = \frac{\ln(6.333)}{0.03922} = \frac{1.8458}{0.03922}
$$

$$
\boxed{n \approx 47.06 \text{ years}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dynamic vs Static R/P — A Critical Insight**: Static R/P says coal will last 133 years, but the *dynamic* (compounding) figure drops to **~47 years** at 4 % growth. KTU examiners love this comparison — it shows that *reserves deplete much faster than naive ratios suggest* when demand grows.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Energy from Coal Combustion and Conversion to Electricity

**Problem Context:**
A thermal power plant consumes **m = 1 tonne of coal per hour**. The coal has a calorific value **CV = 5000 kcal/kg**. The plant's thermal efficiency is **η = 35 %**. Find (a) Heat released per hour, (b) Electrical energy output per hour in kWh, (c) Annual energy output assuming **PLF = 0.75**.

**Step 1**: Heat released per hour from coal combustion.

$$
Q = m \times CV = 1000 \text{ kg} \times 5000 \text{ kcal/kg} = 5{,}000{,}000 \text{ kcal/hr}
$$

**Step 2**: Convert kcal to kJ (1 kcal = 4.1868 kJ).

$$
Q = 5{,}000{,}000 \times 4.1868 = 2.0934 \times 10^{7} \text{ kJ/hr}
$$

**Step 3**: Convert kJ to kWh (1 kWh = 3600 kJ).

$$
Q = \frac{2.0934 \times 10^{7}}{3600} = 5815 \text{ kWh (thermal) per hour}
$$

**Step 4**: Apply thermal efficiency to get electrical output.

$$
E_{\text{elec/hr}} = Q \times \eta = 5815 \times 0.35 = 2035.25 \text{ kWh/hr}
$$

**Step 5**: Annual electrical output with PLF = 0.75.

$$
E_{\text{annual}} = 2035.25 \times 8760 \times 0.75
$$

$$
E_{\text{annual}} = 2035.25 \times 6570 = 1.337 \times 10^{7} \text{ kWh/year}
$$

$$
\boxed{E_{\text{annual}} \approx 13.37 \text{ GWh/year}}
$$

---

| Energy Intensity (toe / million USD PPP) | ~0.11 | ~0.15 | ~0.10 | ~0.18 |
| Coal Share in Mix (%) | ~27 | ~55 | ~10 | ~55 |
| Oil Share (%) | ~31 | ~25 | ~37 | ~19 |

KTU may quote slightly different year data; the **methodology and ratios are what examiners grade.**

### 3.5 Worked Example — Solar Energy Potential at a Site

**Problem**: A location receives **G = 5.5 kWh/m²/day** (PSH – Peak Sun Hours). A solar PV plant has area **A = 10,000 m²**, module efficiency **η = 18 %**, system losses **= 20 %**. Find the daily and annual energy generation.

**Step 1**: Compute the *incident* solar energy per day on the area.

$$
E_{\text{incident}} = G \times A = 5.5 \times 10{,}000 = 55{,}000 \text{ kWh/day}
$$

**Step 2**: Apply module efficiency.

$$
E_{\text{module}} = 55{,}000 \times 0.18 = 9900 \text{ kWh/day}
$$

**Step 3**: Apply system losses (Performance Ratio, PR = 0.80).

$$
E_{\text{output}} = 9900 \times 0.80 = 7920 \text{ kWh/day}
$$

**Step 4**: Annual generation (365 days).

$$
E_{\text{annual}} = 7920 \times 365 = 2.89 \times 10^{6} \text{ kWh/year} = 2.89 \text{ GWh/year}
$$

$$
\boxed{E_{\text{annual}} \approx 2.89 \text{ GWh}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This calculation style is **exactly the format** expected in KTU 14-mark questions. Always show *all* four steps — incident, conversion, losses, and scaling.
