KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** > *Define the Perform, Achieve and Trade (PAT) scheme. State any two objectives of the scheme.* **Model Answer:** The Perform, Achieve and Trade (PAT) scheme is a market-based energy efficiency mechanism launched by the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)** in **2012** under the **National Mission on Enhanced Energy Efficiency (NMEEE)**. It mandates energy-intensive industries, termed **Designated Consumers (DCs)**, to achieve specific energy consumption reduction targets over a 3-year cycle. Industries that exceed their targets receive **Energy Saving Certificates (ESCerts)**, which are tradable on Indian energy exchanges. **Two objectives:** 1. To reduce the **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** of energy-intensive industrial sectors below a notified baseline. 2. To create a **market mechanism for trading energy efficiency** through tradable ESCerts, enabling cost-effective compliance. ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > *What is the role of the Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) in India's energy policy framework? Mention any two programs of BEE.* **Model Answer:** The **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)**, established on **1st March 2002** under the **Energy Conservation Act, 2001**, is the statutory body responsible for spearheading energy efficiency initiatives in India. It functions under the **Ministry of Power, Government of India**, and coordinates with **State Designated Agencies (SDAs)** in all states and Union Territories. **Two programs of BEE:** 1. **Standards and Labeling Program** — A consumer information initiative providing star ratings (1–5) for appliances such as air conditioners, refrigerators, ceiling fans, and LED lamps. 2. **Energy Conservation Building Code (ECBC)** — A code prescribing energy performance standards for commercial buildings with connected load $\geq 100$ kW or contract demand $\geq 120$ kVA. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A: Detailed Question on PAT Scheme (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > *(a) Explain the operational methodology of the Perform, Achieve and Trade (PAT) scheme with the help of a flow diagram. Discuss its significance as a market-based mechanism for promoting industrial energy efficiency.* **(7 Marks)** > > *(b) A steel plant Designated Consumer has the following data for a PAT cycle: Baseline average SEC = 1.20 toe/tonne of crude steel; Production in baseline period = 5.0 × 10⁵ tonnes; Current year SEC = 1.08 toe/tonne; Current year production = 6.0 × 10⁵ tonnes; Target SEC = 1.14 toe/tonne. Calculate (i) Normalized Energy Consumption, (ii) Net Energy Saving in toe, (iii) Number of ESCerts eligible, and (iv) Avoided CO₂ emissions, assuming an emission factor of 2.2 tCO₂/toe.* **(7 Marks)** ### Model Solution for Question A #### Part (a) — Operational Methodology of PAT (7 Marks) **Step 1: Identification of Sectors and DCs** The Ministry of Power, on BEE's recommendation, notifies **energy-intensive sectors** such as thermal power plants, cement, iron and steel, aluminum, fertilizer, paper and pulp, textiles, chlor-alkali, petrochemicals, and refineries. Within each sector, units exceeding a **threshold energy consumption** (e.g., $>$ 30,000 toe/year for steel) are declared as **Designated Consumers (DCs)**. **Step 2: Baseline Determination** For each DC, the **average Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** of the **preceding three years** is established as the **baseline SEC**. This serves as the reference for measuring improvement. **Step 3: Target Notification** BEE notifies a **mandatory reduction target** for each DC, typically between 1.5% to 5% of baseline SEC, depending on sectoral potential and technology maturity. **Step 4: Compliance Period** DCs implement energy efficiency measures over a **3-year cycle**. They can adopt any combination of approaches — process optimization, equipment upgrade, waste heat recovery, etc. **Step 5: Verification and Certification** At the end of the cycle, **BEE-accredited Energy Auditors (EAEm)** verify the achieved SEC through **Mandatory Energy Audits**. Normalization is applied to account for variations in production, weather, and raw material quality. **Step 6: Issuance of ESCerts** - If achieved SEC $\leq$ target SEC → **ESCerts issued** (1 ESCert = 1 toe of verified savings) - If achieved SEC $>$ target SEC → **Shortfall; DC must purchase ESCerts** from the market **Step 7: Trading** ESCerts are traded on the **Indian Energy Exchange (IEX)** and **Power Exchange India Limited (PXIL)**, with market-determined prices. **Significance:** - Achieves energy savings at **lowest cost** across the economy - Provides **financial incentive** (via ESCert sale) to overachievers - Creates a **compliance market** similar to carbon markets globally - Has demonstrated **>10 Mtoe cumulative savings** across Cycles I–VII #### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 Marks) **Given Data:** - Baseline SEC = $1.20$ toe/tonne - Baseline production = $5.0 \times 10^5$ tonnes - Current SEC = $1.08$ toe/tonne - Current production = $6.0 \times 10^5$ tonnes - Target SEC = $1.14$ toe/tonne - Emission factor = $2.2 \; \text{tCO}_2/\text{toe}$ **Step 1: Calculate Baseline Energy Consumption (BEC)** $$ BEC = SEC_{baseline} \times P_{baseline} $$ $$ BEC = 1.20 \times 5.0 \times 10^{5} = 6.0 \times 10^{5} \; \text{toe} $$ **Step 2: Calculate Current Energy Consumption (CEC)** $$ CEC = SEC_{current} \times P_{current} $$ $$ CEC = 1.08 \times 6.0 \times 10^{5} = 6.48 \times 10^{5} \; \text{toe} $$ **Step 3: Calculate Normalized Energy Consumption (NEC)** $$ NEC = SEC_{baseline} \times P_{current} $$ $$ NEC = 1.20 \times 6.0 \times 10^{5} = 7.2 \times 10^{5} \; \text{toe} $$ **Step 4: Calculate Net Energy Saving (NES)** $$ NES = NEC - CEC $$ $$ NES = 7.2 \times 10^{5} - 6.48 \times 10^{5} = 0.72 \times 10^{5} \; \text{toe} $$ $$ \boxed{NES = 72{,}000 \; \text{toe}} $$ **Step 5: Verify Target Compliance** Since $1.08 < 1.14$ (target), the DC has **over-achieved** and is eligible for ESCerts. **Step 6: ESCerts Issued** $$ \boxed{ESCerts = 72{,}000 \; \text{ESCerts}} $$ **Step 7: Avoided CO₂ Emissions** $$ CO_{2,\text{avoided}} = 72{,}000 \times 2.2 $$ $$ \boxed{CO_{2,\text{avoided}} = 158{,}400 \; \text{tCO}_2\text{e}} $$ --- ### Question B: Alternative 14-Mark Question on Energy Policy Framework **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** > *(a) Discuss the salient features of the Energy Conservation Act, 2001 (with its 2010 and 2022 amendments). Explain how it forms the legal backbone of India's energy efficiency regime.* **(7 Marks)** > > *(b) List the eight National Missions under the National Action Plan on Climate Change (NAPCC). Explain in detail the four key initiatives of the National Mission on Enhanced Energy Efficiency (NMEEE).* **(7 Marks)** ### Model Solution for Question B #### Part (a) — Salient Features of EC Act, 2001 (7 Marks) **1. Establishment of Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)** Section 23 establishes BEE as a statutory body, headquartered in New Delhi, with **five zonal offices**. It is governed by a **Governing Council** chaired by the Secretary, Ministry of Power. **2. Standards and Labeling of Equipment (Section 14)** - Mandates **Minimum Energy Performance Standards (MEPS)** for notified equipment - Voluntary **star labeling** for consumer appliances (currently 24 categories) - Mandatory labeling for **transformers, motors, air conditioners** **3. Energy Conservation Building Code (Section 14-A)** - Inserted via the **2010 Amendment** - Applicable to commercial buildings with connected load $\geq 100$ kW - States/UTs adopt and enforce ECBC through municipal bylaws **4. Energy Audit of Designated Consumers (Section 15)** - DCs must conduct **Mandatory Energy Audits** every 3 years - Audits to be performed by **Certified Energy Auditors (CEA)** or **Certified Energy Managers (CEM)** - Records to be maintained and submitted to BEE **5. Establishment of Energy Conservation Fund** - Section 22 provides for a dedicated **Energy Conservation Fund** to support BEE's activities - Funded through parliamentary grants, fees, and penalties **6. Penalties (Section 26)** - Non-compliance with equipment standards: **₹ 10,000 per equipment** + **₹ 1,000 per day** of continued default - Non-compliance of DCs: **Up to ₹ 1 lakh** penalty - Penalties adjudicated by designated **appellate tribunals** **7. 2022 Amendment — Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS)** - Introduced **CCTS** as India's first **compliance-grade carbon market** - Establishes an **Indian Carbon Market (ICM)** governed by a new framework - Aligns India with global Article 6 of the Paris Agreement #### Part (b) — Eight NAPCC Missions and NMEEE Initiatives (7 Marks) **Eight Missions of NAPCC (2008):** 1. **Jawaharlal Nehru National Solar Mission (JNNSM)** — Target: 100 GW solar by 2022; revised to 300 GW by 2030 2. **National Mission for Enhanced Energy Efficiency (NMEEE)** — Industrial & building efficiency 3. **National Mission on Sustainable Habitat** — Urban transport, ECBC 4. **National Water Mission** — 20% increase in water use efficiency 5. **National Mission for Sustaining the Himalayan Ecosystem** — Glacier & biodiversity 6. **National Mission for a Green India** — 6 million hectares of afforestation 7. **National Mission for Sustainable Agriculture** — Climate-resilient crops 8. **National Mission on Strategic Knowledge for Climate Change** — R&D **Four Key Initiatives of NMEEE:** 1. **Perform, Achieve and Trade (PAT)** - Market-based mechanism for industrial energy efficiency - Tradable Energy Saving Certificates (ESCerts) - 7 completed cycles; ongoing expansion to new sectors 2. **Market Transformation for Energy Efficiency (MTEE)** - Risk-sharing financing facility for SMEs - Partial Risk Guarantee Fund for Energy Efficiency (PRGFEE) - Venture Capital Fund for Energy Efficiency (VCEE) 3. **Energy Efficiency Financing Platform (EEFP)** - Coordination with banks/financial institutions - Concessional loans and credit lines for EE projects - Public-Private Partnership (PPP) model 4. **Framework for Energy Efficient Economic Development (FEEED)** - Fiscal incentives including **Accelerated Depreciation (AD)** for EE equipment - Customs duty exemptions on EE capital goods - Framework to enable CDM projects in India > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > > **Common mistakes students make that cost marks:** > > 1. **Confusing BEE and BEE Star Rating** — BEE is the *body*; star rating is a *program* of BEE. Don't use them interchangeably. > 2. **Wrong unit in PAT calculation** — SEC is in **toe/tonne**, not kWh/tonne. Mixing units is the #1 reason for full mark deduction in numerical problems. > 3. **Forgetting Normalization** — In PAT numericals, you **must** calculate NEC using *baseline SEC* with *current production*. Students often use baseline production with both SECs, leading to wrong NES. > 4. **Not distinguishing EC Act 2001 from ECBC** — EC Act is the *law*; ECBC is the *building code* enabled by the Act. The 2010 Amendment added ECBC to the Act. > 5. **Missing the 2022 Amendment** — The CCTS is a recent and frequently tested addition. Students who only memorize pre-2022 content lose 2–3 marks. > 6. **Confusing RPO and REC** — RPO is the *obligation* (mandate); REC is the *certificate* (mechanism to fulfill RPO). > 7. **Writing "NAPCC has 8 missions" but only listing 4–5** — Always list all eight with brief one-line descriptions to secure full marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Energy Policies** - [x] **Energy Policy Definition:** A structured framework of laws, regulations, and plans to govern energy production, distribution, and consumption. - [x] **Three Pillars of Energy Policy:** Energy Security, Energy Equity, Environmental Sustainability — the **"Energy Trilemma"**. - [x] **Energy Conservation Act, 2001:** Primary legislation; amended in **2010** (ECBC added) and **2022** (CCTS added). - [x] **BEE (Bureau of Energy Efficiency):** Established **1st March 2002** under Section 23 of EC Act; functions under **Ministry of Power**. - [x] **Star Labeling:** Voluntary consumer information program covering **24 product categories**; ratings from 1 to 5. - [x] **ECBC:** Applicable to commercial buildings with **connected load $\geq 100$ kW** or **contract demand $\geq 120$ kVA**. - [x] **PAT Scheme:** Launched **2012**; operates in 3-year cycles; current cycle is **PAT Cycle-VII (2021–2024)**; uses **ESCerts (1 ESCert = 1 toe)**. - [x] **PAT Calculation Master Formula:** $$NES = (SEC_{baseline} \times P_{current}) - (SEC_{current} \times P_{current})$$ - [x] **Normalization:** Always use *baseline SEC* with *current production* to calculate **NEC** for fair comparison. - [x] **NAPCC (2008):** Eight National Missions, of which **NMEEE** is the most relevant for this course. - [x] **NMEEE's Four Initiatives:** PAT, MTEE, EEFP, FEEED. - [x] **Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS):** Introduced in **2022** amendment; establishes India's first compliance carbon market. - [x] **RPO (Renewable Purchase Obligation):** Mandate on DISCOMs/industries to procure a minimum % of renewable energy; backed by RECs. - [x] **Penalties under EC Act:** ₹ 10,000 per equipment default + ₹ 1,000/day; up to ₹ 1 lakh for DC non-compliance. - [x] **Emission Factor (India grid):** $\approx 0.71 \; \text{tCO}_2/\text{MWh}$ (CEA published value). - [x] **India's International Commitment:** **500 GW non-fossil capacity by 2030** (announced at COP-26, Glasgow, 2021); **Net Zero by 2070**. ### Quick-Recall Mnemonics > **"BEE" = Body for Energy Efficiency** (not to be confused with bee the insect!) > > **"PAT" = Perform (achieve target), Achieve (comply), Trade (sell excess ESCerts)** > > **"NAPCC" = 8 Ns** — N for Solar, N for Energy Efficiency, N for Habitat, N for Water, N for Himalayas, N for Green India, N for Agriculture, N for Knowledge > > **"ECBC" = Energy Conservation Building Code** — applicable only to Commercial buildings, not residential > > **"RPO" = Renewable Purchase Obligation** — for the buyer; **"REC" = Renewable Energy Certificate** — the tradable instrument

topics.title: Development and Environment. Conventional Energy Conversion Plants - Thermal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT614 - ENERGY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Development and Environment. Conventional Energy Conversion Plants - Thermal

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Development and Environment

**Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
*Development* refers to the progressive economic, social, and technological transformation of a society aimed at improving the standard of living, human welfare, industrialization, and access to modern infrastructure. *Environment* constitutes the sum of all surrounding biotic and abiotic conditions — air, water, soil, flora, fauna — that sustain life and ecological balance. The *Development–Environment Nexus* is the bidirectional relationship in which development activities exploit environmental resources while environmental degradation constrains future developmental potential.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sustainable Development (Brundtland Commission, 1987):** "Development that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs." This is the foundational definition every KTU examiner expects verbatim in Module 1.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a savings bank account. Your salary (natural resources) is deposited monthly, but if you withdraw more than you deposit (over-exploitation), the account empties — and your future (development of next generation) collapses. **Sustainable development** is the discipline of *spending only the interest*, not the principal. Similarly, industrialization (thermal power plants, factories) accelerates "withdrawals" from the Earth's ecological capital, while the planet's regenerative capacity — the "deposit rate" — is fixed. The atmospheric CO₂ concentration has crossed **420 ppm** (2024 average) versus the pre-industrial baseline of **280 ppm** — the account is overdrawn.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Box:**
> - **Energy** = Capacity to do work or transfer heat (SI Unit: **Joule**, J)
> - **Power** = Rate of energy transfer (SI Unit: **Watt**, W = J/s)
> - **Specific Energy** = Energy per unit mass (J/kg)
> - **Energy Density** = Energy per unit volume (J/m³)
> - **Heating Value** = Heat released per unit fuel mass (MJ/kg) or volume (MJ/m³)

### 1.2 Conventional Energy

**Conventional Energy Sources** are non-renewable, fossil-based, hydro-carbon energy resources that have been the dominant primary energy supply since the Industrial Revolution. They include **coal, lignite, petroleum (crude oil), natural gas, and nuclear (fission) energy**. These are termed "conventional" because the technology, supply chain, and extraction methods are mature and globally standardized.

### 1.3 Conventional Energy Conversion Plants — Thermal

**Definition:**
A *Thermal Power Plant* (TPP) is an industrial facility that converts the chemical energy stored in fossil fuels (coal, oil, natural gas) into electrical energy through a thermodynamic cycle in which heat is the intermediate working medium. The conversion follows the chain: **Fuel Chemical Energy → Heat (Combustion) → Mechanical Work (Turbine) → Electrical Energy (Generator).**

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a thermal power plant as a giant tea-kettle wired to a fan. You burn fuel to boil water (heat). The high-pressure steam pushes blades (turbine) that spin a magnet inside copper coils (generator) — and that spinning magnet pushes electrons to flow, giving you electricity. The water condenses back and is reused — a *cyclic* process. The fundamental difference from a kettle: the pressure is **~250 bar** (not atmospheric) and the temperatures reach **600 °C** (not 100 °C), making the thermodynamic efficiency meaningful.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constants for Thermal Power Calculations:**
> - **1 kcal = 4.186 kJ** (mechanical equivalent of heat)
> - **1 kWh = 3.6 MJ = 860 kcal**
> - **Universal Gas Constant:** R = 8.314 kJ/(kmol·K)
> - **Standard Temperature & Pressure (STP):** T = 273.15 K, P = 101.325 kPa
> - **Net Calorific Value (NCV) of Coal** ≈ **20–30 MJ/kg** (vs. GCV 25–35 MJ/kg)
> - **Carbon emission factor of coal** ≈ **0.095 tCO₂/GJ** (IPCC default)

### 1.4 Why Thermal? India's Energy Context

India's installed power capacity (as of 2024) is dominated by thermal sources: **coal (~50%)**, **gas (~6%)**, **diesel (~0.1%)**, with the remainder from renewables, hydro, and nuclear. The KTU 2024 syllabus places strong emphasis on this because Kerala and Indian engineering graduates will, in practice, design, operate, audit, and decommission thermal plants under the Ministry of Power, CEA, and CERC regulatory frameworks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Development–Environment Conflict — Structural Analysis

The relationship between development and environment is governed by three structural facts:

1. **Resource Dependency:** All economic activity ultimately draws inputs from the environment (raw materials, energy carriers, water, land).
2. **Residue Generation:** Every conversion process returns waste — emissions (CO₂, SOₓ, NOₓ), effluents, and solid by-products (fly ash, bottom ash, sludge).
3. **Carrying Capacity Constraint:** Earth's regenerative and assimilative capacities are finite. Beyond a threshold, environmental degradation becomes irreversible (climate tipping points, biodiversity loss).

**The Environmental Kuznets Curve (EKC)** posits an inverted-U relationship between per-capita income and environmental degradation: pollution initially rises with industrialization, peaks at a transitional income, and then declines as societies invest in cleaner technology. While criticized empirically, EKC is a standard KTU reference for the *transition phase* that India is currently traversing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Carrying Capacity (CC):** The maximum population size of a species that an ecosystem can sustain indefinitely, given the available resources. KTU Module 1 expects: *CC = f(Resources, Technology, Lifestyle)* — it is not a fixed number.

### 2.2 Classification of Conventional Energy Conversion Plants

Conventional thermal plants are classified by:

- **By Fuel:** Coal-fired, Oil-fired, Gas-fired, Nuclear (fission), Biomass (sometimes classified as renewable but thermodynamically thermal).
- **By Cycle:** Rankine (steam), Brayton (gas), Combined Cycle, Cogeneration.
- **By Pressure:** Subcritical (< 170 bar), Supercritical (170–250 bar), Ultra-supercritical (> 250 bar).
- **By Condenser Cooling:** Once-through (river/sea), Closed-loop (cooling tower).

### 2.3 Working Principle of a Coal-Based Thermal Power Plant

A modern thermal power plant operates on the **Rankine Cycle** with the following sequential stages:

1. **Coal Handling & Pulverization:** Coal is crushed to powder (< 75 µm) to increase surface area for efficient combustion.
2. **Combustion (Boiler / Furnace):** Pulverized coal is burned with preheated air in a furnace. Heat is transferred to water in tubes lining the furnace walls.
3. **Steam Generation & Superheating:** Water → saturated steam → superheated steam (T = 600 °C, P = 250 bar in supercritical units).
4. **Turbine Expansion:** High-energy steam expands through High-Pressure (HP), Intermediate-Pressure (IP), and Low-Pressure (LP) turbines, converting enthalpy into rotational mechanical work.
5. **Condensation:** Low-pressure exhaust steam is condensed in a condenser using cooling water (CW system) at ~0.05 bar (corresponding saturation temperature ≈ 32 °C).
6. **Feedwater Pumping:** Condensate is pressurized by the Condensate Extraction Pump (CEP) and Boiler Feed Pump (BFP) back into the boiler.
7. **Power Generation:** The turbine shaft drives a synchronous generator (typically 500 MW, 1100 MW, 660 MW units in India).
8. **Auxiliaries:** Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP), Flue Gas Desulfurization (FGD), ash handling, ID/FD/PA fans.

### 2.4 The Rankine Cycle — Theoretical Foundation

The Rankine cycle is the idealized thermodynamic cycle for steam power plants. It consists of four processes:

| Process | Description | Thermodynamic Process |
|---------|-------------|------------------------|
| 1 → 2 | Pump (compress liquid) | Isentropic compression |
| 2 → 3 | Boiler (heat addition) | Isobaric heat addition |
| 3 → 4 | Turbine (expand steam) | Isentropic expansion |
| 4 → 1 | Condenser (heat rejection) | Isobaric heat rejection |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — Use \vert instead of \vert for absolute values to preserve markdown integrity.**

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---------------------|-----------|-------|------------------|
| 1 | Thermal Efficiency (η_th) | η_th = W_net / Q_in | dimensionless | Fraction of heat input converted to work |
| 2 | Net Work Output | W_net = W_turbine − W_pump | kJ/kg | Useful work per kg of steam |
| 3 | Turbine Work | W_T = h₃ − h₄ | kJ/kg | Enthalpy drop across turbine |
| 4 | Pump Work | W_P = h₂ − h₁ | kJ/kg | Enthalpy rise across pump |
| 5 | Heat Added (Boiler) | Q_in = h₃ − h₂ | kJ/kg | Heat input per kg of working fluid |
| 6 | Heat Rejected (Condenser) | Q_out = h₄ − h₁ | kJ/kg | Waste heat to cooling water |
| 7 | Rankine Efficiency | η_Rankine = (h₃ − h₄ − h₂ + h₁) / (h₃ − h₂) | dimensionless | Cycle thermal efficiency |
| 8 | Carnot (Reference) Efficiency | η_Carnot = 1 − T₁ / T₃ | dimensionless | Upper theoretical limit |
| 9 | Specific Steam Consumption (SSC) | SSC = 3600 / (W_net) | kg/kWh | Steam required per kWh |
| 10 | Plant Heat Rate (HR) | HR = Q_in / W_net | kJ/kWh | Heat input per kWh generated |
| 11 | Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC) | SFC = HR / (GCV of fuel) | kg/kWh | Fuel required per kWh |
| 12 | Plant Load Factor (PLF) | PLF = Actual Energy / (Max Capacity × Hours) | dimensionless | Utilization factor |
| 13 | Capacity Utilization Factor (CUF) | CUF = Annual Energy / (Installed Cap × 8760) | dimensionless | Annual performance index |
| 14 | Aux. Power Consumption (APC) | APC = (Aux Load / Gross Load) × 100 | % | In-plant power usage |
| 15 | Net Plant Efficiency | η_net = η_Rankine × (1 − APC) × η_gen | dimensionless | End-to-end efficiency |
| 16 | First-Law Energy Balance | Q_in = W_net + Q_out | kJ/kg | Energy conservation |
| 17 | Carbon Emission Rate | m_CO₂ = m_fuel × EF_fuel | tCO₂/h | CO₂ emitted per hour |
| 18 | Stack Heat Loss | Q_stack = m_gas × Cp × (T_stack − T_amb) | kW | Sensible loss in flue gas |
| 19 | Air-Fuel Ratio (Stoichiometric) | A/F = (O₂% × 32/0.21) / (C% × 32/12 + ...) | kg air/kg fuel | Combustion air requirement |
| 20 | Carnot Factor (Relative Efficiency) | η_relative = η_Rankine / η_Carnot | dimensionless | Approach to ideal |

**Critical Variables:**
- h₁ = saturated liquid enthalpy at condenser pressure
- h₂ = compressed liquid enthalpy at boiler pressure
- h₃ = superheated steam enthalpy at turbine inlet
- h₄ = exhaust steam enthalpy at turbine outlet (often two-phase)
- T₃ = turbine inlet temperature (K); T₁ = condenser temperature (K)

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Rankine cycle governs **every** steam-based industrial process: thermal power generation, nuclear power plants, solar thermal (parabolic trough, solar tower), geothermal binary plants, and even ship propulsion (some marine boilers). Mastering its analysis is a *gateway skill* for any power-plant engineer, refrigeration engineer, or process designer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Standard Benchmarks (KTU Exam Favorites):**
> - Subcritical plant η_net ≈ **33–37 %**
> - Supercritical plant η_net ≈ **40–42 %**
> - Ultra-supercritical plant η_net ≈ **45–47 %**
> - Combined Cycle Gas Turbine (CCGT) η ≈ **58–62 %** (uses Brayton + Rankine)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Rankine Cycle Thermal Efficiency (η_Rankine)

We derive the cycle efficiency using steady-flow energy equations (SFEE) for each component, neglecting kinetic and potential energy changes.

**Step 1: Define the four state points.**

State 1 — saturated liquid at condenser pressure P₁.
State 2 — compressed liquid at boiler pressure P₂, after pump.
State 3 — superheated steam at boiler exit / turbine inlet (P₂, T₃).
State 4 — wet steam at turbine exit (P₁, quality x₄).

**Step 2: Apply SFEE to the Boiler (Heat Addition).**

Energy in + Energy out = 0 (steady state)

Heat added per kg of working fluid:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{in} &= h_3 - h_2 \quad \text{(Boiler, isobaric heat input, kJ/kg)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

The boiler receives saturated water at h₂ and delivers superheated steam at h₃. No work is done in the boiler, so Q_in equals the enthalpy rise of the working fluid.

**Step 3: Apply SFEE to the Turbine (Work Output).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{T} &= h_3 - h_4 \quad \text{(Turbine, isentropic expansion, kJ/kg)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Steam enters at h₃ and exits at h₄, doing work equal to the enthalpy drop.

**Step 4: Apply SFEE to the Condenser (Heat Rejection).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{out} &= h_4 - h_1 \quad \text{(Condenser, isobaric heat rejection, kJ/kg)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Wet steam at h₄ condenses to saturated liquid at h₁, releasing latent heat to the cooling water.

**Step 5: Apply SFEE to the Pump (Work Input).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{P} &= h_2 - h_1 \quad \text{(Pump, isentropic compression, kJ/kg)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

The pump raises the pressure of the condensate from P₁ to P₂. Pump work is small (~0.5–1 % of turbine work) but must be subtracted.

**Step 6: Compute Net Work Output.**

By energy conservation, the net work extracted from the cycle per kg of working fluid:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{net} &= W_{T} - W_{P} \\
        &= (h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1) \\
        &= (h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1) \quad \text{[kJ/kg]} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Apply the First Law to the Cycle.**

For a complete closed cycle, the first law demands:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{in} &= W_{net} + Q_{out} \\
\Rightarrow \quad W_{net} &= Q_{in} - Q_{out} \\
\Rightarrow \quad W_{net} &= (h_3 - h_2) - (h_4 - h_1) \quad \text{[kJ/kg]} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8: Define Cycle Thermal Efficiency.**

Thermal efficiency is the ratio of useful work output to heat input:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{Rankine} &= \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} \\
               &= \frac{(h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1)}{h_3 - h_2} \\
               &= 1 - \frac{(h_4 - h_1)}{(h_3 - h_2)} \quad \text{[dimensionless]} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **canonical KTU formula** for Rankine efficiency.

**Step 9: Compare with Carnot Reference Efficiency.**

For benchmarking, the Carnot efficiency between T₃ (max cycle temperature) and T₁ (condenser temperature) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{Carnot} &= 1 - \frac{T_1}{T_3} \quad \text{[dimensionless]} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

The Rankine cycle is always less efficient than Carnot operating between the same temperature limits because of irreversibilities (finite temperature difference in boiler, two-phase expansion in turbine, sub-cooled liquid pumping).

### 3.2 Derivation: Specific Steam Consumption (SSC)

Specific Steam Consumption is the mass of steam required to generate 1 kWh of electrical energy.

**Step 1:** 1 kWh of electrical energy = 3600 kJ.

**Step 2:** Each kg of steam produces W_net kJ of work.

**Step 3:** Therefore, mass of steam needed for 3600 kJ:

$$
\begin{aligned}
SSC &= \frac{3600}{W_{net}} = \frac{3600}{(h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1)} \quad \text{[kg/kWh]} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Sanity Check:** For a typical supercritical plant, W_net ≈ 1300 kJ/kg, so SSC ≈ 3600/1300 ≈ **2.77 kg/kWh**. This matches industry data.

### 3.3 Derivation: Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC)

**Step 1:** Heat Rate (HR) is heat input per kWh generated:

$$
\begin{aligned}
HR &= \frac{3600 \times Q_{in}}{\eta_{Rankine} \times W_{net}} \quad \text{[kJ/kWh]} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Simpler form** — using 1/W_net ratio:

$$
\begin{aligned}
HR &= \frac{3600}{\eta_{Rankine}} \quad \text{[kJ/kWh]} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — let me correct: HR is the *total* heat input needed to produce 1 kWh, and equals Q_in per kg multiplied by SSC. The cleanest definition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
HR &= Q_{in} \times SSC = (h_3 - h_2) \times \frac{3600}{(h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1)} \quad \text{[kJ/kWh]} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Specific Fuel Consumption is fuel mass per kWh:

$$
\begin{aligned}
SFC &= \frac{HR}{GCV_{fuel}} \quad \text{[kg/kWh]} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

where GCV is the Gross Calorific Value of the fuel (in kJ/kg).

**Numerical Example (India's NTPC standard, 500 MW unit):**
- η_Rankine ≈ 0.40
- HR = 3600 / 0.40 = 9000 kJ/kWh
- GCV_coal ≈ 20,000 kJ/kg
- SFC = 9000 / 20000 = **0.45 kg/kWh** ≈ 0.5 kg of coal per kWh — matches NTPC benchmark!

### 3.4 Derivation: Net Plant Electrical Efficiency

End-to-end efficiency from fuel to grid:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{net} &= \eta_{boiler} \times \eta_{cycle} \times \eta_{mech} \times \eta_{gen} \times (1 - APC) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

where APC is the auxiliary power consumption fraction.

**Typical Indian Super Thermal Power Station:**
- η_boiler (combustion efficiency) ≈ 90 %
- η_cycle (Rankine) ≈ 40 %
- η_mech (turbine-generator) ≈ 98 %
- η_gen (electrical) ≈ 98 %
- APC ≈ 8 %

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{net} &= 0.90 \times 0.40 \times 0.98 \times 0.98 \times (1 - 0.08) \\
           &= 0.90 \times 0.40 \times 0.98 \times 0.98 \times 0.92 \\
           &= 0.318 \approx 31.8\% \\
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the actual figure (~32–35 %) reported for Indian coal-fired plants.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Variables that Increase η_Rankine

| Lever | Mechanism | Effect on η | Practical Limit |
|-------|-----------|-------------|-----------------|
| ↑ Boiler Pressure (P₂) | Higher mean temperature of heat addition | ↑ | Material creep (superalloys) |
| ↑ Turbine Inlet Temp (TIT, T₃) | ↑ T₃ in Carnot reference | ↑↑ | Blade metallurgy (~ 700 °C) |
| ↓ Condenser Pressure (P₁) | ↓ T₁ in Carnot reference | ↑ | Cooling water T; vacuum limits |
| ↑ Reheat | ↑ mean T of heat addition | ↑ | Two-stage turbine complexity |
| ↑ Regeneration (Feedwater Heating) | Recovers bleed steam enthalpy | ↑ | Multiple heat exchangers |
| ↑ Superheat | Moves state 3 to higher h | ↑ | Material limits |
| ↑ Generator efficiency | Electrical conversion | ↑ minor | ~ 99 % ceiling |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reheating, Regeneration, and Superheating are the three "efficiency boosters" you must always discuss in a KTU answer to score full marks on Rankine cycle design questions.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Coal-Based Thermal Power Plant (Functional Architecture Flow)

<!--
Mermaid Safeguards applied:
- All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., node1, stepA, coalBlock)
- Labels contain NO markdown formatting characters
- Flow uses arrow notation
-->

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[COAL STOCKYARD] --> B1[CRUSHER HOUSE]
    B1 --> C1[COAL BUNKER]
    C1 --> D1[PULVERIZER MILL]
    D1 --> E1[FURNACE BOILER]
    F1[FORCED DRAFT FAN AIR] --> E1
    E1 --> G1[SUPERHEATER STEAM AT 600 C]
    G1 --> H1[HIGH PRESSURE TURBINE]
    H1 --> I1[INTERMEDIATE PRESSURE TURBINE]
    I1 --> J1[LOW PRESSURE TURBINE]
    J1 --> K1[CONDENSER]
    K1 --> L1[CONDENSATE EXTRACTION PUMP]
    L1 --> M1[FEEDWATER HEATER]
    M1 --> N1[BOILER FEED PUMP]
    N1 --> E1
    J1 --> O1[GENERATOR]
    O1 --> P1[STEP UP TRANSFORMER]
    P1 --> Q1[GRID TRANSMISSION]
    E1 --> R1[ELECTROSTATIC PRECIPITATOR]
    R1 --> S1[FLUE GAS DESULFURIZATION]
    S1 --> T1[STACK]
    E1 --> U1[ASH HANDLING PLANT]
    K1 --> V1[COOLING TOWER]
    V1 --> K1
```

### 4.2 T-s and P-v Diagrams — Rankine Cycle (Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[STATE 1 SAT LIQUID P1 T1] --> P2[STATE 2 COMPRESSED LIQ P2 T2]
    P2 --> P3[STATE 3 SUPERHEATED STEAM P2 T3]
    P3 --> P4[STATE 4 WET STEAM P1 T4]
    P4 --> P1
    P1 --- P2
    P2 --- P3
    P3 --- P4
    P4 --- P1
    E1[BOILER 2 TO 3 QIN] -.-> P3
    E2[CONDENSER 4 TO 1 QOUT] -.-> P1
    E3[PUMP 1 TO 2 WP] -.-> P2
    E4[TURBINE 3 TO 4 WT] -.-> P4
```

**Interpretation:**
- 1 → 2: Slight rise in T (isentropic compression in pump, almost vertical on T-s).
- 2 → 3: Large rise in T (isobaric heat addition in boiler, horizontal-ish on T-s).
- 3 → 4: Drop in T (isentropic expansion in turbine, vertical line on T-s).
- 4 → 1: Drop in T (isobaric heat rejection in condenser, horizontal line under dome).
- **Area enclosed = Net Work Output W_net** (per unit mass per cycle).

### 4.3 Development-Environment Flow Diagram (Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A2[POPULATION GROWTH] --> B2[INDUSTRIALIZATION]
    B2 --> C2[RESOURCE EXTRACTION]
    C2 --> D2[POLLUTION GENERATION]
    D2 --> E2[ENVIRONMENTAL DEGRADATION]
    E2 --> F2[CARBON EMISSIONS]
    F2 --> G2[CLIMATE CHANGE]
    G2 --> H2[REDUCTION IN CARRYING CAPACITY]
    H2 --> I2[FEEDBACK TO DEVELOPMENT CONSTRAINT]
    I2 --> B2
    J2[SUSTAINABLE PRACTICES] --> B2
    K2[RENEWABLE ENERGY] --> B2
    L2[CIRCULAR ECONOMY] --> B2
```

**Interpretation:** Industrial activity (B2) is simultaneously a *driver* of development and a *source* of environmental pressure. The feedback loop (E2 → H2 → I2 → B2) represents the **carrying-capacity constraint** that limits future development if environmental degradation continues unchecked. Sustainable interventions (J2, K2, L2) are *side inputs* that modify the development pathway.

### 4.4 Schematic — Thermal Plant Layout (Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FUEL_SYSTEM
        FS1[COAL YARD] --> FS2[CRUSHER] --> FS3[BOILER]
    end
    subgraph STEAM_CYCLE
        SS1[BOILER] --> SS2[TURBINE HP] --> SS3[TURBINE LP] --> SS4[CONDENSER] --> SS5[PUMP] --> SS1
    end
    subgraph ELECTRICAL_SYSTEM
        ES1[TURBINE SHAFT] --> ES2[GENERATOR] --> ES3[TRANSFORMER] --> ES4[GRID]
    end
    subgraph COOLING_SYSTEM
        CS1[CONDENSER] --> CS2[COOLING TOWER] --> CS3[COOLING PUMP] --> CS1
    end
    subgraph EMISSION_CONTROL
        EC1[BOILER FLUE GAS] --> EC2[ESP] --> EC3[FGD] --> EC4[STACK]
    end
    subgraph ASH_SYSTEM
        AS1[BOILER ASH] --> AS2[ASH POND] --> AS3[ASH UTILIZATION]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**
*"Define sustainable development. Explain its relevance in the context of energy engineering with two examples."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Sustainable development, as defined by the Brundtland Commission (1987), is *development that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs*. In energy engineering, it is relevant because:

1. **Resource Constraint Example:** Coal reserves in India are estimated to last only ~100 years at current consumption. Sustainable energy planning demands a transition to renewables to avoid exhausting this finite stock — preserving energy security for future generations.
2. **Emission Constraint Example:** Each kg of coal produces ~2.4 kg of CO₂. Unrestricted burning contributes to climate change, jeopardizing the ecological carrying capacity that future development depends upon. Sustainable frameworks (carbon credits, net-zero targets) aim to decouple development from emissions.

This is a *definition + 2-mark* application answer; examiners expect 1 mark for the definition and 1 mark each for the two examples.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
*"List the four main components of a steam power plant and state the function of each."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| Boiler (Steam Generator) | Burns fuel to convert water into high-pressure, high-temperature superheated steam. |
| Turbine | Converts the enthalpy of steam into rotational mechanical work by isentropic expansion. |
| Condenser | Condenses the low-pressure exhaust steam back to saturated liquid, maintaining low back pressure and recovering working fluid. |
| Pump (BFP/CEP) | Pressurizes the condensate from condenser pressure to boiler pressure for re-circulation. |

(0.75 mark per correct component + function. Use a table to organize; KTU examiners reward structured presentation.)

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Many students lose marks because they (a) confuse the Rankine cycle with the Carnot cycle, (b) forget to subtract pump work, (c) skip the auxiliary power factor, or (d) write the formula without defining the state points. **Always state the state points (1, 2, 3, 4) and the process (1→2, 2→3, etc.) clearly before writing any equation.** Marks are reserved for *methodology*, not just *final answer*.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1, 14 marks, Expected Pattern]**

*(a)* With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a coal-based thermal power plant. Discuss the function of the electrostatic precipitator (ESP) and flue gas desulfurization (FGD) unit. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A steam power plant operates on the ideal Rankine cycle. Steam enters the turbine at 3 MPa, 350 °C and is condensed at 10 kPa. Determine:
- (i) Turbine work, pump work, heat added, heat rejected
- (ii) Thermal efficiency
- (iii) Specific steam consumption

Use the following steam table data:
- At 3 MPa, 350 °C: h₃ = 3115.3 kJ/kg, s₃ = 6.7428 kJ/(kg·K)
- Saturated liquid at 10 kPa: h_f = 191.81 kJ/kg, s_f = 0.6492 kJ/(kg·K), s_fg = 7.4996 kJ/(kg·K), v_f = 0.00101 m³/kg
**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

A coal-based thermal power plant converts the chemical energy of coal into electrical energy through the Rankine cycle. The functional architecture flow is:

1. **Coal Handling & Pulverization:** Coal from the stockyard is crushed and pulverized to < 75 µm fineness to ensure efficient combustion.
2. **Combustion in Boiler:** Pulverized coal is burned with preheated air in the furnace, releasing heat at temperatures exceeding 1500 °C.
3. **Steam Generation & Superheating:** Water in boiler tubes is converted to superheated steam at ~ 3 MPa, 350 °C.
4. **Turbine:** Steam expands through HP, IP, and LP turbines, producing mechanical work on the shaft.
5. **Generator:** The turbine shaft drives a synchronous generator (3-phase, 50 Hz) producing electricity at ~ 11–25 kV.
6. **Condenser:** Exhaust steam is condensed at ~ 10 kPa using cooling water, returning saturated liquid condensate.
7. **Pumping:** Condensate is pressurized back to boiler pressure by the BFP.
8. **Transmission:** Step-up transformers raise voltage to 220 kV / 400 kV for grid transmission.

**Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP):**
- Removes **particulate matter (fly ash)** from flue gas by ionizing particles with high-voltage electrodes (40–100 kV DC).
- Charged particles migrate to collecting plates and are dislodged by rapping into hoppers.
- Typical efficiency: **> 99.9 %** for particles > 10 µm; mandated by CPCB/MoEFCC for all Indian thermal plants.

**Flue Gas Desulfurization (FGD):**
- Removes **SO₂** from flue gas to curb acid rain.
- Common methods: Wet limestone (CaCO₃) scrubbing — produces gypsum (CaSO₄·2H₂O) as a useful by-product.
- Reaction: SO₂ + CaCO₃ + ½O₂ + 2H₂O → CaSO₄·2H₂O + CO₂.
- Removal efficiency: **> 90–95 %**.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — State 1 (Saturated liquid at condenser, P₁ = 10 kPa):**
h₁ = h_f @ 10 kPa = 191.81 kJ/kg
v₁ = v_f @ 10 kPa = 0.00101 m³/kg
s₁ = s_f = 0.6492 kJ/(kg·K)

**Step 2 — State 2 (Pump exit, isentropic compression to P₂ = 3 MPa):**

The pump work for an incompressible liquid is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_P &= v_1 \times (P_2 - P_1) \\
    &= 0.00101 \, \text{m}^3/\text{kg} \times (3000 - 10) \, \text{kPa} \\
    &= 0.00101 \times 2990 \quad \text{[kJ/kg]} \\
    &= 3.0199 \, \text{kJ/kg} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_2 &= h_1 + W_P \\
    &= 191.81 + 3.02 = 194.83 \, \text{kJ/kg} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — State 3 (Turbine inlet, superheated):**
h₃ = 3115.3 kJ/kg
s₃ = 6.7428 kJ/(kg·K)

**Step 4 — State 4 (Turbine exit, isentropic expansion at P₁ = 10 kPa):**

For isentropic expansion: s₄ = s₃ = 6.7428 kJ/(kg·K)

Quality of exhaust steam:

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_4 &= s_f + x_4 \cdot s_{fg} \\
\Rightarrow x_4 &= \frac{s_4 - s_f}{s_{fg}} = \frac{6.7428 - 0.6492}{7.4996} \\
              &= \frac{6.0936}{7.4996} = 0.8126 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Enthalpy at state 4:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_4 &= h_f + x_4 \cdot h_{fg} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

At 10 kPa, h_fg = 2392.1 kJ/kg (from steam tables):

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_4 &= 191.81 + 0.8126 \times 2392.1 \\
    &= 191.81 + 1943.83 \\
    &= 2135.64 \, \text{kJ/kg} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Work and Heat Quantities:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_T &= h_3 - h_4 = 3115.3 - 2135.64 = 979.66 \, \text{kJ/kg} \\
W_P &= 3.02 \, \text{kJ/kg} \\
W_{net} &= W_T - W_P = 979.66 - 3.02 = 976.64 \, \text{kJ/kg} \\
Q_{in} &= h_3 - h_2 = 3115.3 - 194.83 = 2920.47 \, \text{kJ/kg} \\
Q_{out} &= h_4 - h_1 = 2135.64 - 191.81 = 1943.83 \, \text{kJ/kg} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Thermal Efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{Rankine} &= \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{976.64}{2920.47} = 0.3344 = 33.44\% \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Specific Steam Consumption:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SSC &= \frac{3600}{W_{net}} = \frac{3600}{976.64} = 3.686 \, \text{kg/kWh} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Summary Table (mark-friendly):**

| Quantity | Value | Unit |
|----------|-------|------|
| Turbine Work W_T | 979.66 | kJ/kg |
| Pump Work W_P | 3.02 | kJ/kg |
| Net Work W_net | 976.64 | kJ/kg |
| Heat Added Q_in | 2920.47 | kJ/kg |
| Heat Rejected Q_out | 1943.83 | kJ/kg |
| Thermal Efficiency | 33.44 | % |
| SSC | 3.686 | kg/kWh |

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice 2 (Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1, 14 marks, Expected Pattern]**

*(a)* Discuss the relationship between development and environment. Explain the concept of carrying capacity with reference to energy resource utilization. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A 210 MW thermal power plant operates at an average plant load factor of 0.75 and uses coal with a Gross Calorific Value of 22 MJ/kg. The plant heat rate is 9500 kJ/kWh. Calculate:
- (i) Daily (24-hour) coal consumption in tonnes.
- (ii) Annual CO₂ emissions in tonnes, given the carbon content of coal is 50 % and the oxidation factor is 0.95.
**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**1. Development–Environment Relationship:**

Development involves industrial growth, infrastructure expansion, and increased consumption of natural resources. Environment provides the resources (fossil fuels, water, minerals, land) and assimilates the residues (emissions, effluents, solid waste). The relationship is **bidirectional and interdependent**:

- *Environment → Development:* Resources drive industrial output and economic growth.
- *Development → Environment:* Emissions, deforestation, and resource depletion degrade ecosystems.

**2. The Carrying Capacity Concept:**

Carrying capacity (CC) is the maximum level of activity that the environment can support indefinitely without irreversible degradation. For energy systems, CC is constrained by:

- **Resource Stock:** Total economically recoverable reserves (e.g., India's coal = ~ 361 billion tonnes, of which proven reserves are ~ 175 billion tonnes).
- **Assimilative Capacity:** Ability of air, water, and soil to absorb pollutants (e.g., atmospheric CO₂ safe limit ~ 350 ppm vs. current ~ 420 ppm).
- **Regenerative Capacity:** Time for natural systems to renew (e.g., forests regenerate over decades, fossil fuels over millions of years — *not* within human timescales).

When utilization exceeds CC, **environmental degradation becomes irreversible** — climate change, biodiversity loss, soil erosion. Sustainable development requires keeping utilization **below CC**, with continuous monitoring and adaptive policy.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given Data:**
- Plant Capacity: P = 210 MW
- Plant Load Factor: PLF = 0.75
- GCV of coal: 22 MJ/kg = 22,000 kJ/kg
- Plant Heat Rate: HR = 9500 kJ/kWh
- Carbon content of coal: C = 50 % = 0.50
- Oxidation factor: OF = 0.95
- Time: 24 hours per day, 365 days per year

**Step 1 — Average Power Output:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{avg} &= P \times PLF = 210 \times 0.75 = 157.5 \, \text{MW} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Daily Energy Generation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{day} &= P_{avg} \times 24 \, \text{h} = 157.5 \times 24 = 3780 \, \text{MWh/day} \\
        &= 3.78 \times 10^6 \, \text{kWh/day} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Daily Coal Consumption:**

Specific coal consumption per kWh:

$$
\begin{aligned}
s &= \frac{HR}{GCV} = \frac{9500}{22000} = 0.4318 \, \text{kg/kWh} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Total daily coal mass:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{coal, day} &= s \times E_{day} = 0.4318 \times 3.78 \times 10^6 \, \text{kg/day} \\
              &= 1.6322 \times 10^6 \, \text{kg/day} \\
              &= 1632.2 \, \text{tonnes/day} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Annual Coal Consumption:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{coal, year} &= 1632.2 \times 365 = 595,754 \, \text{tonnes/year} \approx 5.96 \times 10^5 \, \text{t/y} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Annual CO₂ Emissions:**

Mass of carbon in coal:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_C &= m_{coal, year} \times C = 5.95754 \times 10^5 \times 0.50 = 2.9788 \times 10^5 \, \text{t C/y} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Mass of CO₂ (molecular weight ratio: CO₂ / C = 44/12 = 3.667):

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{CO_2} &= m_C \times \frac{44}{12} \times OF \\
         &= 2.9788 \times 10^5 \times 3.667 \times 0.95 \\
         &= 2.9788 \times 10^5 \times 3.4836 \\
         &= 1.0377 \times 10^6 \, \text{tCO}_2/\text{year} \\
         &\approx 1.04 \times 10^6 \, \text{tonnes CO}_2/\text{year} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Summary of Answers:**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| (i) Daily coal consumption | **1632.2 tonnes/day** |
| (ii) Annual CO₂ emissions | **~ 1.04 million tonnes/year** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning #2:** A common error is forgetting the **oxidation factor (OF)** in CO₂ calculations. The factor accounts for the fact that not all carbon in coal is fully oxidized during combustion. Always multiply by OF. Another common error: students forget to convert MJ to kJ (factor of 1000) — recheck unit consistency before writing the final answer.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Development-Environment & Thermal Power Plants**

- ✅ **Brundtland Definition of Sustainable Development** (1987) — *verbatim* recall.
- ✅ **Carrying Capacity** = function of (Resources, Technology, Lifestyle) — *not a fixed number*.
- ✅ **Conventional Energy** = non-renewable, fossil/nuclear — includes coal, oil, gas, nuclear.
- ✅ **Rankine Cycle** = 4 processes — Pump (1→2), Boiler (2→3), Turbine (3→4), Condenser (4→1).
- ✅ **η_Rankine = (h₃ − h₄ − h₂ + h₁) / (h₃ − h₂)** — the most-tested KTU formula.
- ✅ **Pump work = v₁ (P₂ − P₁)** — for incompressible liquid approximation.
- ✅ **Turbine exit quality:** x₄ = (s₃ − s_f) / s_fg at condenser pressure.
- ✅ **SSC = 3600 / W_net** in **kg/kWh**.
- ✅ **SFC = HR / GCV** in **kg/kWh**.
- ✅ **HR = 3600 / η_Rankine** in **kJ/kWh**.
- ✅ **PLF = Actual Energy / Max Possible Energy** (annual PLF uses 8760 h).
- ✅ **η_net = η_boiler × η_cycle × η_mech × η_gen × (1 − APC)**.
- ✅ **Components of a thermal plant** — Boiler, Turbine, Condenser, Generator, BFP, ESP, FGD, Cooling Tower, Transformer.
- ✅ **ESP removes particulate matter (> 99.9 % efficiency)**.
- ✅ **FGD removes SO₂** using wet limestone scrubbing.
- ✅ **CO₂ emission = m_coal × C% × (44/12) × OF** — use oxidation factor (≈ 0.95).
- ✅ **Efficiency boosters** — Reheat, Regeneration, Superheat — always discuss.
- ✅ **Subcritical (< 170 bar) / Supercritical (170–250 bar) / Ultra-supercritical (> 250 bar)** — pressure classifications.
- ✅ **Carbon emission factor of coal ≈ 0.095 tCO₂/GJ** (IPCC default).
- ✅ **Atmospheric CO₂ ≈ 420 ppm (2024)** vs. pre-industrial 280 ppm.
- ✅ **Net Calorific Value (NCV)** excludes latent heat of water vapor in flue gas; **GCV includes it**.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — OEBBT614 Energy Engineering and Management**

topics.title: Hydro
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT614 - ENERGY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hydro

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Hydropower (Hydroelectric Power)?

**Hydropower** (or **Hydroelectric Power**) is a renewable form of energy that harnesses the kinetic and potential energy of moving or falling water and converts it into electrical energy. It is classified as a **non-conventional/renewable** energy source even though it is one of the oldest and most mature renewable technologies known to mankind.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-aligned):**
> *Hydropower is the electrical energy produced from the potential energy of water stored at a height (hydraulic head) and the kinetic energy of its flow, by directing it through hydraulic turbines coupled to electrical generators.*

The power output from a hydropower plant depends fundamentally on two physical parameters:
1. **Head (H)** — The vertical height (in meters) through which water falls.
2. **Discharge (Q)** — The volume flow rate of water (in m³/s).

The fundamental governing relation is:

$$P = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H \cdot \eta$$

where the symbols have their standard engineering meanings (explained in detail in Section 2).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Water Wheel of a Watermill"

Imagine a **watermill** used historically by farmers to grind grain. Water from a higher pond was channeled down a wooden chute onto a large wheel. The wheel turned, and a shaft connected to the wheel rotated millstones to crush wheat.

Hydropower is essentially the **modern, electrified, scientifically optimized version of that watermill**:
- The **high pond** (reservoir) replaces the higher pond of the mill.
- The **chute** is replaced by an engineered **penstock** (high-pressure pipe).
- The **wheel** is replaced by a precision **turbine** (Pelton, Francis, or Kaplan).
- The **millstones** are replaced by an **electrical generator** (alternator).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Hydropower is Special in the Energy Mix:**
> - It has the **highest conversion efficiency (≈ 90%)** of any commercial energy source — only loses ~10% in turbines and generators.
> - It provides **baseload, peak, and balancing power** — a rare flexibility in renewables.
> - **Energy storage** is "free" in the form of a reservoir.
> - **No fuel cost**, no emissions during operation, and a long asset life (**50–100 years**).

---

## 1.3 Classification of Hydropower Plants (by Installed Capacity)

The **KTU 2024 syllabus** emphasizes the following standardized classification, which is also used globally by the **International Hydropower Association (IHA)** and the **Central Electricity Authority (CEA), India**:

| Category | Installed Capacity | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Large Hydro** | > 25 MW | Utility-scale grid supply; requires major dam and reservoir |
| **Mini Hydro** | 1 MW – 25 MW | Local grids; small rivers with run-of-river or small dam |
| **Micro Hydro** | 100 kW – 1 MW | Village electrification; isolated remote loads |
| **Pico Hydro** | < 100 kW (< 5 kW typical) | Single homes, small farms, off-grid lighting |
| **Pump Storage** | Variable | Peak-load balancing; stores off-peak grid energy |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:**
> In India, projects above **25 MW** are classified as "Large Hydro" and are governed by the **Ministry of Power**. Projects below 25 MW fall under the **Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE)** and are considered renewable energy — eligible for Renewable Purchase Obligations (RPO). This distinction is a frequently asked concept.

---

## 1.4 Site Selection Criteria (KTU Board Exam Favorite)

A hydropower site is selected based on the following key criteria, each of which is a possible 3-mark short-answer question:

1. **Hydrological Head (H):** Greater the head, greater the available power per unit discharge.
2. **Water Availability (Run-off / Discharge):** Continuous, year-round flow is essential; depends on catchment area, rainfall, snowmelt.
3. **Geological Stability:** Foundation must bear the dam weight; **bedrock preferred** over alluvium.
4. **Topography:** Narrow valley at dam site (reduces dam length), wide valley at reservoir (increases storage).
5. **Submergence & Rehabilitation:** Low population, minimal forest loss, low submergence area preferred.
6. **Distance to Load Centre:** Shorter transmission distance reduces cost and losses.
7. **Environmental & Social Impact:** EIA (Environmental Impact Assessment) clearance is mandatory.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hydrological Cycle — Source of All Hydropower

Hydropower is a **continuous renewable** resource because the natural **hydrological cycle** (driven by solar energy) continuously replenishes water at higher elevations via evaporation, condensation, and precipitation. The energy "input" to the cycle is the **solar energy of ≈ 1.74 × 10¹⁷ W** intercepted by the Earth, of which only a tiny fraction is captured by hydropower.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Statement:**
> Hydropower does not "create" energy — it merely captures a small, controlled fraction of the **solar-driven hydrological cycle** and converts the gravitational potential energy of water into electrical energy with high efficiency.

---

## 2.2 Theoretical Basis — Why Water Has Energy

A mass $m$ of water stored at a vertical height $H$ above a reference datum possesses **gravitational potential energy**:

$$E_p = m \cdot g \cdot H$$

Since mass $m = \rho \cdot V$ (where $V$ is volume and $\rho$ is the density of water), the energy becomes:

$$E_p = \rho \cdot V \cdot g \cdot H$$

If this volume of water flows out per unit time (i.e., the **volumetric discharge** is $Q = V/t$ in m³/s), then the available **theoretical hydraulic power** is:

$$P_{th} = \frac{E_p}{t} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H$$

**Standard Engineering Values used universally in KTU exam problems:**

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $\rho$ | Density of water | **1000 kg/m³** |
| $g$ | Acceleration due to gravity | **9.81 m/s²** |
| $Q$ | Volumetric discharge | m³/s |
| $H$ | Net head (gross head − losses) | m |
| $\eta$ | Overall plant efficiency (turbine × generator × transformer) | dimensionless (0.80 – 0.92) |

Therefore, the **actual electrical power output** of a hydropower plant is:

$$\boxed{P_{elec} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H \cdot \eta_{overall}}$$

In S.I. units, this gives power in **Watts (W)**. In practice, hydropower plant capacities are quoted in **kilowatts (kW)** or **megawatts (MW)**.

---

## 2.3 Components of a Hydropower Plant — Functional Breakdown

A typical **impoundment (dam-and-reservoir) hydropower plant** consists of the following key civil and electromechanical components:

1. **Dam / Weir / Barrage:** Creates the reservoir and establishes the head.
2. **Spillway:** Releases excess water safely to prevent overtopping of the dam.
3. **Intake Structure:** Trash racks and gates control water entry into the penstock.
4. **Headrace Tunnel / Channel:** Conveys water from the reservoir to the powerhouse.
5. **Surge Tank / Surge Shaft:** A vertical chamber that absorbs water-hammer pressure fluctuations during sudden load changes or valve closure.
6. **Penstock:** A high-pressure pipe (steel, sometimes reinforced concrete) that carries water from the surge tank to the turbine.
7. **Powerhouse (Machine Hall):** Houses the turbine, generator, control systems, and auxiliary equipment.
8. **Turbine:** Converts the hydraulic energy of water into mechanical rotational energy.
9. **Generator (Alternator):** Converts mechanical rotation into electrical energy (typically 3-phase AC).
10. **Tailrace:** Returns the used water to the river downstream.
11. **Transformers & Switchyard:** Step-up voltage to transmission levels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Concept Check — Why is the Surge Tank necessary?**
> When the turbine inlet valve closes suddenly (load rejection), the water column in the penstock — having enormous momentum — would otherwise produce a destructive **water-hammer pressure wave** (Joukowsky equation: $\Delta p = \rho \cdot a \cdot \Delta v$). The surge tank provides a free water surface that **rises/damps** this wave, protecting the penstock. This is a **guaranteed KTU question**.

---

## 2.4 Types of Hydraulic Turbines — KTU Must-Know

The choice of turbine depends on the **site head** and the **design discharge**. KTU 2024 expects knowledge of the three main reaction/impulse turbines:

| Turbine | Type | Head Range | Specific Speed | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pelton Wheel** | Impulse | High (300 m – 1700 m) | Low (10–35) | Mountainous regions, e.g., Himalayan projects |
| **Francis Turbine** | Reaction (mixed flow) | Medium (30 m – 300 m) | Medium (60–300) | Most common; majority of Indian hydro plants |
| **Kaplan Turbine** | Reaction (axial flow) | Low (2 m – 30 m) | High (300–1000) | Run-of-river, low-head sites |

> [!NOTE]
> **Impulse vs Reaction (KTU Hot Question):**
> - **Impulse turbine (Pelton):** Water jet strikes buckets in **atmospheric pressure**; all pressure head is converted to kinetic (velocity) head in a nozzle before striking the runner.
> - **Reaction turbine (Francis, Kaplan):** Runner is **fully submerged**; water pressure drops as it flows through the runner, providing both pressure and momentum change.

**Efficiencies for KTU Problems:**

| Component | Typical Efficiency |
|---|---|
| Hydraulic efficiency (turbine) | 85% – 95% |
| Mechanical efficiency (bearings, shaft) | 95% – 99% |
| Electrical efficiency (generator) | 95% – 98% |
| Transformer efficiency | 98% – 99% |
| **Overall plant efficiency ($\eta_{overall}$)** | **80% – 92%** |

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the high-yield formula sheet for the KTU ESE. Memorize each equation, its units, and the standard constant values.**

| # | Formula | Description / Use | Typical KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = \rho g Q H \eta$ | Electrical power output (W) | 14-mark design problems |
| 2 | $E = P \times t$ | Annual energy generation (Wh) | Energy output per year |
| 3 | $P_{th} = \rho g Q H$ | Theoretical hydraulic power (W) | Available input power |
| 4 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{elec}}{P_{th}}$ | Overall plant efficiency | Efficiency calculation |
| 5 | $Q = A \cdot v$ | Discharge = Area × velocity | Given channel cross-section |
| 6 | $H_g = Z_{up} - Z_{down}$ | Gross head = elevation difference | Site survey data |
| 7 | $H_{net} = H_g - h_f$ | Net head = Gross head − friction loss | Penstock design |
| 8 | $h_f = \dfrac{4 f L v^2}{2 g D}$ | Darcy–Weisbach head loss in penstock | Frictional loss in penstock |
| 9 | $V = \dfrac{E}{A \times h \times \rho \times g}$ | Reservoir storage volume for given energy | Storage sizing |
| 10 | $LF = \dfrac{E_{actual}}{E_{installed} \times 8760}$ | Plant Load Factor | Capacity utilization studies |
| 11 | $CF = \dfrac{E_{annual}}{P_{rated} \times 8760}$ | Capacity Factor | Hydro plant planning |
| 12 | $\Delta p = \rho \cdot a \cdot \Delta v$ | Joukowsky water-hammer pressure rise | Surge tank design |

**Universal Standard Values to Memorize:**
- $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- $\rho \cdot g = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3 = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3$
- 1 m³/s flowing through 1 m head ≈ **9.81 kW** (theoretical, with 100% efficiency)
- Hours in a year = **8760 h**

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

Hydropower is not a textbook concept — it is the **backbone of renewable baseload power worldwide**:

- **India's installed large hydro capacity ≈ 47 GW** (as of 2024), contributing ~10% of total electricity.
- **Tehri Dam (Uttarakhand):** 2,400 MW — India's tallest dam (260.5 m).
- **Three Gorges Dam (China):** 22,500 MW — world's largest, with annual generation of ≈ 100 TWh.
- **Itaipu Dam (Brazil–Paraguay):** 14,000 MW — runner-up in installed capacity.
- **Pumped Storage:** Increasingly used globally for **grid balancing** with intermittent solar/wind.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Context — Why this matters:**
> Kerala, being a Western Ghats state, has excellent potential for **small and mini hydropower** (estimated potential ≈ 2,000 MW). The Idukki hydroelectric project (780 MW) is a famous example that students should know.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Power Available from a Falling Water Column

**Problem Setup:** Consider a reservoir with a volume $V$ of water held at a height $H$ above the turbine centerline. The water is allowed to fall through a vertical distance $H$ and pass through a turbine connected to a generator. Derive an expression for the electrical power output.

---

### Step 1 — Identify the form of energy stored

The water in the reservoir, by virtue of being elevated above the turbine, possesses **gravitational potential energy** with respect to the turbine datum.

$$E_p = m \cdot g \cdot H$$

*Conversion logic:* Potential energy equals the force of gravity ($m g$) acting through a vertical displacement ($H$).

---

### Step 2 — Express mass in terms of volume and density

By definition of density:

$$m = \rho \cdot V$$

where $\rho$ is the density of water = **1000 kg/m³** and $V$ is the volume of water in m³.

*Conversion logic:* Density is mass per unit volume; rearranged to obtain mass.

---

### Step 3 — Substitute mass into the energy equation

$$E_p = \rho \cdot V \cdot g \cdot H$$

*Conversion logic:* Direct substitution of Step 2's mass relation into Step 1's energy relation.

---

### Step 4 — Express energy in terms of discharge (volume per unit time)

The **discharge** $Q$ is defined as the volume of water passing a section per unit time:

$$Q = \frac{V}{t} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V = Q \cdot t$$

*Conversion logic:* Discharge is the volumetric flow rate. To find energy per unit time (i.e., power), we need to evaluate the energy delivered by a volume of water that passes in time $t$.

---

### Step 5 — Derive theoretical hydraulic power

Substitute $V = Q t$ into the energy equation and divide by $t$ to obtain power:

$$P_{th} = \frac{E_p}{t} = \frac{\rho \cdot V \cdot g \cdot H}{t} = \frac{\rho \cdot (Q t) \cdot g \cdot H}{t}$$

After cancellation of $t$:

$$P_{th} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H$$

*Conversion logic:* The time $t$ cancels out, giving the **rate of energy delivery** (which is power) as a function of the **continuous** discharge $Q$ and the head $H$.

---

### Step 6 — Apply the overall efficiency factor

The turbine-generator-transformer system does not convert 100% of the hydraulic power to electrical power. Let $\eta_{overall}$ denote the cumulative efficiency. The electrical power output is:

$$\boxed{P_{elec} = \eta_{overall} \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H}$$

*Conversion logic:* Real machines have losses — mechanical (friction), hydraulic (turbine), electrical (generator + transformer). The product of all individual efficiencies is the overall efficiency.

---

### Step 7 — Evaluate the constant for quick KTU use

With $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$:

$$\rho \cdot g = 1000 \times 9.81 = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$$

Therefore:

$$P_{elec} \text{ (in Watts)} = 9810 \cdot \eta \cdot Q \cdot H$$

Or, in **kilowatts**, dividing by 1000:

$$P_{elec} \text{ (kW)} = 9.81 \cdot \eta \cdot Q \cdot H$$

This **9.81 factor** is the **single most important KTU numerical constant** for hydropower.

*Conversion logic:* Watts ÷ 1000 = kilowatts. This shortcut saves time in the 14-mark numerical problem.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Net Head and Penstock Friction Loss

In a real plant, water flowing through the penstock loses some head due to friction against the pipe wall. This loss must be deducted from the gross head to get the **net head**, which is what actually drives the turbine.

### Step 1 — Define the gross head

The **gross head** is the difference in water surface elevation between the reservoir (or forebay) and the tailrace:

$$H_g = Z_{reservoir} - Z_{tailrace}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Darcy–Weisbach equation for friction loss

For a penstock of length $L$, diameter $D$, with water flowing at average velocity $v$:

$$h_f = \frac{4 f L v^2}{2 g D}$$

where $f$ is the Darcy friction factor (a dimensionless number, typically 0.01 – 0.03 for commercial steel pipes with moderate turbulence).

### Step 3 — Compute velocity from discharge

By continuity, for a penstock of cross-sectional area $A = \pi D^2 / 4$:

$$v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{4 Q}{\pi D^2}$$

### Step 4 — Express the net head

$$H_{net} = H_g - h_f - h_{minor}$$

where $h_{minor}$ includes losses at bends, valves, trash racks, and the entrance/exit.

### Step 5 — Final power expression using net head

$$P_{elec} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H_{net} \cdot \eta_{overall}$$

*Conversion logic:* The turbine can only convert the energy corresponding to the head actually available **at the runner inlet**, not the gross elevation difference.

---

## 3.3 Solved Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Pattern)

**Problem:**
A run-of-river hydropower plant has a design discharge of $Q = 30 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ and a gross head of $H_g = 25 \text{ m}$. The penstock has a length of 200 m and a diameter of 2.5 m. The friction factor is $f = 0.02$. The overall plant efficiency is 85%. The minor head losses amount to 0.5 m. Calculate:
1. Velocity in the penstock
2. Friction head loss
3. Net head available at the turbine
4. The electrical power output in kW
5. The annual energy generation (in MWh) assuming the plant operates at 100% load for 6 months and 50% of the rated capacity for the remaining 6 months.

---

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Penstock velocity:**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (2.5)^2}{4} = 4.909 \text{ m}^2$$

$$v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{30}{4.909} = 6.111 \text{ m/s}$$

---

**Step 2 — Friction head loss (Darcy–Weisbach):**

$$h_f = \frac{4 f L v^2}{2 g D}$$

$$h_f = \frac{4 \times 0.02 \times 200 \times (6.111)^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times 2.5}$$

$$h_f = \frac{0.08 \times 200 \times 37.345}{49.05} = \frac{597.52}{49.05} = 12.18 \text{ m}$$

---

**Step 3 — Net head:**

$$H_{net} = H_g - h_f - h_{minor} = 25 - 12.18 - 0.5 = 12.32 \text{ m}$$

---

**Step 4 — Electrical power output:**

$$P_{elec} = 9.81 \times \eta \times Q \times H_{net}$$

$$P_{elec} = 9.81 \times 0.85 \times 30 \times 12.32$$

$$P_{elec} = 9.81 \times 0.85 \times 369.6 = 3081.06 \text{ kW} \approx 3.08 \text{ MW}$$

---

**Step 5 — Annual energy generation:**

Period 1 (6 months at 100% rated power):

$$E_1 = 3081.06 \text{ kW} \times 8760/2 \text{ h} = 3081.06 \times 4380 = 13,495,043 \text{ kWh}$$

Period 2 (6 months at 50% rated power):

$$E_2 = 0.5 \times 3081.06 \text{ kW} \times 4380 \text{ h} = 6,747,521 \text{ kWh}$$

Total annual energy:

$$E_{annual} = E_1 + E_2 = 20,242,564 \text{ kWh} \approx 20,243 \text{ MWh}$$

---

### Analytical Matrix — KTU 14-Mark Solution Framework

| Solution Step | What to Write in Exam | Marks Allocated |
|---|---|---|
| 1. List given data and state the governing equation | $P = \rho g Q H \eta$ | 1 |
| 2. Calculate penstock velocity using $Q = Av$ | Show $A = \pi D^2/4$ and $v = Q/A$ | 2 |
| 3. Apply Darcy–Weisbach to find friction loss | Substitute $f, L, D, v, g$ correctly | 3 |
| 4. Compute net head = $H_g − h_f − h_{minor}$ | Show arithmetic | 1 |
| 5. Substitute in $P = 9.81 \cdot \eta \cdot Q \cdot H_{net}$ | Show final answer in kW or MW | 2 |
| 6. Energy calculation $E = P \times t$ | Account for time and load factor | 3 |
| 7. State final answer with correct units (MWh) | Highlight unit consistency | 2 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $H$ to meters** — a common mistake when site data is in feet.
> 2. **Using gross head directly** without subtracting friction losses — costs 3 marks.
> 3. **Mixing units**: Discharge in L/s and head in m → divide by 1000 to convert L/s to m³/s.
> 4. **Not showing the $\rho g = 9.81$ kN/m³ substitution step** — KTU awards a mark for it.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Hydropower Plant

The diagram below shows the **flow of energy** from stored water (potential energy) to electrical energy delivered to the grid, with the major civil and electromechanical components labeled.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reservoir\nStored Water at Head H] -->|Penstock Inlet| B[Trash Racks\nand Intake Gates]
    B --> C[Headrace Tunnel\nor Channel]
    C --> D[Surge Tank\nWater Hammer Protection]
    D --> E[Penstock\nHigh Pressure Pipe]
    E --> F[Control Valve\nand Inlet to Turbine]
    F --> G[Turbine\nPelton or Francis or Kaplan]
    G -->|Rotating Shaft| H[Coupling]
    H --> I[Synchronous Generator\nAlternator]
    I -->|Low Voltage AC| J[Step Up Transformer]
    J --> K[Switchyard\nand Transmission Line]
    K --> L[Electrical Grid\nLoad Center]
    G -.->|Tailrace Water| M[Tailrace Channel\nBack to River]
```

---

## 4.2 Comparative Turbine Selection Architecture

This second Mermaid diagram helps in **selecting the correct turbine** based on the head $H$ and design discharge $Q$, which is a recurring 7-mark KTU question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Site Survey Data\nHead H and Discharge Q] --> Decision1{Is Head greater than 300 m}
    Decision1 -->|Yes| PeltonImp[Use PELTON WHEEL\nImpulse Turbine\nSingle or Multi Jet]
    Decision1 -->|No| Decision2{Is Head between 30 m and 300 m}
    Decision2 -->|Yes| FrancisChoice[Use FRANCIS TURBINE\nReaction Mixed Flow\nMost Common Worldwide]
    Decision2 -->|No| Decision3{Is Head less than 30 m}
    Decision3 -->|Yes| KaplanChoice[Use KAPLAN TURBINE\nReaction Axial Flow\nAdjustable Blades]
    Decision3 -->|No| PumpStorage[Consider PUMP STORAGE\nTwo Way Operation]
    PeltonImp --> Output[Powerhouse Installation]
    FrancisChoice --> Output
    KaplanChoice --> Output
    PumpStorage --> Output
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Energy Conversion Chain

This block diagram shows the **sequential energy conversion stages** in a hydropower plant. Each block represents a transformation between energy domains. Energy losses occur at each block, reducing the overall plant efficiency.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E1[Gravitational Potential Energy\nof Stored Water] -->|At Intake| E2[Pressure Energy\nin Penstock]
    E2 -->|Across Turbine| E3[Mechanical Rotational Energy\non Turbine Shaft]
    E3 -->|Through Coupling| E4[Mechanical Energy\non Generator Shaft]
    E4 -->|In Alternator| E5[Electrical Energy\nAC at Generator Terminals]
    E5 -->|Through Step Up Transformer| E6[Electrical Energy\nat High Voltage Transmission]
    E1 -.->|Loss 5 to 10 percent| E3
    E3 -.->|Loss 2 to 5 percent| E4
    E4 -.->|Loss 3 to 5 percent| E5
    E5 -.->|Loss 1 to 2 percent| E6
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How to read this diagram for KTU:**
> The first stage (water) is the **energy source**. The last stage (grid) is the **energy sink**. The four losses (Hydraulic, Mechanical, Electrical 1, Electrical 2) multiply together to give the overall plant efficiency:
>
> $$\eta_{overall} = \eta_{hydraulic} \times \eta_{mechanical} \times \eta_{generator} \times \eta_{transformer}$$
>
> For a typical plant, $\eta_{overall} \approx 0.85 \times 0.98 \times 0.96 \times 0.99 \approx 0.79$. Exam problems often quote a **rounded value of 0.80 to 0.90**.
