# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define hydropower. List the major components of a typical impoundment type hydropower plant.**

**Model Answer:**

Hydropower is the electrical energy generated by converting the **potential energy of water stored at a height** and the **kinetic energy of its flow** into electrical energy using a hydraulic turbine coupled to an electrical generator.

Major components of a typical impoundment (dam-and-reservoir) type hydropower plant:
1. **Dam / Weir** — creates the reservoir and establishes the head.
2. **Intake structure and trash racks** — control water entry.
3. **Headrace tunnel / channel** — conveys water to the powerhouse.
4. **Surge tank** — absorbs water-hammer pressure fluctuations.
5. **Penstock** — high-pressure pipe that delivers water to the turbine.
6. **Powerhouse** — houses the turbine, generator, and control equipment.
7. **Tailrace** — returns used water to the river downstream.
8. **Switchyard and transmission lines** — dispatch electricity to the grid.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Students often miss the **surge tank** in the list. It is an essential protective component, not an optional accessory. Always include it.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Classify hydropower plants based on installed capacity. Differentiate between impulse and reaction turbines.**

**Model Answer:**

**Classification by Installed Capacity (CEA / MNRE standard):**

| Class | Installed Capacity |
|---|---|
| Pico Hydro | Up to 100 kW |
| Micro Hydro | 100 kW to 1 MW |
| Mini Hydro | 1 MW to 25 MW |
| Large Hydro | Above 25 MW |

**Impulse vs Reaction Turbines:**

| Feature | Impulse Turbine (e.g., Pelton) | Reaction Turbine (e.g., Francis, Kaplan) |
|---|---|---|
| Working principle | Velocity (kinetic energy) change | Pressure and velocity both change |
| Water contact with runner | Atmospheric (jet in air) | Fully submerged under water |
| Pressure at runner inlet | Atmospheric | Above atmospheric |
| Typical head range | 300 m – 1700 m (high) | 2 m – 300 m (low to medium) |
| Specific speed | Low | Medium to high |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **CHOICE 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2024]**
**(a)** Derive the expression for the electrical power output from a hydropower plant in terms of head, discharge, density, gravity, and overall efficiency. State the standard numerical constant used in the formula. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A small hydropower plant has a gross head of 40 m and a design discharge of 20 m³/s. The penstock is 500 m long, has a diameter of 2.0 m, and a friction factor of 0.018. Minor head losses are 0.8 m. If the overall plant efficiency is 0.88, calculate:
1. Velocity of water in the penstock
2. Friction head loss
3. Net head at the turbine
4. Electrical power output in kW
5. The annual energy generation in MWh, assuming a load factor of 0.70 **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Derivation:**

The mass of water stored at a height $H$ above the turbine reference is $m = \rho V$, where $V$ is the volume in m³ and $\rho$ is the water density = 1000 kg/m³.

The gravitational potential energy of this water is:

$$E_p = m g H = \rho V g H$$

If $V$ m³ of water flows in time $t$ s, the discharge is $Q = V/t$, so $V = Q t$.

Substituting:

$$E_p = \rho (Q t) g H$$

The theoretical hydraulic power is the energy delivered per unit time:

$$P_{th} = \frac{E_p}{t} = \rho g Q H$$

The actual electrical power output is reduced by the overall plant efficiency $\eta_{overall}$:

$$\boxed{P_{elec} = \rho g Q H \eta_{overall}}$$

With $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³ and $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\rho g = 9810$ N/m³ = **9.81 kN/m³**, and the formula simplifies to:

$$P_{elec} \text{ (kW)} = 9.81 \times Q \times H \times \eta_{overall}$$

The numerical constant **9.81** is the standard value used in all KTU hydropower problems.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Velocity in penstock:**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (2.0)^2}{4} = 3.1416 \text{ m}^2$$

$$v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{20}{3.1416} = 6.366 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Friction head loss (Darcy–Weisbach):**

$$h_f = \frac{4 f L v^2}{2 g D} = \frac{4 \times 0.018 \times 500 \times (6.366)^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times 2.0}$$

$$h_f = \frac{36 \times 40.527}{39.24} = \frac{1458.97}{39.24} = 37.18 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Net head:**

$$H_{net} = H_g - h_f - h_{minor} = 40 - 37.18 - 0.8 = 2.02 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Electrical power:**

$$P_{elec} = 9.81 \times \eta \times Q \times H_{net} = 9.81 \times 0.88 \times 20 \times 2.02$$

$$P_{elec} = 9.81 \times 0.88 \times 40.4 = 348.78 \text{ kW} \approx 348.8 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5 — Annual energy:**

$$E_{annual} = P_{rated} \times \text{Load Factor} \times 8760 \text{ h}$$

$$E_{annual} = 348.78 \times 0.70 \times 8760 = 2{,}138{,}950 \text{ kWh} \approx 2139 \text{ MWh}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **CHOICE 2 (Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2024]**
**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the working of a hydropower plant. List the advantages and limitations of hydropower. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare Pelton wheel, Francis turbine, and Kaplan turbine with respect to head range, specific speed, type, and typical application. A Pelton wheel operates under a net head of 400 m with a discharge of 0.5 m³/s. If the turbine efficiency is 85% and generator efficiency is 95%, determine the shaft power and the electrical power generated. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Working Principle (as per the block diagram in Section 4.1):**

1. Water stored in the **reservoir** (at elevation $H$) possesses gravitational potential energy.
2. It flows through the intake and penstock, where its pressure energy is delivered to the **turbine** runner.
3. The turbine converts hydraulic energy into **mechanical rotational energy** of the shaft.
4. The shaft drives a **synchronous generator** (alternator) that produces 3-phase AC electricity.
5. The electrical output is stepped up in voltage by a **transformer** and transmitted to the grid.

**Advantages of Hydropower:**
- **Renewable** — driven by the solar-powered hydrological cycle.
- **High efficiency** — overall plant efficiency 80% to 90% (highest among renewables).
- **No fuel cost** and **no emissions** during operation.
- **Long asset life** — dams and turbines last 50 to 100 years.
- Provides **baseload, peak, and balancing** power.
- Enables **energy storage** in pumped storage schemes.
- **Reservoir offers** flood control, water supply, irrigation, and recreation.

**Limitations of Hydropower:**
- **High initial capital cost** and long gestation period.
- **Site-specific** — limited to locations with adequate head and water.
- **Submergence and displacement** of large populations and forest land.
- **Environmental impact** on aquatic ecosystems and river flow patterns.
- **Drought sensitivity** — generation drops in dry years.
- **Seismic risk** — large dams in tectonically active zones are vulnerable.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Pelton Wheel | Francis Turbine | Kaplan Turbine |
|---|---|---|---|
| Type | Impulse | Reaction (mixed flow) | Reaction (axial flow) |
| Head range | 300 m – 1700 m | 30 m – 300 m | 2 m – 30 m |
| Specific speed (metric) | 10 – 35 | 60 – 300 | 300 – 1000 |
| Runner blades | Few, bucket-shaped | Many, fixed | Few, adjustable (pitchable) |
| Application | High head, mountain sites | Medium head, large dams | Low head, run-of-river |

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $H_{net} = 400$ m, $Q = 0.5$ m³/s, $\eta_{turbine} = 0.85$, $\eta_{generator} = 0.95$.

**Hydraulic power input:**

$$P_{th} = \rho g Q H = 9.81 \times 0.5 \times 400 = 1962 \text{ kW}$$

**Shaft power (turbine output):**

$$P_{shaft} = P_{th} \times \eta_{turbine} = 1962 \times 0.85 = 1667.7 \text{ kW}$$

**Electrical power (generator output):**

$$P_{elec} = P_{shaft} \times \eta_{generator} = 1667.7 \times 0.95 = 1584.3 \text{ kW} \approx 1.58 \text{ MW}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up "shaft power" and "electrical power"** — the shaft power is the turbine output; the electrical power is after generator losses. Examiners expect you to compute **both** in 7-mark sub-parts.
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply by $\eta_{generator}$** in the second step. Many students stop at shaft power and lose 3 marks.
> 3. **Unit errors** — keeping head in meters, but using $g = 9.81$ m/s² and $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³ is correct. Mixing with $g = 981$ cm/s² causes unit mismatches.
> 4. **Not labeling the answer** in the correct unit (kW or MW). KTU deducts 1 mark for ambiguous units.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Hydropower (Module 1)**

**Core Concepts:**
- Hydropower = conversion of water's potential + kinetic energy into electricity.
- The power equation is $P = \rho g Q H \eta$ or in the shortcut $P \text{ (kW)} = 9.81 \eta Q H$.
- Standard constants: $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², hours in a year = 8760 h.

**Classification (by capacity):**
- Pico: < 100 kW → Micro: 100 kW – 1 MW → Mini: 1 MW – 25 MW → Large: > 25 MW.
- 25 MW threshold is the **India-specific regulatory boundary** (MNRE vs Ministry of Power).

**Turbines — memorize the mapping:**
- High head (300 m+) → **Pelton** (impulse).
- Medium head (30–300 m) → **Francis** (reaction, mixed flow).
- Low head (2–30 m) → **Kaplan** (reaction, axial flow).

**Plant Components — must list all eight:**
- Dam, Spillway, Intake, Headrace, Surge tank, Penstock, Powerhouse (Turbine + Generator), Tailrace, Switchyard.

**Key Formulas (must memorize):**
- $P = 9.81 \eta Q H$ (kW)
- $h_f = \frac{4 f L v^2}{2 g D}$ (Darcy–Weisbach)
- $H_{net} = H_g - h_f - h_{minor}$
- $E = P \times t$ (or with load factor)
- $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{turbine} \times \eta_{generator} \times \eta_{transformer}$

**Numerical Problem Strategy (14-mark format):**
1. List given data with units.
2. Identify whether gross head or net head is provided.
3. Calculate penstock velocity if needed.
4. Apply Darcy–Weisbach if friction loss is required.
5. Substitute in $P = 9.81 \eta Q H$ with **net head**.
6. Compute annual energy with proper load factor.
7. State final answer with **correct units (kW, MW, MWh)**.

**Common Exam Mistakes to Avoid:**
- Using gross head instead of net head (loses 3 marks).
- Forgetting to multiply efficiencies (shaft vs electrical).
- Confusing turbine head range boundaries.
- Omitting the surge tank from the component list.

**Key Real-World Examples to Remember:**
- **Idukki Dam (Kerala):** 780 MW.
- **Tehri Dam (Uttarakhand):** 2400 MW.
- **Three Gorges (China):** 22,500 MW.
- **Itaipu (Brazil/Paraguay):** 14,000 MW.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: and Nuclear Power Plants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT614 - ENERGY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Nuclear Power Plants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚛️ SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nuclear Power Plant (NPP):** A thermal power station that uses a **nuclear reactor** as the heat source to produce steam, which drives a steam turbine connected to an electrical generator. The heat is generated by a controlled **nuclear fission chain reaction** of fissile isotopes (primarily **Uranium-235**, **Plutonium-239**, or **Uranium-233**), releasing enormous energy per unit mass through **Einstein's mass–energy equivalence principle**.

A nuclear power plant is fundamentally a **heat engine**—the only difference from a coal, oil, or natural gas plant is the **source of thermal energy**. In conventional plants, chemical combustion of fossil fuels (oxidation) liberates ~**24 MJ/kg** of coal, whereas in nuclear fission, the destruction of only ~**0.1 %** of the mass of a heavy nucleus releases ~**82,000 GJ/kg** of U-235 — about **3.4 million times** more energy per kilogram.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Banked Wood" Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a stack of precisely stacked wooden logs in a fireplace. Each log cannot ignite on its own — but once you ignite **one**, it releases energy that ignites its neighbours, and so on. The rate of burning can be controlled by how close the logs are stacked (geometry) and how much oxygen is supplied (moderator/control). If logs are too close, the fire runs away (meltdown); if too far, it dies out (subcritical). A nuclear reactor is essentially a **carefully banked fire** of atomic nuclei, kept stable using *control rods* (log removers) and a *moderator* (oxygen/heat spreader).

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following constants must be memorised for KTU numerical problems:
> - **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$
> - **1 atomic mass unit (u):** $1 \text{ u} = 931.494 \text{ MeV}/c^{2} = 1.6605 \times 10^{-27} \text{ kg}$
> - **Energy released per U-235 fission:** $E_f \approx 200 \text{ MeV} \approx 3.2 \times 10^{-11} \text{ J}$
> - **Avogadro's number:** $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ /mol}$
> - **Mass of 1 kg U-235:** Number of atoms $n = \dfrac{N_A}{235} = 2.56 \times 10^{24}$ atoms
> - **Energy from 1 kg U-235 (complete fission):** $\approx 8.2 \times 10^{13} \text{ J} \approx 24{,}000 \text{ MWh}$ (thermal)

## 1.4 Why Nuclear? — Strategic Importance

| Metric | Coal Plant | Nuclear Plant |
|---|---|---|
| Fuel required for 1000 MW·day (thermal) | ~10,000 tonnes coal | ~3.5 kg U-235 |
| CO₂ emissions (per kWh) | ~820 g | ~12 g (lifecycle) |
| Land footprint (per GW) | Large (mining + ash) | Small (with waste storage) |
| Fuel storage on site | Weeks | 18–24 months refuelling cycle |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Physics of Nuclear Fission

When a **slow (thermal) neutron** strikes a fissile nucleus such as **U-235**, the nucleus becomes unstable (U-236*) and splits into two **fission fragments**, releases **2–3 fast neutrons**, and emits **gamma radiation** plus **kinetic energy of fragments**.

$$^{1}_{0}n + ^{235}_{92}U \rightarrow ^{141}_{56}Ba + ^{92}_{36}Kr + 3\,^{1}_{0}n + Q \text{ (energy)}$$

### 2.1.1 Why U-235 Splits with Thermal (Slow) Neutrons

U-235 has a large **fission cross-section** ($\sigma_f \approx 585 \text{ barns}$) for thermal neutrons of energy ~**0.025 eV**. In contrast, U-238 requires fast neutrons (>1 MeV) and has $\sigma_f \approx 0$. This is why **moderators** (light water, heavy water, graphite) are essential in most reactors.

> [!NOTE]
> **1 barn** $= 10^{-24} \text{ cm}^{2}$ — the standard unit of nuclear cross-section.

## 2.2 The Chain Reaction — Self-Sustaining Multiplication

Each fission releases ~**2.5 neutrons on average** (for U-235). For a self-sustaining reaction, exactly **one neutron from each fission** must cause another fission. This condition is quantified by the **effective multiplication factor** $k_{eff}$.

- $k_{eff} < 1$ → **Subcritical** (reaction dies out)
- $k_{eff} = 1$ → **Critical** (steady, controlled power)
- $k_{eff} > 1$ → **Supercritical** (power rises — used in bombs)

## 2.3 The Four-Factor Formula (Ininite Multiplication Factor $k_\infty$)

The **four-factor formula** is the central KTU equation for reactor physics analysis:

$$k_\infty = \eta \cdot \varepsilon \cdot p \cdot f$$

| Factor | Symbol | Physical Meaning | Typical Value (PWR) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reproduction factor** | $\eta$ | Average neutrons produced per neutron absorbed in fuel | ~1.35 (U-235), ~2.1 (Pu-239) |
| **Fast fission factor** | $\varepsilon$ | Ratio of total fissions to thermal fissions in fuel | ~1.06 |
| **Resonance escape probability** | $p$ | Probability that a neutron avoids U-238 resonance capture while slowing down | ~0.87 |
| **Thermal utilisation factor** | $f$ | Fraction of thermal neutrons absorbed in fuel (vs moderator/coolant/structure) | ~0.71 |

## 2.4 The Six-Factor Formula (Finite Reactor with Leakage)

For a **finite-sized reactor**, neutrons leak from the surface. The **effective multiplication factor** becomes:

$$k_{eff} = k_\infty \cdot P_{NL} \cdot P_{TL} = \eta \cdot \varepsilon \cdot p \cdot f \cdot P_{NL} \cdot P_{TL}$$

where $P_{NL}$ and $P_{TL}$ are **non-leakage probabilities** during slowing-down and thermal stages, respectively.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **exam-critical**. Practise using $\vert$ instead of $\vert$ in all handwritten work for LaTeX safety.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = \Delta m \cdot c^{2}$ | Mass–energy equivalence | Joules |
| 2 | $E_f \approx 200 \text{ MeV}$ | Energy per U-235 fission | MeV |
| 3 | $E_{kg} = \dfrac{N_A}{M} \cdot E_f$ | Energy from complete fission of 1 kg | J/kg |
| 4 | $k_\infty = \eta \cdot \varepsilon \cdot p \cdot f$ | Four-factor formula | dimensionless |
| 5 | $k_{eff} = k_\infty \cdot P_{NL} \cdot P_{TL}$ | Six-factor formula | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\rho = \dfrac{k_{eff}-1}{k_{eff}}$ | Reactivity | dimensionless |
| 7 | $CR = \dfrac{\text{fertile atoms converted}}{\text{fissile atoms consumed}}$ | Conversion Ratio | dimensionless |
| 8 | $BR = \eta - 1$ (ideal) | Breeding Ratio (fast reactor) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $T_{d} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\lambda_{d}}$ | Doubling time ($T_d$) | years |
| 10 | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}}$ | Thermal efficiency | percentage |
| 11 | $P = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat transferred in coolant | Watts |
| 12 | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{P_{th}}{h_{fg}}$ | Steam mass flow rate | kg/s |

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

- **PWR (Pressurized Water Reactor):** ~**70%** of world reactors (US, France, China, Russia). Uses enriched U (~3-5% U-235).
- **BWR (Boiling Water Reactor):** ~**20%** of world reactors. Single loop, lower pressure.
- **PHWR (CANDU):** Uses natural uranium (0.7% U-235) with heavy water moderator. Adopted by India (Tarapur, RAPS, MAPS).
- **FBR (Fast Breeder Reactor):** India's **PFBR at Kalpakkam** (500 MWe) — uses liquid sodium coolant, breeds Pu-239 from U-238.
- **HTGR (High-Temperature Gas-Cooled Reactor):** Uses helium coolant and TRISO fuel; future Gen-IV technology.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Energy Released per Fission from Mass Defect

### Problem Statement
Calculate the energy released when a U-235 nucleus undergoes the following fission:
$$^{235}_{92}U + ^{1}_{0}n \rightarrow ^{141}_{56}Ba + ^{92}_{36}Kr + 3\,^{1}_{0}n + Q$$
Given atomic masses:
- $m(^{235}U) = 235.04393 \text{ u}$
- $m(^{1}n) = 1.00867 \text{ u}$
- $m(^{141}Ba) = 140.91770 \text{ u}$
- $m(^{92}Kr) = 91.88540 \text{ u}$

### Step 1: Mass of Reactants (Initial State)
$$m_{initial} = m(^{235}U) + m(^{1}n)$$
$$m_{initial} = 235.04393 + 1.00867 = 236.05260 \text{ u}$$

### Step 2: Mass of Products (Final State)
$$m_{final} = m(^{141}Ba) + m(^{92}Kr) + 3 \cdot m(^{1}n)$$
$$m_{final} = 140.91770 + 91.88540 + 3(1.00867)$$
$$m_{final} = 140.91770 + 91.88540 + 3.02601 = 235.82911 \text{ u}$$

### Step 3: Mass Defect (Mass Converted to Energy)
$$\Delta m = m_{initial} - m_{final}$$
$$\Delta m = 236.05260 - 235.82911 = 0.22349 \text{ u}$$

### Step 4: Convert Mass Defect to Energy (Using $1 \text{ u} = 931.494 \text{ MeV}/c^{2}$)
$$Q = \Delta m \cdot 931.494 \text{ MeV}$$
$$Q = 0.22349 \times 931.494 = 208.16 \text{ MeV}$$

### Step 5: Verification & Physical Interpretation
The value $\approx 200$ MeV agrees with the empirical figure for U-235 fission. This energy is distributed as:
- Kinetic energy of fission fragments: ~165 MeV (~83%)
- Kinetic energy of neutrons: ~5 MeV (~2.5%)
- Instantaneous gamma rays: ~5 MeV (~2.5%)
- Beta & gamma from decay of fragments: ~15 MeV (~7.5%)
- Neutrinos (lost): ~10 MeV (~5%)

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Energy from Complete Fission of 1 kg of U-235

### Step 1: Number of Atoms in 1 kg of U-235
$$N = \dfrac{N_A}{M} = \dfrac{6.022 \times 10^{23}}{235} = 2.5626 \times 10^{21} \text{ atoms/g}$$
$$N_{kg} = 2.5626 \times 10^{24} \text{ atoms/kg}$$

### Step 2: Total Energy from Complete Fission
$$E_{total} = N_{kg} \cdot E_f$$
$$E_{total} = 2.5626 \times 10^{24} \times 200 \text{ MeV}$$
$$E_{total} = 5.1252 \times 10^{26} \text{ MeV}$$

### Step 3: Convert MeV to Joules (Using $1 \text{ MeV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-13} \text{ J}$)
$$E_{total} = 5.1252 \times 10^{26} \times 1.602 \times 10^{-13}$$
$$E_{total} = 8.21 \times 10^{13} \text{ J}$$

### Step 4: Express in MWh (Thermal)
$$E_{total} = \dfrac{8.21 \times 10^{13}}{3.6 \times 10^{9}} = 22{,}805 \text{ MWh}_{th}$$

### Step 5: Comparison with Coal
Coal calorific value = ~$24 \text{ MJ/kg}$. Equivalent coal mass:
$$m_{coal} = \dfrac{8.21 \times 10^{13}}{24 \times 10^{6}} \approx 3.42 \times 10^{6} \text{ kg} = 3420 \text{ tonnes}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** 1 kg of U-235, if completely fissioned, equals the energy of ~**3,420 tonnes** of coal — this is the strategic rationale for nuclear power.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Multiplication Factor and Critical Size

For a bare spherical reactor (no reflector), the **critical radius** $R_c$ satisfies the **geometric buckling** condition:

$$B^{2} = \left(\dfrac{\pi}{R_c}\right)^{2}$$

and the **material buckling** is:
$$B^{2}_{m} = \dfrac{k_\infty - 1}{L^{2}}$$

where $L$ is the **diffusion length** of thermal neutrons in the core medium.

At criticality:
$$B^{2} = B^{2}_{m}$$
$$\left(\dfrac{\pi}{R_c}\right)^{2} = \dfrac{k_\infty - 1}{L^{2}}$$

Solving for the critical radius:
$$R_c = \pi L \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{k_\infty - 1}}$$

The corresponding **critical mass** is then:
$$m_c = \dfrac{4}{3} \pi R_c^{3} \cdot \rho_{fuel}$$

This derivation is essential for understanding why reactors have a **minimum critical size** (typically ~3 m radius for PWR) and why nuclear weapons require highly enriched (weapons-grade) fuel.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Architecture of a Pressurized Water Reactor (PWR) Nuclear Power Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Reactor Core - Fission Heat Source] --> B[Primary Coolant Loop - Pressurized Water 155 bar 322 C]
    B --> C[Steam Generator - Heat Exchanger]
    C --> D[Turbine - High Pressure and Low Pressure Stages]
    D --> E[Generator - Electrical Output]
    E --> F[Grid - Step Up Transformer 400 kV]
    D --> G[Condenser - Cold Sea or Cooling Tower Water]
    G --> H[Feedwater Pump - Condensate Return]
    H --> C
    I[Pressurizer - Maintains 155 bar Pressure] -.-> B
    J[Control Rods - Boron or Cadmium Neutron Absorbers] -.-> A
    K[Reactor Protection System - SCRAM Logic] -.-> J
    L[Containment Structure - 1 m Reinforced Concrete with Steel Liner] --- A
    M[Spent Fuel Pool - Cooling and Shielding] --- A
    N[Waste Treatment and Storage Facility] --- M
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **three-loop architecture** of a PWR ensures the radioactive primary loop never contacts the turbine — this is a fundamental safety feature. The **pressurizer** maintains water in a liquid state even at ~322 °C by keeping pressure above 155 bar (saturation temperature at 155 bar is ~345 °C).

## 4.2 Nuclear Fuel Cycle Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Uranium Ore Mining] --> B[Milling and Yellowcake U3O8]
    B --> C[Conversion to UF6 Gas]
    C --> D[Enrichment - U235 from 0.7 percent to 3 to 5 percent]
    D --> E[Fabrication of Fuel Pellets and Bundles]
    E --> F[Reactor Core - 3 to 4 Year Burnup]
    F --> G[Spent Fuel - Highly Radioactive]
    G --> H{Reprocessing Decision}
    H -- Yes --> I[PUREX Process - Extract Pu and U]
    I --> J[MOX Fuel Fabrication - Mixed Oxide]
    J --> F
    H -- No --> K[Interim Storage in Spent Fuel Pools]
    K --> L[Deep Geological Repository - Permanent Disposal]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **India follows a closed fuel cycle** due to limited uranium reserves and abundant thorium. The three-stage nuclear programme (Homi Bhabha's vision) proceeds: **Stage 1** — PHWR using natural uranium; **Stage 2** — FBR breeding Pu-239 from U-238; **Stage 3** — Thorium-based reactors using U-233 bred from Th-232.

## 4.3 Comparative Reactor Architecture Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PWR[Pressurized Water Reactor]
        P1[Core] --> P2[Primary Loop 155 bar]
        P2 --> P3[Steam Generator]
        P3 --> P4[Turbine Loop 60 bar saturated steam]
    end
    subgraph BWR[Boiling Water Reactor]
        B1[Core] --> B2[Single Loop - Steam forms in core 70 bar]
        B2 --> B3[Turbine]
    end
    subgraph CANDU[Canada Deuterium Uranium Reactor]
        C1[Core - Heavy Water Moderator] --> C2[Pressure Tubes - Natural Uranium Fuel]
        C2 --> C3[Primary Heavy Water Loop]
    end
    subgraph FBR[Fast Breeder Reactor]
        F1[Core - No Moderator] --> F2[Liquid Sodium Coolant 500 C]
        F2 --> F3[Sodium to Water Heat Exchanger]
        F3 --> F4[Steam Generator and Turbine]
    end
```

## 4.4 Functional Component Interaction Matrix

| Component | Primary Function | Key Material | Operating Parameter |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reactor Core** | Hosts controlled fission | UO₂ pellets in Zircaloy-4 cladding | ~300 °C, 155 bar |
| **Pressurizer** | Prevents water boiling | Stainless steel vessel | 345 °C saturation at 155 bar |
| **Steam Generator** | Transfers heat to secondary loop | Inconel tubes | Tube side: 290 °C / Shell: 270 °C |
| **Turbine** | Converts thermal → mechanical | Alloy steel blades | Inlet: 270 °C / 60 bar |
| **Condenser** | Condenses spent steam | Titanium tubes | 0.05 bar vacuum, ~30 °C |
| **Containment** | Last barrier against release | Pre-stressed concrete + steel liner | Designed for 5 bar overpressure |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: Differentiate between nuclear fission and nuclear fusion. State one example of each reaction.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Nuclear Fission | Nuclear Fusion |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Splitting of a heavy nucleus into two lighter fragments | Combining two light nuclei into a heavier one |
| Fuel | U-235, Pu-239, U-233 | Deuterium (D), Tritium (T) |
| Energy per reaction | ~200 MeV per fission | ~17.6 MeV per D-T fusion |
| Conditions | Thermal neutrons + critical mass | ~10⁸ K plasma + magnetic confinement |
| Example | $^{235}U + n \rightarrow ^{141}Ba + ^{92}Kr + 3n + 200 \text{ MeV}$ | $^{2}_{1}D + ^{3}_{1}T \rightarrow ^{4}_{2}He + ^{1}_{0}n + 17.6 \text{ MeV}$ |
| Current status | Commercial (PWR, BWR, PHWR) | Experimental (ITER project, France) |

*(3 marks — full comparison carries full marks)*

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: What is the role of a moderator and control rods in a nuclear reactor?**

**Model Answer:**
- **Moderator:** Slows down fast neutrons (~2 MeV) released from fission to thermal energies (~0.025 eV) so they can efficiently cause further fission in U-235. Common moderators: **light water (H₂O)** in PWR/BWR, **heavy water (D₂O)** in CANDU/PHWR, **graphite** in older UK Magnox and Russian RBMK reactors.
- **Control Rods:** Made of strong neutron absorbers (**boron carbide B₄C**, **cadmium Cd**, or **silver-indium-cadmium Ag-In-Cd alloy**). Inserted into the core to absorb excess neutrons and reduce $k_{eff}$ when reactor power must be reduced or shut down (**SCRAM**).
*(3 marks — 1.5 marks each role explained with examples)*

---

## PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question B-A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper]

**Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a Pressurized Water Reactor (PWR) nuclear power plant. List its main components and state their functions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 1000 MWe nuclear power plant operates at an overall thermal efficiency of 33%. Calculate: (i) the thermal power input, (ii) the daily U-235 consumption assuming 100% enrichment, and (iii) the equivalent coal consumption per day. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

A PWR is a **two-loop** nuclear power plant where the primary loop (radioactive) is isolated from the secondary loop (non-radioactive) by steam generators.

**Main Components:**
1. **Reactor Core** — Houses fuel assemblies with U-235; produces heat via fission.
2. **Primary Coolant Loop** — Pressurized water at 155 bar, 322 °C; transports heat to steam generator without boiling.
3. **Pressurizer** — Maintains system pressure via electric heaters and water spray.
4. **Steam Generator** — U-tube heat exchanger; transfers heat from primary to secondary loop.
5. **Secondary Loop** — Steam at 60 bar, 270 °C drives the turbine.
6. **Turbine & Generator** — Converts thermal energy to electricity.
7. **Condenser & Cooling System** — Condenses exhaust steam using seawater/cooling towers.
8. **Containment Building** — 1 m thick reinforced concrete + steel liner, prevents release of radioactivity.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $P_{e} = 1000 \text{ MWe}$, $\eta_{th} = 0.33$

**(i) Thermal Power Input:**
$$P_{th} = \dfrac{P_e}{\eta_{th}} = \dfrac{1000}{0.33} = 3030.3 \text{ MW}_{th}$$

**(ii) Daily U-235 Consumption:**
Energy required per day (thermal):
$$E_{day} = P_{th} \times 86400 \text{ s} = 3030.3 \times 10^{6} \times 86400 = 2.618 \times 10^{14} \text{ J/day}$$

Mass of U-235 needed (energy from 1 kg = $8.2 \times 10^{13}$ J):
$$m_{U} = \dfrac{2.618 \times 10^{14}}{8.2 \times 10^{13}} = 3.19 \text{ kg/day}$$

**(iii) Equivalent Coal Consumption:**
Using coal calorific value = $24 \text{ MJ/kg}$:
$$m_{coal} = \dfrac{2.618 \times 10^{14}}{24 \times 10^{6}} = 1.09 \times 10^{7} \text{ kg/day} = 10{,}910 \text{ tonnes/day}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — where students lose marks:**
> 1. Students frequently confuse **thermal efficiency** of NPP (~33%) with **Carnot efficiency**. NPP efficiency is limited by low turbine inlet temperature (~270 °C vs ~600 °C in coal plants).
> 2. In Part (ii), using **daily energy** vs **annual energy** is a common mistake. Always multiply MW by 86400 s for one day.
> 3. Coal calorific value must be quoted as **24 MJ/kg** (not 30 MJ/kg, which is for premium coking coal).

---

### ⭐ Question B-B (Choice 2 — Alternative)

**Q: (a)** Explain the four-factor formula. Define each factor and derive the criticality condition. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A reactor uses U-235 fuel with $\eta = 1.35$, $\varepsilon = 1.06$, $p = 0.87$, $f = 0.71$. If non-leakage probabilities are $P_{NL} = 0.92$ and $P_{TL} = 0.95$, determine: (i) $k_\infty$, (ii) $k_{eff}$, and (iii) whether the reactor is critical, subcritical, or supercritical. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Four-Factor Formula:**
$$k_\infty = \eta \cdot \varepsilon \cdot p \cdot f$$

- **$\eta$ (Reproduction Factor):** Average number of fast neutrons produced per neutron absorbed in the fuel.
- **$\varepsilon$ (Fast Fission Factor):** Ratio of total fissions (thermal + fast) to thermal fissions. > 1 because some fast neutrons cause U-238 fission above 1 MeV threshold.
- **$p$ (Resonance Escape Probability):** Probability that a neutron slowing down from fast to thermal energy escapes resonance absorption (mainly by U-238 at ~7 eV and ~20 eV). Depends on fuel-to-moderator ratio.
- **$f$ (Thermal Utilization Factor):** Fraction of thermal neutrons absorbed in the fuel versus moderator, coolant, and structural materials.

**Criticality Condition:**
For a finite reactor with leakage:
$$k_{eff} = k_\infty \cdot P_{NL} \cdot P_{TL} = 1 \text{ (just critical)}$$

$k_{eff} > 1$ → supercritical; $k_{eff} = 1$ → critical; $k_{eff} < 1$ → subcritical.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) Infinite Multiplication Factor:**
$$k_\infty = \eta \cdot \varepsilon \cdot p \cdot f$$
$$k_\infty = 1.35 \times 1.06 \times 0.87 \times 0.71$$
$$k_\infty = 0.8848$$

**(ii) Effective Multiplication Factor:**
$$k_{eff} = k_\infty \cdot P_{NL} \cdot P_{TL}$$
$$k_{eff} = 0.8848 \times 0.92 \times 0.95$$
$$k_{eff} = 0.7734$$

**(iii) Reactor State:**
Since $k_{eff} = 0.7734 < 1$, the reactor is **SUBCRITICAL** — the chain reaction will not sustain itself. To achieve criticality, one or more of the factors ($\eta$, $\varepsilon$, $p$, $f$, $P_{NL}$, $P_{TL}$) must be increased — typically by:
- **Reducing fuel-to-moderator ratio** (increases $p$ and decreases $f$ trade-off)
- **Using a reflector** around the core (increases $P_{TL}$)
- **Increasing enrichment** (increases $\eta$ effective)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:**
> 1. **Sign error in $k_\infty$:** Always check whether $k_\infty < 1$ means the bare infinite lattice cannot sustain fission alone — leakage always reduces it further.
> 2. **Confusing $p$ and $f$:** $p$ involves **resonance capture during slowing down**; $f$ involves **thermal absorption after slowing down**. They are physically different processes.
> 3. **Units of $k_{eff}$:** It is **dimensionless** — never write any units.

---

## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:**

- ⚛️ **Nuclear Fission Definition:** Splitting of heavy nucleus (U-235, Pu-239) by neutron; releases ~200 MeV per reaction.
- 🔥 **Fusion Definition:** Combining light nuclei (D + T → He); releases ~17.6 MeV per D-T reaction.
- 📐 **Four-Factor Formula:** $k_\infty = \eta \cdot \varepsilon \cdot p \cdot f$ — memorize each symbol and its typical PWR value.
- 📐 **Six-Factor Formula:** $k_{eff} = k_\infty \cdot P_{NL} \cdot P_{TL}$ — used for finite reactors with leakage.
- ✅ **Criticality Rule:** $k_{eff} = 1$ for steady power; $< 1$ subcritical; $> 1$ supercritical.
- 🛡️ **Safety Triad (Defense in Depth):** (1) Fuel matrix (UO₂ ceramic retains fission products), (2) Zircaloy cladding, (3) Reactor coolant pressure boundary, (4) Containment building.
- 🌍 **India's Nuclear Status:** 22+ operating reactors, ~8,000 MWe installed capacity. **Kudankulam (VVER-1000)** is the largest, with **PFBR Kalpakkam** (500 MWe) under commissioning.
- 🔄 **Three-Stage Programme:** PHWR (Stage 1) → FBR (Stage 2) → Th-232/U-233 (Stage 3).
- 🗑️ **Waste Classification:** Low-Level (LLW) → Intermediate (ILW) → High-Level (HLW). HLW requires deep geological disposal (500 m+ underground, vitrified glass form).
- ⛏️ **Site Selection Criteria:** Far from population centres (>5 km from city boundary), abundant cooling water, low seismic risk, stable geology, exclusion zone of ~1.5 km radius.
- 📊 **Mass-Energy Conversion:** Always use $1 \text{ u} = 931.494 \text{ MeV}/c^{2}$ for atomic mass defect calculations.
- 🌡️ **Thermal Efficiency of NPP:** Limited to 33–37% (compared to 40–45% for coal) due to low turbine inlet temperature constrained by cladding integrity (~350 °C max).
- 🧪 **Breeding Ratio:** $BR = \eta - 1$ for fast spectrum; $BR > 1$ implies fuel breeding (more Pu-239 produced than U-235 consumed).
- 📈 **Doubling Time:** $T_d = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\lambda_d}$ where $\lambda_d$ = net breeding rate per year; FBR target is $T_d < 10$ years.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparison
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT614 - ENERGY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Comparison

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Energy Engineering and Management**, the term **"Comparison"** refers to the systematic, quantitative, and qualitative evaluation of two or more energy sources, energy conversion technologies, or energy utilization pathways against a defined set of performance, economic, environmental, and social parameters. The objective is to identify the most suitable energy option for a given application, region, or policy framework.

Formally, comparison in energy engineering can be expressed as a multi-criteria decision analysis (MCDA) problem where each energy alternative $E_i$ is evaluated against $n$ criteria $C_j$ using a normalized scoring function.

$$E_{\text{score}} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot \tilde{S}_{ij}$$

where:
- $E_{\text{score}}$ = Composite score of energy alternative $i$
- $w_j$ = Weight assigned to criterion $j$ (with $\sum w_j = 1$)
- $\tilde{S}_{ij}$ = Normalized score of alternative $i$ on criterion $j$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 emphasizes the comparative study of **Conventional vs Non-Conventional**, **Renewable vs Non-Renewable**, and the **relative merits/drawbacks** of each major energy source. Expect direct comparison-based questions in University examinations.

## 1.2 Classification of Energy Sources for Comparison

Energy sources are broadly classified for comparative study as follows:

1. **Primary Energy Sources** — Found in nature (coal, crude oil, natural gas, solar radiation, wind, hydro, tidal, geothermal, biomass).
2. **Secondary Energy Sources** — Derived from primary sources through conversion (electricity, hydrogen, refined fuels).

For comparison, primary sources are further grouped into:

- **Conventional / Non-Renewable:** Coal, Lignite, Petroleum, Natural Gas, Nuclear (Uranium).
- **Non-Conventional / Renewable:** Solar, Wind, Hydro (small), Biomass, Biogas, Geothermal, Tidal, Wave, OTEC (Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion).

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine you are buying a vehicle for daily commute. You compare Petrol, Diesel, Electric, and CNG models on parameters like **cost per km, fuel availability, maintenance, lifespan, and environmental impact**. The "best" choice depends on *your priorities* (cost-conscious vs eco-conscious vs long-distance traveler). Similarly, energy source comparison is not about declaring one source "winner" — it is about **matching the source to the application, geography, and policy goal**.

Just as a farmer in Kerala may prefer coconut-based biomass, while Rajasthan is ideal for solar, comparison must always be **context-dependent**.

## 1.4 Key Comparison Parameters (Standard Metrics)

The following standard metrics are used in KTU-level comparative analysis:

| S.No. | Parameter | Symbol | Unit |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | Energy density | $\rho_E$ | $\text{MJ/kg}$ or $\text{kWh/L}$ |
| 2 | Calorific value (GCV/NCV) | $CV$ | $\text{kcal/kg}$ or $\text{MJ/kg}$ |
| 3 | Conversion efficiency | $\eta$ | dimensionless ($\%$) |
| 4 | Levelized cost of energy | LCOE | $\text{₹/kWh}$ or $\text{USD/MWh}$ |
| 5 | Carbon dioxide emission factor | $EF_{CO_2}$ | $\text{kg CO}_2\text{/kWh}$ |
| 6 | Plant load factor / Capacity utilization | PLF / CUF | dimensionless ($\%$) |
| 7 | Specific capital cost | $C_{sp}$ | $\text{₹/kW}$ |
| 8 | Payback period | $T_{pb}$ | years |
| 9 | Reserve life | $R_L$ | years |
| 10 | Availability factor | $AF$ | dimensionless ($\%$) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Constant:** The Earth's solar constant $I_{sc} = 1367 \;\text{W/m}^2$ is the benchmark against which all solar-based energy comparisons are normalized. The terrestrial solar insolation for India (average) $\approx 4.5 \text{ to } 5.5 \;\text{kWh/m}^2\text{/day}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Comparison

A rigorous comparison of energy sources is performed along **four dimensions**:

### Dimension A — Technical Feasibility
- Resource availability and geographic distribution
- Conversion technology maturity (TRL — Technology Readiness Level)
- Capacity factor and intermittency handling
- Storage requirement

### Dimension B — Economic Viability
- Capital expenditure (CAPEX)
- Operational expenditure (OPEX)
- **Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE)** — the most important KTU exam metric

### Dimension C — Environmental Sustainability
- Greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions per unit energy
- Land and water footprint
- Decommissioning and waste management cost

### Dimension D — Social Acceptance
- Public perception and NIMBY (Not-In-My-Back-Yard) factor
- Job creation potential
- Energy security and import dependence

## 2.2 The Core Comparison Equations

### 2.2.1 Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE)

LCOE is the **single most important metric** for economic comparison:

$$\text{LCOE} = \frac{\sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{C_t}{(1+r)^t}}{\sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{E_t}{(1+r)^t}}$$

where:
- $C_t$ = Total cost in year $t$ (CAPEX + OPEX + fuel + decommissioning)
- $E_t$ = Energy produced in year $t$ (kWh)
- $r$ = Discount rate
- $n$ = Plant lifetime in years

### 2.2.2 Conversion Efficiency

For any energy conversion device, the comparison of performance uses:

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Useful energy output}}{\text{Energy input}} = \frac{E_{out}}{E_{in}}$$

For a heat engine, the **Carnot (ideal) limit** governs comparison:

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

where $T_H$ and $T_L$ are the absolute temperatures (in **Kelvin**) of the hot and cold reservoirs.

### 2.2.3 Capacity Utilization Factor (CUF)

$$\text{CUF} = \frac{E_{\text{actual, annual}}}{P_{\text{rated}} \times 8760 \;\text{hours}}$$

### 2.2.4 Energy Payback Time (EPBT)

$$\text{EPBT} = \frac{E_{\text{lifetime, embodied}}}{E_{\text{annual, generated}}}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates all comparison-relevant formulas. Use $\vert$ inside LaTeX blocks for absolute values; within markdown table cells, write absolute value as $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ to avoid breaking table syntax.

| S.No. | Formula | Application | Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\text{LCOE} = \sum C_t(1+r)^{-t} \big/ \sum E_t(1+r)^{-t}$ | Economic comparison | $\text{₹/kWh}$ |
| 2 | $\eta = E_{out}/E_{in}$ | Efficiency comparison | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - T_L/T_H$ | Thermodynamic limit | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\text{CUF} = E_{act}/(P_{r} \cdot 8760)$ | Plant performance | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\text{EPBT} = E_{embodied}/E_{annual}$ | Sustainability | years |
| 6 | $EF_{CO_2} = m_{CO_2}/E_{gen}$ | Emission comparison | $\text{kg/kWh}$ |
| 7 | $R_L = R_{total}/C_{annual}$ | Reserve life | years |
| 8 | $T_{pb} = \Delta C/\Delta S$ | Simple payback | years |
| 9 | $\rho_E = E/V$ or $E/m$ | Energy density | $\text{MJ/kg}$ or $\text{MJ/L}$ |
| 10 | $\text{NPV} = \sum CF_t(1+r)^{-t} - I_0$ | Project viability | currency |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In practice, comparison studies are used for:

- **Power plant selection** by utilities (CEA, India).
- **Policy formulation** by MNRE (Ministry of New & Renewable Energy, India).
- **Industrial boiler fuel selection** (coal vs FO vs biomass vs natural gas vs electric).
- **Hybrid system design** (e.g., solar + wind + battery sizing).
- **Carbon credit and ESG reporting** under international frameworks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Numerical Derivation — LCOE Comparison

**Problem Setup:** A KTU-style numerical problem is solved below in full, showing every algebraic step. **A 1 MW solar PV plant** and a **1 MW coal-based thermal plant** are compared for LCOE over a 25-year life.

### 3.1.1 Given Data (Solar PV)
- Capital cost $C_0 = 4.5 \;\text{Crore ₹} = 4.5 \times 10^7 \;\text{₹}$
- Annual O&M cost = $1\%$ of CAPEX = $0.045 \;\text{Crore ₹/yr}$
- Annual energy generation $E_{annual} = 1.5 \times 10^6 \;\text{kWh/yr}$ (assuming CUF $\approx 17\%$)
- Discount rate $r = 8\% = 0.08$
- Lifetime $n = 25$ years

### 3.1.2 Given Data (Coal Thermal)
- Capital cost $C_0 = 6.0 \;\text{Crore ₹}$
- Annual O&M + fuel cost = $0.30 \;\text{Crore ₹/yr}$
- Annual energy generation $E_{annual} = 6.0 \times 10^6 \;\text{kWh/yr}$ (assuming CUF $\approx 68.5\%$)
- Discount rate $r = 0.08$, $n = 25$ years

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step LCOE Derivation for Solar PV

We will use the **Present Worth Factor** approach. The discount factor for year $t$ is $(1+r)^{-t}$.

**Step 1 — Sum of discounted costs (numerator):**

The cost in year 0 is the capital cost (paid upfront), and years 1 to 25 have the O&M cost.

$$N_{\text{solar}} = C_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{25} \frac{C_{OM}}{(1+r)^t}$$

$$N_{\text{solar}} = 4.5 \times 10^7 + (4.5 \times 10^5) \cdot \sum_{t=1}^{25} (1.08)^{-t}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the geometric series $\sum_{t=1}^{n}(1+r)^{-t}$:**

For a finite geometric series with first term $a$ and common ratio $q$:

$$\sum_{t=1}^{n} q^t = \frac{q(1-q^n)}{1-q}$$

Here $q = (1.08)^{-1} = 0.925926$, $n = 25$.

$$\sum_{t=1}^{25} (1.08)^{-t} = \frac{0.925926 \, (1 - 0.925926^{25})}{1 - 0.925926}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $0.925926^{25}$:**

$$0.925926^{25} \approx 0.14602$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back:**

$$\sum_{t=1}^{25} (1.08)^{-t} = \frac{0.925926 \times (1 - 0.14602)}{0.074074}$$

$$= \frac{0.925926 \times 0.85398}{0.074074} = \frac{0.79072}{0.074074} \approx 10.6748$$

**Step 5 — Compute the discounted O&M:**

$$C_{OM, \text{disc}} = 4.5 \times 10^5 \times 10.6748 = 4.8037 \times 10^6 \;\text{₹}$$

**Step 6 — Total discounted cost:**

$$N_{\text{solar}} = 4.5 \times 10^7 + 4.8037 \times 10^6 = 5.0296 \times 10^7 \;\text{₹}$$

**Step 7 — Sum of discounted energy (denominator):**

The annual energy is constant, so:

$$D_{\text{solar}} = E_{annual} \cdot \sum_{t=1}^{25} (1.08)^{-t} = 1.5 \times 10^6 \times 10.6748 = 1.6012 \times 10^7 \;\text{kWh}$$

**Step 8 — LCOE for Solar PV:**

$$\text{LCOE}_{\text{solar}} = \frac{5.0296 \times 10^7}{1.6012 \times 10^7} \approx 3.14 \;\text{₹/kWh}$$

### 3.1.4 Step-by-Step LCOE Derivation for Coal Thermal

**Step 1 — Discounted O&M + Fuel:**

$$C_{OM+F, \text{disc}} = 3.0 \times 10^6 \times 10.6748 = 3.2024 \times 10^7 \;\text{₹}$$

**Step 2 — Total discounted cost:**

$$N_{\text{coal}} = 6.0 \times 10^7 + 3.2024 \times 10^7 = 9.2024 \times 10^7 \;\text{₹}$$

**Step 3 — Total discounted energy:**

$$D_{\text{coal}} = 6.0 \times 10^6 \times 10.6748 = 6.4049 \times 10^7 \;\text{kWh}$$

**Step 4 — LCOE for Coal Thermal:**

$$\text{LCOE}_{\text{coal}} = \frac{9.2024 \times 10^7}{6.4049 \times 10^7} \approx 1.44 \;\text{₹/kWh}$$

### 3.1.5 Comparative Conclusion

$$\boxed{\text{LCOE}_{\text{coal}} \approx 1.44 \;\text{₹/kWh} \;\;<\;\; \text{LCOE}_{\text{solar}} \approx 3.14 \;\text{₹/kWh}}$$

Despite the economic disadvantage, the **emission factor** for coal is $\approx 0.95 \;\text{kg CO}_2\text{/kWh}$ while solar is $\approx 0.045 \;\text{kg CO}_2\text{/kWh}$. Hence the comparison must include **environmental externalities** to be complete.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A pure LCOE comparison can be misleading. A complete comparison must also include **social cost of carbon** $C_{carbon} \approx \$50/\text{tonne CO}_2$ (approx). Adjusted LCOE for coal then becomes:
> $$\text{LCOE}_{\text{coal, adj}} = 1.44 + 0.95 \times 0.05 = 1.4875 \;\text{₹/kWh}$$
> This narrows the gap significantly.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix

The following analytical matrix summarizes the comparative analysis of major energy sources across KTU-relevant parameters:

| Parameter | Coal | Oil | Natural Gas | Nuclear | Solar PV | Wind | Hydro | Biomass |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Calorific value (MJ/kg) | $20$–$30$ | $42$–$44$ | $50$–$55$ | $8 \times 10^7$ | N/A | N/A | N/A | $10$–$20$ |
| $EF_{CO_2}$ (kg/kWh) | $0.95$ | $0.78$ | $0.49$ | $0.012$ | $0.045$ | $0.025$ | $0.024$ | $0.39$ |
| LCOE typical (₹/kWh) | $3$–$5$ | $6$–$9$ | $4$–$6$ | $4$–$7$ | $2.5$–$4$ | $2.5$–$4$ | $2$–$4$ | $4$–$7$ |
| Reserve life (global, yrs) | $130$ | $50$ | $55$ | $100+$ | Infinite | Infinite | Infinite | Renewable |
| CUF / PLF typical | $65\%$–$80\%$ | — | $60\%$ | $80\%$–$90\%$ | $15\%$–$25\%$ | $25\%$–$40\%$ | $40\%$–$60\%$ | $60\%$–$80\%$ |
| Maturity (TRL) | $9$ | $9$ | $9$ | $9$ | $9$ | $9$ | $9$ | $8$ |
| Land footprint (m²/MWh/yr) | High | Med | Low | Low | High | High | Very High | Very High |
| Intermittency | No | No | No | No | Yes (day) | Yes (wind) | Seasonal | Moderate |

> [!NOTE]
> The above values are typical orders of magnitude used in KTU board examination problems. Exact values may vary by region, technology vintage, and policy regime.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Energy Source Comparison Taxonomy

```mermaid

    A[Energy Sources] --> B[Conventional Non-Renewable]
    A --> C[Non-Conventional Renewable]
```

```mermaid
    S3 --> W

    U3 --> W
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Decision Flow for Energy Source Choice

