Electrolysis from Solar or Wind] B -- No, nuclear electricity --> D[PINK H2
Electrolysis from Nuclear] B -- Yes, Natural Gas --> E{Is CO2 captured and stored?} B -- Yes, Coal or Biomass --> F{Is CO2 captured and stored?} E -- Yes --> G[BLUE H2
SMR plus CCS] E -- No --> H[GREY H2
Steam Methane Reforming] F -- No, Coal --> I[BROWN H2
Coal Gasification] F -- Yes --> J[BLUE H2 from Coal or Biomass] style C fill:#28a745,stroke:#1c7430,color:#fff style D fill:#e83e8c,stroke:#a02e63,color:#fff style G fill:#007bff,stroke:#005cbf,color:#fff style H fill:#6c757d,stroke:#3d3f42,color:#fff style I fill:#8b4513,stroke:#5a2d0c,color:#fff style J fill:#007bff,stroke:#005cbf,color:#fff ``` ## 4.3 End-Use Conversion Routes for Hydrogen The Mermaid block below illustrates the *three* principal routes by which the chemical energy stored in H₂ is converted into useful mechanical, electrical, or thermal work. ```mermaid flowchart LR H2[H2 Gas from Storage] --> P1{Conversion Path} P1 -- Electrochemical --> FC[Fuel Cell PEMFC or SOFC] P1 -- Internal Combustion --> ICE[H2 Internal Combustion Engine] P1 -- External Combustion --> GT[Gas Turbine or Jet Engine] FC --> W1[Direct Electricity 50 to 60 percent efficiency] ICE --> W2[Mechanical Work 25 to 35 percent efficiency] GT --> W3[Mechanical Work or Electricity 35 to 45 percent efficiency] W1 --> END1[Power EV motors, Grid, Backup] W2 --> END2[Power vehicles, Pumps, Compressors] W3 --> END3[Power plants, Aircraft, Ships] style H2 fill:#ffeeba,stroke:#856404 style W1 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724 style W2 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724 style W3 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **KTU 2024 Regulation Reminder:** Part A questions test **Remembering** and **Understanding** levels of Bloom's Taxonomy. Each must be answered in **1 to 2 sentences** (50–80 words) for full marks. *One mark* is reserved for neatness/clarity/diagrams. --- ### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP] **"Why is hydrogen classified as an energy carrier and not an energy source? Give two reasons."** (3 Marks) **Model Answer:** Hydrogen is an **energy carrier** because it does not occur freely in usable form on Earth — it must be *manufactured* from compounds such as water, methane, or biomass by investing external energy. Energy sources (e.g., coal, sunlight, wind) exist naturally, while hydrogen merely *stores and transports* that primary energy for later use, exactly like a charged battery. **(2 marks for correct classification + 1 mark for any one valid supporting reason).** --- ### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected] **"Differentiate between grey, blue, and green hydrogen."** (3 Marks) **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Grey H₂ | Blue H₂ | Green H₂ | |---|---|---|---| | Production method | Steam Methane Reforming | SMR + Carbon Capture & Storage | Electrolysis with renewable electricity | | CO₂ emissions | ~9 kg CO₂/kg H₂ | ~2–3 kg CO₂/kg H₂ | < 1 kg CO₂/kg H₂ | | Sustainability | **Not sustainable** | **Transitional / bridge** | **Fully sustainable (end-goal)** | **(2 marks for tabulated differences + 1 mark for naming the production route of each).** --- ## 5.2 PART B — Descriptive Question with Internal Choice (14 Marks) > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split into sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. *Internal choice* means the student attempts *either* (i) *or* (ii) of the same question. Marks are awarded for: **formula → substitution → answer → units**. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `Attempt (a) and (b)` #### **(a) [7 Marks]** A hydrogen fuel-cell vehicle consumes **4.0 kg of compressed H₂** to travel 500 km on a single refuelling. The fuel cell operates at a mean cell voltage of **0.75 V** with 2-electron transfer. **Calculate:** - (i) The total electrical energy delivered (using HHV). - (ii) The number of moles of H₂ consumed. - (iii) The round-trip energy efficiency, given the input electricity for H₂ production = **190 MJ**. #### **(b) [7 Marks]** **Explain with a neat block diagram the working of a Proton Exchange Membrane (PEM) fuel cell.** Also state **any three advantages** of fuel cells over IC engines. --- ### **Model Solution — Part (a)** **Step 1.** Mass of H₂, $m = 4.0$ kg. **Step 2.** Moles of H₂ consumed (i): $$ n = \dfrac{m}{M_{H_2}} = \dfrac{4.0 \;\text{kg}}{2.016 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{kg/mol}} $$ $$ n = 1984.1 \;\text{mol} \approx 1.984 \times 10^{3} \;\text{mol} \quad \text{...(i) [2 marks]} $$ **Step 3.** Electrical energy delivered by the fuel cell = (electrical charge passed) × (cell voltage). Charge passed: $$ Q = n \cdot n_e \cdot F = 1984.1 \times 2 \times 96{,}485 $$ $$ Q = 3.829 \times 10^{8} \;\text{C} $$ **Step 4.** Electrical energy: $$ E_{elec} = Q \times V_{cell} = 3.829 \times 10^{8} \times 0.75 $$ $$ E_{elec} = 2.872 \times 10^{8} \;\text{J} \quad \text{...(ii) [3 marks]} $$ **Step 5.** Round-trip efficiency = electrical output / electrical input: $$ \eta_{RT} = \dfrac{2.872 \times 10^{8} \;\text{J}}{190 \times 10^{6} \;\text{J}} $$ $$ \eta_{RT} = \dfrac{287.2}{190} = 1.511 \quad \text{... [ERROR: efficiency cannot exceed 1]} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Correction & KTU Pitfall:** The electrical energy input here is **190 MJ = 1.9 × 10⁸ J**, NOT 1.9 × 10⁹ J. Re-compute: > $$ \eta_{RT} = \dfrac{2.872 \times 10^{8}}{1.9 \times 10^{8}} \approx 1.51 $$ > The result still exceeds 100% — this signals an inconsistency in the question. The correct physical value is $\eta_{RT} \le 1$ (i.e., ≤ 100%). In a real KTU paper, you would be expected to **state the assumption** (e.g., assume input energy = 480 MJ, giving $\eta_{RT} = 60\%$). Always check the *physical plausibility* of your answer. **Re-working with corrected input energy 480 MJ:** $$ \eta_{RT} = \dfrac{2.872 \times 10^{8}}{4.8 \times 10^{8}} = 0.598 = 59.8\% \quad \text{...(iii) [2 marks]} $$ --- ### **Model Solution — Part (b)** **Block diagram of a PEM fuel cell:** ```mermaid flowchart LR A[H2 Gas Inlet
Anode Side] --> B[Anode Catalyst Layer
Pt Catalyst] B --> C[Proton Exchange Membrane
Nafion - conducts H plus only] C --> D[Cathode Catalyst Layer
Pt Catalyst] D --> E[O2 or Air Inlet
Cathode Side] B --> F[External Circuit
Electron Flow delivers DC Power] D --> F C --> G[H plus ions migrate from anode to cathode] D --> H[H2O and Heat Out
Cathode Side] style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style E fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24 style C fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404 style H fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724 ``` **Working principle (4 marks):** 1. At the **anode**, hydrogen molecules are split by a platinum catalyst into protons and electrons: $$ \text{H}_2 \to 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- $$ 2. The **membrane** allows only **H⁺ ions** to pass through; the **electrons** are forced through the external circuit, producing **direct current (DC) electrical power**. 3. At the **cathode**, oxygen combines with the arriving protons and electrons to form **water and heat**: $$ \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \to \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Heat} $$ **Three advantages of fuel cells over IC engines (3 marks):** - **Higher efficiency** (50–60 % vs. 25–35 % for petrol engines, no Carnot limit). - **Zero tailpipe emissions** — only pure water and heat are released. - **Silent operation** — no moving reciprocating parts; minimal noise & vibration. - *(Optional, for extra credit)* Modular design enables easy scaling from watts to megawatts. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `Alternative to Question A` #### **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Steam Methane Reforming (SMR)** process for industrial hydrogen production. Write down the **two main chemical reactions** involved and state the **operating temperature range**. #### **(b) [7 Marks]** A steel plant requires **10 tonnes of hydrogen per day** for Direct Reduced Iron (DRI) production. Estimate the **daily electrical energy requirement** if this hydrogen is produced by a **PEM electrolyser operating at 75% efficiency** (use HHV of H₂). Also calculate the **annual electricity cost** at ₹6 per kWh if the plant operates **350 days/year**. --- ### **Model Solution — Part (a)** **Block diagram (3 marks):** ```mermaid flowchart LR A[CH4 Natural Gas Feed] --> R1[Desulphurisation Unit] R1 --> R2[Reformer Reactor 800 to 900 C
with Ni Catalyst] W[H2O Steam] --> R2 R2 --> R3[Syngas H2 plus CO plus CO2] R3 --> R4[Water-Gas Shift Reactor 200 to 400 C] R3 --> R5[CO2 Removal PSA Unit] R4 --> R5 R5 --> R6[Purified H2 Product 99.99 percent] R5 --> R7[CO2 By-product for Capture or Vent] style A fill:#cce5ff style W fill:#cce5ff style R2 fill:#fff3cd style R4 fill:#fff3cd style R6 fill:#d4edda style R7 fill:#f8d7da ``` **Two main chemical reactions (2 marks):** 1. **Steam Reforming (endothermic, 800–900 °C, Ni catalyst):** $$ \text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \to \text{CO} + 3\text{H}_2 \quad (\Delta H = +206 \text{ kJ/mol}) $$ 2. **Water-Gas Shift (exothermic, 200–400 °C, Fe/Cr catalyst):** $$ \text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \to \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 \quad (\Delta H = -41 \text{ kJ/mol}) $$ **Operating temperature range (2 marks):** - Reformer: **800 – 900 °C** - Shift reactor: **200 – 400 °C** --- ### **Model Solution — Part (b)** **Step 1.** Daily H₂ demand: $$ m = 10{,}000 \;\text{kg/day} $$ **Step 2.** Chemical energy content of this H₂ (using HHV = 141.86 MJ/kg): $$ E_{chem} = m \times HHV = 10{,}000 \times 141.86 = 1.4186 \times 10^{6} \;\text{MJ/day} $$ **Step 3.** Electrical input required at 75% electrolyser efficiency: $$ E_{elec} = \dfrac{E_{chem}}{\eta_{el}} = \dfrac{1.4186 \times 10^{6}}{0.75} $$ **Step 4.** Compute: $$ E_{elec} = 1.8915 \times 10^{6} \;\text{MJ/day} $$ **Step 5.** Convert to kWh (1 kWh = 3.6 MJ): $$ E_{elec} = \dfrac{1.8915 \times 10^{6}}{3.6} = 525{,}416 \;\text{kWh/day} $$ **Step 6.** Annual energy: $$ E_{annual} = 525{,}416 \times 350 = 1.839 \times 10^{8} \;\text{kWh/year} $$ **Step 7.** Annual cost: $$ C = 1.839 \times 10^{8} \times 6 = \text{₹} 110.3 \;\text{crore/year} \quad \text{...(b) [7 marks]} $$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Where students LOSE marks in this module (read carefully before the exam):** > > 1. **HHV vs. LHV Confusion (–2 to –3 marks):** The most common error. PEM fuel cells & domestic boilers use **different bases**. *Always state which one you are using and why.* > 2. **"Hydrogen is a source" mistake (–2 marks):** Hydrogen is an *energy carrier*. Saying it is a source will lose credit. > 3. **Units inconsistency (–1 to –2 marks):** Mixing kJ with MJ, or kg with tonnes, without conversion. Examiners do check the dimensional consistency. > 4. **Missing the electrolyser efficiency step (–2 marks):** When asked "energy required to *produce* H₂", students often forget the electrolyser's 65–80% efficiency and quote HHV directly. > 5. **No block diagram (–2 marks):** For 7-mark "explain with diagram" questions, the diagram carries a *minimum* 2 marks even if the text is correct. > 6. **Stoichiometry of combustion (–1 mark):** The balanced equation for H₂ + O₂ is **H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O**, *not* H₂ + O₂ → H₂O₂. > 7. **Trick questions on density (–1 mark):** Always specify whether the H₂ is **gaseous at STP, compressed at 350/700 bar, or liquefied at 20 K** — its energy density changes by orders of magnitude. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - 🔑 **Hydrogen is an energy CARRIER, not a SOURCE.** Do not confuse the two in definitions. - 🔑 **HHV = 141.86 MJ/kg; LHV = 119.96 MJ/kg.** Memorize both. Difference is 21.9 MJ/kg (the latent heat of water vapour → liquid). - 🔑 **Reversible cell voltage:** 1.481 V (HHV basis) and 1.229 V (LHV basis) — the difference of 0.252 V is entropic. - 🔑 **Colours to remember:** Grey = SMR (dirty); Blue = SMR + CCS (transitional); Green = renewable electrolysis (target); Brown = coal gasification; Pink = nuclear electrolysis; Turquoise = methane pyrolysis. - 🔑 **Flammability range in air: 4–75 vol%** — extremely wide. Minimum ignition energy **0.02 mJ** — extremely low. ⇒ Hydrogen is *much* more hazardous to handle than gasoline. - 🔑 **Combustion reaction:** H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O (clean, single product). - 🔑 **Mass energy density advantage:** ~2.78× gasoline. **Volumetric energy density drawback:** ~1/2700 of gasoline at STP → compression or liquefaction mandatory. - 🔑 **Hydrogen economy pillars:** Production → Storage → Transport → End-use. - 🔑 **Faraday constant:** F = 96 485 C/mol. Use in any electrochemical calculation. - 🔑 **Round-trip efficiency (electricity → H₂ → electricity):** typically **35–45 %**, far below Li-ion battery's 85 %+. So hydrogen is *not* a competitor to batteries for short-duration storage; it dominates *long-duration* and *sectoral coupling*. - 🔑 **Top 3 end-use sectors being decarbonized by H₂:** Heavy transport, Steel (DRI), Ammonia/fertilizers. - 🔑 **Key safety note for valve answer:** Hydrogen's high buoyancy (it is 14× lighter than air) means it disperses *upwards* rapidly in open spaces, reducing accumulation risk — this is one of its few *safety advantages*. - 🔑 **KTU favourite mnemonics:** - Colours: ***"G**reen is **G**oal, **B**lue is **B**ridge, **G**rey is **G**o-now-but-pay-later"* - HHV > LHV: *"H_HV catches the water as **L**iquid; **L**HV lets it escape as **V**apour"*

topics.title: Sources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBBT614 - ENERGY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Sources # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Energy Sources** are natural or processed reservoirs, substances, or physical phenomena from which useful energy can be extracted, converted, and utilized to perform work, generate electricity, or produce heat for industrial, commercial, and domestic applications. In the context of **Energy Engineering and Management (OEBBT614)**, energy sources form the foundational input to every downstream topic — conversion technologies, economics, auditing, and policy. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 1, Topic: Sources)** > *"Energy sources are the physical means by which energy is captured, stored, or made available for human use. They are broadly classified by origin (fossil, nuclear, renewable), by availability (primary/secondary), and by renewability (conventional/non-conventional)."* ### 1.2 Hierarchical Classification of Energy Sources Energy sources are systematically classified along three orthogonal axes, as required by the KTU 2024 scheme: **Axis A — Stage of Conversion** - **Primary Energy Sources:** Energy harnessed directly from nature (coal, crude oil, sunlight, wind, uranium). - **Secondary Energy Sources:** Energy derived by converting primary forms (electricity, hydrogen, gasoline, diesel, LPG). **Axis B — Renewability** - **Renewable (Non-Conventional):** Replenished on human timescale — solar, wind, hydro, biomass, geothermal, tidal, wave, OTEC. - **Non-Renewable (Conventional):** Finite geological reserves — coal, petroleum, natural gas, nuclear (uranium). **Axis C — End Use Maturity** - **Conventional Sources:** Commercially deployed for over a century (coal, oil, gas, large hydro). - **Non-Conventional Sources:** Recent large-scale deployment (PV solar, wind farms, fuel cells, nuclear fusion R&D). ### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Energy Piggy Bank" > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** > Think of Earth's energy sources as a **family piggy bank** with three jars: > 1. **The "Cookie Jar" (Fossil Fuels)** — Your grandparents saved cookies decades ago. They taste great now, but once eaten, they're gone for **thousands of years**. > 2. **The "Salary Jar" (Renewables)** — Cookies that arrive fresh every month (sunlight, wind, rain). You can spend them without guilt because more keep coming. > 3. **The "Pension Jar" (Nuclear)** — A huge lump-sum jar that releases enormous energy slowly. Handle carefully, but the cookie supply is vast. > > As an **energy engineer**, your job is to decide which jar to dip into, when, and how efficiently — this is the essence of *Energy Management*. ### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics The following constants are **must-memorize** for KTU examinations: | Constant | Symbol | Value | Unit | |----------|--------|-------|------| | Solar Constant | $G_{sc}$ | **1367** | $\text{W/m}^2$ | | Standard Gravity | $g$ | **9.81** | $\text{m/s}^2$ | | Density of Water | $\rho_{w}$ | **1000** | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | | Density of Air | $\rho_{a}$ | **1.225** | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | | Density of Seawater | $\rho_{sw}$ | **1025** | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | | Latent Heat of Vaporization (water) | $h_{fg}$ | **2257** | $\text{kJ/kg}$ | | 1 TOE (Tonne of Oil Equivalent) | — | **41.868** | **GJ** | | 1 kWh | — | **3.6** | **MJ** | | Earth's Tilt | $\phi$ | **23.45** | **degrees** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always state units in every numerical answer. The absence of units is one of the top reasons students lose 0.5–1 mark per question in ESE. ### 1.5 Quick Glance — Global vs. Indian Energy Mix | Source | Global Share (%) | India Share (%) | |--------|------------------|-----------------| | Coal | ~27 | ~44 | | Oil | ~31 | ~25 | | Natural Gas | ~23 | ~6 | | Renewables | ~13 | ~12 | | Nuclear | ~4 | ~3 | | Hydro (Large) | ~7 | ~10 | *(Approximate figures as per recent IEA & MNRE reports; KTU accepts approximate values within ±2%.)* # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Detailed Operational Breakdown of Each Energy Source ### 2.1.1 Coal — The King of Conventional Sources - **Origin:** Fossilized plant matter subjected to high pressure and temperature over millions of years. - **Types:** Anthracite (highest carbon ~92%) → Bituminous → Lignite → Peat. - **Calorific Value Range:** **8,000 – 35,000 kJ/kg**. - **India's Reserves:** ~360 billion tonnes (5th globally). - **Major Issue:** Highest $\text{CO}_2$ emission per unit electricity (~1000 g $\text{CO}_2$/kWh). ### 2.1.2 Petroleum (Crude Oil & Derivatives) - **Refining Products:** LPG, gasoline, kerosene, diesel, fuel oil, bitumen. - **Calorific Value:** **42,000 – 45,000 kJ/kg** for gasoline/diesel. - **Energy Density:** Highest among liquid fuels — makes it dominant for transport. ### 2.1.3 Natural Gas (Methane $\text{CH}_4$) - **Calorific Value:** **38,000 – 55,000 kJ/m}^3$ (depending on composition). - **Cleanest Fossil Fuel:** ~50% less $\text{CO}_2$ per unit energy than coal. - **India's Position:** Heavy importer (LNG from Qatar, USA); domestic production declining. ### 2.1.4 Solar Energy — The Inexhaustible Reservoir - **Mechanism:** Direct conversion via **photovoltaic (PV)** cells or **concentrated solar power (CSP)**. - **Solar Constant:** $G_{sc} = 1367 \text{ W/m}^2$ (energy received on a surface perpendicular to Sun's rays at the outer edge of atmosphere at mean Earth–Sun distance). - **India's Solar Potential:** ~300 sunny days/year → **~5000 trillion kWh/year** theoretical potential (MNRE). ### 2.1.5 Wind Energy - **Source:** Differential heating of Earth's surface by Sun creates pressure gradients → kinetic energy of air masses. - **Power in Wind:** $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho_{air} A v^3$ - **Betz Limit:** Maximum extractable fraction = **59.3% (16/27)** of kinetic energy. ### 2.1.6 Hydropower - **Source:** Gravitational potential energy of stored water. - **Power:** $P = \rho_w g Q H$ (kW, when $Q$ in m³/s and $H$ in m). - **Global Share:** ~16% of world electricity; India's installed capacity ~46 GW (4th globally). ### 2.1.7 Biomass & Biofuels - **Forms:** Wood, agricultural residue, biogas, biodiesel, ethanol. - **Calorific Value:** **15,000 – 20,000 kJ/kg** for dry biomass. - **Carbon Neutrality:** $\text{CO}_2$ released during combustion is reabsorbed during regrowth. ### 2.1.8 Geothermal Energy - **Source:** Heat from Earth's core (~6000 °C) and radioactive decay. - **Types:** Dry steam, flash steam, binary cycle. - **India:** Puga Valley (Ladakh) and Manikaran (Himachal Pradesh) identified as prospective sites. ### 2.1.9 Tidal, Wave & OTEC - **Tidal:** Exploits Moon–Sun gravitational pull (potential energy of tides). - **Wave:** Kinetic energy of ocean surface waves. - **OTEC (Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion):** Uses temperature difference ($\Delta T \geq 20°\text{C}$) between warm surface and cold deep seawater. ### 2.1.10 Nuclear Energy - **Fuel:** Uranium-235, Plutonium-239, Thorium-232. - **Energy Density:** 1 kg U-235 ≈ 20,000 tonnes of coal (via $E = mc^2$). - **India's Strategy:** Three-stage nuclear program (Pressurized Heavy Water → Fast Breeder → Thorium-based). ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these formulas — they cover ~80% of numerical questions in Module 1 ESE.** | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $\text{GCV} = 8080\,C + 34500\!\left(H - \tfrac{O}{8}\right) + 2240\,S$ | Dulong's Formula (coal, in kcal/kg) | kcal/kg | | 2 | $\text{NCV} = \text{GCV} - 0.09\,H\,(584 - T_v)$ | Net CV from Gross CV; $T_v$ = flue gas temp | kcal/kg | | 3 | $P_{wind} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,A\,v^{3}$ | Power in wind | W | | 4 | $C_p^{max} = \tfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$ | Betz coefficient (max power coefficient) | — | | 5 | $P_{hydro} = \rho_w\,g\,Q\,H$ | Hydroelectric power | W (when $\rho$ in kg/m³, $Q$ in m³/s, $H$ in m) | | 6 | $G_{on} = G_{sc}\!\left(1 + 0.033\cos\!\tfrac{360\,n}{365}\right)$ | Extraterrestrial radiation on day $n$ | W/m² | | 7 | $I_T = I_b R_b + I_d\!\left(\tfrac{1 + \cos\beta}{2}\right) + I\,\rho_g\!\left(\tfrac{1 - \cos\beta}{2}\right)$ | Radiation on tilted surface | W/m² | | 8 | $E = mc^{2}$ | Mass-energy equivalence (nuclear) | J | | 9 | $P_{tidal} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho_{sw}\,g\,A^{2}\,F$ | Tidal barrage power | W | | 10 | $\eta_{OTEC} = \tfrac{T_w - T_c}{T_w}$ | Carnot efficiency of OTEC | — | > **Note:** In the table above, $C, H, O, S$ are mass fractions of Carbon, Hydrogen, Oxygen, Sulphur in the fuel on a dry basis. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Formula | Practical Application | |---------|----------------------| | Dulong's CV | Coal purchase contracts, power plant efficiency | | Wind Power Eq. | Wind farm site selection, blade sizing | | Hydro Power Eq. | Dam design, turbine selection (Pelton/Francis/Kaplan) | | Solar Geometry | PV panel tilt optimization in Kerala (latitude ~10°N) | | Betz Limit | Wind turbine aerodynamic design | | $E=mc^2$ | Justifies nuclear energy density arguments in policy debates | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Wind Power Equation (Betz Limit Foundation) ### Statement Derive an expression for the power available in wind passing through a swept area $A$ at velocity $v$, and hence find the maximum extractable power. ### Step-by-Step Derivation Consider an idealized wind turbine with a circular swept area $A$ ($\text{m}^2$). Let: - $\rho$ = density of air = **1.225 kg/m³** at STP - $v_1$ = wind velocity upstream of rotor (m/s) - $v_2$ = wind velocity at the rotor plane (m/s) - $v_3$ = wind velocity downstream of rotor (m/s) For an ideal turbine, the air flow is assumed uniform and incompressible, and $v_2$ equals the average of $v_1$ and $v_3$ (this assumption comes from continuity and is essential for maximum power extraction). **Step 1 — Mass flow rate through the rotor:** $$\dot{m} = \rho A v_2$$ **Step 2 — Force exerted by the rotor on the air (rate of momentum change):** $$F = \dot{m}\,(v_1 - v_3) = \rho A v_2 (v_1 - v_3)$$ **Step 3 — Power extracted by the rotor (force × velocity at rotor):** $$P = F \cdot v_2 = \rho A v_2^{2} (v_1 - v_3)$$ **Step 4 — Apply the optimal condition $v_2 = \dfrac{v_1 + v_3}{2}$:** $$P = \rho A \left(\frac{v_1 + v_3}{2}\right)^{2}(v_1 - v_3)$$ **Step 5 — Expand and simplify:** $$P = \frac{\rho A}{4}(v_1 + v_3)^{2}(v_1 - v_3)$$ **Step 6 — Introduce axial induction factor $a$ such that $v_3 = v_1(1 - 2a)$ and $v_2 = v_1(1-a)$:** $$\boxed{P = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v_1^{3} \cdot 4a(1-a)^{2}}$$ **Step 7 — Define power coefficient $C_p$:** $$C_p = \frac{P}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A v_1^{3}} = 4a(1-a)^{2}$$ **Step 8 — Maximize $C_p$ w.r.t. $a$ (set $\dfrac{dC_p}{da} = 0$):** $$\frac{dC_p}{da} = 4(1-a)^{2} - 8a(1-a) = 4(1-a)(1 - 3a) = 0$$ This gives either $a = 1$ (trivial, no flow) or $a = \tfrac{1}{3}$. **Step 9 — Substitute $a = \tfrac{1}{3}$:** $$C_p^{max} = 4 \cdot \tfrac{1}{3} \cdot \left(\tfrac{2}{3}\right)^{2} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$$ **Step 10 — Final maximum power (Betz Limit):** $$\boxed{P_{max} = \frac{16}{27} \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho A v^{3} = \frac{8}{27}\rho A v^{3}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Interpretation:** No turbine can extract more than **59.3%** of the kinetic energy of wind. The remaining 40.7% must be left in the wake to keep the air flowing through the rotor. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Calorific Value (Dulong's Formula) ### Statement The calorific value of a coal sample can be theoretically estimated from its ultimate analysis. Derive Dulong's formula for Higher (Gross) Calorific Value. ### Step-by-Step Derivation Let the ultimate analysis of coal (mass fractions, dry basis) be: - $C$ = carbon fraction - $H$ = hydrogen fraction - $O$ = oxygen fraction - $S$ = sulphur fraction - $N$ = nitrogen (inert, ignored) - $A$ = ash (inert, ignored) **Step 1 — Combustion of Carbon:** $$\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 \quad ; \quad \Delta H = 8080 \text{ kcal/kg of C}$$ **Step 2 — Combustion of Hydrogen:** $$\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} \quad ; \quad \Delta H = 34{,}500 \text{ kcal/kg of H}_2$$ **Step 3 — Oxygen correction:** Hydrogen already combined with oxygen in the fuel forms water but does NOT release heat. The mass of "external" hydrogen available for combustion is: $$H_{effective} = H - \frac{O}{8}$$ *(Since $\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2$ corresponds to 16 kg O per 2 kg H₂, i.e., O/8 kg of oxygen consumes H/8 kg of hydrogen. Wait — recheck: 16 g O combines with 2 g H₂ → so 1 g O consumes 1/8 g H₂. So effective free H = H − O/8.)* **Step 4 — Combustion of Sulphur:** $$\text{S} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{SO}_2 \quad ; \quad \Delta H = 2240 \text{ kcal/kg of S}$$ **Step 5 — Add all contributions (per kg of fuel):** $$\boxed{\text{GCV} = 8080\,C + 34500\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 2240\,S \;\; [\text{kcal/kg}]}$$ **Step 6 — Convert to SI units (1 kcal = 4.186 kJ):** $$\text{GCV}_{kJ/kg} = 4.186 \times \left[8080\,C + 34500\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 2240\,S\right]$$ $$\text{GCV}_{kJ/kg} \approx 33{,}800\,C + 144{,}000\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 9414\,S$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why "O/8" and not "O/16"?** Because oxygen combines with hydrogen as $\text{H}_2\!:\!\text{O} = 2\!:\!16$, so for every 1 kg of oxygen in fuel, 1/8 kg of hydrogen is locked up as water and does not combust. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Extraterrestrial Radiation on a Horizontal Surface (Solar Constant Variation) ### Statement The solar constant at the outer atmosphere varies throughout the year due to Earth's elliptical orbit. Derive the day-number correction. ### Derivation Earth's orbit is elliptical with eccentricity $e = 0.0167$. The instantaneous Earth–Sun distance $r$ varies as: $$r = r_0\,(1 - e\cos\theta)$$ where $\theta$ is the true anomaly. Since $r_0$ is the mean distance and $G_{sc}$ is the radiation at $r_0$, by the inverse-square law: $$G_{on} = G_{sc}\left(\frac{r_0}{r}\right)^{2} = G_{sc}(1 - e\cos\theta)^{-2}$$ Approximating $(1-x)^{-2} \approx 1 + 2x$ for small $e$: $$G_{on} \approx G_{sc}\,(1 + 2e\cos\theta)$$ The day number $n$ (1 = Jan 1) relates to $\theta$ approximately as $\theta \approx \tfrac{360(n-3)}{365}$. Substituting $e = 0.0167$: $$\boxed{G_{on} = G_{sc}\left(1 + 0.033\cos\frac{360\,n}{365}\right)}$$ This gives $G_{on} = 1412 \text{ W/m}^2$ around Jan 3 (perihelion) and $G_{on} = 1322 \text{ W/m}^2$ around July 4 (aphelion) — a ±3.3% variation. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Selection of Sources | Criterion | Coal | Oil | Natural Gas | Solar PV | Wind | Hydro | Nuclear | |-----------|------|-----|-------------|----------|------|-------|---------| | Initial Capital Cost | High | Medium | High | High | Medium-High | Very High | Very High | | Operating Cost | Low | Medium | Low | Very Low | Very Low | Very Low | Low | | $\text{CO}_2$ Emissions | Very High | High | Medium | Zero | Zero | Zero | Zero | | Plant Load Factor | 60–80% | — | — | 15–25% | 20–40% | 30–60% | 85–95% | | Energy Density | Medium | High | High | Low | Very Low | Medium | Extremely High | | Land Footprint | High | Medium | Medium | Very High | Very High | Very High (flooded) | Low | | Construction Time | 4–6 yr | — | — | <1 yr | 1–2 yr | 5–10 yr | 8–15 yr | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Comprehensive Classification of Energy Sources ```mermaid flowchart TD A["ENERGY SOURCES"]:::root --> B["PRIMARY"] A --> C["SECONDARY"] B --> D["RENEWABLE"] B --> E["NON RENEWABLE"] D --> D1["SOLAR"] D --> D2["WIND"] D --> D3["HYDRO"] D --> D4["BIOMASS"] D --> D5["GEOTHERMAL"] D --> D6["TIDAL WAVE OTEC"] E --> E1["COAL"] E --> E2["PETROLEUM"] E --> E3["NATURAL GAS"] E --> E4["NUCLEAR U235 TH232"] C --> C1["ELECTRICITY"] C --> C2["HYDROGEN"] C --> C3["GASOLINE DIESEL LPG"] classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff classDef renewable fill:#16a34a,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff classDef nonrenew fill:#dc2626,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff classDef secondary fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#000,color:#000000 D1:::renewable D2:::renewable D3:::renewable D4:::renewable D5:::renewable D6:::renewable E1:::nonrenew E2:::nonrenew E3:::nonrenew E4:::nonrenew C1:::secondary C2:::secondary C3:::secondary ``` > **Reading Guide:** Green nodes = renewable; Red nodes = non-renewable; Orange = secondary (derived) sources. --- ## 4.2 Energy Conversion Pathway — From Source to End Use ```mermaid flowchart LR SUN["SUN
FUSION REACTOR"]:::sun --> SOLAR["SOLAR
PV AND CSP"] SUN --> WIND["WIND
KINETIC"] SUN --> HYDRO["HYDRO
POTENTIAL"] SUN --> BIO["BIOMASS
PHOTOSYNTHESIS"] COAL["COAL
GEOLOGICAL"]:::fossil --> STEAM["STEAM TURBINE"] OIL["OIL"]:::fossil --> ICE["IC ENGINE"] GAS["NATURAL GAS"]:::fossil --> GT["GAS TURBINE"] URANIUM["URANIUM
FISSION"]:::nuclear --> NUC["NUCLEAR REACTOR"] SOLAR --> GEN1["GENERATOR"] WIND --> GEN2["GENERATOR"] HYDRO --> GEN3["GENERATOR"] BIO --> GEN4["GENERATOR"] STEAM --> GEN5["GENERATOR"] ICE --> MECH["MECHANICAL"] GT --> GEN6["GENERATOR"] NUC --> GEN7["GENERATOR"] GEN1 --> GRID["ELECTRICAL GRID"]:::grid GEN2 --> GRID GEN3 --> GRID GEN4 --> GRID GEN5 --> GRID GEN6 --> GRID GEN7 --> GRID MECH --> TRANSPORT["TRANSPORT SECTOR"] GRID --> IND["INDUSTRY"] GRID --> DOM["DOMESTIC"] GRID --> COMM["COMMERCIAL"] classDef sun fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef fossil fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff classDef nuclear fill:#581c87,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff classDef grid fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff class SUN sun class COAL,OIL,GAS fossil class URANIUM nuclear class GRID grid ``` --- ## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture of an Integrated Energy System (Functional Mapping) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SRC["SOURCE LAYER"] S1["COAL PLANT"] S2["GAS PLANT"] S3["NUCLEAR PLANT"] S4["SOLAR PV"] S5["WIND FARM"] S6["HYDRO DAM"] end subgraph CONV["CONVERSION LAYER"] C1["BOILER STEAM CYCLE"] C2["GAS CYCLE"] C3["RANKINE NUCLEAR"] C4["DC TO AC INVERTER"] C5["AC GENERATOR"] C6["FRANCIS PELTON TURBINE"] end subgraph TRANS["TRANSMISSION LAYER"] T1["HV STEP UP"] T2["HV TRANSMISSION LINES"] T3["HV STEP DOWN"] end subgraph USE["END USE LAYER"] U1["INDUSTRIAL MOTORS"] U2["DOMESTIC LIGHTING"] U3["TRANSPORT EV"] U4["COMMERCIAL HVAC"] end S1 --> C1 --> T1 S2 --> C2 --> T1 S3 --> C3 --> T1 S4 --> C4 --> T1 S5 --> C5 --> T1 S6 --> C6 --> T1 T1 --> T2 --> T3 T3 --> U1 T3 --> U2 T3 --> U3 T3 --> U4 ``` --- ## 4.4 India's Energy Resource Distribution (Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart TD IND["INDIA ENERGY PROFILE"]:::title --> COALR["COAL
360 BT RESERVES
5TH GLOBALLY"]:::coal IND --> OILR["PETROLEUM
0.3 PC RESERVES
85 PERCENT IMPORT"]:::oil IND --> GASR["NATURAL GAS
1.4 TCM RESERVES
50 PERCENT IMPORT"]:::gas IND --> SOLARR["SOLAR
3000 GW POTENTIAL
75 GW INSTALLED 2024"]:::solar IND --> WINDR["WIND
302 GW POTENTIAL
47 GW INSTALLED"]:::wind IND --> HYDROR["HYDRO
145 GW POTENTIAL
46 GW INSTALLED"]:::hydro IND --> NUCR["NUCLEAR
22 GW OPERATIONAL
2032 TARGET 63 GW"]:::nuclear IND --> BIOR["BIOMASS
25 GW CAPACITY
RURAL DOMINANT"]:::bio classDef title fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px classDef coal fill:#404040,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff classDef oil fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff classDef gas fill:#a16207,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff classDef solar fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#000,color:#000000 classDef wind fill:#0891b2,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff classDef hydro fill:#1d4ed8,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff classDef nuclear fill:#7e22ce,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff classDef bio fill:#15803d,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff class COALR coal class OILR oil class GASR gas class SOLARR solar class WINDR wind class HYDROR hydro class NUCR nuclear class BIOR bio ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **"Distinguish between primary and secondary energy sources. Give two examples of each."** **Model Answer:** Primary energy sources are those obtained directly from nature without any conversion process, while secondary energy sources are derived from primary sources through conversion or refinement processes. | Aspect | Primary | Secondary | |--------|---------|-----------| | Source | Natural | Derived | | Conversion | Not required | Required | | Examples | Coal, Crude Oil, Sunlight, Uranium | Electricity, LPG, Hydrogen, Gasoline | | Storage | Often site-specific | Generally transportable | **Examples:** - *Primary:* (i) Coal, (ii) Sunlight - *Secondary:* (i) Electricity (from hydro/thermal), (ii) Diesel (refined from crude oil) > **Word Count Target:** ~80–100 words for full 3 marks. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **"What is the significance of the solar constant? State its value."** **Model Answer:** The solar constant $G_{sc}$ is defined as the rate at which solar energy is received per unit area on a surface placed perpendicular to the Sun's rays at the mean Earth–Sun distance, **outside the Earth's atmosphere**. It is the fundamental input parameter for all solar energy calculations — incident radiation on tilted surfaces, PV sizing, and CSP plant design. $$\boxed{G_{sc} = 1367 \text{ W/m}^2}$$ **Significance:** 1. Establishes the **upper limit** of solar resource available at Earth's orbit. 2. Acts as the **baseline** for atmospheric attenuation studies (AM 1.5 = 1000 W/m²). 3. Forms the basis of the **extraterrestrial radiation** equation: $G_{on} = G_{sc}(1 + 0.033\cos\tfrac{360n}{365})$. > **Examiner Tip:** Always write both the value AND units — missing units = −0.5 mark. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Module carries one 14-mark question with internal choice (either Q1 OR Q2). Solve ONE option only. --- ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **(a)** Classify energy sources in detail with a neat sketch. Discuss the advantages and limitations of renewable energy sources. **\[7 Marks\]** **(b)** A coal sample has the following ultimate analysis by mass: C = 68%, H = 5%, O = 12%, S = 1.5%, N = 1.0%, Ash = 12.5%. Calculate: (i) Gross Calorific Value using Dulong's formula. (ii) Net Calorific Value if the flue gas leaves at 200°C. **\[7 Marks\]** #### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a) **Classification:** Energy sources are classified into three categories: 1. **Primary vs. Secondary** (based on conversion stage) 2. **Renewable vs. Non-Renewable** (based on replenishment rate) 3. **Conventional vs. Non-Conventional** (based on historical usage) *(Refer to SECTION 4.1 for the classification diagram.)* **Renewable Energy Sources — Advantages:** - Inexhaustible on human timescale. - Zero/low $\text{CO}_2$ emissions → mitigate climate change. - Decentralized generation possible (rural electrification). - Low operating and maintenance costs after installation. - No fuel cost, hence price stability. **Limitations:** - **Intermittency** (solar at night, wind calm periods). - **Low energy density** → large land area required. - **High initial capital cost.** - **Storage challenges** — need batteries/hydrogen. - **Grid integration issues** — variability affects stability. #### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b) **Given (mass fractions on dry basis):** - $C = 0.68$, $H = 0.05$, $O = 0.12$, $S = 0.015$ **Step (i) — Apply Dulong's Formula:** $$\text{GCV} = 8080\,C + 34500\!\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 2240\,S$$ Compute each term separately: $$8080 \times 0.68 = 5494.4 \text{ kcal/kg}$$ $$H - \frac{O}{8} = 0.05 - \frac{0.12}{8} = 0.05 - 0.015 = 0.035$$ $$34500 \times 0.035 = 1207.5 \text{ kcal/kg}$$ $$2240 \times 0.015 = 33.6 \text{ kcal/kg}$$ $$\text{GCV} = 5494.4 + 1207.5 + 33.6 = \boxed{6735.5 \text{ kcal/kg}}$$ In SI units: $$\text{GCV} = 6735.5 \times 4.186 \approx 28{,}193 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step (ii) — Calculate NCV at $T_v = 200°\text{C}$:** $$\text{NCV} = \text{GCV} - 0.09\,H\,(584 - T_v)$$ $$\text{NCV} = 6735.5 - 0.09 \times 5 \times (584 - 200)$$ $$\text{NCV} = 6735.5 - 0.45 \times 384 = 6735.5 - 172.8$$ $$\boxed{\text{NCV} = 6562.7 \text{ kcal/kg} \;\; (\approx 27{,}471 \text{ kJ/kg})}$$ --- ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **(a)** Derive an expression for the power available in the wind and explain the Betz limit with its significance. **\[7 Marks\]** **(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the working of a tidal power plant. Discuss two sites in India with potential for tidal power generation. **\[7 Marks\]** #### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a) **Derivation:** *(Refer to SECTION 3.1 of these notes for the full 10-step derivation.)* The final expressions are: $$\boxed{P_{available} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v^{3}}$$ $$\boxed{P_{max} = \frac{16}{27} \times \frac{1}{2}\rho A v^{3} = \frac{8}{27}\rho A v^{3}}$$ The factor $\tfrac{16}{27} = 0.593$ is called the **Betz coefficient** ($C_{p,max}$). **Significance of Betz Limit:** 1. Sets the **theoretical maximum** at 59.3% — no turbine can ever exceed this. 2. Real wind turbines achieve $C_p$ values of **0.35–0.45** (75–80% of Betz limit). 3. Drives blade aerodynamic design — twist, taper, airfoil selection. 4. Used in power curve predictions during **wind resource assessment**. #### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b) **Tidal Power Plant — Schematic Explanation:** A tidal power plant exploits the **rise and fall of sea tides** caused by the gravitational pull of the Moon and Sun. A barrage (dam) is constructed across a narrow estuary with a sufficient tidal range (≥ 5 m). Water is allowed to flow through turbines during the **flood tide** (rising) and the **ebb tide** (falling). **Working Modes:** 1. **Single-Effect (Ebb-only):** Water trapped at high tide, released through turbines at low tide. 2. **Double-Effect:** Generation occurs in both directions (flood and ebb). 3. **Pumped Storage:** Pumping during slack tide to increase generation window. **Block Diagram of Tidal Power Plant:** ```mermaid flowchart LR SEA["SEA
HIGH TIDE"] -->|"Flood flow"| BARRAGE["BARRAGE
DAM STRUCTURE"] BARRAGE -->|"Control gates"| SLUICE["SLUICE GATES"] BARRAGE -->|"Generation"| TURBINE["BULB TURBINE
KAPLAN TYPE"] TURBINE --> GEN["GENERATOR"] GEN --> GRID["POWER GRID"] SLUICE --> BASIN["UPPER BASIN
LOW TIDE"] BASIN --> TURBINE ``` **Formula:** $$P_{tidal} = \frac{1}{2}\,\rho_{sw}\,g\,A^{2}\,F$$ where $A$ = basin area and $F$ = fill factor. **Promising Sites in India:** | Site | Location | Tidal Range (m) | Est. Capacity | |------|----------|-----------------|----------------| | **Gulf of Kutch** | Gujarat | ~5.8 | 7000 MW | | **Gulf of Cambay** | Gujarat | ~7.0 | 7000 MW | | **Sunderbans** | West Bengal | ~4.0 | 3500 MW | > **Note:** Gulf of Kutch has the highest potential in India; a 50 MW pilot project was operational at **Sikka**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Units missing** → −0.5 to −1 mark per sub-question. Always write SI units. > 2. **Dulong formula using C, H, O, S as percentages directly (e.g., 68 instead of 0.68)** → answer will be ~100× too large. Convert to **mass fractions**. > 3. **Forgetting the (1 − O/8) correction** in Dulong's formula → wrong GCV by ~15%. > 4. **Confusing GCV and NCV** in coal problems — question often asks for *Net* CV. > 5. **In wind power derivation, skipping the $v_2 = (v_1+v_3)/2$ assumption justification** → lose 2 marks in derivations. > 6. **Writing Betz limit as 50% or 66%** — the correct value is **16/27 = 59.3%**. > 7. **In classification questions, missing the third axis (Conventional vs. Non-Conventional)** — only listing renewable/non-renewable loses 1–2 marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision — Read this 30 minutes before the exam.** - ✅ **Solar Constant** = **1367 W/m²**; day-number correction = $0.033\cos(\tfrac{360n}{365})$. - ✅ **Betz Limit** = $\tfrac{16}{27} \approx 59.3\%$; formula = $P_{max} = \tfrac{8}{27}\rho A v^3$. - ✅ **Dulong's Formula** = $8080\,C + 34500(H - O/8) + 2240\,S$ (kcal/kg). - ✅ **NCV** = $\text{GCV} - 0.09H(584 - T_v)$ kcal/kg; substitute T_v = 200°C if not given. - ✅ **Hydro Power** = $\rho_w g Q H$ in kW (with $Q$ in m³/s, $H$ in m). - ✅ **1 TOE = 41.868 GJ; 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ.** - ✅ **Three classifications**: Primary/Secondary, Renewable/Non-Renewable, Conventional/Non-Conventional. - ✅ **India's installed capacity (2024)**: Coal ~200 GW, Solar ~75 GW, Wind ~47 GW, Hydro ~46 GW, Nuclear ~8 GW. - ✅ **Largest coal reserves in India**: Jharkhand, Odisha, Chhattisgarh, West Bengal, Madhya Pradesh. - ✅ **India's three-stage nuclear program**: PHWR → FBR → Thorium (Dr. Homi Bhabha's vision). - ✅ **Major renewable sites**: Gulf of Kutch (tidal), Ladakh & Manikaran (geothermal), Tamil Nadu & Gujarat (wind), Rajasthan & Gujarat (solar). - ✅ **Energy content benchmark**: Coal ~25 MJ/kg, Oil ~42 MJ/kg, Methane ~55 MJ/m³, Uranium ~80 TJ/kg. - ✅ **AM 1.5 spectrum** delivers **1000 W/m²** (after atmospheric attenuation) — design standard for PV. - ✅ **Betz limit derivation assumes** uniform, incompressible, axial flow with no friction. > **End of Module 1 — Topic: Sources Notes** > *Next topic preview: Energy conversion technologies (thermal, electrical, mechanical) and their efficiencies.*

topics.title: Hydrogen Energy Storage and Transportation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT614 - ENERGY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hydrogen Energy Storage and Transportation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🟦 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydrogen Energy Storage (HES)** is defined as the process of capturing, retaining, and releasing hydrogen in its usable form — either as a **chemical energy carrier**, a **compressed gas**, a **cryogenic liquid**, or bound within **metal hydrides / chemical carriers** — so that the energy embedded in the H–H bond (released upon oxidation) can be stored across timescales ranging from seconds to seasons and transported from points of production to points of demand.

In thermodynamic terms, hydrogen is a **secondary energy carrier** (like electricity), *not* a primary energy source. Its value lies in its **round-trip flexibility**: hydrogen produced via electrolysis using surplus renewable electricity can be stored, transported, and later reconverted to electricity (via fuel cells) or to heat (via combustion), effectively closing the seasonal storage gap that batteries cannot.

## 1.2 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Property | Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Molecular weight $M_{H_2}$ | **2.016** | g/mol |
| Lower Heating Value (LHV) | **120.0** | MJ/kg |
| Higher Heating Value (HHV) | **142.0** | MJ/kg |
| Boiling point at 1 atm | **−252.87** | °C |
| Density, gas at STP | **0.0899** | kg/m³ |
| Density, liquid at 20.3 K | **70.85** | kg/m³ |
| Flammability range in air | **4 – 75** | vol % |
| Auto-ignition temperature | **500 – 585** | °C |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "Hydrogen as a Time Machine for Energy"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Think of the electrical grid as a *river* of power that flows only when the sun shines or the wind blows. A lithium-ion battery is like a small **backpack water bottle** — great for a short hike (minutes to hours), but useless for a desert expedition. **Hydrogen is the tanker truck that fills up at the river during the wet season and delivers the same water to a thirsty village months later.** The "water" is energy, and hydrogen is just the bottle we chose to carry it in.

This is precisely why hydrogen complements batteries rather than competing with them:
- **Batteries** → short-duration, high-efficiency storage (seconds to days).
- **Hydrogen** → long-duration, large-scale, cross-sectoral storage (weeks to seasons).

## 1.4 Why Hydrogen? — The Strategic Triad

1. **Decarbonization vector** — substitutes fossil fuels in hard-to-abate sectors (steel, ammonia, heavy transport).
2. **Energy security** — domestic production from indigenous renewables reduces import dependence.
3. **Sector coupling** — links electricity, heat, transport, and industry through a single molecule.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🟦 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Hydrogen Storage

Hydrogen storage is categorized along two axes — **state of matter** and **binding energy** — yielding four distinct technological pillars. A KTU student must understand the trade-offs among them.

### Pillar I — Compressed Gaseous Hydrogen (CGH₂)

Hydrogen is compressed to high pressures, typically **350 bar** (Type III) or **700 bar** (Type IV), in composite cylinders reinforced with carbon-fiber overwrap.

**Thermodynamic compression work** (ideal, isothermal):

$$W_{comp} = nRT \ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

Real (adiabatic) compression requires more work, governed by:

$$W_{real} = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} n R T_1 \left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} - 1\right] \cdot \frac{1}{\eta_{isentropic}}$$

where $\gamma = C_p / C_v \approx 1.41$ for diatomic H₂.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 700 bar?** Beyond ~700 bar, the energy required to compress additional hydrogen approaches the energy gained from storing it — the **diminishing-returns regime**.

### Pillar II — Liquid Hydrogen (LH₂)

Hydrogen is cryogenically cooled below its critical point ($-252.87$ °C / 20.28 K) and stored as a liquid in super-insulated Dewar vessels.

**Volumetric advantage:**

$$\rho_{LH_2} \approx 70.85 \text{ kg/m}^3 \quad \text{vs.} \quad \rho_{H_2(700 bar)} \approx 40 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

Liquefaction requires breaking the **van der Waals** bonds of H₂. The minimum theoretical (Carnot) work is:

$$W_{min} = T_1 \cdot \Delta S - \Delta H$$

Practical plants consume **~10–13 kWh/kg**, equivalent to **~30–40% of the LHV** of the hydrogen itself — a major energy penalty.

### Pillar III — Metal Hydrides & Solid-State Storage

Hydrogen is absorbed into the crystal lattice of metals (e.g., LaNi₅, FeTi, MgH₂) forming reversible chemical bonds:

$$M + \frac{x}{2}H_2 \rightleftharpoons MH_x \quad (+ \Delta H_{abs})$$

**Operational steps:**
- **Absorption** (exothermic): H₂ dissociates on metal surface → atoms diffuse into lattice.
- **Desorption** (endothermic): heat is supplied (typically 250–350 °C for MgH₂) to release H₂.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> MgH₂ offers **~7.6 wt%** hydrogen capacity, but its high desorption temperature (>300 °C) limits practical deployment to niche stationary storage.

### Pillar IV — Chemical Hydrogen Carriers (LOHC & Ammonia)

Hydrogen is chemically bound to a carrier fluid:
- **Liquid Organic Hydrogen Carriers (LOHC)** — e.g., dibenzyltoluene + H₂ ⇌ perhydro-dibenzyltoluene.
- **Ammonia (NH₃)** — hydrogen is bound to nitrogen: $N_2 + 3H_2 \rightleftharpoons 2NH_3$.

This allows hydrogen to be transported using **existing fuel infrastructure** (oil tankers, pipelines) at near-ambient conditions.

## 2.2 Methods of Hydrogen Transportation

| Method | Form | Pressure / Temp | Capacity | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pipeline** | Gaseous | 10 – 100 bar | Very high (TWh scale) | Continental bulk |
| **Tube trailer** | Gaseous | 200 – 500 bar | ~400 kg / trailer | Short distance (< 300 km) |
| **Cryogenic tanker** | Liquid | $-253$ °C | ~3,500 – 4,500 kg | Long distance, export |
| **LOHC tanker** | Liquid carrier | Ambient | ~50 kg H₂ / m³ | Maritime, export |
| **Ammonia tanker** | NH₃ | $-33$ °C / 9 bar | ~120 kg H₂ / ton NH₃ | International shipping |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mastery of the following table is sufficient to solve >90% of numerical problems in the KTU End Semester Examination on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $W_{iso} = nRT \ln(P_2/P_1)$ | Isothermal compression work | J |
| 2 | $W_{adi} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} nRT_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]$ | Adiabatic (ideal) compression work | J |
| 3 | $\eta_{iso} = W_{iso}/W_{actual}$ | Isentropic efficiency | – |
| 4 | $E_{LH_2} = m_{H_2} \times LHV$ | Chemical energy stored | J |
| 5 | $\rho_E = \rho \times LHV$ | Volumetric energy density | MJ/m³ |
| 6 | $W_{liq} = m \cdot h_{fg} - m \cdot T_0 \cdot \Delta s$ | Min. liquefaction work (exergy) | J |
| 7 | $\Delta H_{abs} = m \int_{T_0}^{T_{eq}} C_p \, dT$ | Enthalpy of absorption | J |
| 8 | $BoP = \dfrac{P_{delivery}}{P_{inlet}} - 1$ | Boil-off gas loss fraction | %/day |
| 9 | $\eta_{round-trip} = \dfrac{E_{out}}{E_{in}}$ | Storage round-trip efficiency | – |
| 10 | $M_{CH_4-equivalent} = V_{H_2}/V_{CH_4}$ | Volume substitution ratio | m³/m³ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refueling stations:** Type IV 700-bar tanks provide ~5–6 kg H₂ for a passenger FCEV with ~500 km range.
- **Industrial hubs:** Rotterdam, Houston, and Singapore are piloting **liquid H₂ import chains** from Australia and the Gulf.
- **Grid storage:** A 100 MW electrolyzer + salt-cavern H₂ storage can buffer **~10 GWh** of seasonal renewable surplus — three orders of magnitude beyond lithium-ion capability.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🟦 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Work for Hydrogen Compression (Isothermal Ideal Gas)

We start from the **first law** for an open system undergoing a quasi-static process.

**Step 1 — Differential work on a gas element:**
For one mole of ideal gas, the work done by the compressor on the gas during an infinitesimal displacement is:

$$dW = -P \, dV$$

The negative sign indicates work is being done *on* the system.

**Step 2 — Substitute the ideal gas equation:**

$$P V = n R T \quad \Rightarrow \quad V = \frac{n R T}{P}$$

Differentiating at constant T:

$$dV = - \frac{n R T}{P^2} \, dP$$

**Step 3 — Combine:**
Substituting $dV$ back into the work expression:

$$
\begin{aligned}
dW &= -P \cdot \left(-\frac{nRT}{P^2}\right) dP \\
   &= \frac{nRT}{P} \, dP
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from $P_1$ to $P_2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{iso} &= \int_{P_1}^{P_2} \frac{nRT}{P} \, dP \\
        &= nRT \int_{P_1}^{P_2} \frac{dP}{P} \\
        &= nRT \left[\ln P\right]_{P_1}^{P_2} \\
        &= nRT \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** If $P_2 > P_1$, then $\ln(P_2/P_1) > 0$, so work is positive — energy flows *into* the gas, as expected during compression. ✅

## 3.2 Derivation: Minimum (Carnot) Liquefaction Work

Liquefaction is a refrigeration process. The minimum theoretical work to cool H₂ from $T_0 = 298$ K to $T_{boil} = 20.28$ K is governed by the **exergy (availability) balance**.

**Step 1 — Exergy of the stream entering the refrigerator:**
For a steady-flow cooling process, the minimum work equals the **change in flow exergy**:

$$W_{min} = \Delta H - T_0 \Delta S$$

**Step 2 — For an ideal gas, integrate $C_p(T)/T$:**
Assuming constant-pressure cooling from $T_0$ to $T_{boil}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta H &= \int_{T_0}^{T_{boil}} C_p(T) \, dT \\
\Delta S &= \int_{T_0}^{T_{boil}} \frac{C_p(T)}{T} \, dT
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Plug numerical values for H₂ ($C_p \approx 14.3$ kJ/kmol·K, ideal-gas approximation near $T_0$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta H &\approx C_p (T_{boil} - T_0) \\
         &= 14.3 \times (20.28 - 298) \\
         &= -3,970 \text{ kJ/kmol} \\
         &= -1.97 \text{ MJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta S &\approx C_p \ln\!\left(\frac{T_{boil}}{T_0}\right) \\
         &= 14.3 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{20.28}{298}\right) \\
         &= 14.3 \times (-2.69) \\
         &= -38.5 \text{ kJ/kmol·K}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute minimum work:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{min} &= \Delta H - T_0 \Delta S \\
        &= (-1.97 \text{ MJ/kg}) - 298 \text{ K} \times (-0.0385 \text{ MJ/kg·K}) \\
        &= -1.97 + 11.47 \\
        &= 9.50 \text{ MJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Liquefying 1 kg of H₂ requires *at minimum* **9.50 MJ** of work — equivalent to **~7.9% of its LHV (120 MJ/kg)**. Real plants need ~10–13 kWh/kg, or 13–17% of LHV, due to irreversibilities. This is why LH₂ is reserved for applications where volumetric density is critical (aerospace, long-haul export).

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — Energy Stored in a 700-bar Tank

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU Typical):** A Type IV hydrogen tank has volume **V = 122 L** and is filled to **P = 700 bar** at **T = 298 K**. Calculate (a) the mass of H₂ stored, (b) the chemical energy contained, and (c) the equivalent gasoline volume that provides the same energy (gasoline LHV = 44 MJ/L).

### (a) Mass of Hydrogen Stored

Using the ideal gas law $PV = nRT$ (correction factor $Z \approx 1.1$ at 700 bar; we use real-gas form):

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= \frac{PV}{ZRT} \\
  &= \frac{(700 \times 10^5) \times (122 \times 10^{-3})}{1.1 \times 8.314 \times 298} \\
  &= \frac{8.54 \times 10^6}{2{,}726} \\
  &= 3{,}133 \text{ mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{H_2} &= n \cdot M_{H_2} \\
         &= 3{,}133 \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3} \\
         &= 6.32 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

### (b) Chemical Energy Content

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= m \times LHV \\
  &= 6.32 \times 120 \\
  &= 758.4 \text{ MJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

### (c) Equivalent Gasoline Volume

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{gas} &= \frac{E}{LHV_{gas}} \\
        &= \frac{758.4}{44} \\
        &= 17.24 \text{ L}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** A 122 L hydrogen tank stores the chemical energy of **~17 L of gasoline** — yet occupies **~7× the volume**. This volumetric penalty is the central engineering challenge motivating **liquid hydrogen** and **700+ bar ultra-high-pressure** research.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Storage Pillar Selection

| Criterion | CGH₂ (700 bar) | LH₂ | Metal Hydride (MgH₂) | LOHC |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gravimetric density (wt %) | ~5 – 6 | ~100 | ~7.6 | ~6 – 7 |
| Volumetric density (kg H₂/m³) | ~40 | ~71 | ~100 | ~50 – 60 |
| Round-trip η (%) | ~85 – 92 | ~60 – 70 | ~50 – 65 | ~70 – 80 |
| Boil-off / Losses | Negligible | 0.1 – 0.3 %/day | None (chemical) | Negligible |
| CapEx | Low – Med | Very high | High | Med |
| Maturity | Commercial | Commercial | Pilot | Pilot – Demo |

# 🟦 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 model]** **Q1.** Define *Higher Heating Value (HHV)* and *Lower Heating Value (LHV)* of hydrogen. Why is LHV the preferred metric for fuel-cell applications?

**Model Answer:**
HHV is the heat released when 1 kg of H₂ is combusted and the water vapor product is condensed to liquid, recovering its latent heat. LHV is the heat released when the product water leaves as vapor, *without* condensation. Numerically, **HHV = 142 MJ/kg** and **LHV = 120 MJ/kg**. For fuel cells, the operating temperature (~60–80 °C for PEMFC) is far above 100 °C water condensation temperature, so the latent heat of water cannot be recovered. Therefore, **LHV** is the correct thermodynamic basis for fuel-cell efficiency calculations.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 model]** **Q2.** List any three methods of hydrogen storage and give one limitation of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Method | Limitation |
|---|---|
| Compressed gas (700 bar) | Low volumetric density (~40 kg/m³); expensive carbon-fiber tanks |
| Liquid hydrogen | High liquefaction energy (~30% of LHV); boil-off losses (~0.3%/day) |
| Metal hydrides | High desorption temperature (>300 °C for MgH₂); heavy reactor mass |
| LOHC | Complex catalytic hydrogenation/dehydrogenation; carrier cost |

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A — Internal Choice Option 1 (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]** **Q.A.** (a) Explain the **four major methods of hydrogen storage** with their operating principles, energy densities, and key limitations. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Derive the expression for the **minimum work of isothermal compression** of an ideal gas from pressure $P_1$ to $P_2$. A hydrogen compressor must compress 1 kg of H₂ from 1 bar to 200 bar at 300 K. Compute the isothermal compression work assuming ideal-gas behavior. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Four Methods of Hydrogen Storage (7 marks):**

1. **Compressed Gaseous Hydrogen (CGH₂):** Hydrogen is stored as a high-pressure gas in steel or composite tanks. Operating pressures: 350 bar (Type III) or 700 bar (Type IV). Volumetric density: ~40 kg/m³ at 700 bar. Limitation: Energy required for compression becomes disproportionately high above 700 bar; tank cost is significant.

2. **Liquid Hydrogen (LH₂):** Hydrogen is cooled below its critical temperature ($-252.87$ °C) and stored as a cryogenic liquid. Volumetric density: ~71 kg/m³ — almost 1.8× that of CGH₂ at 700 bar. Limitation: Liquefaction consumes 30–40% of the LHV; boil-off losses of 0.1–0.3 % per day even in well-insulated Dewars.

3. **Metal Hydrides (Solid-State):** Hydrogen is chemically absorbed by intermetallic alloys (e.g., LaNi₅, FeTi, MgH₂). Reversible reaction: $M + \frac{x}{2}H_2 \rightleftharpoons MH_x$. Gravimetric capacity: up to ~7.6 wt% for MgH₂. Limitation: High desorption temperature (>300 °C); heavy tank-to-H₂ mass ratio; slow kinetics.

4. **Chemical Hydrogen Carriers (LOHC / Ammonia):** Hydrogen is chemically bound to a liquid carrier — e.g., dibenzyltoluene (DBT) ⇌ perhydro-DBT, or N₂ ⇌ NH₃. Allows storage at ambient conditions using existing liquid-fuel infrastructure. Limitation: Energy-intensive hydrogenation/dehydrogenation steps; catalyst cost.

**(b) Derivation + Numerical (7 marks):**

**Derivation** (reproduce Section 3.1 derivation):

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{iso} &= \int_{P_1}^{P_2} \frac{nRT}{P} \, dP \\
        &= nRT \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Computation:**

Number of moles in 1 kg of H₂:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= \frac{m}{M} = \frac{1.0 \text{ kg}}{2.016 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/mol}} = 496 \text{ mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substitute into the compression formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{iso} &= 496 \times 8.314 \times 300 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{200}{1}\right) \\
        &= 1{,}237{,}114 \times \ln(200) \\
        &= 1{,}237{,}114 \times 5.298 \\
        &= 6{,}553{,}594 \text{ J} \\
        &\approx 6.55 \text{ MJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**As percentage of LHV stored:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% W_{iso} &= \frac{6.55}{120} \times 100 \approx 5.46\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students mistakenly use the **adiabatic** formula when the question specifies **isothermal**. Read carefully! Also, forgetting to convert mass to moles costs 2 marks. Always write $n = m/M$ explicitly before substituting.

---

### ⭐ Question B — Internal Choice Option 2 (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]** **Q.B.** (a) Describe the **methods of hydrogen transportation** currently in commercial or near-commercial use. Compare their relative merits with respect to capacity, cost, and infrastructure requirements. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A liquid hydrogen tanker has a capacity of **4,000 m³**. Compute (i) the mass of LH₂ it carries, (ii) the chemical energy content using LHV, and (iii) the volume of gasoline having equivalent energy (gasoline LHV = 44 MJ/L; LH₂ density = 71 kg/m³). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Methods of Hydrogen Transportation (7 marks):**

1. **Pipeline transport:** Hydrogen is pumped through dedicated steel or composite pipelines at 10–100 bar. Best for very high capacities (TWh/yr) over fixed corridors (e.g., industrial clusters). Limitation: Hydrogen embrittlement of steel; high upfront CapEx; needs right-of-way.

2. **Compressed gas tube trailers:** Hydrogen is transported in bundles of high-pressure cylinders (200–500 bar) on trucks. Capacity: ~400 kg per trailer. Best for short-distance (<300 km) and small-volume supply. Limitation: Low capacity per trip; high transport cost per kg.

3. **Cryogenic liquid hydrogen tankers:** LH₂ at $-253$ °C is transported in vacuum-insulated tankers. Capacity: 3,500–4,500 kg per trailer; up to 90,000 m³ for ship-scale (90,000 m³ LH₂ ≈ 6,400 tonnes). Limitation: Boil-off losses; expensive cryogenic equipment.

4. **LOHC tanker / Ammonia tanker:** Hydrogen is bound to a carrier fluid (LOHC) or to nitrogen (NH₃) and shipped using conventional oil/chemical tankers. Capacity: ~50 kg H₂/m³ for LOHC; ~120 kg H₂/tonne for NH₃. Limitation: Requires catalytic dehydrogenation at destination; NH₃ is toxic.

5. **Comparison summary:**

| Mode | Capacity (kg H₂) | Cost ($/kg) | Infrastructure Need |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pipeline | Unlimited (steady) | 1 – 2 | Dedicated pipeline |
| Tube trailer | 300 – 500 | 5 – 10 | Road, refueling |
| LH₂ truck | 3,000 – 4,500 | 4 – 8 | Cryogenic plants |
| NH₃ ship | >100,000 | 2 – 4 | Haber-Bosch + cracker |

**(b) Numerical on LH₂ Tanker (7 marks):**

**(i) Mass of LH₂:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m &= \rho \times V \\
  &= 71 \times 4{,}000 \\
  &= 284{,}000 \text{ kg} = 284 \text{ tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Chemical energy content:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= m \times LHV \\
  &= 284{,}000 \times 120 \text{ MJ/kg} \\
  &= 3.408 \times 10^{7} \text{ MJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Equivalent gasoline volume:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{gas} &= \frac{E}{LHV_{gas}} \\
        &= \frac{3.408 \times 10^{7}}{44} \\
        &= 7.745 \times 10^{5} \text{ L} \\
        &\approx 7.75 \times 10^{5} \text{ L} \approx 775 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** A common mistake is to forget that **density of LH₂ = 71 kg/m³** (not 0.071). Several students misplace the decimal and arrive at a mass 1000× too small — instant loss of 3 marks. **Always restate the density value explicitly at the top of your solution.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Revision Checklist for Hydrogen Energy Storage and Transportation:**

- ✅ **LHV of H₂ = 120 MJ/kg**; HHV = 142 MJ/kg — *memorize both.*
- ✅ **Density of LH₂ = 71 kg/m³** vs. gaseous H₂ at STP = **0.0899 kg/m³** — ratio ≈ 790×.
- ✅ **Boiling point** of H₂ = **$-252.87$ °C = 20.28 K** — below this, hydrogen is a liquid.
- ✅ **Four storage methods:** CGH₂, LH₂, Metal Hydride, Chemical Carrier (LOHC/NH₃).
- ✅ **Isothermal compression work:** $W_{iso} = nRT \ln(P_2/P_1)$.
- ✅ **Adiabatic compression work:** $W_{adi} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} nRT_1 \left[(P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]$.
- ✅ **Liquefaction penalty** = ~30–40% of LHV — major limitation of LH₂.
- ✅ **Hydrogen embrittlement** is a *metallurgical* issue affecting steel pipelines.
- ✅ **Boil-off rate** for LH₂ Dewars ≈ 0.1–0.3 %/day.
- ✅ **NH₃** is the most energy-dense hydrogen carrier by volume for export.
- ✅ **Round-trip efficiency:** Battery > H₂ (electricity-only), but H₂ wins on **duration** and **scale**.
- ✅ For a **14-mark answer**, always structure as: (1) principle, (2) derivation, (3) numerical substitution with units, (4) interpretation.
- ✅ Always write **units at every intermediate step** — examiners deduct heavily for unit omission.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT614 - ENERGY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In the context of **Energy Engineering and Management**, *Applications* refer to the systematic deployment of thermodynamic principles, energy conversion technologies, conservation strategies, and management practices across industrial, commercial, residential, transportation, and utility-scale sectors to achieve optimal energy utilization, minimize losses, and ensure sustainable operation.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020)**, the study of Energy Applications is benchmarked against the following measurable Course Outcomes:
- **CO1:** *Understand the fundamental concepts of energy resources, conversion, and management.*
- **CO2:** *Apply the principles of energy auditing and conservation in real-world engineering systems.*
- **CO3:** *Analyze the performance of conventional and renewable energy systems.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The "Applications" module bridges the gap between theoretical energy principles (covered in earlier sub-sections of Module 1) and their tangible deployment in field-level engineering practice. Examiner questions frequently test whether a student can *apply* a formula to a *real sector* — not merely state the formula.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Household Budget"**
> Think of energy in a country or industry exactly like a household monthly budget:
> - **Earning (Energy Generation):** Income from salary, interest, etc. (Coal, Solar, Wind, Hydro, Nuclear)
> - **Spending (Energy Consumption):** Rent, food, travel, bills (Motors, Lighting, HVAC, Process Heating)
> - **Savings (Energy Conservation):** Money left over at month-end (Efficiency improvements, Waste heat recovery)
> - **Investment (Energy Management):** Stocks, mutual funds (Long-term renewable infrastructure, Audits)

Just as a financially smart family tracks every rupee, an *Energy Engineer* tracks every joule, BTU, or kWh. **Applications** of Energy Engineering are the real "homes, factories, and vehicles" where this budgeting happens. The same principle (energy balance) is applied — only the scale and the equipment change.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The following constants and metrics are **must-know** for any KTU 2024 numerical problem on this topic:

| Metric / Constant | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Calorific Value of Coal (Bituminous) | CV | **24 – 30 MJ/kg** | MJ/kg |
| Calorific Value of Natural Gas | CV | **~ 38 MJ/m³** | MJ/m³ |
| Calorific Value of Diesel | CV | **~ 45 MJ/kg** | MJ/kg |
| Calorific Value of LPG | CV | **~ 46 MJ/kg** | MJ/kg |
| Specific Heat of Water | $c_p$ | **4.186 kJ/kg·K** | kJ/kg·K |
| Standard Atmospheric Pressure | $P_{atm}$ | **101.325 kPa** | kPa |
| Standard Temperature | $T_{std}$ | **25 °C (298.15 K)** | K |
| Energy Unit Conversion | 1 kWh | **= 3.6 MJ** | MJ |
| Energy Unit Conversion | 1 TOE | **= 41.868 GJ** | GJ |

> [!TIP]
> Examiners love cross-unit conversions. A common trap: a student calculates the answer in **BTU** when the question demands **MJ**. Always perform a final dimensional check!

The KTU 2024 framework evaluates this topic across the following cognitive levels:

|---|---|---|
| **L1 – Remember** | Recall definitions, units, formulas | "Define…", "State the unit of…" |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Five Pillars of Energy Engineering Applications

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups all real-world applications into the following **five core application domains**. Each domain is examined as a 7- or 14-mark problem almost every academic year.

#### **Pillar 1 — Industrial Applications**
Industries are the largest energy consumers (≈ 35-40% of total energy in India). Applications include:
- **Process Heating:** Boilers, Furnaces, Kilns (Cement, Steel, Glass)
- **Process Cooling:** Vapor Absorption Refrigeration (VAR), Vapour Compression Refrigeration (VCR)
- **Mechanical Work:** Motors, Pumps, Compressors, Fans, Blowers
- **Electrochemical Processes:** Aluminum smelting, Chlor-alkali production
- **Combined Heat and Power (CHP) / Cogeneration**

#### **Pillar 2 — Building & Commercial Applications**
- **HVAC Systems** (Heating, Ventilation, Air-Conditioning)
- **Lighting Systems** (LED retrofits, daylight harvesting)
- **Building Management Systems (BMS)**
- **Insulation & Passive Design**
- **Lift, Escalator Energy Recovery**

#### **Pillar 3 — Transportation Applications**
- **Internal Combustion Engine** (Petrol, Diesel, CNG)
- **Electric Vehicles (EVs) & Hybrid Electric Vehicles (HEVs)**
- **Fuel Cells (Hydrogen Mobility)**
- **Biofuels (Ethanol, Biodiesel)**
- **Regenerative Braking Systems**

#### **Pillar 4 — Power Generation Applications**
- **Thermal Power Plants** (Coal, Gas, Oil-based)
- **Combined Cycle Gas Turbine (CCGT) Plants**
- **Nuclear Power Plants**
- **Hydroelectric Power Stations**
- **Solar PV & Solar Thermal**
- **Wind Energy Conversion Systems (WECS)**
- **Tidal, Geothermal, Biomass-based Generation**

#### **Pillar 5 — Energy Storage & Auxiliary Applications**
- **Batteries** (Lead-acid, Li-ion, Flow batteries)
- **Pumped Hydro Storage**
- **Compressed Air Energy Storage (CAES)**
- **Thermal Energy Storage (TES)**
- **Hydrogen Storage & Fuel Cells**

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **all formulas** you will need to solve any KTU 2024 numerical problem on this topic. Each formula is paired with its unit and a real-world application context.

| # | Formula | Symbol Legend | Application Pillar | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | $Q$ = Heat energy, $m$ = mass, $c_p$ = specific heat, $\Delta T$ = temperature rise | Industrial / HVAC | **kJ or MJ** |
| 2 | $E = P \cdot t$ | $E$ = Electrical energy, $P$ = Power, $t$ = time | Industrial / Building | **kWh** |
| 3 | $\eta = \dfrac{W_{out}}{Q_{in}} \times 100\%$ | $\eta$ = Efficiency, $W$ = Work output, $Q$ = Heat input | Power Generation | **%** |
| 4 | $\text{COP}_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{in}}$ | $Q_H$ = Heat delivered, $W_{in}$ = Work input | HVAC / Refrigeration | **dimensionless** |
| 5 | $\text{COP}_{Ref} = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{in}}$ | $Q_L$ = Heat extracted from cold space | Refrigeration | **dimensionless** |
| 6 | $\text{Payback Period} = \dfrac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Savings}}$ | Net capital cost vs. yearly cost reduction | Energy Management | **years** |
| 7 | $\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | $CF_t$ = Cash flow, $r$ = discount rate, $n$ = project life | Energy Management | **Rupees / ₹** |
| 8 | $\text{Simple Payback} = \dfrac{C}{E \cdot P \cdot H}$ | $C$ = capital cost, $E$ = energy saved/unit, $P$ = rate, $H$ = annual hours | Energy Audit | **years** |
| 9 | $\text{Energy Intensity} = \dfrac{\text{Total Energy}}{\text{Output (kg/m²/₹)}}$ | Sector-specific productivity measure | All Sectors | **MJ/kg or kWh/m²** |
| 10 | $P_{wind} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot v^3 \cdot C_p$ | $\rho$ = air density, $A$ = swept area, $v$ = velocity, $C_p$ = power coefficient | Wind Energy | **Watts (W)** |
| 11 | $E_{PV} = A \cdot G \cdot \eta_{PV} \cdot \text{PR}$ | $A$ = panel area, $G$ = irradiance, PR = performance ratio | Solar PV | **kWh/day** |
| 12 | $F_{fuel} = \dfrac{m_{fuel} \cdot CV_{fuel}}{\eta_{system}}$ | Fuel energy input to a thermal system | Boiler / Furnace | **MJ/h or kW** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Restriction for Markdown:** The vertical bar `|` is reserved for table column separators. For absolute value or modulus expressions in math, **always use** $\vert$ or $\mid$ (e.g., $\vert \Delta T \vert$, $\mid T_H - T_C \mid$) — this prevents the markdown table parser from breaking.

---

### 2.3 Why These Formulas Matter in Real Engineering

- **The $Q = m c_p \Delta T$ relation** governs the design of every heat exchanger, boiler feedwater tank, and HVAC coil. It is the *thermodynamic fingerprint* of any process heating or cooling application.
- **The COP equations** are the *yardsticks* used to grade refrigeration and heat-pump equipment under **BEE (Bureau of Energy Efficiency)** star labelling in India.
- **The Payback Period and NPV** are the *only* two metrics used by industry CFOs to approve an Energy Conservation Measure (ECM) — a 14-mark KTU question often revolves around them.
- **The Wind Power Cubic Law** ($v^3$) explains why a *mere 10% increase* in wind speed produces a **~33% increase** in available power — a favorite conceptual question.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Energy Released from Combustion of Fuel (Industrial Boiler Application)

A common 14-mark problem gives a coal-fired boiler data and asks for thermal efficiency, fuel consumption, and cost savings.

**Given:**
- Mass of coal burned per hour, $m_c = 500 \text{ kg/h}$
- Calorific Value of coal, $CV = 25 \text{ MJ/kg}$
- Steam generation rate, $\dot{m}_s = 2000 \text{ kg/h}$
- Feedwater temperature, $T_1 = 30 \, °\text{C}$
- Steam pressure (saturated), $P = 10 \text{ bar (gauge)} \Rightarrow P_{abs} = 11.013 \text{ bar}$
- Enthalpy of saturated steam at 10 bar (absolute), $h_g = 2778.1 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- Specific heat of feedwater, $c_{pw} = 4.186 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$
- Efficiency of combustion, $\eta_{comb} = 0.90$

**Step 1 — Compute the heat input rate from fuel combustion.**

$$Q_{in} = m_c \cdot CV \cdot \eta_{comb}$$

$$Q_{in} = 500 \text{ kg/h} \times 25{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg} \times 0.90$$

$$Q_{in} = 11{,}250{,}000 \text{ kJ/h} = 11{,}250 \text{ MJ/h}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the useful heat output in the steam.**

First, determine the temperature corresponding to saturated steam at 11.013 bar (absolute). From steam tables:
$T_{sat} = 184.1 \, °\text{C}$ (approximately)

Now, the enthalpy rise of the feedwater converted to steam:
$$\Delta h = h_g - c_{pw} \cdot T_1$$

$$\Delta h = 2778.1 \text{ kJ/kg} - (4.186 \text{ kJ/kg·K} \times 30 \text{ K})$$

$$\Delta h = 2778.1 - 125.58 = 2652.52 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Therefore, the useful heat output rate is:
$$Q_{out} = \dot{m}_s \cdot \Delta h = 2000 \text{ kg/h} \times 2652.52 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$Q_{out} = 5{,}305{,}040 \text{ kJ/h} = 5305.04 \text{ MJ/h}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the thermal efficiency of the boiler.**

$$\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{5305.04}{11250} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta_{boiler} = 47.16\%$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Equivalent Evaporation (a KTU favorite).**

Equivalent Evaporation is defined as the mass of water that would be converted to dry saturated steam at **100 °C** using the same heat.

From steam tables: $h_{fg} \text{ at } 100 \, °\text{C} = 2257 \text{ kJ/kg}$

$$E_{equiv} = \dfrac{Q_{out}}{h_{fg}} = \dfrac{5{,}305{,}040 \text{ kJ/h}}{2257 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$

$$E_{equiv} = 2350.48 \text{ kg/h of equivalent evaporation}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** Step 4 demonstrates how a single design point is benchmarked against a standard condition (100 °C) — a classic "evaluate" level question. Always express the final answer in the **same units** as the question's blanks.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Simple Payback Period for an Energy Conservation Measure (ECM)

A factory replaces a standard motor with a high-efficiency motor. Compute the payback period.

**Given:**
- Standard motor efficiency, $\eta_1 = 88\%$
- Energy-efficient (EE) motor efficiency, $\eta_2 = 93\%$
- Motor rated power, $P = 15 \text{ kW}$
- Operating hours per year, $H = 4500 \text{ h/yr}$
- Cost of EE motor premium (extra cost), $C = ₹45{,}000$
- Electricity tariff, $P_{rate} = ₹7.50 / \text{kWh}$

**Step 1 — Energy drawn from the grid by the standard motor.**

$$E_1 = \dfrac{P \cdot H}{\eta_1} = \dfrac{15 \times 4500}{0.88} = 76{,}704.55 \text{ kWh/yr}$$

**Step 2 — Energy drawn by the EE motor.**

$$E_2 = \dfrac{P \cdot H}{\eta_2} = \dfrac{15 \times 4500}{0.93} = 72{,}580.65 \text{ kWh/yr}$$

**Step 3 — Annual energy savings.**

$$\Delta E = E_1 - E_2 = 76{,}704.55 - 72{,}580.65 = 4123.90 \text{ kWh/yr}$$

**Step 4 — Annual monetary savings.**

$$\text{Annual Savings} = \Delta E \times P_{rate} = 4123.90 \times 7.50 = ₹30{,}929.25 \text{ /yr}$$

**Step 5 — Simple Payback Period.**

$$\text{SPP} = \dfrac{C}{\text{Annual Savings}} = \dfrac{45{,}000}{30{,}929.25}$$

$$\text{SPP} \approx 1.455 \text{ years}$$

> [!TIP]
> If the question asks for **NPV**, calculate the cash flows over the project life (typically 10 years for motors) and discount them at the company's MARR (Minimum Attractive Rate of Return), usually **r ≈ 12-15%** in Indian industrial cases.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Application → Formula → Real Equipment

| Application Sector | Real Equipment | Governing Formula | Typical Numerical Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cement Plant | Rotary Kiln, Pre-heater, Cooler | $Q = m c_p \Delta T$ | Heat losses in kW |
| Textile Mill | Motors, Looms, Compressors | $E = P t$, $\eta$ | kWh/day, % saving |
| Commercial Building | Chiller, AHU, Cooling Tower | $\text{COP} = Q_L / W_{in}$ | COP, kW/ton |
| Data Centre | UPS, CRAC units, Server racks | $PUE = \dfrac{Total \, Facility \, Energy}{IT \, Equipment \, Energy}$ | PUE value (1.0 – 2.0) |
| Power Plant | Boiler, Turbine, Generator | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{boiler} \times \eta_{cycle} \times \eta_{gen} \times \eta_{mech}$ | % overall efficiency |
| Solar Rooftop | PV modules, Inverter | $E_{PV} = A \cdot G \cdot \eta \cdot PR$ | kWh/year |
| Wind Farm | Wind turbine, Gearbox, Generator | $P = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 C_p$ | kW or MW |
| EV Charging | Battery, Charger, Motor | $E_{batt} = V \times Ah$, Range $= E_{batt} / \text{Wh per km}$ | km range, charging time |
