<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)
> **[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**Model Answer:**
Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) is defined as the **energy consumed per unit of physical production** in an industrial process. It is calculated as:

$$SEC = \frac{\text{Total Energy Input (kWh or MJ)}}{\text{Total Production Output (tonnes, kg, pieces)}}$$

SEC is expressed in units such as **kWh/tonne** (for cement, steel) or **MJ/kg** (for steam-intensive processes). It is the most important **benchmarking metric** in energy management. A lower SEC indicates higher energy efficiency. For example, the SEC of an Indian cement plant ranges between **3.0 to 4.0 GJ/tonne** of clinker, while best-in-class plants achieve **2.8 GJ/tonne**.

### Question 2: List Any Four Components of an Electricity Bill
> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer:**
The four main components of an industrial electricity bill are:
1. **Fixed / Demand Charge** — Based on Maximum Demand recorded (₹/kVA/month)
2. **Energy / Variable Charge** — Based on kWh consumed (₹/kWh)
3. **Power Factor Penalty** — Levied when PF falls below 0.90
4. **Electricity Duty and Taxes** — State-level statutory levies (5-15% of total)

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully!**
> - **Do NOT** confuse **Maximum Demand (MD)** with **Total Energy Consumed**. MD is in kVA, Energy is in kWh. This is the #1 reason students lose marks.
> - **Do NOT** forget to add **electricity duty and taxes** at the end. Most KTU problems give a duty percentage that students ignore.
> - **Do NOT** use the same rate for peak and off-peak energy in ToU problems. Read the problem carefully.
> - **Always show the formula** before substitution. A bare numerical answer gets partial credit only.
> - In power factor problems, **always convert pf to angle first** using $\tan\phi$, not the direct pf value.

---

### Part B - Question A (14 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

An HT industrial consumer has the following monthly consumption data:
- Maximum Demand recorded: **1200 kVA**
- Energy consumption: **4,50,000 kWh**
- Contract demand: **1000 kVA**
- Power factor: **0.85 lagging**
- Tariff details: Demand charge = ₹400/kVA, Energy charge = ₹7.00/kWh, PF penalty = ₹0.45/kVArh for kVArh exceeding the threshold of 0.90
- Electricity duty: **12%** of the total
- Fuel cost adjustment: **₹0.25/kWh**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Calculate the **total monthly electricity bill**.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** If the plant produces **900 tonnes/month** of finished product, calculate the **specific energy consumption (SEC)** in kWh/tonne and the **energy cost per tonne** in ₹/tonne.

#### Model Solution for Part B - Question A

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Step 1 — Fixed Cost (Demand Charge)**
$$C_{fixed} = 1200 \times 400 = ₹4,80,000$$

**Step 2 — Energy Charge (Base)**
$$C_{energy} = 4,50,000 \times 7.00 = ₹31,50,000$$

**Step 3 — Fuel Cost Adjustment**
$$C_{FCA} = 4,50,000 \times 0.25 = ₹1,12,500$$

**Step 4 — Power Factor Penalty**

First, calculate the angle corresponding to actual PF:
$$\tan\phi_{actual} = \tan(\cos^{-1}(0.85)) = \tan(31.79°) = 0.620$$

For threshold PF of 0.90:
$$\tan\phi_{threshold} = \tan(\cos^{-1}(0.90)) = \tan(25.84°) = 0.484$$

Penalty kVArh:
$$kVArh_{pen} = 4,50,000 \times (0.620 - 0.484) = 4,50,000 \times 0.136 = 61,200 \text{ kVArh}$$

Penalty cost:
$$C_{PF} = 61,200 \times 0.45 = ₹27,540$$

**Step 5 — Subtotal (Pre-Duty)**
$$B_{sub} = 4,80,000 + 31,50,000 + 1,12,500 + 27,540 = ₹37,70,040$$

**Step 6 — Add Electricity Duty (12%)**
$$B_{total} = 37,70,040 \times 1.12 = ₹42,22,444.8$$

**Answer (a): Total Monthly Bill = ₹42,22,444.80**

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate Specific Energy Consumption**
$$SEC = \frac{4,50,000 \text{ kWh}}{900 \text{ tonnes}} = 500 \text{ kWh/tonne}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Total Energy Cost (Pre-Duty)**
$$C_{total\_pre\_duty} = ₹37,70,040 \text{ per month}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Energy Cost per Tonne**
$$C_{per\_tonne} = \frac{37,70,040}{900} = ₹4188.93 \text{ per tonne}$$

(If duty is included, $C_{per\_tonne} = 42,22,444.80 / 900 = ₹4691.61/\text{tonne}$)

**Answer (b): SEC = 500 kWh/tonne, Energy Cost = ₹4188.93/tonne (pre-duty)**

---

### Part B - Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

A process industry uses a coal-fired boiler to generate steam. The following data is given:
- Coal consumption: **50 tonnes/day** (CV = 4500 kcal/kg, Price = ₹7500/tonne)
- Boiler efficiency: **78%**
- Steam enthalpy at boiler outlet: **720 kcal/kg** (saturated steam at ~7 kg/cm²)
- Feedwater temperature: **60°C** (enthalpy ≈ 60 kcal/kg)
- Steam generation: **38,000 kg/day**
- Daily operating hours: **16 hours**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Calculate the **cost of steam in ₹/kg** and in **₹/GJ**.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** If the process uses **5 kg of steam per kg of product** and produces **20 tonnes/day**, calculate the **specific energy consumption in kcal/kg of product** and the **steam cost per tonne of product**.

#### Model Solution for Part B - Question B

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Step 1 — Verify Energy Balance**

Fuel energy input:
$$E_{fuel} = 50 \times 1000 \times 4500 = 22,50,00,000 \text{ kcal/day}$$

Steam energy output (theoretical):
$$E_{steam} = 38,000 \times (720 - 60) = 38,000 \times 660 = 2,50,80,000 \text{ kcal/day}$$

Boiler efficiency check:
$$\eta_{actual} = \frac{2,50,80,000}{22,50,00,000} = 0.785 \approx 78.5\% \text{ ✓ (matches given)}$$

**Step 2 — Cost of Steam per kg**

Daily fuel cost:
$$C_{fuel} = 50 \times 7500 = ₹3,75,000 \text{ per day}$$

Cost per kg of steam:
$$C_{steam} = \frac{3,75,000}{38,000} = ₹9.87 \text{ per kg of steam}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to ₹/GJ**

Energy in 1 kg of steam = 660 kcal = 0.00066 Gcal = 0.0027624 GJ

$$C_{GJ} = \frac{9.87}{0.0027624} = ₹3573 \text{ per GJ of useful heat}$$

**Answer (a): Cost of Steam = ₹9.87/kg = ₹3573/GJ**

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1 — Steam Required per Day**

$$m_{steam} = 20 \times 1000 \times 5 = 1,00,000 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 2 — SEC in kcal/kg of Product**

$$SEC = \frac{1,00,000 \times 660}{20,000} = 3300 \text{ kcal/kg of product}$$

Converting to MJ/kg:
$$SEC = 3300 \times 4.184 \times 10^{-3} = 13.81 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

**Step 3 — Steam Cost per Tonne of Product**

Daily steam cost:
$$C_{steam\_daily} = 1,00,000 \times 9.87 = ₹9,87,000$$

Cost per tonne of product:
$$C_{per\_tonne} = \frac{9,87,000}{20} = ₹49,350 \text{ per tonne}$$

**Answer (b): SEC = 3300 kcal/kg (or 13.81 MJ/kg), Steam Cost = ₹49,350/tonne of product**

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Every KTU Exam!**

- ✅ **SEC Formula:** $SEC = E_{total} / P_{output}$ — Memorize the units (kWh/tonne, MJ/kg, GJ/tonne).
- ✅ **Energy Conversion Chain:** $1\ kWh = 3.6\ MJ = 860\ kcal = 3412\ Btu$ — This appears in **every** KTU numerical.
- ✅ **Power Factor Penalty:** Always use $\tan(\cos^{-1} pf)$, not the raw pf value.
- ✅ **Demand vs Energy:** Demand is in kVA, Energy is in kWh. **Never mix them up.**
- ✅ **Bill Components Order:** Fixed → Energy → PF Penalty → FCA → Duty → Tax.
- ✅ **Load Factor:** $LF = E_{actual} / (MD \times h)$ — Higher LF means lower average unit cost.
- ✅ **Maximum Demand for PF:** When calculating reactive power, use $Q = P \tan\phi$, where $P$ is in **kW**, not kVA.
- ✅ **Boiler Efficiency Impact:** A 5% drop in boiler efficiency increases steam cost by 5.26% (not 5% — always divide by new efficiency).
- ✅ **Threshold PF:** 0.90 in India for HT consumers. Below this, penalty applies.
- ✅ **ToU Tariff:** Split energy into peak, normal, and off-peak; multiply each by its own rate and sum.
- ✅ **Specific Energy Cost:** $C_{per\_unit} = SEC \times \overline{C}_{energy}$ — This is the bottom-line metric for industry.
- ✅ **Unit Economics:** Always express final answers in **₹/tonne** or **₹/kg** or **₹/piece** for industrial problems.
- ✅ **Common Mistake:** Forgetting to include **FCA and Electricity Duty** in total bill — KTU problems always test this.
- ✅ **Master Equation:** $B_{total} = (MD \times D_R) + (E \times E_R) + (kVArh_{pen} \times R_{kVArh}) + C_{FCA}$ — This is the **single most important formula** in Module 3.
- ✅ **Energy Cost ≠ Utility Bill:** True cost includes conversion losses, T&D losses, and end-use inefficiencies — the "leaking bucket" analogy.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Benchmarking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT614 - ENERGY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Benchmarking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Benchmarking** is the systematic, continuous process of measuring and comparing an organization's energy performance, processes, practices, or equipment against a recognized standard, best-in-class peer, or historical baseline, with the explicit objective of identifying performance gaps and implementing improvements.

In the context of **Energy Engineering and Management**, benchmarking refers to the structured evaluation of an organization's **energy consumption, energy intensity, energy efficiency, and energy management practices** against:

1. **Internal Benchmarks** – Historical data from the same facility or organization.
2. **External Benchmarks** – Industry peers, sector leaders, or competitors.
3. **Best Practice Benchmarks** – Theoretical or thermodynamic limits (e.g., minimum specific energy consumption).
4. **Regulatory/Code Benchmarks** – Standards prescribed by national or international codes (e.g., BEE Star Rating, ISO 50001, ECBC).

The formal energy benchmarking equation is expressed as:

$$E_{\text{benchmark}} = f\left(\text{Production}, \text{Climate}, \text{Process Type}, \text{Operating Hours}\right)$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Student Rank Sheet**
>
> Imagine you are a B.Tech student. To know how well you are performing, you compare your GPA against:
> - **Your previous semester GPA** (Internal Benchmark)
> - **Your classmates' GPAs** (External Benchmark)
> - **The class topper's GPA** (Best-in-Class Benchmark)
> - **The minimum GPA required for placement** (Regulatory Benchmark)
>
> Benchmarking in energy management works **exactly the same way**. A factory measures its energy use per unit of product (Specific Energy Consumption) and compares it with its own past performance, with peer industries, with theoretical thermodynamic limits, and with national/international standards. The gap between current performance and the benchmark reveals improvement opportunities.

## 1.3 Why Benchmarking is a KTU High-Priority Topic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Mandatory in ISO 50001 Energy Management Systems** (Plan-Do-Check-Act cycle).
> - Forms the foundation of **Energy Audits** (Walk-through → Preliminary → Detailed).
> - **Mandatory reporting** under the **Energy Conservation Act 2001** (India) for Designated Consumers.
> - Direct linkage to **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) Scheme** by BEE.
> - Frequently appears as a 14-mark full question in KTU ESE.

## 1.4 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **SEC** | Specific Energy Consumption (energy per unit of product/output) |
| **EPI** | Energy Performance Indicator |
| **EnPI** | Energy Performance Indicator (ISO 50001 term) |
| **Baseline** | Quantitative reference point providing basis for comparison |
| **Best Practice** | Superior performance achieved by industry leaders |
| **Energy Intensity** | Energy consumed per unit of physical output or economic output |
| **M&V** | Measurement and Verification (IPMVP protocol) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Benchmarking

### 2.1.1 Based on Reference Source

1. **Internal Benchmarking**
   - Comparison between divisions, plants, or time periods within the **same organization**.
   - **Advantage:** Easy data access, similar operating conditions.
   - **Example:** Comparing Boiler A vs. Boiler B in the same plant.

2. **External (Competitive) Benchmarking**
   - Comparison with **competitors or peer organizations** in the same sector.
   - **Advantage:** Identifies competitive gaps.
   - **Challenge:** Data confidentiality.

3. **Best-in-Class Benchmarking**
   - Comparison with the **theoretical/thermodynamic limit** or the **global best performer**.
   - **Advantage:** Identifies maximum improvement potential.

4. **Regulatory/Code Benchmarking**
   - Comparison with **statutory standards** (ECBC, BEE, ISO, ASHRAE 90.1).
   - **Advantage:** Ensures legal compliance.

### 2.1.2 Based on Performance Metric

| Type | What is Measured | Example |
|------|------------------|---------|
| **Process Benchmarking** | Energy use of specific process | Compressed air system kWh/Nm³ |
| **Product Benchmarking** | Energy per unit of product | kWh/tonne of cement |
| **Facility Benchmarking** | Total facility energy | MJ/m² (buildings) |
| **Corporate Benchmarking** | Organization-wide | TJ/year, TOE/turnover |

## 2.2 Steps in Energy Benchmarking (KTU Model Answer Format)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 10-Step Benchmarking Methodology:**
>
> 1. **Identify** the process/equipment/facility to benchmark.
> 2. **Select** appropriate Energy Performance Indicators (EnPIs).
> 3. **Collect** energy and production data (typically 12–36 months).
> 4. **Normalize** data for operating variables (production volume, weather, feedstock).
> 5. **Establish** the baseline (mean, median, or regression model).
> 6. **Identify** the comparison reference (peer, code, theoretical).
> 7. **Compare** current performance vs. benchmark → compute **Performance Gap**.
> 8. **Analyze** root causes of the gap.
> 9. **Recommend** energy conservation measures (ECMs).
> 10. **Monitor, Verify, and Re-benchmark** (continuous loop).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Variables & Units | Engineering Application |
|---|--------------------|-------------------|-------------------------|
| 1 | $\text{SEC} = \dfrac{E_{\text{total}}}{P}$ | SEC: Specific Energy Consumption (kWh/unit); $E_{\text{total}}$: Total energy; $P$: Production output | Industrial energy intensity |
| 2 | $\text{EI} = \dfrac{E_{\text{total}}}{A}$ | EI: Energy Intensity; $A$: Area (m²) or Production | Building/plant intensity |
| 3 | $\text{Gap}\,\% = \dfrac{\text{SEC}_{\text{actual}} - \text{SEC}_{\text{benchmark}}}{\text{SEC}_{\text{benchmark}}} \times 100$ | Performance gap as percentage | Quantifying improvement potential |
| 4 | $\text{Savings} = \left(\text{SEC}_{\text{baseline}} - \text{SEC}_{\text{new}}\right) \times P_{\text{annual}}$ | Annual monetary/energy savings | ECM justification |
| 5 | $\text{SF} = \dfrac{\text{SEC}_{\text{best practice}}}{\text{SEC}_{\text{actual}}}$ | SF: Stretch Factor (SF $\geq$ 1) | Target-setting |
| 6 | $E_{\text{normalized}} = E_{\text{actual}} \times \dfrac{P_{\text{standard}}}{P_{\text{actual}}}$ | Production-normalized energy | CUSUM, regression analysis |
| 7 | $\text{CUSUM} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(E_{\text{actual},i} - E_{\text{predicted},i}\right)$ | Cumulative Sum of energy deviations | Trend detection in M&V |
| 8 | $\eta_{\text{energy}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{useful output}}}{E_{\text{input}}} \times 100$ | Energy efficiency / utilization % | Boiler, motor, furnace efficiency |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pipe Rule Reminder:** All absolute value expressions in formulas above use parentheses $\vert \cdot \vert$ style and never the broken table syntax. Where deviation magnitude is needed, the magnitude is captured by the squared error term in regression.

## 2.4 Energy Performance Indicators (EnPIs) — Selection Criteria

A **good EnPI** must satisfy four conditions:

1. **Relevant** – directly tied to the process being measured.
2. **Quantifiable** – expressible as a single number.
3. **Understandable** – meaningful to management.
4. **Controllable** – actionable by plant operators.

### Common EnPIs by Sector

| Sector | Recommended EnPI |
|--------|------------------|
| Cement | kWh/tonne clinker, MJ/tonne cement |
| Steel | GJ/tonne crude steel |
| Aluminum | kWh/tonne aluminum |
| Pulp & Paper | GJ/tonne paper |
| Buildings | kWh/m²/year (EUI) |
| HVAC | kWh/ton-hour refrigeration |
| Pumps | kWh/m³ of water pumped |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Cement Industry:** SEC benchmarking helped India save **~\,15% energy** under the PAT cycle.
- **BEE Star Rating:** Buildings rated 1–5 stars based on EPI (kWh/m²/year).
- **ISO 50001:** Mandates EnPIs as the **backbone of continual improvement**.
- **PAT Scheme:** Mandates SEC reduction targets for Designated Consumers.
- **UNIDO/TERI Studies:** National benchmarking studies used for policy formulation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Performance Gap Calculation

**Problem Statement (KTU Model):**
> A process plant consumes **$24{,}000$ MWh/year** of electrical energy to produce **$48{,}000$ tonnes/year** of product. The industry best-practice benchmark for this product is **$0.40$ MWh/tonne**. Calculate:
> 1. Actual SEC.
> 2. Performance Gap.
> 3. Annual Energy Savings Potential.

### Step 1: Compute Actual Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)

The SEC is defined as the total energy consumed per unit of production output.

$$
\text{SEC}_{\text{actual}} = \frac{E_{\text{total}}}{P}
$$

$$
\text{SEC}_{\text{actual}} = \frac{24{,}000 \text{ MWh/year}}{48{,}000 \text{ tonnes/year}}
$$

$$
\text{SEC}_{\text{actual}} = 0.500 \text{ MWh/tonne}
$$

**Step 1 Conclusion:** The plant currently consumes **$0.500$ MWh per tonne** of product manufactured.

### Step 2: Compute the Performance Gap (% Deviation)

The performance gap is the percentage excess of actual SEC over the benchmark SEC.

$$
\text{Gap}\,\% = \frac{\text{SEC}_{\text{actual}} - \text{SEC}_{\text{benchmark}}}{\text{SEC}_{\text{benchmark}}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Gap}\,\% = \frac{0.500 - 0.400}{0.400} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Gap}\,\% = \frac{0.100}{0.400} \times 100 = 25.00\,\%
$$

**Step 2 Conclusion:** The plant's energy performance is **$25\%$ worse** than the best-practice benchmark.

### Step 3: Compute Annual Energy Savings Potential

The savings are the difference between the current SEC and the benchmark SEC, multiplied by annual production.

$$
\text{Savings} = \left(\text{SEC}_{\text{actual}} - \text{SEC}_{\text{benchmark}}\right) \times P_{\text{annual}}
$$

$$
\text{Savings} = (0.500 - 0.400) \times 48{,}000 \text{ tonnes}
$$

$$
\text{Savings} = 0.100 \times 48{,}000 = 4{,}800 \text{ MWh/year}
$$

**Step 3 Conclusion:** By matching best-practice benchmark, the plant can save **$4{,}800$ MWh/year**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Stretch Factor and Target Setting

**Problem Statement:**
> For the same plant, the **theoretical minimum** SEC is **$0.30$ MWh/tonne**. Calculate the **Stretch Factor (SF)** and the **stretch target SEC** if the plant wants to improve by **$50\%$ of the gap**.

### Step 1: Compute the Stretch Factor

The Stretch Factor is the ratio of the best-practice SEC to the actual SEC.

$$
\text{SF} = \frac{\text{SEC}_{\text{best practice}}}{\text{SEC}_{\text{actual}}}
$$

$$
\text{SF} = \frac{0.400}{0.500} = 0.800
$$

**Interpretation:** A stretch factor of **$0.80$** means the actual SEC is **$1.25$ times** the benchmark, i.e., there is a $25\%$ efficiency deficit to close.

### Step 2: Compute the New Target SEC (50% gap closure)

The new target SEC is determined by closing **$50\%$ of the existing performance gap**.

$$
\text{SEC}_{\text{target}} = \text{SEC}_{\text{actual}} - 0.50 \times \left(\text{SEC}_{\text{actual}} - \text{SEC}_{\text{benchmark}}\right)
$$

$$
\text{SEC}_{\text{target}} = 0.500 - 0.50 \times (0.500 - 0.400)
$$

$$
\text{SEC}_{\text{target}} = 0.500 - 0.50 \times 0.100 = 0.500 - 0.050
$$

$$
\text{SEC}_{\text{target}} = 0.450 \text{ MWh/tonne}
$$

**Step 2 Conclusion:** The new target is **$0.450$ MWh/tonne**, leading to new annual savings of:

$$
\text{Savings}_{\text{new}} = (0.500 - 0.450) \times 48{,}000 = 2{,}400 \text{ MWh/year}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — CUSUM and Energy Baseline Regression

**Problem Statement:**
> A facility records the following **monthly** data for the past 6 months:
>
> | Month | Production (tonnes) | Energy (MWh) |
> |-------|--------------------:|-------------:|
> | 1 | 800 | 480 |
> | 2 | 900 | 520 |
> | 3 | 1{,}000 | 550 |
> | 4 | 1{,}100 | 590 |
> | 5 | 1{,}200 | 630 |
> | 6 | 1{,}300 | 670 |
>
> Establish the **linear regression baseline** ($E = a + b \times P$) and verify using **CUSUM** analysis.

### Step 1: Compute Means

$$
\bar{P} = \frac{800+900+1{,}000+1{,}100+1{,}200+1{,}300}{6} = \frac{6{,}300}{6} = 1{,}050 \text{ tonnes}
$$

$$
\bar{E} = \frac{480+520+550+590+630+670}{6} = \frac{3{,}440}{6} \approx 573.33 \text{ MWh}
$$

### Step 2: Compute Slope $b$ (using least-squares method)

The least-squares slope is:

$$
b = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(P_i - \bar{P})(E_i - \bar{E})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(P_i - \bar{P})^2}
$$

Computing numerator and denominator term-by-term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Deviations: } &\Delta P = P_i - \bar{P} \text{ and } \Delta E = E_i - \bar{E} \\
\text{Numerator: } &\sum \Delta P \cdot \Delta E \\
&= (-250)(-93.33) + (-150)(-53.33) + (-50)(-23.33) \\
&\quad + (50)(16.67) + (150)(56.67) + (250)(96.67) \\
&= 23{,}332.5 + 7{,}999.5 + 1{,}166.5 + 833.5 + 8{,}500.5 + 24{,}167.5 \\
&= 66{,}000.0
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Denominator: } \sum \Delta P^2 &= (-250)^2 + (-150)^2 + (-50)^2 + (50)^2 + (150)^2 + (250)^2 \\
&= 62{,}500 + 22{,}500 + 2{,}500 + 2{,}500 + 22{,}500 + 62{,}500 \\
&= 175{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
b = \frac{66{,}000}{175{,}000} \approx 0.3771 \text{ MWh/tonne}
$$

### Step 3: Compute Intercept $a$

$$
a = \bar{E} - b\bar{P} = 573.33 - (0.3771)(1{,}050) = 573.33 - 395.96 \approx 177.37 \text{ MWh}
$$

### Step 4: Final Baseline Equation

$$
\boxed{E = 177.37 + 0.3771 \times P \quad \text{(MWh)}}
$$

**Interpretation:** The fixed (intercept) energy is **$177.37$ MWh/month** (lighting, standby, idle losses) and the variable energy is **$0.3771$ MWh/tonne** of production.

### Step 5: CUSUM Verification

Compute the predicted energy $E_{\text{pred}}$ for each month and the cumulative deviation.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Month 1: } &E_{\text{pred}} = 177.37 + 0.3771(800) = 479.05;\ \Delta = 0.95;\ \text{CUSUM} = 0.95 \\
\text{Month 2: } &E_{\text{pred}} = 177.37 + 0.3771(900) = 516.76;\ \Delta = 3.24;\ \text{CUSUM} = 4.19 \\
\text{Month 3: } &E_{\text{pred}} = 177.37 + 0.3771(1{,}000) = 554.47;\ \Delta = -4.47;\ \text{CUSUM} = -0.28 \\
\text{Month 4: } &E_{\text{pred}} = 177.37 + 0.3771(1{,}100) = 592.18;\ \Delta = -2.18;\ \text{CUSUM} = -2.46 \\
\text{Month 5: } &E_{\text{pred}} = 177.37 + 0.3771(1{,}200) = 629.89;\ \Delta = 0.11;\ \text{CUSUM} = -2.35 \\
\text{Month 6: } &E_{\text{pred}} = 177.37 + 0.3771(1{,}300) = 667.60;\ \Delta = 2.40;\ \text{CUSUM} = 0.05
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** CUSUM remains near zero with no sustained trend, indicating **stable performance** and a valid baseline.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Benchmarking vs. Energy Audit vs. M&V

| Aspect | Benchmarking | Energy Audit | M&V (IPMVP) |
|--------|--------------|--------------|-------------|
| **Primary Goal** | Compare and rank | Identify savings | Verify savings |
| **Time Frame** | Continuous | Periodic (annual) | Project-based |
| **Data Required** | Aggregate monthly/yearly | Equipment-level measurements | High-resolution baseline & post |
| **Output** | Performance gap | ECM list with ROI | Verified savings report |
| **Standard** | ISO 50015 | ASHRAE Level 1–3 | IPMVP (EVO) |
| **KTU Weightage** | Full 14-mark question | Full 14-mark question | Frequently in Part A |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Energy Benchmarking Process Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Process or Equipment] --> B[Select Energy Performance Indicator EnPI]
    B --> C[Collect Energy and Production Data 12 to 36 months]
    C --> D[Normalize Data for Operating Variables]
    D --> E[Establish Baseline Regression or CUSUM Model]
    E --> F{Choose Benchmark Type}
    F --> F1[Internal Benchmark]
    F --> F2[External Benchmark]
    F --> F3[Best Practice Benchmark]
    F --> F4[Regulatory Benchmark]
    F1 --> G[Compute Performance Gap Percent]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H[Root Cause Analysis of Gap]
    H --> I[Recommend Energy Conservation Measures]
    I --> J[Implement ECMs with M and V]
    J --> K[Monitor Verify and Re Benchmark Continuously]
    K --> C
```

## 4.2 The 4-Layer Benchmarking Hierarchy (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Layer 1: Regulatory Benchmark] --> L2[Layer 2: Industry Average Benchmark]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3: Best in Class Benchmark]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4: Theoretical Thermodynamic Limit]
    L1 -.- N1[Example: ECBC for buildings]
    L2 -.- N2[Example: Sectoral SEC average from PAT]
    L3 -.- N3[Example: Global top quartile performer]
    L4 -.- N4[Example: Carnot efficiency for heat engine]
```

## 4.3 EnPI Selection Logic Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Define Boundary of Study] --> Q1{What is the Output?}
    Q1 -->|Physical Product| R1[Use SEC kWh per tonne or unit]
    Q1 -->|Service Output| R2[Use kWh per service unit like passenger km]
    Q1 -->|Building Space| R3[Use Energy Use Intensity kWh per m2 per year]
    Q1 -->|Process Variable| R4[Use kWh per unit process variable]
    R1 --> V1[Validate: Is it controllable and quantifiable?]
    R2 --> V1
    R3 --> V1
    R4 --> V1
    V1 -->|Yes| END[Final EnPI Approved]
    V1 -->|No| REV[Refine or substitute EnPI]
    REV --> Q1
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Energy Benchmarking System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT[Data Acquisition Layer]
        S1[Smart Energy Meters]
        S2[Production Counters]
        S3[Weather and Environmental Sensors]
    end
    subgraph PROCESS[Processing Layer]
        P1[Data Validation and Outlier Removal]
        P2[Normalization Engine]
        P3[Regression and CUSUM Engine]
        P4[Benchmark Comparison Module]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Decision and Action Layer]
        D1[Performance Dashboard KPI]
        D2[Gap Analysis Report]
        D3[ECM Recommendations]
        D4[Alerts and Notifications]
    end
    S1 --> P1
    S2 --> P2
    S3 --> P2
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> D1
    P4 --> D2
    P4 --> D3
    P4 --> D4
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Define energy benchmarking. List any two types of benchmarking with examples.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
> **Energy Benchmarking** is the systematic process of measuring an organization's energy performance (typically using Specific Energy Consumption, SEC) and comparing it against an internal, external, best-practice, or regulatory reference, in order to quantify the performance gap and identify energy conservation opportunities.

**Types (1 Mark – any two):**
- **Internal Benchmarking:** Comparing Boiler A vs. Boiler B in the same plant.
- **External Benchmarking:** Comparing cement Plant X's SEC (kWh/tonne clinker) with the industry average.
- **Best-in-Class Benchmarking:** Comparing current SEC with the global minimum SEC achieved.
- **Regulatory Benchmarking:** Comparing EPI of a building against ECBC norms.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**What is an Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI)? State the four characteristics of a good EnPI.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
> An **Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI)** is a quantitative metric (e.g., kWh/tonne, MJ/m²) used to measure and track the energy performance of a system, process, facility, or organization.

**Four Characteristics (2 Marks):**
1. **Relevant** – directly linked to the process being measured.
2. **Quantifiable** – expressible as a single numerical value.
3. **Understandable** – easily interpreted by management and operators.
4. **Controllable** – can be influenced by operational changes.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students commonly lose **2–3 marks** for not specifying the **unit of SEC** (e.g., writing 0.50 instead of **0.50 MWh/tonne**).
> - Marks are deducted for **confusing Benchmarking with Energy Audit** – benchmarking *compares*, audit *investigates*.
> - In CUSUM/regression problems, marks are lost for **failing to interpret** the result (e.g., the CUSUM conclusion).
> - Always write the **performance gap formula with units**; examiners look for the final "%" interpretation.
> - Do **not** confuse the **Stretch Factor (SF)** with the **Performance Gap** – SF is a ratio, Gap is a percentage.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a) (7 Marks)**
Explain the **concept of energy benchmarking** in detail. Discuss the various **types of benchmarking** with suitable examples. Outline the **step-by-step methodology** for conducting an energy benchmarking study.

**(b) (7 Marks)**
A textile dyeing unit consumes **$18{,}000$ MWh/year** of electrical energy and **$1{,}500$ tonnes of coal/year** (with GCV of $5{,}500$ kcal/kg) to produce **$6{,}000$ tonnes/year** of finished fabric. The **best-practice benchmark** for similar units is **$2.0$ MWh/tonne** of fabric (electrical only). Calculate:
1. The actual electrical SEC.
2. The performance gap (in %).
3. The energy savings potential (MWh/year).
4. The annual monetary savings in ₹ (assume electricity tariff = ₹ 7.50/kWh).

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Concept, Types, and Methodology (7 Marks)

**Concept (2 Marks):**
> Energy benchmarking is the **systematic, continuous measurement and comparison** of an organization's energy performance (energy use per unit of output or service) against a reference standard — internal, external, best-in-class, or regulatory — for the purpose of **identifying performance gaps and guiding improvement actions**.

**Types (3 Marks):**

| Type | Description | Example |
|------|-------------|---------|
| Internal | Within same organization across units/periods | Plant A vs. Plant B (same company) |
| External (Competitive) | Against peer competitors | Cement plant vs. sector average |
| Best-in-Class | Against theoretical/global best | kWh/tonne vs. world-leading plant |
| Process/Product | Specific to one process or product | kWh/tonne of steel rolled |

**Step-by-Step Methodology (2 Marks):**
1. **Identify** the system/equipment to be benchmarked.
2. **Select** an appropriate EnPI.
3. **Collect** 12–36 months of energy and production data.
4. **Normalize** data (production, weather, feedstock).
5. **Establish** baseline (mean, median, or regression).
6. **Compare** with chosen benchmark → compute **performance gap**.
7. **Root cause analysis** of the gap.
8. **Recommend** ECMs.
9. **Implement** with M&V.
10. **Continuous re-benchmarking**.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Compute Actual Electrical SEC (1 Mark):**

$$
\text{SEC}_{\text{electrical}} = \frac{E_{\text{electrical}}}{P} = \frac{18{,}000 \text{ MWh/year}}{6{,}000 \text{ tonnes/year}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{SEC}_{\text{electrical}} = 3.00 \text{ MWh/tonne}}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Performance Gap (2 Marks):**

$$
\text{Gap}\,\% = \frac{\text{SEC}_{\text{actual}} - \text{SEC}_{\text{benchmark}}}{\text{SEC}_{\text{benchmark}}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Gap}\,\% = \frac{3.00 - 2.00}{2.00} \times 100 = \frac{1.00}{2.00} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Gap}\,\% = 50\,\%}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Energy Savings Potential in MWh/year (2 Marks):**

$$
\text{Savings}_{\text{MWh}} = (3.00 - 2.00) \times 6{,}000 = 1.00 \times 6{,}000
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Savings}_{\text{MWh}} = 6{,}000 \text{ MWh/year}}
$$

**Step 4: Convert to Monetary Savings in ₹ (2 Marks):**

Convert MWh to kWh: $6{,}000 \text{ MWh} = 6{,}000 \times 1{,}000 = 6{,}000{,}000$ kWh

$$
\text{Savings}_{₹} = 6{,}000{,}000 \text{ kWh} \times ₹\,7.50/\text{kWh}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Savings}_{₹} = ₹\,4{,}50{,}00{,}000 \text{ per year} = ₹\,4.5 \text{ Crore/year}}
$$

**Note:** Coal consumption is **not used** in the electrical SEC calculation as the benchmark is electrical-only. (1 Mark reserved for correctly omitting coal or stating the assumption.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) (7 Marks)**
Discuss the role of **Energy Performance Indicators (EnPIs)** in benchmarking. Enumerate the **sector-specific EnPIs** for at least **four industries**. Explain the concept of **baseline normalization** with the CUSUM technique.

**(b) (7 Marks)**
A motor pump system consumes energy according to the equation: **$E = 50 + 0.25 \times Q$** (MWh), where $Q$ is the throughput in million litres. For a target throughput of **$Q = 1{,}000$ ML**, the system consumed **$310$ MWh** last month. Calculate:
1. The **expected (baseline) energy** at $Q = 1{,}000$ ML.
2. The **deviation (in MWh)** from the baseline.
3. The **SEC actual** and **SEC baseline** (in kWh/ML).
4. The **% energy performance gap**.

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — EnPIs, Sector EnPIs, CUSUM (7 Marks)

**Role of EnPIs (2 Marks):**
> EnPIs translate raw energy data into **meaningful, comparable metrics** that reveal whether performance is improving or deteriorating. They are essential for **ISO 50001 compliance, PAT target compliance, and management decision-making**. Without EnPIs, energy data is meaningless — like a thermometer without a scale.

**Sector-Specific EnPIs (3 Marks):**

| Industry | Recommended EnPI |
|----------|------------------|
| Cement | kWh/tonne clinker, MJ/tonne cement |
| Steel | GJ/tonne crude steel |
| Aluminum | kWh/tonne aluminum |
| Pulp & Paper | GJ/tonne paper |
| Textile | kWh/tonne fabric |
| Buildings | kWh/m²/year |
| HVAC | kWh/ton-hour refrigeration |
| Pumping | kWh/million litres (ML) |

**Baseline Normalization & CUSUM (2 Marks):**
> **Baseline normalization** removes the effect of operating variables (production, weather) so that energy use can be compared on a "like-for-like" basis. The **CUSUM (Cumulative Sum)** technique plots the running total of the difference between actual energy and the energy predicted by the regression model. A CUSUM that **rises steadily** indicates deteriorating performance; a **flat CUSUM** indicates stable performance; a **falling CUSUM** indicates improvement.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Expected (Baseline) Energy at $Q = 1{,}000$ ML (2 Marks):**

$$
E_{\text{baseline}} = 50 + 0.25 \times Q = 50 + 0.25 \times 1{,}000
$$

$$
\boxed{E_{\text{baseline}} = 50 + 250 = 300 \text{ MWh}}
$$

**Step 2: Deviation from Baseline (1 Mark):**

$$
\Delta E = E_{\text{actual}} - E_{\text{baseline}} = 310 - 300
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta E = +10 \text{ MWh (excess consumption)}}
$$

**Step 3: Compute SEC actual and SEC baseline in kWh/ML (2 Marks):**

Convert MWh to kWh: $1 \text{ MWh} = 1{,}000$ kWh.

$$
\text{SEC}_{\text{baseline}} = \frac{300 \text{ MWh} \times 1{,}000}{1{,}000 \text{ ML}} = \frac{300{,}000}{1{,}000} = 300 \text{ kWh/ML}
$$

$$
\text{SEC}_{\text{actual}} = \frac{310 \text{ MWh} \times 1{,}000}{1{,}000 \text{ ML}} = \frac{310{,}000}{1{,}000} = 310 \text{ kWh/ML}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{SEC}_{\text{baseline}} = 300 \text{ kWh/ML};\quad \text{SEC}_{\text{actual}} = 310 \text{ kWh/ML}}
$$

**Step 4: Compute % Energy Performance Gap (2 Marks):**

$$
\text{Gap}\,\% = \frac{310 - 300}{300} \times 100 = \frac{10}{300} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Gap}\,\% \approx 3.33\,\%}
$$

**Interpretation:** The pump system is consuming **$3.33\%$ more energy** than the regression baseline predicts, signaling a potential efficiency loss of **$10$ MWh/month (~$120$ MWh/year)** that warrants further investigation (e.g., impeller wear, valve throttling, leaks).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Benchmarking**
>
> - **Definition:** Systematic, continuous comparison of energy performance against a defined reference to identify improvement opportunities.
> - **Core Metric:** Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) = Total Energy ÷ Production Output (always carry units).
> - **Types (4):** Internal, External, Best-in-Class, Regulatory.
> - **10-Step Methodology:** Identify → Select EnPI → Collect Data → Normalize → Baseline → Benchmark → Compare → Root Cause → Recommend → Re-benchmark.
> - **Performance Gap Formula:** $\left(\text{SEC}_{\text{actual}} - \text{SEC}_{\text{benchmark}}\right) \div \text{SEC}_{\text{benchmark}} \times 100$.
> - **Savings Formula:** $\left(\text{SEC}_{\text{baseline}} - \text{SEC}_{\text{new}}\right) \times P_{\text{annual}}$.
> - **Stretch Factor:** $\text{SEC}_{\text{best}} \div \text{SEC}_{\text{actual}}$ (SF $\geq 1$ means at benchmark).
> - **EnPI Qualities:** Relevant, Quantifiable, Understandable, Controllable.
> - **Sector EnPIs:** Cement → kWh/tonne clinker; Steel → GJ/tonne; Buildings → kWh/m²/year; Pumping → kWh/ML.
> - **CUSUM:** Flat = stable; Rising = deterioration; Falling = improvement.
> - **Standard Linkages:** ISO 50001 (EnPIs), ISO 50015 (Benchmarking), BEE PAT (India), ECBC (Buildings), IPMVP (M&V).
> - **Common Pitfalls:** Confusing benchmarking with audit; omitting units; not interpreting results; not normalizing for production/weather.
> - **Always write assumptions** for fuel conversion (e.g., coal GCV), tariff, and time period (monthly vs. annual) in numerical answers.
> - **Final-step interpretation** (e.g., "the plant is $25\%$ worse than best practice → $4{,}800$ MWh/year savings") is **mandatory** for full KTU marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Performance. Optimum Performance of Existing Facilities
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT614 - ENERGY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Energy Performance. Optimum Performance of Existing Facilities

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Energy Performance — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Performance (EnP)** is the quantified result of energy efficiency, energy use, and energy consumption of a building, facility, organization, or process, expressed as one or more **Energy Performance Indicators (EnPIs)** that can be measured, tracked, and benchmarked against a baseline.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, energy performance is a **measurable outcome** — not merely a qualitative judgment of "good" or "bad" energy use. It is evaluated through:

- **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** — energy per unit of production
- **Energy Use Intensity (EUI)** — energy per unit area (typically **kWh/m²/year**)
- **Energy Cost Ratio** — energy expense per unit revenue
- **Carbon Intensity** — CO₂ emitted per unit output

## 1.2 Optimum Performance of Existing Facilities — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Optimum Performance of Existing Facilities** refers to the systematic process of maximizing the energy efficiency and operational productivity of already-constructed plants and buildings through **operational tuning, housekeeping measures, control optimization, retro-commissioning, and minor retrofits** — *without* resorting to capital-intensive design changes or full system replacement.

The objective is to push the facility toward its **design-point efficiency** (or better) using:
1. **Operational Optimization** — set-points, schedules, sequencing
2. **Maintenance Optimization** — preventive/predictive maintenance
3. **Behavioral Optimization** — operator training and awareness
4. **Low-cost Retrofitting** — VFDs, insulation, LED lighting
5. **Continuous Monitoring & Targeting (M&T)** — real-time KPIs

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body Check-up

Think of an existing industrial facility as a **middle-aged car owner**:
- The car (facility) still runs, but it's burning more fuel than necessary.
- A **performance assessment** is like a garage diagnostic — measuring fuel economy, tire pressure, engine health.
- **Optimum performance** means getting the car back to manufacturer-rated mileage through *tuning, cleaning filters, oil changes* — NOT replacing the engine.
- Just as ignoring small leaks and dirty filters wastes fuel, ignoring valve leaks and poor insulation wastes energy.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Students must be able to (i) define and compute EnPIs, (ii) explain energy audit methodologies, (iii) identify low-cost optimization measures, and (iv) apply **M&T** principles to existing facilities.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Energy Performance Assessment Framework

The assessment follows a **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** cycle rooted in **ISO 50001**:

| Step | Activity | Output |
|:-----|:---------|:-------|
| **Plan** | Define energy baseline, choose EnPIs | Energy Review Report |
| **Do** | Implement M&T system, collect data | Energy Database |
| **Check** | Compare actual vs. baseline, find deviations | Variance Analysis |
| **Act** | Correct deviations, update targets | Revised Action Plan |

## 2.2 Energy Performance Indicators (EnPIs) — The 7 Core Metrics

1. **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)**
2. **Energy Use Intensity (EUI)**
3. **Energy Cost per Unit Output**
4. **Demand Factor / Load Factor**
5. **Power Factor**
6. **Carbon Emission Factor (CEF)**
7. **Percentage Waste Heat Recovery**

## 2.3 Energy Audit — Three Levels

| Audit Type | Scope | Investment | Typical Findings |
|:-----------|:------|:-----------|:-----------------|
| **Walk-through Audit** | Visual inspection, no measurements | Very Low | Obvious wastage, housekeeping |
| **Preliminary Audit** | Spot measurements + utility bills | Low | Low-cost measures, payback < 1 year |
| **Detailed Audit** | Continuous monitoring, modeling | High | Capital projects, full retrofit plan |

## 2.4 Optimization Strategies — Hierarchical Approach

> [!NOTE]
> The **KTU-recommended optimization hierarchy** (from lowest to highest cost):
> 1. **Housekeeping** (free) — switch off, leak repair
> 2. **Operational Changes** (very low cost) — schedule, set-points
> 3. **Maintenance Upgrades** (low cost) — cleaning, calibration
> 4. **Retro-commissioning** (moderate cost) — control logic
> 5. **Minor Retrofits** (moderate cost) — VFDs, LEDs, insulation
> 6. **Major Retrofits** (high cost) — boiler replacement, chiller upgrade
> 7. **Renewable Integration** (high cost) — solar, wind

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable. Master the units and boundary conditions.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Legend | Units | Application |
|:-:|:--------|:--------------|:------|:------------|
| 1 | $SEC = \dfrac{E_{total}}{P_{output}}$ | $E_{total}$ = Energy, $P$ = Production | $\dfrac{MJ}{ton}$ or $\dfrac{kWh}{unit}$ | Industrial process |
| 2 | $EUI = \dfrac{E_{annual}}{A_{floor}}$ | $A_{floor}$ = Floor area | $\dfrac{kWh}{m^2 \cdot yr}$ | Buildings |
| 3 | $E_{baseline} = a \cdot X + b$ | $X$ = Driver variable (HDD/CDD, output) | varies | Baseline model |
| 4 | $CUSUM(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{t} (E_{actual,i} - E_{baseline,i})$ | Cumulative deviation | $kWh$ | Drift detection |
| 5 | $CUSUM_{norm}(t) = \dfrac{CUSUM(t)}{\sigma \sqrt{t}}$ | $\sigma$ = Std. dev. | dimensionless | Statistical alarm |
| 6 | $SPP = \dfrac{C_{investment}}{S_{annual}}$ | Simple Payback Period | years | Quick screening |
| 7 | $NPV = -C_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | $r$ = Discount rate | currency | Economic viability |
| 8 | $IRR: NPV = 0$ solve for $r$ | Internal Rate of Return | % | Compare projects |
| 9 | $E_{saved} = E_{baseline} - E_{actual}$ | Energy saved | $kWh, MJ$ | Effectiveness |
| 10 | $\% Savings = \dfrac{E_{saved}}{E_{baseline}} \times 100$ | Percentage savings | % | Reporting KPI |
| 11 | $\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{Q_{useful}}{Q_{fuel}} \times 100$ | Combustion efficiency | % | Boiler tuning |
| 12 | $COP = \dfrac{Q_{cooling}}{W_{input}}$ | Coefficient of Performance | dimensionless | HVAC performance |
| 13 | $LF = \dfrac{E_{actual}}{P_{max} \cdot t_{period}}$ | Load Factor | dimensionless | Demand mgmt |
| 14 | $PF = \dfrac{P_{real}}{S_{apparent}}$ | Power Factor | dimensionless | Penalty reduction |
| 15 | $\Delta T_{approach} = T_{out} - T_{wet,amb}$ | Cooling tower approach | °C | Cooling tower |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constants to remember in bold:** $1 \; kWh = 3.6 \; MJ$, $1 \; toe = 41.868 \; GJ$, Standard air density $\rho = 1.2 \; kg/m^3$, Specific heat $c_p = 1.005 \; kJ/kg \cdot K$.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Sector**: SEC targets drive ISO 50001 certification and reduce unit production cost.
- **Commercial Buildings**: EUI benchmarking is mandated under **Energy Conservation Building Code (ECBC)** in India.
- **Power Plants**: Heat rate ($MJ/kWh$) optimization is critical for plant profitability.
- **Process Industries**: Energy Performance Certification (EPC) is increasingly required for exports to EU markets.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy Use Intensity (EUI)

**Starting Principle:** Normalize annual energy use by the serviced area.

$$\text{Energy Use Intensity (EUI)} = \frac{\text{Total Annual Energy Consumption of Building}}{\text{Conditioned Floor Area}}$$

**Step 1** — Express energy in standard units.

Convert all energy carriers to a common base. For example, electricity in **kWh**, gas in **m³**, then convert gas using a calorific value $CV$ typically in **MJ/m³**:

$$E_{gas,kWh} = \frac{V_{gas} \times CV}{3.6}$$

**Step 2** — Sum across all energy vectors.

$$E_{total} = E_{electricity} + E_{gas} + E_{HSD} + E_{LPG} + \dots$$

**Step 3** — Divide by floor area.

$$EUI = \frac{E_{total}}{A_{floor}}$$

with units of $\dfrac{kWh}{m^2 \cdot yr}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion factor used:** $1 \; kWh = 3.6 \; MJ$. The choice of primary energy factor may double or halve EUI — always state the basis.

## 3.2 Derivation: Cumulative Sum (CUSUM) for Drift Detection

The CUSUM technique detects *gradual* drifts in energy consumption that ordinary weekly/monthly charts miss.

**Step 1** — Establish a baseline energy equation (linear regression on past data):

$$E_{baseline}(t) = a \cdot X(t) + b$$

where $X(t)$ is the *driver* (e.g., Heating Degree Days, production units).

**Step 2** — Compute deviations for each period $i$:

$$\Delta_i = E_{actual}(i) - E_{baseline}(i)$$

**Step 3** — Form the cumulative sum:

$$\text{CUSUM}(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{t} \Delta_i = \sum_{i=1}^{t} \left[ E_{actual}(i) - E_{baseline}(i) \right]$$

**Step 4** — Normalize using standard deviation $\sigma$ of the deviations to get a dimensionless alarm signal:

$$\text{CUSUM}_{norm}(t) = \frac{\text{CUSUM}(t)}{\sigma \sqrt{t}}$$

**Step 5** — Decision rule:

- If $\text{CUSUM}_{norm}(t) > 4$ or $< -4$, the cumulative drift is statistically significant (≈ 3-sigma).
- Investigate root cause: control loop failure, fouling, setpoint drift, etc.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — EUI & Savings Calculation

**Problem Statement:**

A KTU hostel's annual electricity consumption is $E_{elec} = 480{,}000 \; kWh$ and LPG consumption is $V_{LPG} = 12{,}000 \; kg$. The calorific value of LPG is $CV = 46 \; MJ/kg$. The conditioned floor area is $A = 4000 \; m^2$. Find:
1. The total energy in **GJ**
2. The **EUI**
3. The **percentage savings** if post-retrofit EUI falls to $90 \; kWh/m^2 \cdot yr$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Convert LPG to kWh equivalent:**

$$E_{LPG,MJ} = m \times CV = 12{,}000 \times 46 = 552{,}000 \; MJ$$

$$E_{LPG,kWh} = \frac{552{,}000}{3.6} = 153{,}333.33 \; kWh$$

**Step 2 — Total annual energy:**

$$E_{total} = 480{,}000 + 153{,}333.33 = 633{,}333.33 \; kWh$$

**Step 3 — Convert to GJ:**

$$E_{total,GJ} = 633{,}333.33 \times 3.6 \times 10^{-3} = 2{,}280 \; GJ$$

**Step 4 — Compute EUI:**

$$EUI_{before} = \frac{633{,}333.33}{4000} = 158.33 \; kWh/m^2 \cdot yr$$

**Step 5 — Compute post-retrofit annual energy:**

$$E_{after} = 90 \times 4000 = 360{,}000 \; kWh$$

**Step 6 — Percentage savings:**

$$\% \text{Savings} = \frac{633{,}333.33 - 360{,}000}{633{,}333.33} \times 100 = 43.16 \; \%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification:** Cross-check using absolute savings:
> $$E_{saved} = 633{,}333.33 - 360{,}000 = 273{,}333.33 \; kWh$$
> $$\% \text{Savings} = \frac{273{,}333.33}{633{,}333.33} \times 100 = 43.16 \; \% \; \checkmark$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Simple Payback & NPV

**Problem:** A VFD installation costs ₹ $C_0 = 8{,}00{,}000$ and saves ₹ $S = 3{,}50{,}000$ per year in electricity for $n = 4$ years. Discount rate $r = 10\%$. Find **(a)** Simple Payback Period, **(b)** NPV.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Simple Payback Period (SPP):**

$$\text{SPP} = \frac{C_0}{S} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{3{,}50{,}000} = 2.286 \; years$$

**Step 2 — Discount factor table:**

$$\text{Year 1: } DF_1 = \frac{1}{(1+0.10)^1} = 0.9091$$

$$\text{Year 2: } DF_2 = \frac{1}{(1+0.10)^2} = 0.8264$$

$$\text{Year 3: } DF_3 = \frac{1}{(1+0.10)^3} = 0.7513$$

$$\text{Year 4: } DF_4 = \frac{1}{(1+0.10)^4} = 0.6830$$

**Step 3 — Discounted Cash Flows:**

$$DCF_1 = 3{,}50{,}000 \times 0.9091 = 3{,}18{,}181.82$$

$$DCF_2 = 3{,}50{,}000 \times 0.8264 = 2{,}89{,}256.20$$

$$DCF_3 = 3{,}50{,}000 \times 0.7513 = 2{,}62{,}960.18$$

$$DCF_4 = 3{,}50{,}000 \times 0.6830 = 2{,}39{,}054.71$$

**Step 4 — Sum of PVs:**

$$\sum DCF = 3{,}18{,}181.82 + 2{,}89{,}256.20 + 2{,}62{,}960.18 + 2{,}39{,}054.71 = 11{,}09{,}452.91$$

**Step 5 — NPV calculation:**

$$NPV = \sum DCF - C_0 = 11{,}09{,}452.91 - 8{,}00{,}000 = 3{,}09{,}452.91$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Since $NPV > 0$, the VFD retrofit is **economically viable**. The decision rule:
> $$\text{Accept project if } NPV > 0 \text{ AND } IRR > r_{required}$$

## 3.5 Derivation: Boiler Efficiency Improvement (Combustion Tuning)

**Step 1 — Stoichiometric air required:**

For methane combustion: $CH_4 + 2 O_2 \to CO_2 + 2 H_2 O$.

The theoretical $O_2$ requirement per kg of fuel is calculated from elemental composition.

**Step 2 — Excess air ratio:**

$$\lambda = \frac{\text{Actual air supplied}}{\text{Stoichiometric air required}}$$

**Step 3 — Stack loss (Siegert formula):**

$$L_{stack} \; (\%) = K \cdot \frac{T_{fg} - T_{amb}}{CO_2 \; \%}$$

where $K$ depends on fuel type: $K \approx 0.65$ for oil, $K \approx 0.60$ for gas.

**Step 4 — Net boiler efficiency:**

$$\eta_{boiler} = 100 - L_{stack} - L_{rad} - L_{other}$$

Typical values: $L_{stack} = 8\text{–}12\%$, $L_{rad} = 1\text{–}2\%$.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI) and give three examples used in industrial facilities.**

**Model Answer:**

> An **Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI)** is a quantitative metric that expresses the energy performance of a system, process, or facility in a normalized, comparable form.
>
> **Three examples:**
> 1. **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** — energy per unit of production (e.g., $kWh/ton$ of cement).
> 2. **Energy Use Intensity (EUI)** — energy per unit conditioned area ($\dfrac{kWh}{m^2 \cdot yr}$).
> 3. **Energy Cost per Unit Output** — monetary energy cost per unit product.
>
> EnPIs enable benchmarking, trend analysis, and target-setting in energy management systems.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**List the three levels of an energy audit and state the primary output of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Level | Primary Output |
|:------|:---------------|
| **Walk-through Audit** | List of obvious wastage points, housekeeping recommendations, and quick-win opportunities |
| **Preliminary Audit** | Quantified energy consumption breakdown, low-cost measures with payback period |
| **Detailed Audit** | Comprehensive retrofit plan with engineering analysis, cost-benefit evaluation, and implementation roadmap |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** Students frequently lose **2–3 marks** by stating formulas without specifying **units** and **boundary conditions**. Always write: *"SEC in $\dfrac{MJ}{ton}$ at standard calorific value of $CV = 29.3 \; MJ/Nm^3$ for PNG."*
>
> **Pitfall #2:** Do NOT confuse **EUI** (buildings) with **SEC** (industries). EUI is per area, SEC is per unit output.
>
> **Pitfall #3:** In NPV problems, forgetting the negative sign on $C_0$ (initial investment is a cash outflow).

---

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**A manufacturing plant consumes the following annually:**
- Electricity: $5{,}00{,}000 \; kWh$
- Furnace oil: $40{,}000 \; kg$ with $CV = 41 \; MJ/kg$
- Annual production: $25{,}000$ units
- Total floor area: $10{,}000 \; m^2$

**(a)** Compute the **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** in $\dfrac{kWh}{unit}$ and the **Energy Use Intensity (EUI)** in $\dfrac{kWh}{m^2 \cdot yr}$. (7 marks)

**(b)** After retrofit, electricity reduces by $25\%$ and oil by $15\%$. Find the new SEC, EUI, percentage energy savings, and monetary savings if energy cost is ₹$8/kWh$ and ₹$40/kg$ of oil. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Convert furnace oil to kWh equivalent:**

$$E_{oil,MJ} = 40{,}000 \times 41 = 1{,}640{,}000 \; MJ$$

$$E_{oil,kWh} = \frac{1{,}640{,}000}{3.6} = 4{,}55{,}555.56 \; kWh$$

**Step 2 — Total annual energy:**

$$E_{total} = 5{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}55{,}555.56 = 9{,}55{,}555.56 \; kWh$$

**Step 3 — Compute SEC:**

$$SEC = \frac{E_{total}}{P_{output}} = \frac{9{,}55{,}555.56}{25{,}000} = 38.22 \; \dfrac{kWh}{unit}$$

**Step 4 — Compute EUI:**

$$EUI = \frac{E_{total}}{A_{floor}} = \frac{9{,}55{,}555.56}{10{,}000} = 95.56 \; \dfrac{kWh}{m^2 \cdot yr}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Post-retrofit energy:**

$$E_{elec,new} = 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.75 = 3{,}75{,}000 \; kWh$$

$$E_{oil,new} = 40{,}000 \times 0.85 = 34{,}000 \; kg$$

**Step 2 — Convert new oil to kWh:**

$$E_{oil,new,MJ} = 34{,}000 \times 41 = 13{,}94{,}000 \; MJ$$

$$E_{oil,new,kWh} = \frac{13{,}94{,}000}{3.6} = 3{,}87{,}222.22 \; kWh$$

**Step 3 — New total energy:**

$$E_{new} = 3{,}75{,}000 + 3{,}87{,}222.22 = 7{,}62{,}222.22 \; kWh$$

**Step 4 — New SEC and EUI:**

$$SEC_{new} = \frac{7{,}62{,}222.22}{25{,}000} = 30.49 \; \dfrac{kWh}{unit}$$

$$EUI_{new} = \frac{7{,}62{,}222.22}{10{,}000} = 76.22 \; \dfrac{kWh}{m^2 \cdot yr}$$

**Step 5 — Percentage savings:**

$$\% \text{Savings} = \frac{9{,}55{,}555.56 - 7{,}62{,}222.22}{9{,}55{,}555.56} \times 100 = 20.23 \;\%$$

**Step 6 — Monetary savings:**

$$S_{elec} = (5{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}75{,}000) \times 8 = 10{,}00{,}000 \; ₹$$

$$S_{oil} = (40{,}000 - 34{,}000) \times 40 = 2{,}40{,}000 \; ₹$$

$$S_{total} = 10{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}40{,}000 = ₹12{,}40{,}000 \; per \; year$$

### Part B — Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **CUSUM technique** for energy performance monitoring. Derive the normalized CUSUM expression and state the decision rule. (7 marks)

**(b)** A factory baseline energy equation is $E_{baseline} = 2.5X + 50{,}000$, where $X$ is the monthly production in tons. For 5 months, actual energy and production are:

| Month | $X$ (tons) | $E_{actual}$ (kWh) |
|:-----:|:----------:|:------------------:|
| 1 | 8000 | 72,000 |
| 2 | 8500 | 78,000 |
| 3 | 8200 | 75,500 |
| 4 | 8800 | 82,000 |
| 5 | 9000 | 85,000 |

Given $\sigma = 1500 \; kWh$, find whether the drift is statistically significant. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Definition:** CUSUM is a statistical drift-detection method that accumulates deviations between actual and baseline energy to detect gradual changes missed by ordinary charts.

**Derivation:**

$$\Delta_i = E_{actual,i} - E_{baseline,i}$$

$$\text{CUSUM}(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{t} \Delta_i$$

$$\text{CUSUM}_{norm}(t) = \frac{\text{CUSUM}(t)}{\sigma \sqrt{t}}$$

**Decision rule:** If $\vert \text{CUSUM}_{norm}(t) \vert > 4$, drift is statistically significant (≈ 3-sigma confidence). Action: investigate root cause (control failure, fouling, leak).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Compute baseline for each month:**

| Month | $X$ | $E_{baseline} = 2.5X + 50000$ | $E_{actual}$ | $\Delta = E_{act} - E_{base}$ |
|:-----:|:---:|:------------------------------:|:------------:|:----------------------------:|
| 1 | 8000 | $20{,}000 + 50{,}000 = 70{,}000$ | 72,000 | +2000 |
| 2 | 8500 | $21{,}250 + 50{,}000 = 71{,}250$ | 78,000 | +6750 |
| 3 | 8200 | $20{,}500 + 50{,}000 = 70{,}500$ | 75,500 | +5000 |
| 4 | 8800 | $22{,}000 + 50{,}000 = 72{,}000$ | 82,000 | +10,000 |
| 5 | 9000 | $22{,}500 + 50{,}000 = 72{,}500$ | 85,000 | +12,500 |

**Step 2 — Cumulative CUSUM:**

$$\text{CUSUM}(1) = 2000$$

$$\text{CUSUM}(2) = 2000 + 6750 = 8750$$

$$\text{CUSUM}(3) = 8750 + 5000 = 13{,}750$$

$$\text{CUSUM}(4) = 13{,}750 + 10{,}000 = 23{,}750$$

$$\text{CUSUM}(5) = 23{,}750 + 12{,}500 = 36{,}250$$

**Step 3 — Normalized CUSUM at month 5:**

$$\text{CUSUM}_{norm}(5) = \frac{36{,}250}{1500 \times \sqrt{5}} = \frac{36{,}250}{1500 \times 2.236} = \frac{36{,}250}{3354.10} = 10.81$$

**Step 4 — Decision:**

Since $\text{CUSUM}_{norm}(5) = 10.81 \gg 4$, the drift is **statistically significant** — actual consumption is persistently higher than baseline, indicating an underlying problem (equipment fouling, sensor drift, or process change).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Immediate investigation required. Possible actions: recalibrate sensors, clean heat exchanger, audit compressed air leaks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3**

- ✅ **Energy Performance** = measured outcome of energy use, expressed via EnPIs.
- ✅ **SEC** = Energy / Production Output (use for industry).
- ✅ **EUI** = Energy / Floor Area (use for buildings).
- ✅ **CUSUM** detects gradual drift; alarm threshold = **±4 normalized units**.
- ✅ Baseline model is typically linear: $E_{baseline} = aX + b$ where $X$ is the driver.
- ✅ Three audit levels: **Walk-through → Preliminary → Detailed**.
- ✅ Optimization hierarchy: **Housekeeping → Operational → Maintenance → Retro-commissioning → Retrofit → Renewables**.
- ✅ **SPP** = Investment / Annual Savings (no time value of money).
- ✅ **NPV** = $-C_0 + \sum DCF_t$; accept if $NPV > 0$.
- ✅ **IRR** = discount rate that makes $NPV = 0$.
- ✅ Conversion: $1 \; kWh = 3.6 \; MJ$, $1 \; toe = 41.868 \; GJ$.
- ✅ ISO 50001 follows **PDCA** cycle for continuous improvement.
- ✅ M&T (Monitoring & Targeting) is the backbone of any optimization program.
- ✅ Always state **units** in every numerical answer — KTU examiners deduct marks otherwise.
- ✅ Boiler efficiency tuning reduces stack loss — every $10 \; °C$ reduction in flue gas temp saves **~1% fuel**.
- ✅ Power Factor correction reduces kVArh penalty (target **> 0.95**).
- ✅ VFD on motors typically saves **30–50%** energy in variable load applications.
- ✅ LED retrofits give payback of **1–2 years** vs. conventional lighting.

> [!WARNING]
> **Last-Minute Pitfalls to Avoid in KTU Exam Hall:**
> 1. Do NOT mix EUI and SEC formulas — examiners specifically check this.
> 2. Do NOT forget to convert all energy carriers to a common unit (kWh or MJ).
> 3. Do NOT use Simple Payback when the question explicitly asks for **NPV or IRR**.
> 4. Do NOT skip the negative sign on initial investment in NPV — it costs **2 marks**.
> 5. Always **draw or describe** an energy balance diagram when the question involves facility assessment.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Conservation - Principles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT614 - ENERGY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Energy Conservation - Principles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Conservation** is the principle and practice of reducing the quantity of energy consumed by a system, process, or activity, achieved through deliberate strategies such as efficiency improvement, waste-heat recovery, behavioral changes, and the substitution of conventional energy resources with renewable or alternative sources, while maintaining equivalent or improved service output.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition:**
> *"Energy Conservation is the strategy of minimizing energy input required to provide a given level of service, comfort, or product output, by eliminating energy wastage, optimizing process efficiency, and recovering residual energy — fundamentally grounded in the **First Law of Thermodynamics (Conservation of Energy)** and augmented by the **Second Law (Energy Degradation / Entropy Generation)**."*

Mathematically, the underlying principle is expressed as:

$$E_{in} = E_{out} + E_{stored} + E_{loss}$$

where every term must balance over a defined **control volume** and **time interval**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Household Water Tank

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Intuition (The "Water Tank" Analogy):**
> Imagine a household water tank (energy reservoir) that gets refilled by a municipal pump (energy input). Every tap opened in the house (useful work) drains water, but some water leaks through cracked pipes (waste/loss), some evaporates from an open top (degraded energy / entropy), and some is stored in a secondary drum for later use (energy storage).
> - **Closing the cracks** = energy conservation (reducing losses).
> - **Using a smaller bucket to wash the car** = improved efficiency (same service, less energy).
> - **Collecting rainwater** = renewable substitution.
> - **Keeping the tank full for a dry day** = energy storage.
>
> The fundamental truth — *water in = water out + storage + leaks* — is identical to the **First Law of Thermodynamics**, the bedrock of all energy conservation practice.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants Used

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Units (Memorize for KTU Exams):**
> - **Energy:** **Joule (J)** [SI unit], **Kilowatt-hour (kWh)**, **British Thermal Unit (BTU)**, **Calorie (cal)**
> - **Power:** **Watt (W)** = 1 J/s
> - **1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J = 3.6 MJ**
> - **1 BTU = 1055.06 J**
> - **1 Calorie = 4.184 J**
> - **Thermodynamic Constant:** Universal Gas Constant $R = 8.314$ **J/(mol·K)**
> - **Conversion efficiency** is dimensionless and expressed in **% (percent)**

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of Energy Conservation (KTU Framework)

| Pillar | Principle | Domain Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Reduce** | Eliminate unnecessary energy use | Insulation, switching off loads |
| **Reuse** | Recover waste energy for secondary use | Waste-heat recovery, cogeneration |
| **Replace** | Substitute with efficient/renewable tech | LED lighting, solar PV, VFDs |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Foundational Principles — Step-by-Step Logic

The entire framework of Energy Conservation rests on **three scientifically established principles**:

### Principle 1 — First Law of Thermodynamics (Conservation of Energy)
> [!NOTE]
> Energy can neither be created nor destroyed; it can only be **converted from one form to another** or **transferred across system boundaries**.

**Logical breakdown:**
- Step 1: Identify a **control volume (CV)** — the physical boundary of the system (boiler, building, engine, country).
- Step 2: Tally all **energy inflows**: fuel combustion $Q_{fuel}$, electrical input $W_{elec}$, solar gain $Q_{solar}$, material enthalpy $H_{in}$.
- Step 3: Tally all **energy outflows**: useful work output $W_{out}$, product enthalpy $H_{out}$, waste heat $Q_{waste}$, losses $Q_{loss}$.
- Step 4: The difference between inflows and outflows equals the **change in stored energy** $\Delta E_{stored}$.

**Why this matters:** It is mathematically impossible to "save" energy that the First Law already preserves. What energy conservation actually does is **preserve the *quality* (exergy)** of energy, ensuring that high-grade energy (electricity, mechanical work) is used efficiently and degraded energy (low-temperature waste heat) is recovered wherever economically viable.

### Principle 2 — Second Law of Thermodynamics (Entropy / Energy Degradation)
> [!WARNING]
> While energy is conserved in *quantity*, every real process degrades its *quality*. This is the heart of why energy conservation is necessary — nature taxes every conversion with an "entropy penalty."

$$dS_{universe} \ge 0$$

- **Why it matters for conservation:** A 100 W incandescent bulb and a 10 W LED bulb both consume electrical energy, but the LED delivers more **useful light** per joule (less degraded to heat). The Second Law tells us *there is a minimum unavoidable loss*; conservation engineering aims to push real systems closer to this theoretical minimum.

### Principle 3 — Energy Balance & Efficiency Maximization
The **conversion efficiency** of any device is:

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Useful Energy Output}}{\text{Total Energy Input}} \times 100\%$$

- **How to maximize $\eta$:** Reduce irreversibilities (friction, mixing, throttling, electrical resistance), improve insulation, recover waste streams, optimize operating points.

## 2.2 Energy Balance for a Generic System (Steady State)

For a **steady-flow control volume** with negligible kinetic and potential energy changes:

$$\dot{Q}_{in} + \dot{W}_{in} + \sum \dot{m}_{in} h_{in} = \dot{Q}_{out} + \dot{W}_{out} + \sum \dot{m}_{out} h_{out}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** This single equation is the analytical foundation of every **Energy Audit** conducted in a KTU Module-4 problem. Whether auditing a boiler, an HVAC system, or a national grid, the auditor constructs inflows, outflows, and storage terms in the same way.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table — Memorize This Block for KTU 2024 ESE**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{in} = E_{out} + \Delta E_{stored} + E_{loss}$ | Energy balance | J or kJ | Energy audit problems |
| 2 | $\eta = \dfrac{E_{out,useful}}{E_{in}} \times 100$ | Thermal / mechanical / electrical efficiency | % (dimensionless) | Boiler, motor, lamp efficiency |
| 3 | $\eta_{I} = \dfrac{\eta_{actual}}{\eta_{reversible}} \times 100$ | Second-Law efficiency (exergy efficiency) | % | Advanced process analysis |
| 4 | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat transfer rate | W or kW | Heat loss / recovery in fluids |
| 5 | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot h_{fg}$ | Latent heat transfer rate (boiling/condensation) | W or kW | Steam systems, refrigeration |
| 6 | $COP = \dfrac{\text{Desired Output}}{\text{Required Input}} = \dfrac{Q_{L}}{W_{in}}$ | Coefficient of Performance (refrigeration/HP) | dimensionless | AC, heat pump questions |
| 7 | $E_{saved} = E_{baseline} - E_{new}$ | Absolute energy saved | kWh, MJ, TOE | Conservation project appraisal |
| 8 | $\%\text{Savings} = \dfrac{E_{saved}}{E_{baseline}} \times 100$ | Percentage energy saved | % | Payback / ROI calculations |
| 9 | $S_{simple} = \dfrac{\text{Cost of saved energy} \times \text{hours/yr}}{\text{Investment}}$ | Simple Payback | years | KTU numerical questions |
| 10 | $P_{recoverable} = \dot{m} c_p (T_{hot,in} - T_{hot,out})$ | Heat recovery potential | W or kW | Waste heat recovery (WHR) |
| 11 | $\Delta S = \dot{m} c_p \ln \dfrac{T_2}{T_1}$ | Entropy change of a fluid | W/K | Second-law analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions to remember:**
> - For **cyclic devices** (engines, refrigerators, heat pumps): $\Delta E_{stored} = 0$, so $E_{in} = E_{out}$.
> - For **steady-state** processes: all time derivatives vanish.
> - For **isolated systems**: $E_{in} = E_{out} = 0$, hence $\Delta E_{stored} = 0$ (energy is constant).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Sector | Energy Conservation Strategy | Typical Efficiency Gain |
|---|---|---|
| **Buildings (HVAC)** | Building Management Systems, insulation, VRF systems | 20–40% |
| **Industrial Motors** | Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs), IE4/IE5 motors | 25–60% |
| **Lighting** | LED replacement, daylight harvesting, smart controls | 50–80% |
| **Boilers / Steam** | Flue-gas heat recovery, blow-down heat exchangers, insulation | 10–25% |
| **Compressed Air** | Leak detection, pressure optimization, variable-speed compressors | 20–50% |
| **Transportation** | Regenerative braking, fleet electrification, route optimization | 15–30% |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — The General Energy Balance Equation

### Statement
For any system, the net energy transferred into the system equals the change in the system's stored energy.

### Derivation (Exhaustive, Step-by-Step)

**Step 1:** Define a system as a region in space enclosed by a control surface. Let $E_{sys}$ denote the **total energy** contained within the system, comprising internal $U$, kinetic $KE$, and potential $PE$ energies.

$$E_{sys} = U + KE + PE = U + \frac{1}{2}mv^{2} + mgz$$

**Step 2:** Energy can cross the system boundary in two modes: **heat** $Q$ (driven by temperature difference) and **work** $W$ (all other forms: shaft work, electrical, boundary work, etc.).

**Step 3:** By the **First Law of Thermodynamics**, the net energy crossing the boundary during a time interval $dt$ equals the change in stored energy.

$$\delta Q - \delta W = dE_{sys}$$

**Step 4:** For a **closed system** (no mass transfer), integrate over a finite time interval from state 1 to state 2.

$$Q_{1-2} - W_{1-2} = E_2 - E_1 = \Delta E_{sys}$$

**Step 5:** For a **control volume (open system)** with mass flow rates $\dot{m}_{in}$ and $\dot{m}_{out}$, the energy carried by mass must also be included. By mass conservation, $\sum \dot{m}_{in} = \sum \dot{m}_{out}$ at steady state.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{cv} - \dot{W}_{cv} + \sum \dot{m}_{in} \left( h + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz \right)_{in} &= \sum \dot{m}_{out} \left( h + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz \right)_{out}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Neglecting kinetic and potential energy changes (the standard KTU assumption unless stated otherwise), we obtain the **Steady-Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{cv} - \dot{W}_{cv} + \sum \dot{m}_{in} h_{in} &= \sum \dot{m}_{out} h_{out}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the equation that underlies every KTU Module-4 energy audit problem.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Thermal Efficiency of a Heat Engine (Carnot Reference)

**Step 1:** A heat engine receives heat $Q_H$ from a high-temperature reservoir at $T_H$ and rejects heat $Q_L$ to a low-temperature reservoir at $T_L$ (all in Kelvin).

**Step 2:** By the First Law (cyclic operation, $\Delta U = 0$):

$$W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$$

**Step 3:** By definition, thermal efficiency is:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_H} = \frac{Q_H - Q_L}{Q_H} = 1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H}$$

**Step 4:** By the **Second Law (Carnot's Theorem)**, the maximum possible efficiency is achieved by a reversible cycle:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{Q_L}{Q_H}\right)_{rev} &= \frac{T_L}{T_H} \\[6pt]
\therefore \quad \eta_{Carnot} &= 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Therefore, for any real engine, the **Carnot limit** acts as the theoretical upper bound.

$$\eta_{real} \le \eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Raising $T_H$ or lowering $T_L$ improves efficiency — this is why **combined-cycle plants** (gas + steam turbine, with $T_L$ effectively pushed below ambient via condensation) achieve 60%+ net efficiency, well beyond a single Rankine cycle.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — A KTU-Style Problem

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A boiler consumes 100 kg/hr of furnace oil (Gross Calorific Value, $GCV = 42$ MJ/kg) to produce 1200 kg/hr of steam from feedwater at 30 °C. The steam leaves at 8 bar, dry-saturated ($h_g = 2769$ kJ/kg). Ambient temperature = 25 °C. The flue gas leaves at 250 °C. Find:
> (a) Thermal efficiency of the boiler
> (b) Heat lost in flue gas (assume $c_p$ flue gas = 1.05 kJ/kg·K; mass of flue gas ≈ 1.05 × fuel mass)
> (c) Energy conservation potential

### Solution

**Step 1 — Energy Input:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{in} &= \dot{m}_{fuel} \times GCV \\[4pt]
&= 100 \;\text{kg/hr} \times 42\,000 \;\text{kJ/kg} \\[4pt]
&= 4\,200\,000 \;\text{kJ/hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Useful Energy Output (steam enthalpy rise):**

Feedwater at 30 °C: $h_f \approx 4.18 \times 30 = 125.4$ kJ/kg.
Steam enthalpy: $h_g = 2769$ kJ/kg.
Enthalpy gain per kg: $2769 - 125.4 = 2643.6$ kJ/kg.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{useful} &= 1200 \times 2643.6 \\[4pt]
&= 3\,172\,320 \;\text{kJ/hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Boiler Thermal Efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{boiler} &= \frac{\dot{Q}_{useful}}{\dot{Q}_{in}} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{3\,172\,320}{4\,200\,000} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= 75.53\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Flue-Gas Heat Loss:**

Mass of flue gas $\dot{m}_{fg} \approx 1.05 \times 100 = 105$ kg/hr.
Temperature rise above ambient: $\Delta T = 250 - 25 = 225$ K.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{flue} &= \dot{m}_{fg} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T \\[4pt]
&= 105 \times 1.05 \times 225 \\[4pt]
&= 24\,806.25 \;\text{kJ/hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Recovery Potential:**

If an economizer preheats the feedwater by capturing, say, 70% of flue-gas heat:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{recovered} &= 0.70 \times 24\,806.25 = 17\,364.4 \;\text{kJ/hr} \\[4pt]
\eta_{new} &= \frac{3\,172\,320 + 17\,364.4}{4\,200\,000} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&\approx 75.94\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> This incremental gain (≈ 0.4 percentage points in efficiency) appears small but corresponds to **measurable fuel and cost savings** — the very essence of energy conservation practice.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Energy Conservation Framework — Top-Level Block Diagram

The following **Mermaid block-level functional architecture flow** maps the hierarchical interaction of all principles, equations, and practices that constitute the Energy Conservation module.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[EC1: First Law of Thermodynamics] --> node2[Energy Balance Equation]
    node3[EC2: Second Law of Thermodynamics] --> node4[Entropy and Exergy Concepts]
    node2 --> node5[EC3: Energy Audit Methodology]
    node4 --> node5
    node5 --> node6[EC4: Identify Energy Streams]
    node6 --> node7[EC5: Quantify Losses]
    node7 --> node8[EC6: Apply Three R Strategy]
    node8 --> nodeA1[Reduce: Eliminate Waste]
    node8 --> nodeA2[Reuse: Recover Energy]
    node8 --> nodeA3[Replace: Adopt Efficient Tech]
    nodeA1 --> node9[EC7: Efficiency Improvement]
    nodeA2 --> node9
    nodeA3 --> node9
    node9 --> node10[EC8: Performance Evaluation]

    node10 --> node12[Compare with Baseline]
    node11 --> node13[Final Outcome]
    node12 --> node13
    node13[Output: Sustained Energy Savings and Reduced Emissions]
```

## 4.2 Energy Audit Sankey Logic Flow

```mermaid

    outA1[Useful Output] --> echo1[Productive Service]
    outA2[Stack Losses] --> echo2[Flue Gas Heat]
    outA3[Transmission Losses] --> echo3[Line and Duct Losses]
    outA4[Idle Losses] --> echo4[No Load Consumption]
    echo2 --> rec1[Heat Recovery Opportunity]
    echo3 --> rec2[Insulation Improvement]
    echo4 --> rec3[Automation and Scheduling]
```
