The remaining ~49% leaves as CO₂ plus a few percent locked in cell biomass. **Step 5 — Cross-check with CO₂ balance.** $$ \begin{aligned} m_{CO_2} &= 2 \times 44.01 = 88.02 \;\text{g} \\ Y_{CO_2/S} &= \dfrac{88.02}{180.16} = 0.489 \;\text{g g}^{-1} \end{aligned} $$ Mass balance closure: $0.511 + 0.489 = 1.000 \checkmark$ (neglecting biomass, which accounts for ~5% in practice). ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — First-Law Energy Efficiency **Step 1 — Define the energy efficiency as the ratio of energy recovered in ethanol to the energy input from glucose.** $$ \begin{aligned} \eta = \dfrac{E_{out,EtOH}}{E_{in,Gluc}} = \dfrac{m_{EtOH} \cdot LHV_{EtOH}}{m_{Gluc} \cdot LHV_{Gluc}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute standard LHV values.** $$ \begin{aligned} LHV_{EtOH} &= 26.9 \;\text{kJ g}^{-1} \\ LHV_{Gluc} &= 15.6 \;\text{kJ g}^{-1} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — For a 1-gram basis of glucose.** $$ \begin{aligned} m_{EtOH} &= 0.511 \;\text{g} \\ E_{out} &= 0.511 \times 26.9 = 13.75 \;\text{kJ} \\ E_{in} &= 1 \times 15.6 = 15.6 \;\text{kJ} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Compute efficiency.** $$ \begin{aligned} \eta = \dfrac{13.75}{15.6} = 0.881 \;\text{or}\; 88.1\% \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering insight:** Although the theoretical mass yield is 51.1%, the energy retention efficiency is **88.1%** because ethanol is a much more energy-dense molecule than glucose on a per-gram basis. This is the key reason why ethanol is an excellent liquid biofuel. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Dark Fermentation H₂ Yield **Step 1 — Write the acetate-route stoichiometry.** $$ \begin{aligned} C_6H_{12}O_6 + 2\,H_2O \;\longrightarrow\; 2\,CH_3COOH + 2\,CO_2 + 4\,H_2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compute the theoretical H₂ yield per mole of glucose.** From the equation, 1 mole glucose yields 4 moles H₂. $$ \begin{aligned} Y_{H_2/S} = \dfrac{4 \times 2.016}{180.16} = 0.0448 \;\text{g H}_2 \text{ per g glucose} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the energy content of produced H₂.** $$ \begin{aligned} E_{H_2} = 0.0448 \;\text{g} \times 142 \;\text{kJ g}^{-1} = 6.36 \;\text{kJ per g glucose} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Compare with glucose energy input.** $$ \begin{aligned} \eta_{H_2} = \dfrac{6.36}{15.6} = 40.8\% \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Result:** Even at the theoretical maximum of 4 mol H₂/mol glucose, only about **41%** of glucose energy is recovered as H₂. The rest is distributed between acetate, biomass, and other by-products. This is why dark fermentation is usually paired with a downstream methanogenic step. ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Mass Balance on a Steady-State CSTR Fermenter For a continuous stirred-tank reactor (CSTR) operating at steady state, the general mass balance on substrate is: $$ \begin{aligned} \underbrace{F_{S,in}}_{\text{in}} - \underbrace{F_{S,out}}_{\text{out}} + \underbrace{r_S \cdot V}_{\text{consumed}} = 0 \end{aligned} $$ where $r_S$ is the volumetric rate of substrate consumption (negative, since glucose is consumed). For steady-state operation with inlet substrate concentration $S_{in}$ and outlet $S_{out}$: $$ \begin{aligned} Q \cdot S_{in} - Q \cdot S_{out} + r_S \cdot V = 0 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 1 — Solve for the volumetric reaction rate.** $$ \begin{aligned} r_S = -\dfrac{Q\,(S_{in} - S_{out})}{V} = -D \cdot (S_{in} - S_{out}) \end{aligned} $$ where $D = Q/V$ is the dilution rate (h⁻¹). **Step 2 — Express conversion in terms of D.** $$ \begin{aligned} X_{conv} = \dfrac{S_{in} - S_{out}}{S_{in}} = \dfrac{-r_S}{D \cdot S_{in}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Apply Monod kinetics to substitute $r_S$.** With $r_S = -\dfrac{\mu \cdot X}{Y_{X/S}}$ and $\mu = \dfrac{\mu_{max} \, S}{K_S + S}$: $$ \begin{aligned} S_{out} = \dfrac{K_S \cdot D}{\mu_{max} - D} \end{aligned} $$ This critical equation lets the engineer size the reactor (set $D$) and predict the residual sugar in the outlet. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Fermentation Pathways Compared | Feature | Ethanol (Yeast) | Dark Ferm. (H₂) | ABE (Butanol) | Lactic Acid | |---|---|---|---|---| | Microorganism | *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* | *Clostridium butyricum* | *Clostridium acetobutylicum* | *Lactobacillus* spp. | | Optimum Temp. (°C) | 30–35 | 35–37 | 30–37 | 37–42 | | Optimum pH | 4.0–5.0 | 5.0–6.5 | 4.5–5.5 | 5.5–6.5 | | Theoretical Yield (g g⁻¹) | 0.511 | 0.0448 (H₂) | 0.207 (BuOH) | 1.000 (theor. lactic) | | Practical Yield (g g⁻¹) | 0.45–0.48 | 0.020–0.025 | 0.18–0.20 | 0.90–0.95 | | Energy Efficiency (%) | 85–90 | 30–40 | 75–85 | N/A (chem. feedstock) | | Main By-product | CO₂ | Acetate, butyrate | Acetone, ethanol | None | | Industrial Scale | Very High | Pilot | Pilot–Demo | High (PLA route) | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Ethanol Fermentation Plant ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Feedstock Storage
Rice straw or molasses] --> B[Pre-treatment
Grinding and washing] B --> C[Hydrolysis
Acid or enzymatic] C --> D[Sugar Solution
Glucose syrup C6H12O6] D --> E[Sterilization
Heat 121C 20 min] E --> F[Inoculum Tank
Saccharomyces cerevisiae] F --> G[Fermenter CSTR
30C anaerobic pH 4.5] D --> G G --> H[CO2 Vent
2 mol CO2 per mol glucose] G --> I[Beer Column
Ethanol water separation] I --> J[Rectification Column
Concentration to 95 percent] J --> K[Dehydration
Molecular sieve to 99.5 percent] K --> L[Bioethanol Product
E100 fuel grade] I --> M[Spent Mash
Solid residue] M --> N[Cogeneration Boiler
Process heat and power] ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Biohydrogen via Dark Fermentation ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Upstream_Block UA[Biomass Feed
Agricultural waste] --> UB[Pre-treatment
Size reduction] UB --> UC[Hydrolysis Reactor
Cellulose to glucose] end subgraph Fermentation_Block UC --> FA[Buffer Tank
pH 5.5 control] FA --> FB[Dark Fermenter CSTR
Clostridium sp 37C] FB --> FC[Gas Liquid Separator
H2 and CO2 capture] FB --> FD[Effluent Acetate Stream
Rich in volatile fatty acids] end subgraph Downstream_Block FC --> DA[H2 Purification
Pressure swing adsorption] DA --> DB[Compressed H2 Storage
350 bar or 700 bar] FD --> DC[Photo Fermenter or
Methanogenic Digester] DC --> DD[Secondary H2 or Biogas
Energy recovery] end subgraph Control_Block CA[SCADA and PLC
Process control system] --> FB CA --> FA CA --> DA end ``` ## 4.3 Microbial Metabolic Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Glucose C6H12O6
Enters Cytoplasm] --> B[Glycolysis
Net 2 ATP and 2 NADH] B --> C{Pyruvate
Fate} C -->|Yeast| D[Pyruvate Decarboxylase
Acetaldehyde] D --> E[Alcohol Dehydrogenase
NADH consumed] E --> F[Ethanol and CO2
Alcoholic Ferm] C -->|Lactic bacteria| G[Lactate Dehydrogenase
NADH consumed] G --> H[Lactic Acid
Homolactic Ferm] C -->|Clostridium| I[Pyruvate Ferredoxin
Oxidoreductase] I --> J[Acetyl CoA and CO2
Electrons to Ferredoxin] J --> K[Hydrogenase Enzyme
H2 release] K --> L[H2 and Acetate
Dark Ferm] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **"List any three industrially relevant fermentation pathways used in bioenergy production. For each, name the key microorganism and the main end-product."** **Model Answer:** 1. **Alcoholic fermentation** — *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* (yeast) → **ethanol + CO₂**. 2. **Dark fermentation** — *Clostridium butyricum* (anaerobic bacteria) → **biohydrogen + CO₂ + acetate**. 3. **ABE (Acetone–Butanol–Ethanol) fermentation** — *Clostridium acetobutylicum* → **butanol + acetone + ethanol**. Each pathway is industrially relevant: ethanol is a mainstream transport biofuel; biohydrogen is a clean fuel-cell feed; biobutanol is a gasoline substitute with superior properties. --- ### Question A2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **"Explain why fermentation is fundamentally an anaerobic process. What role does NAD⁺ regeneration play?"** **Model Answer:** Fermentation occurs in the **absence of external electron acceptors** like O₂. Without oxygen, the electron transport chain halts and the cell cannot regenerate NAD⁺ via oxidative phosphorylation. To sustain glycolysis (the only ATP source available), the cell must regenerate NAD⁺ by **transferring the electrons from NADH onto an organic intermediate** (pyruvate or its derivatives). The reduced organic compound — ethanol, lactate, acetate — is the end-product we harvest. Hence, NAD⁺ regeneration is the **driving purpose** of the fermentation step. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > KTU 2024 Scheme mandates **internal choice** between two Part-B questions. Both alternatives are provided below. --- ### Part B — Question A (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question]** **A. (a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the process of bioethanol production from sugarcane molasses via yeast fermentation. State the operating temperature, pH, and residence time. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Process Description (Step-by-Step):** 1. **Feedstock Preparation:** Sugarcane molasses (containing ~50% fermentable sugars, mostly sucrose) is diluted with water to a sugar concentration of 12–18% (w/v). 2. **Sterilization:** The diluted mash is pasteurized at 80–90°C for 30 minutes to eliminate contaminating microbes. 3. **pH Adjustment:** The mash pH is adjusted to **4.0–5.0** using sulfuric acid. 4. **Inoculation:** A starter culture of *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* (5–10% v/v) is added. 5. **Fermentation:** Conducted in large stainless-steel CSTRs under **anaerobic conditions**, at **30–35°C**, for **48–72 hours**. Yeast inverts sucrose to glucose + fructose, then ferments them to ethanol + CO₂ via glycolysis and pyruvate decarboxylation. 6. **Beer Stripping:** The fermented broth ("beer") contains 6–10% ethanol. It is sent to a distillation column where ethanol is concentrated to ~95% (azeotrope). 7. **Dehydration:** Molecular sieves (3Å zeolite) remove the residual water, producing anhydrous **99.5% bioethanol (E100)**. 8. **By-products:** The CO₂ vented from the fermenter is captured and sold for beverage carbonation or dry-ice production. **Operating Parameters (Tabulated):** | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | Temperature | 30–35°C | | pH | 4.0–5.0 | | Residence time | 48–72 h | | Inoculum size | 5–10% v/v | | Initial sugar | 12–18% (w/v) | | Final ethanol | 6–10% v/v in broth | | Final product | 99.5% (anhydrous) | --- **A. (b)** For alcoholic fermentation of glucose, derive the theoretical ethanol yield (g g⁻¹) and the corresponding first-law energy efficiency. Take LHV of ethanol = 26.9 kJ g⁻¹ and LHV of glucose = 15.6 kJ g⁻¹. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Balanced reaction.** $$ \begin{aligned} C_6H_{12}O_6 \;\longrightarrow\; 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Molar masses.** $$ \begin{aligned} M_{Gluc} &= 180.16 \;\text{g mol}^{-1} \\ M_{EtOH} &= 46.07 \;\text{g mol}^{-1} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Theoretical mass yield.** For 1 mole glucose → 2 moles ethanol: $$ \begin{aligned} Y_{E/S} = \dfrac{2 \times 46.07}{180.16} = 0.511 \;\text{g g}^{-1} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Energy balance per gram of glucose.** $$ \begin{aligned} E_{out} &= 0.511 \times 26.9 = 13.75 \;\text{kJ} \\ E_{in} &= 1.000 \times 15.6 = 15.60 \;\text{kJ} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — First-law efficiency.** $$ \begin{aligned} \eta = \dfrac{13.75}{15.60} = 0.881 = 88.1\% \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** Theoretical mass yield is **51.1%** and energy efficiency is **88.1%**. In practice, yields drop to 90–95% of theoretical due to cell biomass formation (~5%) and minor side products (glycerol, organic acids). --- ### Part B — Question B (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model Question]** **B. (a)** Explain dark fermentation for biohydrogen production. Discuss the acetate and butyrate pathways and derive the maximum theoretical H₂ yield (mol H₂ per mol glucose). **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Concept:** Dark fermentation is an **anaerobic process** in which facultative or obligate anaerobic bacteria (e.g., *Clostridium butyricum*, *Enterobacter aerogenes*) degrade sugars to H₂, CO₂, and volatile fatty acids (VFAs) — primarily acetate and butyrate. **Pathway 1 — Acetate-Dominant Route:** $$ \begin{aligned} C_6H_{12}O_6 + 2\,H_2O \;\longrightarrow\; 2\,CH_3COOH + 2\,CO_2 + 4\,H_2 \end{aligned} $$ This yields **4 mol H₂ per mol glucose**. The high H₂ yield is favored thermodynamically (ΔG°' = −184 kJ per reaction). **Pathway 2 — Butyrate-Dominant Route:** $$ \begin{aligned} C_6H_{12}O_6 \;\longrightarrow\; CH_3CH_2CH_2COOH + 2\,CO_2 + 2\,H_2 \end{aligned} $$ This yields only **2 mol H₂ per mol glucose**. **Derivation of Maximum Theoretical H₂ Yield:** Step 1 — Glucose oxidation level: each C is in oxidation state 0 (in C₆H₁₂O₆). Step 2 — In acetate (CH₃COOH), each C has an average oxidation state of 0 (CH₃ carbon is −3, COOH carbon is +3, average = 0). So glucose carbon oxidation state doesn't change in the acetate pathway, but H₂O provides electrons: $$ \begin{aligned} C_6H_{12}O_6 + 2\,H_2O \;\longrightarrow\; 2\,CH_3COOH + 2\,CO_2 + 4\,H_2 \end{aligned} $$ Step 3 — Maximum H₂ yield = **4 mol H₂ per mol glucose** (acetate route). Step 4 — If only butyrate is formed: yield drops to **2 mol H₂ per mol glucose**. Step 5 — Mixed cultures typically achieve **2.5–3.5 mol H₂/mol glucose**. Step 6 — Mass-based yield: $$ \begin{aligned} Y_{H_2/S} = \dfrac{4 \times 2.016}{180.16} = 0.0448 \;\text{g H}_2 \text{ per g glucose} \end{aligned} $$ **Operating Conditions:** | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | Temperature | 35–37°C (mesophilic) | | pH | 5.0–6.5 | | HRT | 12–24 h | | Inoculum | Heat-treated mixed culture | --- **B. (b)** Compare alcoholic fermentation with ABE fermentation. State the key microorganism, end-products, theoretical yields, and one industrial advantage of each. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** | Aspect | Alcoholic Fermentation | ABE Fermentation | |---|---|---| | Microorganism | *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* | *Clostridium acetobutylicum* | | Substrate | Glucose, sucrose | Glucose, starch | | Main product | Ethanol | Butanol (with acetone + ethanol) | | By-products | CO₂ | Acetone, ethanol, CO₂, H₂ | | Theoretical yield | 0.511 g EtOH / g glucose | 0.207 g BuOH / g glucose | | Optimum pH | 4.0–5.0 | 4.5–5.5 (acidogenic); 4.0–4.5 (solventogenic) | | Optimum temp | 30–35°C | 30–37°C | | Industrial use | Fuel ethanol, beverages | Biobutanol as gasoline additive | | Advantage | Mature technology, high yield | Butanol has higher energy density (33 MJ/kg vs 27 MJ/kg for EtOH), lower water solubility, miscible with gasoline | **Key Advantage:** - **Ethanol process:** Established at massive scale; 51.1% theoretical yield translates to high productivity. - **ABE process:** Biobutanol is a **superior gasoline substitute** — less hygroscopic, can be shipped via existing pipelines, and has higher energy density. **Stoichiometry of ABE:** $$ \begin{aligned} C_6H_{12}O_6 \;\longrightarrow\; C_4H_9OH + CH_3COOH + C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2 + H_2O \end{aligned} $$ Butanol molar mass = 74.12 g mol⁻¹; theoretical mass yield: $$ \begin{aligned} Y_{BuOH/S} = \dfrac{74.12}{180.16} = 0.411 \;\text{g g}^{-1} \text{ (BuOH + co-products basis)} \end{aligned} $$ Considering only butanol from the above stoichiometry (1 mol BuOH per mol glucose): $$ \begin{aligned} Y_{BuOH/S} = \dfrac{74.12}{180.16} = 0.411 \;\text{g g}^{-1} \end{aligned} $$ (Industry reports cite ~0.20–0.25 g g⁻¹ in practice due to co-product formation.) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Forgetting units in stoichiometric calculations** — always state g g⁻¹, mol mol⁻¹, or %. > 2. **Confusing LHV with HHV** — for ethanol combustion calculations, the question typically expects LHV = 26.9 kJ/g. > 3. **Omitting biomass correction** — practical yields are 90–95% of theoretical; mention this to show industrial awareness. > 4. **Mixing up fermentation with anaerobic digestion** — fermentation is a *microbial enzymatic* process producing alcohols/acids/H₂; anaerobic digestion is a *multi-stage microbial* process terminating in CH₄. > 5. **Skipping the CO₂ mass-balance check** — always verify that mass in = mass out + biomass in numerical problems. > 6. **Neglecting to draw a labeled block diagram** — KTU examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for neat flow diagrams in 7-mark sub-questions. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before the Exam:** - ✅ Fermentation = anaerobic, enzyme-catalyzed conversion of sugars to alcohols/acids/H₂. - ✅ Key redox driver = **NAD⁺ regeneration** via reduction of pyruvate or its derivatives. - ✅ Three principal reactions to memorize: - Ethanol: $C_6H_{12}O_6 \to 2C_2H_5OH + 2CO_2$ - Dark fermentation (acetate): $C_6H_{12}O_6 + 2H_2O \to 2CH_3COOH + 2CO_2 + 4H_2$ - Lactic acid: $C_6H_{12}O_6 \to 2C_3H_6O_3$ - ✅ Theoretical ethanol yield = **0.511 g g⁻¹** (51.1% by mass). - ✅ Energy efficiency (1st law) for ethanol = **88.1%**. - ✅ Max H₂ yield = **4 mol H₂ per mol glucose** (acetate route). - ✅ LHV glucose = 15.6 kJ g⁻¹; LHV ethanol = 26.9 kJ g⁻¹; HHV H₂ = 142 MJ kg⁻¹. - ✅ Operating window for yeast: 30–35°C, pH 4.0–5.0, anaerobic. - ✅ Operating window for *Clostridium* (dark ferm.): 35–37°C, pH 5.0–6.5. - ✅ Monod kinetics: $\mu = \mu_{max} S / (K_S + S)$. - ✅ CSTR substrate outlet: $S_{out} = K_S D / (\mu_{max} - D)$. - ✅ Industrial advantage of butanol: higher energy density and lower hygroscopicity than ethanol. - ✅ By-products of alcoholic fermentation: CO₂ (captured industrially); of ABE: acetone + ethanol + CO₂ + H₂O. - ✅ Fermentation vs. anaerobic digestion: fermentation → alcohols/acids/H₂; AD → CH₄ + CO₂. > **Final Exam Tip from Senior Examiners:** When a numerical problem asks for a yield or efficiency, *always show all five calculation steps explicitly*: (1) write the balanced equation, (2) state molar masses, (3) compute product moles, (4) compute mass yield, (5) compute efficiency with units. KTU valuation rewards stepwise clarity, not just the final number.

topics.title: Anaerobic Digestion: Compressed Biogas. Energy Conservation Opportunities - Electrical ECOs
topics.content: > **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBBT614 - ENERGY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Anaerobic Digestion: Compressed Biogas. Energy Conservation Opportunities - Electrical ECOs # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Compressed Biogas (CBG) — Formal Definition **Compressed Biogas (CBG)** is a purified and pressurized form of biogas, obtained by removing impurities such as **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)**, **Hydrogen Sulfide (H₂S)**, water vapor, and siloxanes from raw biogas, followed by compression to high pressure (typically **200 bar to 250 bar**). The purified gas contains approximately **92 % to 98 % Methane (CH₄)** by volume, making it equivalent in performance and composition to Compressed Natural Gas (CNG), and suitable for use as an automotive fuel, industrial fuel, or feedstock for chemical synthesis. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** CBG is biomethane compressed to a high pressure (≥ 200 bar) to reduce storage volume and enable transport and direct use as a substitute for CNG/LPG. The Methane number should be ≥ 65 as per **IS 16087:2016**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cleaning and Canning" of Biogas Think of raw biogas as a freshly squeezed fruit juice that contains pulp, water, and bitter compounds. - **Purification** is like filtering the juice through a strainer (removing CO₂, H₂S, moisture) so that only the sweet essence (pure methane) remains. - **Compression** is like bottling that filtered juice into a small, pressurized can so it occupies less space and can be transported to distant markets. Just as a pressurized soda bottle can carry more fizz (gas) in less space, CBG packs enormous energy into a small cylinder. ### 1.3 Composition of Raw Biogas vs. CBG | Constituent | Raw Biogas (Vol %) | Compressed Biogas (Vol %) | |-------------|-------------------|---------------------------| | Methane (CH₄) | 55 – 70 | **≥ 92** (Target: 95 – 98) | | Carbon Dioxide (CO₂) | 30 – 40 | < 4 | | Hydrogen Sulfide (H₂S) | 0.1 – 0.5 % | < 5 mg/m³ | | Water Vapor | Saturated | < 30 mg/m³ | | Siloxanes / Silicones | Trace | < 0.03 mg/m³ | | Nitrogen (N₂) | < 2 | < 2 | | Oxygen (O₂) | < 1 | < 1 | > [!NOTE] > **Methane Number (MN)** of CBG is typically **80 – 95**, very close to that of CNG (MN ≈ 80 – 85). The minimum threshold of MN ≥ 65 ensures resistance to engine knock in spark-ignition engines. --- ## 1.4 Electrical Energy Conservation Opportunities (ECOs) — Formal Definition **Electrical Energy Conservation Opportunities (ECOs)** are systematic, technically feasible, and economically viable measures implemented in industrial, commercial, and institutional electrical systems to **reduce electricity consumption**, **improve power quality**, and **lower energy costs** while maintaining or enhancing the operational output and service quality. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Context:** Electrical ECOs fall under Module 4 of Energy Engineering & Management, and are critical for industries covered under the **Perform, Achieve, and Trade (PAT) Scheme** of the Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE), Government of India. ### 1.5 Conceptual Analogy — "Tightening the Leaky Bucket" Imagine your factory's electrical system is a large bucket full of water (electrical energy). The bucket has many small holes — leaky motors running on full speed at all times, lights burning in empty rooms, capacitors not working, poor power factor. Electrical ECOs are like **plugging those leaks** so you retain more of what you paid for, and therefore need to pour in less water (purchase less electricity) to keep the bucket full. > [!NOTE] > The motto of every electrical ECO is the **3E Principle: Energy saved = Energy generated (cheaper, cleaner, immediate).** ]<]minimax[>[ # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Compressed Biogas (CBG) — Process Architecture The conversion of raw biogas to CBG involves **four sequential unit operations** (post anaerobic digestion): ### Stage 1: Raw Biogas Pretreatment - **Cooling / Chilling** to below 5 °C to condense water vapor (dehumidification). - **Iron-oxide / activated-carbon polishing bed** to remove trace H₂S that escapes H₂S scrubbers. - **Siloxane removal** via activated carbon (critical — siloxanes form abrasive silica deposits on engine pistons). ### Stage 2: Biogas Upgrading (CO₂ Removal) — Four Main Technologies | Technology | Principle | CH₄ Purity | CH₄ Loss | Energy Demand (kWh/kg CH₄) | |------------|-----------|------------|----------|------------------------------| | **Pressure Swing Adsorption (PSA)** | Adsorption of CO₂ on zeolite/carbon molecular sieve at high pressure; regeneration at low pressure. | **95 – 98 %** | < 2 % | **0.20 – 0.25** | | **Water Scrubbing** | CO₂ is more soluble in water than CH₄. Counter-current contact. | 95 – 98 % | < 1 % | 0.25 – 0.30 | | **Chemical Scrubbing (Amine)** | CO₂ reacts with mono-ethanol-amine (MEA) or MDEA. | 99 % | < 0.1 % | 0.30 – 0.50 | | **Membrane Separation** | Selective permeation of CO₂ through polymeric membrane. | 92 – 96 % | 5 – 10 % | 0.20 – 0.35 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **PSA is the most widely adopted technology in India** under the SATAT (Sustainable Alternative Towards Affordable Transportation) scheme, due to its modular design, low methane slip, and operating cost advantage at small-to-medium scale (< 20 TPD feedstock). ### Stage 3: Compression - **Single-stage compressors** are used for low-pressure applications (boiler fuel: 4 – 7 bar). - **Multi-stage reciprocating compressors (3 – 4 stages)** with **intercoolers** are used for CBG dispensing (200 – 250 bar). - Inter-stage cooling reduces the **compressor work per kg of gas** and the **discharge temperature** (critical for safety — auto-ignition of CH₄ is 537 °C). ### Stage 4: Storage and Dispensing - Storage cascades of CNG-type cylinders (Type 1 steel, Type 2 hoop-wrapped, Type 3 fully-wrapped composite) at **250 bar**. - Dispensing pressure: **200 bar** for light vehicles, **250 bar** for heavy-duty. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Compressed Biogas The **compressor work** (ideal, isothermal compression) per kg of gas is given by: $$W_{iso} = R \cdot T \cdot \ln \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)$$ For **polytropic compression** (real compressor): $$W_{poly} = \frac{n}{n-1} \cdot R \cdot T_1 \cdot \left[ \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1 \right]$$ For a **multi-stage compressor with intercooling**, the optimum pressure ratio per stage is: $$r_{opt} = \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{\frac{1}{N_s}}$$ where $N_s$ is the number of stages and the **minimum total compression work** becomes: $$W_{multi-stage} = N_s \cdot \frac{n}{n-1} \cdot R \cdot T_1 \cdot \left[ r_{opt}^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1 \right]$$ The **volume reduction** upon compression is governed by the **Real Gas Law** (Peng-Robinson EOS), but approximated by: $$\frac{V_1}{V_2} = \frac{P_2}{P_1} \cdot \frac{Z_2}{Z_1}$$ For a typical **energy density** comparison: | Fuel | Energy Density (MJ/m³ at NTP) | Energy Density (MJ/kg) | |------|--------------------------------|-------------------------| | Raw Biogas | 18 – 22 | 35 – 50 | | CBG (250 bar, 95 % CH₄) | ~ 8 900 (as compressed gas) | 50 – 55 | | CNG (250 bar) | ~ 9 000 | 50 – 52 | | LNG (–162 °C) | ~ 22 000 | 50 | | Diesel (liquid) | — | 45 | > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** CBG at 200 bar stores roughly **200× the energy per unit volume** compared to atmospheric biogas, making pipeline-free distribution feasible. ### 2.3 Energy Yield from CBG The **calorific value of CBG** is given by: $$CV_{CBG} = 0.95 \times 55.5 \approx 52.7 \; \text{MJ/kg}$$ (assuming 95 % CH₄ purity, with the standard calorific value of pure CH₄ = 55.5 MJ/kg). --- ## 2.4 Electrical ECOs — Engineering Categories The **eight pillars of electrical energy conservation** in industry: 1. **Energy-Efficient Motors** (replacing IE1 with IE3/IE4/IE5 class induction motors). 2. **Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs)** on pumps, fans, blowers, compressors. 3. **Power Factor Correction (PFC)** using capacitor banks / APFC panels. 4. **Lighting Retrofits** (CFL → LED, electronic ballasts, occupancy sensors, daylight harvesting). 5. **Maximum Demand (MD) Control** and load scheduling. 6. **Transformer Efficiency Upgradation** (amorphous metal core, CRGO M4/M5 grade). 7. **Harmonic Mitigation** using active/passive filters, K-rated transformers. 8. **Power Quality Improvement** (voltage optimization, soft starters). --- ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Electrical ECOs ### 2.5.1 Power, Energy, and Power Factor The **three-phase real power** is: $$P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$$ The **apparent power** is: $$S = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \; \; [\text{kVA}]$$ The **reactive power** is: $$Q = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \sin\phi \; \; [\text{kVAR}]$$ The **power factor** is: $$\cos\phi = \frac{P}{S}$$ The **capacitor kVAR required for PF correction** from $\cos\phi_1$ to $\cos\phi_2$ is: $$Q_c = P \cdot \left[ \tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2 \right] \; \; [\text{kVAR}]$$ > [!NOTE] > **Penalty Avoidance:** Most state electricity boards in India (KSEB in Kerala) levy **PF penalty** when the average power factor drops below **0.90 (lagging)**. Improving PF from 0.85 to 0.95 typically reduces the electricity bill by **8 – 12 %**. ### 2.5.2 Energy-Efficient Motor Savings The **annual energy saving** from replacing a standard motor with a high-efficiency motor is: $$\Delta E = \left( \eta_{new} - \eta_{old} \right) \cdot P_{out} \cdot t_{op} \; \; [\text{kWh/year}]$$ The **simple payback period** is: $$PP = \frac{\text{Extra cost of IE3 motor}}{\Delta E \cdot \text{Unit cost of electricity}}$$ ### 2.5.3 VFD Energy Savings (Affinity Laws) For **centrifugal pumps and fans**, the power varies as the cube of the speed ratio: $$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left( \frac{N_2}{N_1} \right)^3$$ The flow rate is: $$\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}$$ The pressure (head) developed is: $$\frac{H_2}{H_1} = \left( \frac{N_2}{N_1} \right)^2$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **VFD Insight:** Reducing a fan speed to 80 % by a VFD cuts the power consumption to **(0.8)³ = 51.2 %**, a saving of **~ 49 %** — one of the highest ROI electrical ECOs available in HVAC and process industries. ### 2.5.4 Harmonic Distortion (THD) The **Total Harmonic Distortion** of current is: $$THD_i = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{H} I_h^2}}{I_1} \times 100 \; \; [\%]$$ > [!NOTE] > **IEEE 519 Standard** limits the THD in voltage at the point of common coupling (PCC) to **5 %** for general power systems, and current THD to **8 %** for h < 11. ]<]minimax[>[ # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Compression Work for a Multi-Stage Reciprocating Compressor with Intercooling to Initial Temperature ### 3.1.1 Statement Show that for a multi-stage compressor with perfect intercooling (i.e., gas temperature after each intercooler is returned to the inlet temperature $T_1$), the **optimum pressure ratio per stage is the same for all stages**, and the total work is minimum when: $$r_{opt} = \left( \frac{P_N}{P_1} \right)^{\frac{1}{N_s}}$$ ### 3.1.2 Solution **Step 1 — Single-stage polytropic work** The work input to compress 1 kg of gas polytropically from $P_1$ to $P_2$ is: $$w_1 = \frac{n}{n-1} \cdot R \cdot T_1 \cdot \left[ \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1 \right]$$ **Step 2 — Generalization to Ns stages with intercooling** If $N_s$ stages compress from $P_1$ to $P_2 = P_{N+1}$, with intermediate pressures $P_2, P_3, \ldots, P_{N_s}$, the total work is: $$W_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_s} \frac{n}{n-1} \cdot R \cdot T_1 \cdot \left[ \left( \frac{P_{i+1}}{P_i} \right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1 \right]$$ **Step 3 — Constraint: Product of pressure ratios** The product of the stage pressure ratios must equal the overall ratio: $$\prod_{i=1}^{N_s} \frac{P_{i+1}}{P_i} = \frac{P_{N_s+1}}{P_1} = r_{overall}$$ Let $r_i = \dfrac{P_{i+1}}{P_i}$. Then $\prod_{i=1}^{N_s} r_i = r_{overall}$. **Step 4 — Define a function and apply AM–GM inequality** Let $a = \frac{n-1}{n}$. We seek the minimum of: $$W_{total} = K \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{N_s} (r_i^a - 1)$$ subject to $\prod r_i = r_{overall}$, where $K = \frac{n}{n-1} R T_1$. Using the **AM–GM inequality**: $$\frac{1}{N_s} \sum_{i=1}^{N_s} r_i^a \geq \left( \prod_{i=1}^{N_s} r_i \right)^{\frac{a}{N_s}} = (r_{overall})^{\frac{a}{N_s}}$$ Equality holds when **all $r_i$ are equal**, i.e., $r_1 = r_2 = \cdots = r_{N_s} = r_{opt}$. **Step 5 — Result for optimum stage pressure ratio** Therefore: $$r_{opt} = \left( \frac{P_{N+1}}{P_1} \right)^{\frac{1}{N_s}}$$ **Step 6 — Minimum total compression work** Substituting into the work equation: $$W_{min} = N_s \cdot \frac{n}{n-1} \cdot R \cdot T_1 \cdot \left[ r_{opt}^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1 \right]$$ This is the **classic minimum-work result** for a perfect intercooled multi-stage compressor (proven in all thermodynamic textbooks, e.g., Spalding & Cole, *Engineering Thermodynamics*, 1958 ed., Chapter on Reciprocating Compressors — derivations are universally accepted; not specific edition cited). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Practical Implication:** A 4-stage CBG compressor compressing biogas from **1.013 bar to 200 bar** has $r_{opt} = (200/1.013)^{1/4} \approx 3.76$. The inter-stage pressures are therefore approximately **3.76, 14.1, 53.0, 200 bar**. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — CBG Energy Recovery from Biogas Plant A dairy waste-based biogas plant produces **1 000 m³/day of raw biogas** at NTP. After upgrading to CBG (95 % CH₄) and compression, calculate the daily CBG energy output and the equivalent diesel displaced. ### Given - Daily raw biogas = 1 000 m³/day at 1.01325 bar, 25 °C (298 K) - CH₄ in raw biogas = 60 % (by volume) - CH₄ recovery in PSA = 96 % - Final CBG pressure = 200 bar ### Solution **Step 1 — Moles of CH₄ in raw biogas** Using ideal gas law, total moles of gas per day: $$n_{total} = \frac{P \cdot V}{R \cdot T} = \frac{101325 \times 1000}{8.314 \times 298} \approx 40\,890 \; \text{mol/day}$$ **Step 2 — Moles of CH₄ input** $$n_{CH_4} = 0.60 \times 40\,890 = 24\,534 \; \text{mol/day}$$ **Step 3 — Mass of CH₄** With M(CH₄) = 16 g/mol: $$m_{CH_4} = 24\,534 \times 16 = 392\,544 \; \text{g/day} = 392.5 \; \text{kg/day}$$ **Step 4 — Mass of CBG (95 % purity by volume, equivalent to mass basis for this problem)** $$m_{CBG} = 0.96 \times 392.5 = 376.8 \; \text{kg/day}$$ **Step 5 — Energy content of CBG** $$E_{CBG} = 376.8 \; \text{kg/day} \times 52.7 \; \text{MJ/kg} = 19\,857 \; \text{MJ/day}$$ $$E_{CBG} = \frac{19\,857}{3.6} \approx 5\,516 \; \text{kWh/day}$$ **Step 6 — Diesel equivalent** Diesel calorific value = 45 MJ/kg, density 0.85 kg/L. Energy per litre of diesel = $45 \times 0.85 = 38.25$ MJ/L. $$\text{Diesel displaced} = \frac{19\,857}{38.25} \approx 519 \; \text{L/day}$$ **Step 7 — Annual diesel displacement** $$\approx 519 \times 365 \approx 1.89 \times 10^5 \; \text{L/year} \approx 189.4 \; \text{kL/year}$$ > [!NOTE] > At a market price of ₹95 / litre of diesel (indicative, 2024 Kerala prices), the **annual diesel offset cost** is approximately **₹1.8 crore/year** — illustrating the strong economics of even a 1 TPD CBG plant under the SATAT scheme. --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Power Factor Correction and Annual Savings A 500 kW industrial load operates at an average lagging power factor of **0.78**. The plant operates **6 000 hours/year** at a tariff of **₹7.50 / kVAh (KSEB HT tariff slab)**, with a PF penalty clause below 0.90. Calculate the kVAR of capacitor bank required to improve PF to 0.95 and the annual savings. ### Solution **Step 1 — Original kVA and kVAR** $$S_1 = \frac{P}{\cos\phi_1} = \frac{500}{0.78} = 641.0 \; \text{kVA}$$ $$Q_1 = P \cdot \tan\phi_1 = 500 \times \tan(\cos^{-1} 0.78)$$ $\cos^{-1}(0.78) \approx 38.7°$, so $\tan(38.7°) \approx 0.802$. $$Q_1 = 500 \times 0.802 = 401.0 \; \text{kVAR}$$ **Step 2 — New kVAR at PF = 0.95** $\cos^{-1}(0.95) \approx 18.2°$, $\tan(18.2°) \approx 0.329$. $$Q_2 = 500 \times 0.329 = 164.5 \; \text{kVAR}$$ **Step 3 — Capacitor bank rating** $$Q_c = Q_1 - Q_2 = 401.0 - 164.5 = 236.5 \; \text{kVAR}$$ **Step 4 — New kVA after correction** $$S_2 = \frac{500}{0.95} = 526.3 \; \text{kVA}$$ **Step 5 — Annual billing demand (kVA) reduction** $$\Delta S = 641.0 - 526.3 = 114.7 \; \text{kVA}$$ **Step 6 — Annual monetary savings** $$\text{Savings} = \Delta S \times \text{hours} \times \text{tariff} = 114.7 \times 6\,000 \times 7.50$$ $$\text{Savings} = 5.16 \times 10^6 \approx ₹51.6 \; \text{lakh/year}$$ **Step 7 — Cost of 236.5 kVAR capacitor bank (indicative ₹350 / kVAR)** $$C = 236.5 \times 350 \approx ₹82\,800$$ **Step 8 — Simple payback** $$PP = \frac{82\,800}{51.6 \times 10^5} \times 12 \approx 0.19 \; \text{months} \approx 6 \; \text{days}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Tip:** PF correction is one of the most lucrative electrical ECOs in Kerala industries (KSEB tariff is high) — expect this as a frequent 14-mark question. --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — VFD Energy Savings on a Centrifugal Pump A centrifugal pump delivers **Q₁ = 100 m³/h** against **H₁ = 30 m**, driven by a **15 kW motor** at **N₁ = 1 450 rpm** for 6 000 hours/year. A VFD is proposed to reduce speed to **80 %** to match the new process demand of 80 m³/h. Compare the annual energy consumption with and without VFD. Assume the system curve is such that the pump head is proportional to $N^2$. ### Solution **Step 1 — Original power input** $$P_1 = 15 \; \text{kW (assumed equal to rated, for baseline)}$$ **Step 2 — New speed** $$N_2 = 0.80 \times 1450 = 1160 \; \text{rpm}$$ **Step 3 — New flow (affinity law)** $$Q_2 = 100 \times 0.80 = 80 \; \text{m}^3/\text{h} \;\checkmark$$ **Step 4 — New head** $$H_2 = 30 \times (0.80)^2 = 19.2 \; \text{m}$$ **Step 5 — New power (affinity law, cube law)** $$P_2 = 15 \times (0.80)^3 = 15 \times 0.512 = 7.68 \; \text{kW}$$ **Step 6 — Annual energy with VFD (assuming 95 % VFD efficiency)** $$E_{VFD} = \frac{7.68}{0.95} \times 6\,000 = 48\,505 \; \text{kWh/year}$$ **Step 7 — Annual energy without VFD (throttle-valve control, assumed constant 15 kW)** $$E_{throttle} = 15 \times 6\,000 = 90\,000 \; \text{kWh/year}$$ **Step 8 — Energy saved** $$\Delta E = 90\,000 - 48\,505 = 41\,495 \; \text{kWh/year}$$ **Step 9 — Monetary savings at ₹8 / kWh** $$\text{Savings} = 41\,495 \times 8 \approx ₹3.32 \; \text{lakh/year}$$ **Step 10 — VFD cost (indicative ₹12 000 for a 15 kW VFD)** $$PP = \frac{12\,000}{3.32 \times 10^5} \times 12 \approx 0.43 \; \text{months} \approx 13 \; \text{days}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Reality Check:** Real-world pump/ fan VFD retrofits in Kerala MSMEs typically yield **payback periods of 4 – 18 months**, depending on load profile and tariff structure. This is the highest-impact electrical ECO in process industries. ]<]minimax[>[ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — CBG Production Chain from Biomass to Cylinder ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Biomass Feedstock - cow dung - food waste - press mud] --> B[Anaerobic Digester - 35 to 55 deg C - 20 to 40 days HRT] B --> C[Raw Biogas 60 percent CH4 40 percent CO2 saturated H2O] C --> D[Cooler Dehumidifier - 5 deg C] D --> E[H2S Scrubber Iron Oxide Bed] E --> F[Siloxane Removal Activated Carbon] F --> G[CO2 Removal - PSA Unit - 95 to 98 percent CH4] G --> H[Polishing Filter - activated carbon] H --> I[Compressor Stage 1 - 1 bar to 4 bar] I --> J[Intercooler 1 - back to 25 deg C] J --> K[Compressor Stage 2 - 4 bar to 16 bar] K --> L[Intercooler 2 - back to 25 deg C] L --> M[Compressor Stage 3 - 16 bar to 60 bar] M --> N[Intercooler 3 - back to 25 deg C] N --> O[Compressor Stage 4 - 60 bar to 250 bar] O --> P[CBG Storage Cascade Type 1 cylinders - 250 bar] P --> Q[Dispenser - 200 bar to vehicle - bus - three wheeler] P --> R[Pipeline to PNG network - domestic] P --> S[Industrial Boiler Burner] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** Each block represents a unit operation. The block diagram is read left-to-right. Saturation of arrows indicates mass flow; temperature and pressure of gas streams are shown only at critical control points (digestion, post-cooler, post-compressor). --- ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Electrical Energy Audit Approach & ECO Identification ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Energy Audit Entry Point] --> B1[Walk-through Audit - identify obvious losses] B1 --> C1[Detailed Measurement - power analyser - clamp meter - lux meter] C1 --> D1[Load Survey - identify motors - lighting - HVAC - compressors] D1 --> E1[Quantify Losses - idle running - poor PF - harmonics - over-rated equipment] E1 --> F1[Match Equipment to Load - right-sizing of motors - trim of impellers] F1 --> G1[Identify ECOs - VFD - IE3 motors - capacitor bank - LED retrofit - harmonic filter] G1 --> H1[Economic Analysis - NPV - IRR - payback period] H1 --> I1[Implementation - prioritized based on ROI and technical feasibility] I1 --> J1[Monitoring and Verification M and V - ISO 50015] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Visual Aid Insight:** The above block diagram represents the **ISO 50002 Energy Audit Methodology** as recommended by BEE (Bureau of Energy Efficiency, Government of India) for Designated Consumers (DCs) under the PAT scheme. --- ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Hierarchy of Electrical ECOs in Industry ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Top Priority - High ROI - 0 to 12 months payback] --> A2[Switch off idle equipment] A1 --> A3[Power factor correction to above 0.95] A1 --> A4[Lighting retrofit to LED with occupancy sensors] B1[Medium Priority - 6 to 24 months] --> B2[Variable Frequency Drives on pumps fans blowers] B1 --> B3[Replacement of standard motors with IE3 IE4 class] C1[Long-term - above 24 months] --> C2[Harmonic filters for VFD clusters] C1 --> C3[Maximum demand controller with smart load scheduling] C1 --> C4[Captive renewable - solar PV to offset daytime HT demand] C1 --> C5[Amorphous core transformer for distribution] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Strategic Insight:** A well-structured Electrical ECO program in a medium-scale Kerala industry (e.g., 1 MW connected load) typically achieves a **15 – 25 % reduction in annual electricity cost** through implementation across all three tiers of the above hierarchy. ]<]minimax[>[ # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP Set B] **What is Compressed Biogas (CBG)? How is it different from raw biogas?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Compressed Biogas (CBG) is the purified and pressurized form of raw biogas containing **≥ 92 % methane (CH₄)** by volume, obtained by removing CO₂, H₂S, water vapor, and siloxanes, and compressed to **200 – 250 bar** for use as an automotive or industrial fuel. (1 mark) Differences from raw biogas: (1) Raw biogas contains 55 – 70 % CH₄ and 30 – 40 % CO₂; CBG contains ≥ 92 % CH₄ and < 4 % CO₂. (1 mark) (2) Raw biogas is at near-atmospheric pressure; CBG is at 200 – 250 bar for compact storage. (1 mark) --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, QP Code OEBBT614] **List any three electrical energy conservation opportunities commonly adopted in Indian industries.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Power factor correction** by installing capacitor banks / APFC panels to maintain PF ≥ 0.95 (1 mark) 2. **Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs)** on centrifugal pumps, fans, and blowers to enable speed-based load matching (1 mark) 3. **Replacement of standard efficiency motors with IE3/IE4 class** energy-efficient induction motors (1 mark) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A — Compressed Biogas [14 marks] **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]** **(a)** Describe the **process flow of Compressed Biogas production** from raw biogas, with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of **PSA (Pressure Swing Adsorption)** in CO₂ removal. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Introduction (1 mark):** CBG is biomethane compressed to ≥ 200 bar, with CH₄ purity ≥ 92 %, suitable for use as automotive fuel (substitute for CNG) and industrial fuel, as per IS 16087:2016. **Process Flow (4 marks):** The unit operations are: 1. **Pretreatment:** Cooling/dehumidification (5 °C), H₂S scrubbing (iron oxide bed), siloxane removal (activated carbon). 2. **CO₂ Removal (Upgrading):** Primarily by PSA. Biogas is compressed to 4 – 8 bar and passed through a bed of zeolite 13X or activated carbon. CO₂ is selectively adsorbed; CH₄ passes through. On pressure swing (regeneration at ~ 1 bar), CO₂ is desorbed and vented. Two or more beds operate in cyclic mode for continuous flow. 3. **Compression:** Multi-stage reciprocating compressor (3 – 4 stages) with intercooling, compressing the upgraded gas from ~ 7 bar to 250 bar. 4. **Storage and Dispensing:** Stored in high-pressure cascade cylinders; dispensed at 200 bar (light vehicles) or 250 bar (heavy vehicles). **Role of PSA (2 marks):** - Achieves **CH₄ purity 95 – 98 %** with **CH₄ loss < 2 %**. - Modular, scalable, low OPEX for small-to-medium scale (< 20 TPD). - Operates at moderate pressure (4 – 10 bar) and ambient temperature, consuming ~ 0.20 – 0.25 kWh / kg CH₄ for compression + adsorption. - Regeneration uses the pressure drop (no thermal energy input) — making it energy-efficient compared to amine scrubbing. --- **(b)** A dairy plant produces **800 m³/day** of raw biogas (60 % CH₄, 40 % CO₂) at 25 °C and 1 atm. After PSA upgrading (96 % CH₄ recovery) and compression to 200 bar, calculate: **(i)** Daily mass of CBG produced, **(ii)** daily energy content of CBG in MJ, and **(iii)** payback period if the CBG plant costs **₹3.2 crore** and the displaced diesel price is **₹95 / litre**. Assume CBG is sold at energy parity to diesel (₹2.48 / MJ). **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **(i) Mass of CBG (3 marks):** Moles of CH₄ in raw biogas per day: $$n = \frac{101325 \times 800}{8.314 \times 298} = 32\,712 \; \text{mol/day}$$ Moles of CH₄: $$n_{CH_4} = 0.60 \times 32\,712 = 19\,627 \; \text{mol/day}$$ Mass of CH₄: $$m_{CH_4} = 19\,627 \times 16 = 314\,036 \; \text{g/day} = 314.0 \; \text{kg/day}$$ Mass of CBG (96 % recovery): $$m_{CBG} = 0.96 \times 314.0 = 301.5 \; \text{kg/day}$$ **(ii) Energy content (2 marks):** $$E = 301.5 \; \text{kg} \times 52.7 \; \text{MJ/kg} = 15\,889 \; \text{MJ/day}$$ **(iii) Payback period (2 marks):** Annual revenue from CBG sales: $$R = 15\,889 \times 365 \times 2.48 = ₹1.44 \times 10^7 \approx ₹1.44 \; \text{crore/year}$$ Simple payback: $$PP = \frac{3.2}{1.44} \approx 2.22 \; \text{years}$$ --- ### Question B — Electrical ECOs (Alternative Choice) [14 marks] **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]** **(a)** Explain the **eight major electrical energy conservation opportunities (ECOs)** suitable for a typical Indian industry. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** 1. **Power Factor Correction (PFC):** Installation of shunt capacitor banks (fixed or APFC panels) to maintain PF ≥ 0.95, avoiding KSEB penalty and reducing kVA demand. (0.875 marks) 2. **Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs):** On centrifugal pumps, fans, blowers, and compressors. Power varies as N³; 20 % speed reduction → ~ 49 % power saving. (0.875 marks) 3. **Energy-Efficient Motors (IE3/IE4):** Replacement of IE1/IE2 standard motors. Each IE class typically offers 2 – 3 % efficiency gain, translating to ₹5 000 – ₹15 000 saving per kW per year in continuous-duty applications. (0.875 marks) 4. **Lighting Retrofits:** Replace conventional / CFL with LED; install occupancy sensors, daylight dimming, task lighting. Lighting accounts for 5 – 15 % of industrial electricity; LED retrofits save 50 – 70 %. (0.875 marks) 5. **Maximum Demand Control:** Using MD controllers and load schedulers to shift non-critical loads to off-peak hours, reducing the kVA peak billing demand. (0.875 marks) 6. **Transformer Efficiency:** Replace conventional transformers with **amorphous metal core (AMDT)** distribution transformers, especially in high-load-factor continuous operations. (0.875 marks) 7. **Harmonic Mitigation:** Use of active / passive harmonic filters, K-rated transformers, and 18-pulse rectifier-fed VFDs to reduce THD below IEEE 519 limits (5 % voltage, 8 % current). (0.875 marks) 8. **Power Quality and Voltage Optimization:** Use of servo voltage stabilizers or line-voltage regulators to optimize supply voltage to equipment rated values, reducing iron and copper losses. (0.875 marks) --- **(b)** A 500 kW industrial load operates at an average PF of 0.78. Calculate: **(i)** the rating of the capacitor bank required to improve PF to 0.95, **(ii)** the new kVA demand, and **(iii)** the annual monetary savings in billing demand (energy charge) at ₹7.50 / kVAh with 6 000 operating hours / year. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **(i) Capacitor kVAR (3 marks):** At PF₁ = 0.78: $\phi_1 = 38.7°$, $\tan\phi_1 = 0.802$. $$Q_1 = 500 \times 0.802 = 401 \; \text{kVAR}$$ At PF₂ = 0.95: $\phi_2 = 18.2°$, $\tan\phi_2 = 0.329$. $$Q_2 = 500 \times 0.329 = 164.5 \; \text{kVAR}$$ $$Q_c = 401 - 164.5 = 236.5 \; \text{kVAR} \approx 240 \; \text{kVAR (bank rating)}$$ **(ii) New kVA demand (2 marks):** $$S_2 = \frac{500}{0.95} = 526.3 \; \text{kVA}$$ **(iii) Annual savings (2 marks):** Original kVA: $S_1 = 500/0.78 = 641.0$ kVA. Reduction: $\Delta S = 641.0 - 526.3 = 114.7$ kVA. $$\text{Savings} = 114.7 \times 6\,000 \times 7.50 = ₹51.6 \; \text{lakh/year}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Confusing units of kW, kVA, kVAR** — always draw a power triangle and label $P$, $Q$, $S$, $\cos\phi$ clearly. > 2. **Forgetting to convert kVA to monetary savings** in PF problems — students often compute $Q_c$ correctly but skip Step 3. > 3. **In CBG problems**, students often take CH₄ volume % as equivalent to mass % — they are different (CH₄ density vs. CO₂ density differ by ~ 1.6×). > 4. **In VFD problems**, do NOT use the linear relation $P \propto N$; ALWAYS use the cube law $P \propto N^3$ for centrifugal equipment. > 5. **Real-gas effects** at 200 bar are non-negligible — for > 5-mark numericals, mention the **compressibility factor Z ≈ 0.85 for CH₄ at 200 bar, 25 °C**. > 6. **CAPEX vs. OPEX** must be separated in payback calculations — sometimes students forget to include electricity cost escalation in lifecycle cost analysis. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### Compressed Biogas (CBG) — Rapid Revision - **CBG = Purified (≥ 92 % CH₄) + Compressed (200 – 250 bar) biogas.** - **IS 16087:2016** sets the specification for CBG in India. - **Raw biogas** has 55 – 70 % CH₄ + 30 – 40 % CO₂ + trace H₂S + water vapor. - **PSA** is the most common upgrading technology in India — achieves 95 – 98 % CH₄ purity. - **Multi-stage compression with intercooling** is mandatory for 200 bar output. - **Optimum pressure ratio per stage:** $r_{opt} = (P_2/P_1)^{1/N_s}$ — derived from AM–GM inequality. - **Energy density of CBG at 200 bar ≈ 8 900 MJ/m³** (as compressed gas), 200× atmospheric biogas. - **Calorific value of CBG ≈ 52.7 MJ/kg** (for 95 % CH₄ purity). - **Government initiative:** SATAT scheme by MoPNG, GoI (target 5 000 CBG plants by 2025 – indicative). - **Methane Number ≥ 65** is required for automotive use. ### Electrical ECOs — Rapid Revision - **PF correction formula:** $Q_c = P(\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2)$ kVAR. - **KSEB penalty below PF 0.90; aim for 0.95–0.99.** - **VFD cube law:** $P \propto N^3$ for centrifugal pumps and fans. - **Motor efficiency classes:** IE1 (standard) < IE2 (high) < IE3 (premium) < IE4 (super premium) < IE5 (ultra-premium). - **Lighting share:** 5 – 15 % of industrial electricity; LED saves 50 – 70 %. - **THD limit (IEEE 519):** Voltage 5 %, Current 8 % at PCC. - **MD control** shifts non-critical loads to off-peak, reducing kVA demand charges. - **Amorphous core transformers (AMDT)** reduce no-load losses by ~ 70 % vs. CRGO. - **Most lucrative ECOs in Kerala industries (ranked):** (1) PF correction, (2) VFD on pumps/fans, (3) LED retrofit, (4) IE3 motor replacement, (5) MD control. ### Key KTU Exam Watch-Words (Use in 14-Mark Answers) - **"Pressure Swing Adsorption"** — never write "Pressure Swing Absorption". - **"Variable Frequency Drive"** — sometimes called VSD (Variable Speed Drive); both are accepted. - **"Methane Number"** — distinct from "Octane Number"; do not interchange. - **"Power Triangle"** — must accompany any PF-related answer for full marks. - **"Affinity Laws"** — must be quoted for any VFD question; just stating "saves energy" is insufficient. - **"PAT Scheme"** — Perform, Achieve, and Trade — first mention in any industrial energy-efficiency question. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Note for KTU 2024 Students:** This topic is a high-weightage module (typically 18 – 22 % of the End Semester Examination, i.e., 14 – 18 marks). Memorize the formula sheet in Section 2.5 and the two numerical worked examples (Sections 3.3 and 3.4) — these problem patterns recur almost every KTU examination cycle. ]<]minimax[>[

topics.title: Thermodynamic ECOs in Chemical Process Industry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT614 - ENERGY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Thermodynamic ECOs in Chemical Process Industry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Thermodynamic Energy Conservation Opportunities (ECOs)

In the context of the **Chemical Process Industry (CPI)**, *Thermodynamic Energy Conservation Opportunities* (ECOs) are systematic, science-based interventions rooted in the **First Law** (energy balance) and the **Second Law** (entropy/exergy analysis) of Thermodynamics, applied to identify, quantify, and recover waste energy in chemical plants, refineries, and bulk-process manufacturing facilities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> *Thermodynamic ECOs in the CPI* refer to engineering methods that exploit the **temperature–enthalpy profile** of process streams to minimize external hot and cold utility demand. They span the entire hierarchy: process modification → heat recovery → utility optimization → power recovery.

The **thermodynamic hierarchy of energy conservation** in a chemical plant can be arranged as:

1. **Source reduction** (eliminating the energy demand itself)
2. **Energy recovery** (heat integration, waste-heat recovery)
3. **Energy substitution** (using cheaper or renewable utilities)
4. **Energy conversion efficiency improvement** (cogeneration, heat pumps)

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy: The "Building Heating Loop"

Imagine a multi-storey building where the top floors generate heat (sun-exposed roof, server rooms, kitchen exhaust) while the bottom floors need heating (basement offices, lobbies). A naive solution is to **dump the top-floor heat into the atmosphere** and **buy fresh heat from a boiler for the basement**. This is what an un-integrated chemical plant does with hot and cold streams.

A thermodynamic ECO is the equivalent of installing an **internal heat-recovery loop** (like a heat-recovery ventilator) so that the roof's hot air pre-warms the cold air entering the basement. The boiler (hot utility) is now smaller; the chiller (cold utility) is smaller. The same idea, scaled up to reactors, distillation columns, condensers, and reboilers, is what **Pinch Analysis, Heat Integration, Cogeneration, and Heat Pumps** deliver to a chemical plant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industry Standard Metric:** The **Minimum Energy Requirement (MER)** is the absolute thermodynamic floor below which external hot and cold utility consumption cannot be reduced, no matter how sophisticated the heat-exchanger network. It is expressed in **kW** or **MJ/hr** of hot utility ($Q_{H,min}$) and cold utility ($Q_{C,min}$).

## 1.3 Classification of Thermodynamic ECOs in CPI

| ECO Family | Underlying Principle | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Pinch Analysis / HEN** | Second Law (ΔT_min targeting) | Oil refineries, petrochemical complexes |
| **Heat Pump Integration** | Reverse Carnot cycle (COP > 1) | Distillation columns, evaporators |
| **Cogeneration (CHP)** | Combined Rankine + process heating | Sugar, paper, fertilizer industries |
| **Exergy Analysis** | Availability / irreversibility | Ammonia, cement, chlor-alkali plants |
| **Process Modification** | Reaction/separation thermodynamics | Endothermic reactors, multi-effect evaporators |
| **Mechanical Vapour Recompression (MVR)** | Latent heat recompression | Desalination, dairy, pulp & paper |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Thermodynamic Foundation of Process Integration

Chemical processes contain **hot streams** (requiring cooling) and **cold streams** (requiring heating). Each stream has:

- A **supply temperature** ($T_S$): the temperature at which the stream exists in the process
- A **target temperature** ($T_T$): the temperature the stream must reach
- A **heat-capacity flow rate** ($CP$): defined as $CP = \dot{m} \cdot C_p$ in **kW/°C**

The **enthalpy change** required by a stream is:

$$\Delta H = CP \cdot (T_T - T_S)$$

This is the **First Law** in action: the energy a stream must release or absorb is fixed by mass flow, specific heat, and temperature change.

## 2.2 Pinch Analysis — The Cornerstone Thermodynamic ECO

### 2.2.1 Concept
The **Pinch Point** is the location in the process where the hot and cold composite curves are closest — separated only by the **minimum allowable approach temperature** ($\Delta T_{min}$). Below the pinch, only hot utility should be used; above the pinch, only cold utility should be used. **No heat should cross the pinch.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight**
> The value of $\Delta T_{min}$ is a **trade-off parameter** — a smaller $\Delta T_{min}$ reduces utility demand but increases capital cost (more area, more exchangers). The optimum is found by **Total Cost Targeting**:
> $$\min_{ \Delta T_{min} } \; C_{total} = C_{utility}(\Delta T_{min}) + C_{capital}(\Delta T_{min})$$

### 2.2.2 Composite Curves and the Grand Composite Curve

- **Composite Curve:** A plot of enthalpy ($H$) versus temperature for all hot streams combined and all cold streams combined. The vertical gap between curves is the heat available for recovery at any temperature interval.
- **Grand Composite Curve (GCC):** A "shifted" version used for placing utilities, heat pumps, and CHP. Streams are shifted by $\Delta T_{min}/2$ on the appropriate side.

### 2.2.3 The Problem Table Algorithm (PTA)

The PTA converts a list of stream data into a **heat cascade** and the **MER**. The steps are:

1. Convert all stream temperatures into **interval temperatures** by adding or subtracting $\Delta T_{min}/2$.
2. Compute the **net enthalpy surplus/deficit** in each interval.
3. Cascade the heat from the top interval downward, accumulating residual heat flow.
4. The **minimum value** in the cascade (just above zero to avoid negative flow) is the **minimum hot utility** $Q_{H,min}$.
5. The **heat flow entering the topmost interval** is the **minimum cold utility** $Q_{C,min}$.

## 2.3 Heat Pump Integration

A heat pump **upgrades** low-temperature waste heat to a higher temperature by consuming work (electricity, shaft work). The thermodynamic metric is the **Coefficient of Performance (COP)**:

$$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_{H}}{W_{net}} = \frac{T_{H}}{T_{H} - T_{C}}$$

where the second equality is the **reversible (Carnot) limit**. Real refrigeration cycles (vapour compression, absorption) have COP values 30–60% of Carnot.

> [!NOTE]
> A heat pump is justified whenever the **value of the upgraded heat** (in terms of hot-utility displaced) exceeds the **cost of the work input** (electricity or shaft work). In the GCC, a heat pump is placed by connecting an enthalpy **deficit** (below pinch) to an enthalpy **surplus** (above pinch).

## 2.4 Cogeneration (Combined Heat and Power — CHP)

Cogeneration simultaneously produces **process heat** ($Q_{process}$) and **electrical/shaft power** ($W$) from a single fuel input. Two common schemes:

- **Topping cycle:** Fuel $\rightarrow$ High-temperature power generation $\rightarrow$ Low-grade heat to process.
- **Bottoming cycle:** Fuel $\rightarrow$ High-temperature process heat $\rightarrow$ Power from waste-heat boiler.

Key performance metric is the **Overall Energy Efficiency**:

$$\eta_{CHP} = \frac{W_{net} + Q_{process}}{Q_{fuel}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** When asked to compare cogeneration with conventional generation, always quote the **Second-Law efficiency advantage**: a conventional thermal power plant rejects ~50% of fuel energy as low-grade heat, while CHP captures this heat usefully, raising overall efficiency from **~35% to ~70–80%**.

## 2.5 Exergy (Availability) Analysis for Chemical Processes

Unlike energy, **exergy** is destroyed by irreversibilities. The exergy of a heat stream at temperature $T$ relative to a dead-state $T_0$ is:

$$Ex_{Q} = Q \cdot \left( 1 - \frac{T_0}{T} \right)$$

For a material stream, **specific physical exergy** is:

$$ex_{ph} = (h - h_0) - T_0(s - s_0)$$

For a reactive mixture, **chemical exergy** $ex_{ch}$ is added. The **exergy destruction rate** in any process unit is given by the **Gouy-Stodola theorem**:

$$\dot{E}_{D} = T_0 \cdot \dot{S}_{gen}$$

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta H = CP \cdot \Delta T$ | Stream enthalpy change | kW |
| 2 | $CP = \dot{m} \cdot C_p$ | Heat-capacity flow rate | kW/°C |
| 3 | $Q_{H,min} = \max_k \left[ \sum_{i \le k} \Delta H_{C,i} - \sum_{j \le k} \Delta H_{H,j} \right]$ | Min. hot utility (PTA, $k$ = interval index) | kW |
| 4 | $Q_{C,min} = Q_{H,min} - \sum_i \Delta H_{C,i}$ | Min. cold utility | kW |
| 5 | $COP_{HP,Carnot} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_C}$ | Reversible heat-pump COP | – |
| 6 | $\eta_{CHP} = \dfrac{W_{net} + Q_{process}}{Q_{fuel}}$ | CHP overall efficiency | – |
| 7 | $Ex_Q = Q(1 - T_0/T)$ | Exergy of heat stream | kW |
| 8 | $ex_{ph} = (h - h_0) - T_0(s - s_0)$ | Specific physical exergy | kJ/kg |
| 9 | $\dot{E}_D = T_0 \dot{S}_{gen}$ | Gouy–Stodola exergy destruction | kW |
| 10 | $\eta_{II} = \dfrac{Ex_{recovered}}{Ex_{supplied}}$ | Second-Law (exergetic) efficiency | – |
| 11 | $\Delta T_{min,opt} : \dfrac{dC_{utility}}{d\Delta T_{min}} = -\dfrac{dC_{capital}}{d\Delta T_{min}}$ | Optimum approach (capital-energy trade-off) | °C |

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU answer sheets, always show **units** at every step of the calculation. Marks are deducted for missing or inconsistent units in numerical sub-questions.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Hot Utility from the Problem Table

We consider a process with $N$ hot streams and $M$ cold streams, each shifted to its interval temperature. The interval $k$ has a **net enthalpy balance**:

$$D_k = \sum_{cold \; i \in k} CP_{C,i} \cdot \Delta T_k - \sum_{hot \; j \in k} CP_{H,j} \cdot \Delta T_k$$

The heat cascade equation, starting from the topmost interval (interval 1) and accumulating downward, gives the heat flow in each interval:

$$f_k = f_{k-1} + D_k$$

where $f_0 = Q_{H,min}$. The constraint is $f_k \ge 0$ for all $k$ (no negative heat flow is physically meaningful without a heat pump crossing the pinch). Maximizing $Q_{H,min}$ subject to $f_k \ge 0$ yields:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{H,min} &= \max_{k} \left[ -\sum_{j=1}^{k} D_j \right] \\
&= \max_{k} \left[ \sum_{j=1}^{k} \left( \sum_{hot \in j} \Delta H_{H,j} - \sum_{cold \in j} \Delta H_{C,j} \right) \right]
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In the alternate convention used by KTU textbooks, $Q_{H,min}$ emerges as the **most positive cumulative sum** of (cold demand − hot supply) over successive intervals from the top.

## 3.2 Numerical Example: PTA for a 3-Stream Process

**Stream Data:**

| Stream | Type | $T_S$ (°C) | $T_T$ (°C) | $CP$ (kW/°C) | $\Delta H$ (kW) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| H1 | Hot | 180 | 60 | 2.0 | $-240$ |
| C1 | Cold | 40 | 160 | 1.5 | $+180$ |
| C2 | Cold | 80 | 200 | 1.0 | $+120$ |

**Step 1: Choose** $\Delta T_{min} = 20$°C. Shift cold streams up by $10$°C, hot streams down by $10$°C.

**Step 2: Interval boundaries** (shifted temperatures, sorted descending):

| Interval | Range (°C) | Hot in interval | Cold in interval | $D_k$ (kW) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 200 – 190 | – | C2: $CP=1.0$ | $+1.0 \times 10 = +10$ |
| 2 | 190 – 170 | – | C2: $CP=1.0$ | $+1.0 \times 20 = +20$ |
| 3 | 170 – 150 | H1: $CP=2.0$ (from 180→150 ⇒ shifted 170→150) | C1: $CP=1.5$, C2: $CP=1.0$ | $+(1.5+1.0)(20) - 2.0(20) = +50 - 40 = +10$ |
| 4 | 150 – 100 | H1: $CP=2.0$ | C1: $CP=1.5$ | $+1.5(50) - 2.0(50) = 75 - 100 = -25$ |
| 5 | 100 – 70 | H1: $CP=2.0$ (shifted 110→70 since original 100→60 ⇒ shifted 90→50; recheck) | C1: $CP=1.5$ | (recheck below) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sorting and shifting of interval boundaries** must be done carefully: for **each stream**, the shifted $T_S$ and shifted $T_T$ are used; for H1 shifted from 180→60, the range becomes 170→50 (subtract 10). For C1 shifted from 40→160, the range becomes 50→170. For C2 shifted from 80→200, the range becomes 90→210. Recompute intervals using the actual shifted endpoints.

**Recomputed Interval Table (corrected):**

| Interval | Shifted range (°C) | Hot $CP$ sum | Cold $CP$ sum | $\Delta T$ (°C) | $D_k$ (kW) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 210 – 190 | 0 | 1.0 (C2) | 20 | $+20$ |
| 2 | 190 – 170 | 0 | 1.0 (C2) | 20 | $+20$ |
| 3 | 170 – 110 | 2.0 (H1) | 1.5 (C1) + 1.0 (C2) = 2.5 | 60 | $2.5(60) - 2.0(60) = +30$ |
| 4 | 110 – 90 | 2.0 (H1) | 1.5 (C1) + 1.0 (C2) = 2.5 | 20 | $2.5(20) - 2.0(20) = +10$ |
| 5 | 90 – 50 | 2.0 (H1) | 1.5 (C1) | 40 | $1.5(40) - 2.0(40) = -20$ |

**Step 3: Heat Cascade (accumulating $D_k$ from top):**

| Interval $k$ | $D_k$ (kW) | Cumulative $\sum D_j$ from top | Heat flow $f_k = Q_{H,min} - \sum_{j \le k} D_j$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | +20 | +20 | $Q_{H,min} - 20$ |
| 2 | +20 | +40 | $Q_{H,min} - 40$ |
| 3 | +30 | +70 | $Q_{H,min} - 70$ |
| 4 | +10 | +80 | $Q_{H,min} - 80$ |
| 5 | −20 | +60 | $Q_{H,min} - 60$ |

**Step 4: Set the minimum heat flow to zero** (since the cascade cannot be negative without a heat pump). The minimum cumulative surplus is at interval 4: $\sum D_j = +80$ kW. Therefore:

$$Q_{H,min} = 80 \; \text{kW}$$

**Step 5: Compute $Q_{C,min}$ using the overall energy balance:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{C,min} &= Q_{H,min} + \sum_{cold} \Delta H_C - \sum_{hot} \Delta H_H \\
&= 80 + (180 + 120) - 240 \\
&= 80 + 300 - 240 \\
&= 140 \; \text{kW}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The total energy recovered by the heat-exchanger network is $300 - 140 - 80 = 80$ kW (matches the net cascade), confirming **consistency of the First Law**.

## 3.3 Derivation: Carnot COP of an Ideal Heat Pump

The reversible cycle operating between a low-temperature reservoir ($T_C$) and a high-temperature reservoir ($T_H$) absorbs heat $Q_C$ from the cold source, rejects $Q_H$ to the hot source, and receives work $W$:

$$Q_H = Q_C + W$$

For a reversible (Carnot) cycle, the entropy balance gives:

$$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} = \frac{Q_C}{T_C}$$

Solving for the COP:

$$\begin{aligned}
COP_{HP} &= \frac{Q_H}{W} = \frac{Q_H}{Q_H - Q_C} \\
&= \frac{1}{1 - Q_C/Q_H} = \frac{1}{1 - T_C/T_H} \\
&= \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_C}
\end{aligned}$$

This shows that as the temperature lift $(T_H - T_C)$ decreases, COP rises sharply, making heat-pump placement in the GCC economically attractive whenever the lift is small.

## 3.4 Derivation: Exergy Destruction in a Heat Exchanger

For a counter-flow heat exchanger with no work and negligible kinetic/potential energy changes, the **exergy balance** gives:

$$\dot{E}_{D} = T_0 \cdot \dot{m}_{hot} C_{p,hot} \ln \frac{T_{H,in}}{T_{H,out}} + T_0 \cdot \dot{m}_{cold} C_{p,cold} \ln \frac{T_{C,out}}{T_{C,in}}$$

Equivalently, using the **Gouy–Stodola theorem**:

$$\dot{E}_{D} = T_0 \cdot \dot{S}_{gen}$$

$$\dot{S}_{gen} = \dot{m}_{hot} C_{p,hot} \ln \frac{T_{H,out}}{T_{H,in}} + \dot{m}_{cold} C_{p,cold} \ln \frac{T_{C,out}}{T_{C,in}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In a **reversible** heat exchanger, the entropy generation is zero, which is only possible if there is no temperature difference between the streams — physically impossible. Hence, **every real heat exchanger destroys exergy**, and a smaller $\Delta T_{min}$ means **less exergy destruction** but **more area cost**.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix for Selecting a Thermodynamic ECO

| Decision Criterion | Pinch / HEN | Heat Pump | Cogeneration | Exergy Retrofit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Streams close in T? | Yes | Yes (small lift) | No (needs HP steam) | Any |
| Plant is grass-root? | Yes (target) | No (retrofit) | Yes (CHP boiler) | No |
| Cost of fuel vs. electricity? | Either | High electricity | High fuel | Either |
| Capital availability? | High | Medium | Very High | Medium |
| KTU exam relevance | High (Module 4) | High | Medium-High | Medium |
