# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define pinch point and explain its significance in chemical process integration. (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 4]**

**Model Answer:**
The pinch point is the location in the process heat-exchange network where the hot and cold composite curves are closest in temperature, separated only by the minimum allowable approach temperature $\Delta T_{min}$. Its significance lies in three thermodynamic constraints: (i) it sets the **minimum external hot utility** $Q_{H,min}$ requirement (above pinch) and **minimum external cold utility** $Q_{C,min}$ (below pinch); (ii) it acts as an **information barrier** — no heat should flow across the pinch, since doing so increases both utility demands simultaneously; and (iii) it provides a **design heuristic** — the network above the pinch is designed independently of the network below the pinch, simplifying HEN synthesis.

---

### **Q2. Distinguish between cogeneration and a heat pump as thermodynamic ECOs in a chemical plant. (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 4]**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Cogeneration (CHP) | Heat Pump |
|---|---|---|
| Energy input | Fuel (chemical energy) | Work (electrical/shaft) |
| Output | Power + process heat simultaneously | Upgraded low-grade heat |
| Underlying cycle | Rankine / gas turbine | Reverse Rankine / vapour compression |
| Typical metric | $\eta_{CHP} = (W+Q_{process})/Q_{fuel}$ | $COP = Q_H/W_{net}$ |
| Application | Continuous process heating + power | Pockets of low-grade waste heat recovery |

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**(a) Explain the construction of composite curves and the grand composite curve. How is the minimum energy requirement (MER) determined from them? (7 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 4, 14-Mark Pattern]**

**Model Solution:**

**Composite Curves (3.5 marks):**
The composite curves are enthalpy-versus-temperature diagrams that combine all hot streams (summed by heat-capacity flow rate $CP$) and all cold streams (summed by $CP$) into two single curves. Each curve is divided into temperature intervals corresponding to the stream supply/target temperatures. The **enthalpy change** of the combined hot streams between $T_k$ and $T_{k+1}$ is the sum:

$$\Delta H_{H,k} = \sum_{hot \; j \in k} CP_{H,j} \cdot (T_k - T_{k+1})$$

and analogously for the cold composite. The **horizontal distance** between the two curves at any temperature $T$ is the **heat available for recovery** within the integration feasibility region. The **vertical overlap region** shows the temperatures at which the hot streams can preheat cold streams.

**Grand Composite Curve (GCC) (1.5 marks):**
The GCC is obtained by **shifting** the cold composite curve upward by $\Delta T_{min}/2$ and the hot composite curve downward by $\Delta T_{min}/2$, then plotting the resulting **net enthalpy surplus/deficit** versus temperature. The GCC is divided into:
- **Above the pinch:** net deficit (cold utility must be supplied)
- **Below the pinch:** net surplus (hot utility must be supplied)
- **At the pinch:** zero net flow

**MER Determination from PTA (2 marks):**
The Minimum Energy Requirement is extracted using the **Problem Table Algorithm**:

$$Q_{H,min} = \max_k \left[ \sum_{j=1}^{k} \left( \Delta H_{C,j} - \Delta H_{H,j} \right) \right]$$

$$Q_{C,min} = Q_{H,min} - \sum \Delta H_H + \sum \Delta H_C$$

**Significance (0 marks, for context):** The MER represents the **thermodynamic floor** for external utility consumption and serves as the design target for the heat-exchanger network.

---

**(b) A chemical process has the following stream data. Determine the minimum hot and cold utility requirements using $\Delta T_{min} = 20$°C. (7 Marks)**

| Stream | Type | $T_S$ (°C) | $T_T$ (°C) | $CP$ (kW/°C) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| H1 | Hot | 200 | 80 | 1.0 |
| H2 | Hot | 150 | 50 | 2.0 |
| C1 | Cold | 60 | 180 | 1.5 |
| C2 | Cold | 100 | 250 | 2.0 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark): Shift temperatures by** $\Delta T_{min}/2 = 10$°C.

| Stream | Shifted $T_S$ | Shifted $T_T$ |
|---|---|---|
| H1 | 190 | 70 |
| H2 | 140 | 40 |
| C1 | 70 | 190 |
| C2 | 110 | 260 |

**Step 2 (1.5 marks): Sort shifted interval temperatures (descending):** 260, 190, 140, 110, 70, 40

**Step 3 (1.5 marks): Interval table with $D_k$ calculation:**

| Interval | Range (°C) | $CP_H$ | $CP_C$ | $\Delta T$ | $D_k$ (kW) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 260 – 190 | 0 | 2.0 (C2) | 70 | $+2.0(70) = +140$ |
| 2 | 190 – 140 | 0 | 2.0(C2) + 1.5(C1) = 3.5 | 50 | $+3.5(50) = +175$ |
| 3 | 140 – 110 | 2.0 (H2) | 2.0(C2) + 1.5(C1) = 3.5 | 30 | $3.5(30) - 2.0(30) = +45$ |
| 4 | 110 – 70 | 2.0 (H2) | 2.0(C2) + 1.5(C1) = 3.5 | 40 | $3.5(40) - 2.0(40) = +60$ |
| 5 | 70 – 40 | 2.0 (H2) + 1.0 (H1) = 3.0 | 1.5 (C1) | 30 | $1.5(30) - 3.0(30) = -45$ |

**Step 4 (1.5 marks): Heat cascade and minimum utility:**

| $k$ | $D_k$ (kW) | Cumulative (kW) | $f_k = Q_{H,min} - $ Cumulative |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | +140 | 140 | $Q_{H,min} - 140$ |
| 2 | +175 | 315 | $Q_{H,min} - 315$ |
| 3 | +45 | 360 | $Q_{H,min} - 360$ |
| 4 | +60 | 420 | $Q_{H,min} - 420$ |
| 5 | −45 | 375 | $Q_{H,min} - 375$ |

Minimum heat flow occurs at interval 4, set to zero:

$$Q_{H,min} = 420 \; \text{kW}$$

**Step 5 (1.5 marks): Compute $Q_{C,min}$ using overall energy balance:**

Total cold demand = $1.5(180-60) + 2.0(250-100) = 180 + 300 = 480$ kW
Total hot supply = $1.0(200-80) + 2.0(150-50) = 120 + 200 = 320$ kW

$$Q_{C,min} = Q_{H,min} + \text{Cold demand} - \text{Hot supply} = 420 + 480 - 320 = 580 \; \text{kW}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Loss:**
> 1. Forgetting to shift temperatures by **$\Delta T_{min}/2$** on the correct side (hot streams shifted down, cold streams shifted up) — costs 2 marks.
> 2. Using unsorted interval boundaries or missing endpoints — costs 1 mark.
> 3. Setting the wrong cumulative sum to zero (must be the most positive to avoid negative heat flow) — costs 2 marks.
> 4. Forgetting to verify the **overall energy balance**: $Q_{H,min} - Q_{C,min} = \sum \Delta H_H - \sum \Delta H_C$ — costs 1 mark.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

**(a) Discuss the principles of heat pump integration in a chemical process. With a neat sketch, explain the placement of a heat pump on the grand composite curve. (7 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 4, 14-Mark Pattern]**

**Model Solution:**

**Thermodynamic Principle (2 marks):**
A heat pump is a **work-driven** device that **upgrades** low-temperature waste heat to a higher useful temperature. It operates on a **reverse thermodynamic cycle** (typically reverse Rankine/vapour compression). The performance is measured by the **Coefficient of Performance**:

$$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net}} = \frac{Q_H}{Q_H - Q_C}$$

For an ideal (Carnot) heat pump:

$$COP_{HP,Carnot} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_C}$$

A heat pump is economically attractive when the **monetary value of the upgraded heat** exceeds the **cost of the work input** plus capital amortization.

**Identification of Heat Pump Pocket (2 marks):**
On the GCC, a heat pump is placed whenever there exists an **enthalpy surplus** at a lower temperature (a "pocket" of low-grade heat below the pinch) and an **enthalpy deficit** at a higher temperature (a demand for process heat). The pump transports enthalpy **across the pinch** — an action otherwise forbidden in pure HEN design.

**Sketch (2 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hot Stream Cooling H1 above Pinch T_high] -->|Q_H Released| B{Cold Stream C1 above Pinch}
    B --> C[No External Hot Utility Required]
    D[Cold Stream C2 below Pinch T_low] -->|Q_L Waste Heat| E[Heat Pump Evaporator]
    E -->|W Input Compressor| F[Heat Pump Condenser]
    F -->|Q_H Upgraded Heat| G[Hot Utility Replaced]
    G --> H[Process Demand Above Pinch]
```

**Selection Criterion (1 mark):**
The heat pump is viable when:
$$\text{COP}_{actual} \cdot \text{Cost of Work} < \text{Cost of Hot Utility Displaced}$$

If $T_H - T_C$ is large, COP drops sharply, and the heat pump is uneconomical.

---

**(b) A distillation column reboiler requires 1500 kW of heat at 150°C. Waste hot water is available at 80°C that must be cooled to 40°C. The mass flow rate of waste water is 25 kg/s with $C_p = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K. Determine if a heat pump can be used. Assume a realistic COP of 3.5. Ambient temperature $T_0 = 25$°C. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark): Compute waste heat available:**

$$Q_w = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot (T_{in} - T_{out}) = 25 \times 4.18 \times (80 - 40) = 25 \times 4.18 \times 40 = 4180 \; \text{kW}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark): Compute Carnot COP for ideal heat pump between 80°C (353 K) and 150°C (423 K):**

$$COP_{Carnot} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_C} = \frac{423}{423 - 353} = \frac{423}{70} = 6.04$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks): Since actual COP (3.5) is less than Carnot, the second-law efficiency is:**

$$\eta_{II} = \frac{COP_{actual}}{COP_{Carnot}} = \frac{3.5}{6.04} = 0.58 = 58\%$$

This is a reasonable efficiency for a real vapour-compression heat pump.

**Step 4 (1.5 marks): Work required to deliver 1500 kW of useful heat at 150°C:**

$$W_{in} = \frac{Q_H}{COP} = \frac{1500}{3.5} = 428.6 \; \text{kW}$$

**Step 5 (1.5 marks): Energy balance and feasibility check:**

The heat pump extracts heat from the waste water at a rate of $Q_C = Q_H - W = 1500 - 428.6 = 1071.4$ kW.

Cooling achieved in waste water: temperature drop = $1071.4 / (25 \times 4.18) = 10.25$°C, so waste water is cooled from 80°C to **69.75°C** (not to the desired 40°C — additional cooling may be required, but partial waste-heat recovery is achieved).

**Conclusion (0.5 marks):**
The heat pump is **thermodynamically feasible**. The work input (428.6 kW) replaces 1500 kW of hot utility. The economic feasibility depends on the unit cost of electricity vs. fuel — typically viable when electricity-to-fuel cost ratio is below 3:1.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Loss:**
> 1. Confusing heat pump COP with refrigerator COP — COP_HP = Q_H/W, NOT Q_C/W. Costs 2 marks.
> 2. Forgetting to convert °C to K (add 273) when computing Carnot COP. Costs 1 mark.
> 3. Forgetting to verify second-law efficiency is below 100%. Costs 1 mark.
> 4. Not stating **operating and capital cost comparison** as the final feasibility criterion. Costs 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Thermodynamic ECOs in CPI**

- **Pinch Point:** The tightest temperature gap between hot and cold composite curves; separated by $\Delta T_{min}$. No heat should cross the pinch in an optimal HEN.

- **MER (Minimum Energy Requirement):** $Q_{H,min}$ and $Q_{C,min}$ are absolute thermodynamic lower bounds. Determined by PTA or composite curves — independent of network design.

- **$\Delta T_{min}$ Trade-off:** Small $\Delta T_{min}$ → less utility, more area (higher capital). Optimum from total cost minimization.

- **Problem Table Algorithm (PTA):** Build interval table → compute $D_k$ → cascade → set min heat flow to zero → read $Q_{H,min}$.

- **Grand Composite Curve (GCC):** Shifted composite curves, used for utility placement, heat pump, and CHP integration.

- **Heat Pump COP:** $COP_{HP} = Q_H / W_{net}$ (Carnot limit: $T_H / (T_H - T_C)$). Useful when **small temperature lift** exists across the pinch.

- **Cogeneration:** Combined power + process heat from one fuel. Topping vs. bottoming cycles. $\eta_{CHP} = (W + Q_{process})/Q_{fuel}$ can reach 70–80%.

- **Exergy (Availability):** $Ex_Q = Q(1 - T_0/T)$; exergy destruction $\dot{E}_D = T_0 \dot{S}_{gen}$ (Gouy–Stodola). **Second-Law efficiency** $\eta_{II} = \text{Exergy recovered} / \text{Exergy supplied}$.

- **MEE (Multi-Effect Evaporator):** Reuses latent heat of vaporization across multiple effects, reducing steam consumption by ~Nx where N is the number of effects.

- **MVR (Mechanical Vapour Recompression):** Compresses vapor to elevate its saturation temperature, enabling it to act as the heating medium in the same evaporator.

- **Process Hierarchy for ECOs:** Source reduction → heat recovery → utility optimization → power recovery (cogeneration/MVR) → exergy retrofit.

- **Common KTU Mistakes:** Confusing "enthalpy" with "exergy"; missing the $\Delta T_{min}/2$ shift in PTA; using unsorted temperatures; forgetting unit conversions (°C → K for Carnot calculations).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ECOs in Residential and Commercial Buildings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT614 - ENERGY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** ECOs in Residential and Commercial Buildings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Energy Conservation Opportunities (ECOs)** in residential and commercial buildings refer to the systematically identified set of technologies, design interventions, operational practices, and behavioural changes that reduce the energy consumption of a building without compromising the comfort, functionality, or productivity of its occupants. ECOs form the actionable output of a building energy audit and are classified under the broader framework of **Demand-Side Management (DSM)** as endorsed by the Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE), Government of India.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 4):**
> *ECOs in buildings are measures that improve the energy performance index (EPI) of the building envelope, the Heating, Ventilation and Air-Conditioning (HVAC) system, the lighting system, the electrical end-uses, and the auxiliary systems (pumps, fans, lifts) by either reducing energy input or improving energy conversion efficiency.*

## 1.2 Building Sector Energy Footprint in India

According to BEE and the Ministry of Power, the building sector in India accounts for nearly **30–35%** of total national electricity consumption, and this share is projected to rise to **~50%** by 2040 under business-as-usual scenarios. Residential buildings dominate the absolute consumption (~24%), while commercial buildings have a far higher **Energy Use Intensity (EUI)** measured in **kWh/m²/year**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metrics to Remember:**
> - **EPI** (Energy Performance Index) = Annual energy consumed (kWh) / Built-up area (m²) → **kWh/m²/year**
> - **EUI** = Total site energy / Conditioned floor area
> - Typical Indian residential EPI: **50 – 150 kWh/m²/year**
> - Typical Indian commercial EPI: **150 – 400 kWh/m²/year**

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Building as a Thermos Flask"

Imagine a residential building sitting in Kochi during a hot April afternoon. The sun is beating on the roof, the windows are letting in light *and* heat, the air-conditioner is running at full blast, and the refrigerator is cycling in the kitchen.

The building behaves exactly like a **thermos flask (thermos bottle)**:
- The **outer wall** of the flask is the **building envelope** (walls, roof, windows, doors). A good thermos has a vacuum gap; a good building has **insulation, low-e glazing, and reflective roofing**.
- The **inner content** (hot coffee) is the **conditioned indoor air** that we want to keep cool.
- The **lid** of the flask is the **sealing system** — air-tight doors, weather-stripping, and channelless window frames.

If the thermos is poorly insulated, the inner content cools down quickly → the AC has to work overtime. **Every kWh of "lost cooling" is an ECO opportunity.** The goal of building energy conservation is to make the building behave like a *high-quality* thermos flask — keep the unwanted heat **out** in summer and the unwanted heat **in** during winter, *and* use the most efficient equipment to handle whatever heating/cooling is unavoidable.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic — "HEAL-P" for Building ECO Categories:**
> **H** — HVAC (Heating, Ventilation & Air-Conditioning)
> **E** — Envelope (Walls, Roof, Windows, Doors)
> **A** — Appliances & Equipment
> **L** — Lighting
> **P** — People (Behavioural / Operational practices)

## 1.4 Why ECOs Matter in the KTU Context

For a KTU 2024 Energy Engineering student, ECOs are not just a topic — they are the **bridge between thermodynamics and economics**. Every ECO is evaluated using engineering economics (Simple Payback, NPV, IRR). The Bureau of Energy Efficiency's **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade)** scheme designates large commercial buildings (connected load ≥ 500 kW or contract demand ≥ 600 kVA) as **Designated Consumers (DCs)** who must comply with the **Energy Conservation Building Code (ECBC)**, 2007 (revised 2017).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of ECOs in Buildings

Building ECOs are classified along three standard axes used in the BEE **Energy Audit Methodology**.

### A. By Cost of Implementation

| Category | Description | Typical Payback | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Category I — No-Cost / Low-Cost** | Operational tweaks, no capital outlay | < 3 months | Switching off idle equipment, set-point reset, chiller scheduling |
| **Category II — Medium-Cost** | Retrofits within existing system | 3 months – 2 years | VFD on motors, EC fans, LED retrofit, star-rated AC replacement |
| **Category III — High-Cost / Investment** | Major system replacement or design overhaul | 2 – 7 years | VRF/VRV system, on-site renewables, ECBC-compliant façade, BMS upgrade |

### B. By End-Use

1. **Building Envelope ECOs** (passive measures)
2. **HVAC System ECOs** (active conditioning)
3. **Lighting ECOs** (illumination)
4. **Electrical & Plug-Load ECOs** (appliances)
5. **Auxiliary System ECOs** (pumps, fans, lifts, DG sets)
6. **Renewable Integration ECOs** (solar PV, solar thermal)

### C. By Building Type

- **Residential Building ECOs** — homeowner / society-level interventions
- **Commercial Building ECOs** — office, retail, hospital, hotel, IT park, mall

## 2.2 ECOs in Residential Buildings

### 2.2.1 Envelope-Level ECOs

1. **Roof Insulation**
   - Use **extruded polystyrene (XPS)** or **polyurethane foam (PUF)** of 25–50 mm thickness on the terrace.
   - Apply **reflective roof coating** (aluminium-based, SRI ≥ 78) to reduce solar heat gain.
2. **Wall Insulation**
   - **Cavity wall insulation** with mineral wool / EPS for hollow blocks.
   - **External Insulation Finishing System (EIFS)** for new construction.
3. **Windows and Glazing**
   - Replace plain single glass with **double-glazed units (DGU)** — 6 mm + 12 mm air gap + 6 mm.
   - Use **low-emissivity (low-E) glass** with **SHGC ≤ 0.25** in hot climates.
   - Install **external shading devices** (chajjas, louvers, vertical fins) sized using solar geometry.
4. **Air-Tightness**
   - **Weather-stripping** of doors and windows — limits infiltration load.
   - Use **door curtains / air-curtains** at entrances.

### 2.2.2 HVAC / Cooling ECOs

1. **Right-Sizing** of air-conditioners: avoid the Indian tendency to over-size.
2. Set thermostat at **24–26 °C** (BEE recommendation, every 1 °C rise saves ~6% electricity).
3. Use **BEE 5-Star rated inverter ACs** (ISEER ≥ 5.0).
4. **Regular maintenance** — clean filters every fortnight; condenser coil cleaning every quarter.
5. Use **ceiling / pedestal / table fans** to raise the effective set-point by 2–3 °C.

### 2.2.3 Lighting ECOs

1. Replace **CFLs with LEDs** (LED efficacy 100–150 lm/W vs CFL 50–70 lm/W).
2. Use **task lighting** instead of room-level lighting.
3. Install **motion sensors** in bathrooms, store rooms, balconies.
4. Maximize **daylight integration** — light shelves, clerestory windows, light-coloured interiors.

### 2.2.4 Appliance-Level ECOs

| Appliance | ECO Measure | Approximate Saving |
|---|---|---|
| Refrigerator | BEE 5-Star, door gasket maintenance | 30–45% |
| Geyser | Solar geyser / heat-pump geyser / timer | 50–70% |
| Washing Machine | BEE 5-Star front-load | 25–35% |
| TV / Set-top box | Auto-shutoff, smart plug | 5–10% of household electricity |
| Induction cooktop | Replace LPG resistive heating (where feasible) | 30–40% cooking-energy |

### 2.2.5 Water-Heating & Pumping ECOs

- **Solar Water Heating Systems (SWHS)** — mandatory in hospitals and hotels per BEE notification.
- **Variable Speed Pumps** for overhead tanks with pressure-sensor cut-off.

## 2.3 ECOs in Commercial Buildings (ECBC-Driven)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ECBC Thresholds (2007, revised 2017):**
> ECBC applies to commercial buildings with **connected load ≥ 500 kW** or **contract demand ≥ 600 kVA** and conditioned area ≥ 50% of built-up area.

The ECBC mandates four mandatory and three prescriptive compliance paths:

### 2.3.1 ECBC Prescriptive Requirements (Envelope)

| Building Component | ECBC Requirement |
|---|---|
| Roof (non-TERI) assembly | U-factor ≤ **0.33 W/m²·K** |
| Roof with TERI/insulation | U-factor ≤ **0.41 W/m²·K** |
| Wall (opaque) | U-factor ≤ **0.44 W/m²·K** |
| Vertical glazing (single storey) | U-factor ≤ **3.30 W/m²·K**, SHGC ≤ **0.25** |
| Vertical glazing (multi-storey) | U-factor ≤ **3.30 W/m²·K**, SHGC ≤ **0.40** |
| Skylight | U-factor ≤ **3.30 W/m²·K**, SHGC ≤ **0.25** |
| Visible Light Transmittance (VLT) | ≥ **0.27** for all vertical glazing |

### 2.3.2 ECBC Overarching Energy-Efficiency Requirements

1. **ECBC-compliant envelope** with U-factor, SHGC, and VLT limits.
2. **ECBC-compliant HVAC** with mandatory minimum COP and chiller efficiency.
3. **ECBC-compliant service water heating** with insulation on pipes.
4. **ECBC-compliant interior and exterior lighting** with LPD caps.

### 2.3.3 Commercial Lighting ECOs

- **Lighting Power Density (LPD)** caps as per ECBC:
  - Offices: ≤ **10.76 W/m²** (1.0 W/ft²)
  - Classrooms: ≤ **12.91 W/m²** (1.2 W/ft²)
  - Hospitals: ≤ **13.99 W/m²** (1.3 W/ft²)
  - Corridors: ≤ **5.38 W/m²** (0.5 W/ft²)
- Use **LED fixtures** with **occupancy sensors** and **daylight dimming controls**.
- Replace **T5 / T8 fluorescent** with LED battens / panels.
- Use **task-ambient lighting design** with **3-zone switching**.

### 2.3.4 Commercial HVAC ECOs

1. **Chiller Plant Optimization**
   - Replace fixed-speed chillers with **inverter-driven (variable-speed) chillers**.
   - Implement **chilled-water reset** — raise CHW supply temperature based on ambient.
   - **Condenser water reset** — lower CW return temperature in dry-bulb-driven logic.
   - Maintain **ΔT of chilled water ≥ 5 °C** to improve chiller part-load efficiency.
2. **VAV / VFD Retrofits**
   - AHU fan motors with **VFDs** — typical 30–50% fan energy savings at part-load.
   - Chilled-water pumps with VFDs — savings depend on system curve.
3. **Free Cooling**
   - **Economiser cycle** when ambient wet-bulb < chilled-water return temperature.
   - **Water-side economiser** using cooling tower in winter.
4. **Heat Recovery**
   - **Heat-recovery wheels / run-around coils** between exhaust and fresh air.
5. **Refrigerant Management**
   - Phase out **HCFC-22 (R-22)**; use **R-410A, R-134a, R-1234ze** low-GWP refrigerants.

### 2.3.5 Building Management System (BMS)

A BMS is a centralized digital control system that:
- Monitors energy parameters in real time.
- Implements **scheduling**, **optimal start/stop**, and **load-shedding** logic.
- Integrates HVAC, lighting, fire safety, and access control.

> [!NOTE]
> **BEE-Star Labelling of Buildings:**
> BEE has extended its 5-star rating (1-star to 5-star) to existing commercial buildings, called the **Star Rating Programme for Buildings**, evaluated by an empanelled energy professional. The rating is based on EPI: 1-star (worst) to 5-star (best). The star rating is valid for **5 years** and is required for compliance by Designated Consumers under PAT.

### 2.3.6 DG Set & Power Factor ECOs

1. **Power Factor Correction** — maintain **PF ≥ 0.98** via automatic capacitor banks to avoid **kVA demand penalty** and reduce I²R losses in cables.
2. **DG Set Optimization** — right-size DG sets, run at **> 50% loading**, install **biogas dual-fuel kits** for hospitality sector.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` (not the vertical pipe `|`) inside the formula cells below to avoid breaking the markdown table.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol / Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heat transfer through envelope | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | W | Conduction load calculation |
| 2 | Solar heat gain through glass | $Q_{solar} = A_{glass} \cdot SHGC \cdot I_{solar}$ | W | Cooling load from glazing |
| 3 | Energy Performance Index | $EPI = E_{annual} / A_{floor}$ | kWh/m²·yr | Building star rating |
| 4 | Lighting Power Density | $LPD = P_{total} / A_{floor}$ | W/m² | ECBC compliance check |
| 5 | Coefficient of Performance (COP) | $COP = Q_{cooling} / W_{input}$ | dimensionless | Chiller / AC efficiency |
| 6 | Energy Efficiency Ratio (EER) | $EER = Q_{cooling,Btu/h} / W_{input}$ | Btu/Wh | AC rating comparison |
| 7 | Seasonal COP / ISEER | $SCOP = \sum Q_{cooling} / \sum W_{input}$ | dimensionless | Real AC performance |
| 8 | Annual Cooling Energy | $E_{cooling} = Q_{cooling} \cdot hrs / 1000$ | kWh | Energy-saving calculation |
| 9 | Percent Saving | $\%S = (E_{before} - E_{after}) / E_{before} \times 100$ | % | ECO quantification |
| 10 | Simple Payback Period | $SPP = \Delta I / \Delta S$ | years | Economic feasibility |
| 11 | Net Present Value | $NPV = -I_0 + \sum \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | ₹ | Long-term viability |
| 12 | Internal Rate of Return | $NPV(r) = 0$ | % | Comparing mutually exclusive ECOs |
| 13 | Fan / Pump Affinity Law (Flow) | $Q_2 / Q_1 = (N_2 / N_1)$ | — | VFD savings |
| 14 | Fan / Pump Affinity Law (Power) | $P_2 / P_1 = (N_2 / N_1)^3$ | — | Cubic power reduction with speed |
| 15 | Effective Temperature Setpoint Lift | $\%S_{AC} \approx 6\% \text{ per } 1\,°C$ rise | % | AC setpoint reset ECO |
| 16 | Chiller Plant Load Factor | $LF = Q_{actual} / Q_{rated}$ | dimensionless | Chiller part-load efficiency |
| 17 | Boiler Efficiency (Direct) | $\eta = Q_{output} / Q_{input} \times 100$ | % | Furnace / boiler audit |
| 18 | Stack Heat Loss | $\%L = (T_{flue} - T_{amb}) / (T_{flue} - T_{amb,ref}) \times f$ | % | Combustion ECOs |
| 19 | PF Penalty / Bonus | $kVA = kW / PF$ | kVA | Electricity bill optimization |
| 20 | Solar PV Energy Yield | $E_{PV} = P_{peak} \cdot PSH \cdot \eta_{sys}$ | kWh | Rooftop PV sizing |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

These ECOs translate into **measurable economic value** in every KTU graduate's professional life. A junior energy auditor at a BEE-empanelled firm will quantify these savings; an HVAC design engineer at a consultancy will apply ECBC limits; a maintenance engineer at an IT park will operate a BMS; a sustainability consultant will prepare green-building documentation (GRIHA, LEED, IGBC). The ECO framework is the **lingua franca** of the Indian energy-services sector.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Conduction Heat Gain Through a Composite Building Wall

A typical Indian residential **non-TERI wall** consists of the following layers (outside to inside):

1. **20 mm cement plaster** (outer)
2. **200 mm brick masonry** (common burnt-clay brick)
3. **12 mm cement plaster** (inner)

Let us derive the total **U-factor** (Overall Thermal Transmittance) and the cooling load through a 10 m × 3 m east-facing wall (A = 30 m²) when the outside temperature is 38 °C and inside is 26 °C.

### 3.1.1 Material Thermal Conductivities (k)

| Material | k (W/m·K) | Thickness x (m) | R = x / k (m²·K/W) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Outer plaster | 0.72 | 0.020 | 0.0278 |
| Brick wall | 0.81 | 0.200 | 0.2469 |
| Inner plaster | 0.72 | 0.012 | 0.0167 |
| **R_total (conduction)** | — | — | **0.2914** |
| R_i (inside air film) | — | — | 0.1300 |
| R_o (outside air film) | — | — | 0.0400 |
| **R_total (including films)** | — | — | **0.4614** |

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Calculation

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{total} &= R_{o} + R_{plaster,o} + R_{brick} + R_{plaster,i} + R_{i} \\
&= 0.0400 + 0.0278 + 0.2469 + 0.0167 + 0.1300 \\
&= 0.4614 \; \text{m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= \frac{1}{R_{total}} = \frac{1}{0.4614} = 2.167 \; \text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The ECBC 2017 limit for walls is **0.44 W/m²·K**. Our uninsulated wall at **2.167 W/m²·K** is nearly **5 times worse** than the ECBC limit — a clear ECO opportunity.

Now the heat transfer Q through this wall:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T \\
&= 2.167 \times 30 \times (38 - 26) \\
&= 2.167 \times 30 \times 12 \\
&= 780.2 \; \text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

So this single wall leaks **780 W** of heat into the room continuously.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem — ECO Quantification: VFD on AHU Fan

### Problem Statement
A commercial office building has an **Air-Handling Unit (AHU)** with a **15 kW fan motor** that runs continuously for **12 hours/day, 300 days/year**. The fan is damped at the inlet and operates at **80% flow** for **70% of the operating hours** and at **100% flow** for the remaining 30%. The utility rate is **₹ 8.00/kWh**.

An ECO is proposed: replace the inlet damper with a **Variable Frequency Drive (VFD)**. Estimate the annual energy saved and the simple payback if the VFD cost is **₹ 2,40,000**.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Baseline Energy Consumption**

Case A (80% flow, 70% of hours):
Power fraction using fan affinity law (Power ∝ Flow³):

$$
P_{80\%} / P_{100\%} = (0.80)^3 = 0.512
$$

Hours = 12 × 300 × 0.70 = 2520 hours

$$
E_A = 15 \times 0.512 \times 2520 = 19{,}353.6 \; \text{kWh}
$$

Case B (100% flow, 30% of hours):
Power fraction = 1.0
Hours = 12 × 300 × 0.30 = 1080 hours

$$
E_B = 15 \times 1.0 \times 1080 = 16{,}200 \; \text{kWh}
$$

$$
E_{baseline} = E_A + E_B = 19{,}353.6 + 16{,}200 = 35{,}553.6 \; \text{kWh}
$$

**Step 2 — Energy Consumption with VFD**

With VFD, motor speed tracks the flow (Power ∝ Speed³, Speed ∝ Flow). Plus a typical **inverter efficiency** of **97%** and **motor efficiency at reduced load** of approximately **92%** (assume combined drive loss = 6%).

Energy saved in Case A: 70% of motor power is no longer wasted in damping.

$$
E_{A,VFD} = 15 \times 0.512 \times 0.97 \times 0.94 \times 2520 \approx 18{,}300 \; \text{kWh}
$$

(Higher accuracy accounts for actual VFD efficiency curve; for KTU exam we may approximate.)

Energy saved in Case B: VFD operates near nominal speed; marginal loss is **~3%**.

$$
E_{B,VFD} = 15 \times 1.0 \times 0.97 \times 1080 \approx 15{,}714 \; \text{kWh}
$$

$$
E_{after} \approx 18{,}300 + 15{,}714 = 34{,}014 \; \text{kWh}
$$

**Step 3 — Annual Energy Saved**

$$
\Delta E = E_{baseline} - E_{after} = 35{,}553.6 - 34{,}014 = 1{,}540 \; \text{kWh/year}
$$

Wait — this looks small. **Recheck the assumption**: The VFD does *not* just remove damping loss; it directly reduces fan speed to deliver 80% flow, so power becomes truly 0.512 × rated, **without the damping loss component**. The actual saved power at 80% flow vs damped baseline is the difference between **damped full power (1.0)** and **VFD reduced speed power (0.512)**.

For 80% flow hours:

$$
\Delta P_{A} = 15 \times (1.0 - 0.512) = 15 \times 0.488 = 7.32 \; \text{kW}
$$

$$
\Delta E_A = 7.32 \times 2520 = 18{,}446.4 \; \text{kWh}
$$

For 100% flow hours, VFD provides negligible savings (~2–3% due to soft-start and current limit):

$$
\Delta E_B = 15 \times 0.03 \times 1080 = 486 \; \text{kWh}
$$

$$
\Delta E_{annual} = 18{,}446.4 + 486 = 18{,}932 \; \text{kWh/year}
$$

**Step 4 — Annual Monetary Savings**

$$
\Delta S = 18{,}932 \times 8.00 = ₹ 1{,}51{,}456 \; \text{per year}
$$

**Step 5 — Simple Payback Period**

$$
SPP = \frac{\Delta I}{\Delta S} = \frac{2{,}40{,}000}{1{,}51{,}456} \approx 1.58 \; \text{years}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always show the cubic affinity law explicitly; the cubes of 0.8 (= 0.512) and the resulting savings are the heart of the question.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem — Air-Conditioner Setpoint Reset ECO

### Problem Statement
A 1.5 TR (5.275 kW cooling) split AC operates for **8 months/year, 12 hours/day**. The owner currently sets the thermostat at **22 °C**. BEE recommends **26 °C**. Estimate the annual energy saved and money saved (₹ 8.00/kWh) if the owner resets the thermostat to 26 °C. Assume each 1 °C rise in setpoint saves 6% of cooling energy.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Baseline Annual Energy**

$$
E_{baseline} = 5.275 \; \text{kW} \times 12 \; \text{hr/day} \times 30 \times 8 = 15{,}192 \; \text{kWh}
$$

**Step 2 — Temperature Lift**

$$
\Delta T_{set} = 26 - 22 = 4 \,°C
$$

**Step 3 — Percent Energy Saved**

$$
\%S = 4 \times 6\% = 24\%
$$

**Step 4 — Annual Energy Saved**

$$
\Delta E = 15{,}192 \times 0.24 = 3{,}646.08 \; \text{kWh}
$$

**Step 5 — Annual Cost Saved**

$$
\Delta S = 3{,}646.08 \times 8.00 = ₹ 29{,}168.64 \; \text{per year}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> No investment is required; the SPP is **immediate** — a classic **Category I No-Cost ECO** used in every BEE audit.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Major ECOs Ranked by Payback & Saving Potential

| # | ECO Measure | Applicable Building | Annual Saving Potential | Indicative Payback | Capital Intensity |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | AC setpoint reset to 24–26 °C | Residential / Commercial | 5–24% of cooling bill | 0 (No-Cost) | Nil |
| 2 | LED retrofit (T5/T8 → LED) | Both | 50–60% of lighting bill | 1–2 yr | Low |
| 3 | Occupancy sensors | Both | 15–30% of lighting bill | 0.5–1.5 yr | Low |
| 4 | VFD on AHU / pump motor | Commercial | 25–50% of fan/pump bill | 1.5–3 yr | Medium |
| 5 | 5-star AC / chiller replacement | Both | 25–40% of cooling bill | 3–6 yr | High |
| 6 | DGU / low-E glazing | Both | 8–12% of cooling bill | 7–12 yr | High |
| 7 | Roof insulation (XPS / PUF) | Residential | 6–10% of cooling bill | 2–4 yr | Medium |
| 8 | Solar water heating system | Both (hospitals, hostels) | 60–80% of water-heating bill | 3–6 yr | Medium |
| 9 | Power Factor correction to 0.98 | Commercial | kVA penalty avoided (₹/kVA) | 0.5–1 yr | Low |
| 10 | BMS / smart controls | Commercial | 8–15% of total bill | 4–7 yr | High |
| 11 | Rooftop Solar PV | Both | 80–100% of daytime bill offset | 4–6 yr | High |
| 12 | Heat-recovery ventilator | Commercial | 10–20% of AHU bill | 3–5 yr | Medium |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Building Energy Flow Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid

    B[Building Boundary<br/>Envelope Walls Roof Glazing Doors]:::envNode

    G[Occupants + Internal Gains<br/>People Lights Equipment Heat]:::gainNode
    H[Indoor Conditioned Space<br/>Comfort Zone 24 to 26 C]:::comfortNode
    I[Waste Heat Rejection<br/>To Atmosphere]:::wasteNode
    J[Energy Metering and Submetering<br/>BMS Energy Audit]:::meterNode

    A --> J
    J --> B

```

## 4.2 ECO Classification Tree (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question Paper, Module 4, Q1.a]**
> **Q1. Define the term "Energy Performance Index (EPI)" of a building. Mention typical EPI ranges for an Indian residential and an Indian commercial building.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Energy Performance Index (EPI) of a building is defined as the annual electrical energy consumed (in kWh) per unit built-up floor area (in m²) of the building, expressed in **kWh/m²/year**. It is the single most important metric used by the Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) to rate the energy efficiency of a building.

$$
EPI = \frac{E_{annual} \; \text{(kWh)}}{A_{floor} \; \text{(m}^2\text{)}}
$$

Typical values:

- Indian **residential** building: **50 – 150 kWh/m²/year**
- Indian **commercial** building: **150 – 400 kWh/m²/year**

A lower EPI indicates a more energy-efficient building, and is rewarded with a higher BEE star rating.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Q1.b]**
> **Q2. What are the three broad categories of Energy Conservation Opportunities (ECOs) classified by the cost of implementation? Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The three categories of ECOs classified by cost are:

1. **No-Cost / Low-Cost ECOs (Category I):** Measures requiring no capital investment. *Example:* Resetting the AC thermostat from 22 °C to 26 °C — every 1 °C rise saves ~6% of cooling energy.

2. **Medium-Cost ECOs (Category II):** Retrofits with modest investment and a payback of 3 months to 2 years. *Example:* Replacing existing T8 fluorescent tubes with LED battens, which have 50–60% lower energy consumption.

3. **High-Cost / Investment ECOs (Category III):** Major system overhauls with paybacks of 2 to 7 years. *Example:* Installing a Variable-Frequency Drive (VFD) on the chilled-water pump motor of a central AC plant.

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Q7, End-Semester, 14 Marks]**

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**(a)** *With a neat block diagram, explain the major Energy Conservation Opportunities (ECOs) in a **commercial building**, classified by end-use. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *The roof of a commercial building in Kochi (coastal humid climate) measures 30 m × 20 m and consists of 100 mm RCC slab. The thermal conductivity of RCC is 1.58 W/m·K, the inside air film resistance is 0.17 m²·K/W, and the outside air film resistance is 0.04 m²·K/W. Calculate the cooling load on the roof when the outside dry-bulb temperature is 34 °C and the inside is 24 °C. If 50 mm thick PUF insulation (k = 0.024 W/m·K) is added, calculate the percentage reduction in cooling load. (7 marks)*

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**(a)** *Discuss in detail the **building envelope ECOs** that a homeowner can adopt in a Kerala residential house to reduce annual cooling energy consumption. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *A 1.5 TR (1 TR = 3.517 kW) split AC operates for 8 months in a year, 10 hours per day. The owner currently sets the thermostat at 20 °C. (i) Estimate the annual energy consumed in kWh. (ii) Estimate the annual energy saved if the thermostat is reset to 26 °C. (iii) Calculate the annual monetary saving at ₹ 9.00/kWh. Assume each 1 °C setpoint rise saves 6% of cooling energy. (7 marks)*

---

### ✅ Model Solutions for Question A

#### Part (a) — Solution: Commercial Building ECOs (7 marks)

A commercial building's energy consumption is dominated by HVAC (~40–50%), lighting (~20–30%), plug loads (~15–20%), and auxiliary systems (lifts, pumps, fans) (~5–10%). The end-use classified ECOs are:

1. **HVAC ECOs** (4 sub-measures, 2 marks)
   - Chiller plant optimization (CHW reset, CW reset, VFD on chillers).
   - Variable Air Volume (VAV) and VFD on AHU fans and pumps.
   - Economiser / free-cooling cycle during low ambient conditions.
   - Heat-recovery wheels on exhaust air.

2. **Lighting ECOs** (2 marks)
   - LED retrofit with LPD compliance to ECBC caps.
   - Occupancy sensors, daylight dimming, and task-ambient zoning.

3. **Plug-Load & Equipment ECOs** (1 mark)
   - Energy-efficient IT equipment (Energy Star 5.0 servers), smart power strips, sleep-mode policies.

4. **Auxiliary System ECOs** (1 mark)
   - VFDs on lift motors, EC fans, capacitor banks for PF improvement to 0.98.

5. **Envelope & Renewable ECOs** (1 mark)
   - ECBC-compliant glazing, roof insulation, and rooftop solar PV (net-metered).

*(A clear block diagram of the kind in Section 4.1 should be drawn by the student.)*

#### Part (b) — Solution: Roof Cooling Load Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Roof area A = 30 × 20 = 600 m²
- Roof material: 100 mm RCC, k_RCC = 1.58 W/m·K
- R_i = 0.17 m²·K/W, R_o = 0.04 m²·K/W
- ΔT = 34 – 24 = 10 K

**Step 1 — R-value of bare roof**

$$
R_{RCC} = \frac{0.100}{1.58} = 0.0633 \; \text{m}^2\text{K/W}
$$

$$
R_{total,bare} = R_o + R_{RCC} + R_i = 0.04 + 0.0633 + 0.17 = 0.2733 \; \text{m}^2\text{K/W}
$$

**Step 2 — U-value of bare roof**

$$
U_{bare} = \frac{1}{0.2733} = 3.659 \; \text{W/m}^2\text{K}
$$

**Step 3 — Cooling load (bare roof)**

$$
Q_{bare} = U_{bare} \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 3.659 \times 600 \times 10 = 21{,}956 \; \text{W} \approx 21.96 \; \text{kW}
$$

**Step 4 — R-value of PUF insulation layer**

$$
R_{PUF} = \frac{0.050}{0.024} = 2.0833 \; \text{m}^2\text{K/W}
$$

**Step 5 — Total R-value with insulation**

$$
R_{total,ins} = 0.04 + 0.0633 + 2.0833 + 0.17 = 2.3566 \; \text{m}^2\text{K/W}
$$

**Step 6 — U-value with insulation**

$$
U_{ins} = \frac{1}{2.3566} = 0.4243 \; \text{W/m}^2\text{K}
$$

**Step 7 — Cooling load (insulated)**

$$
Q_{ins} = 0.4243 \times 600 \times 10 = 2{,}545.8 \; \text{W} \approx 2.55 \; \text{kW}
$$

**Step 8 — Percentage reduction**

$$
\%S = \frac{21.96 - 2.55}{21.96} \times 100 = 88.4\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Roof Load Problem:**
> - Students frequently **forget the air-film resistances** R_i and R_o. Without them, the answer is grossly off. **Always include both film resistances.**
> - Never compare U-values directly without computing actual Q in W. A unitless percentage is meaningless without ΔT.
> - Carry the decimal to at least 3 places; rounding 0.4243 to 0.4 will lose 1 mark.

### ✅ Model Solutions for Question B

#### Part (a) — Solution: Residential Envelope ECOs (7 marks)

A Kerala homeowner has a high humidity load from the monsoon (June–September) and a high sensible cooling load from pre-monsoon summer (March–May). The recommended envelope ECOs are:

1. **Roof insulation (1.5 marks):** Apply 50 mm PUF or XPS on the terrace; this reduces roof cooling load by up to **88%** (as shown in the worked example above). Add a reflective coating (SRI ≥ 78) for additional benefit.

2. **Wall insulation (1.5 marks):** Use cavity-wall insulation in hollow-block construction; or external thermal insulation plaster system (EIFS) for a 200–230 mm laterite-stone wall — improves U-value from 2.1 to ~0.5 W/m²·K.

3. **Glazing (2 marks):** Replace plain 3 mm glass with double-glazed units (6 mm + 12 mm air gap + 6 mm) and low-E coating (SHGC ≤ 0.25). Add **chajjas** of width 600–800 mm to block direct summer sun on south and west windows.

4. **Air-tightness (1 mark):** Weather-strip the main door and all windows; use door curtains. Infiltration of 1 m³/h per metre crack length is responsible for ~15% of cooling load.

5. **Cross-ventilation design (1 mark):** Orient the long axis east–west; provide 25–30% window-to-wall ratio on the prevailing wind (south-west in Kerala) side; use stack-effect clerestory windows in high ceilings (3.3 m+).

#### Part (b) — Solution: Setpoint Reset ECO (7 marks)

**Given:**
- 1.5 TR AC → Q_cooling = 1.5 × 3.517 = **5.275 kW**
- 8 months × 30 days × 10 hours = **2400 hours/year**
- Current setpoint = 20 °C
- Proposed setpoint = 26 °C
- ΔT_set = 6 °C
- % saving per °C = 6%
- Tariff = ₹ 9.00/kWh

**Step 1 — Annual baseline energy (at 20 °C setpoint)**

$$
E_{baseline} = 5.275 \times 2400 = 12{,}660 \; \text{kWh}
$$

**Step 2 — Total percent energy saved**

$$
\%S = 6 \times 6\% = 36\%
$$

**Step 3 — Annual energy saved**

$$
\Delta E = 12{,}660 \times 0.36 = 4{,}557.6 \; \text{kWh}
$$

**Step 4 — Annual monetary saving**

$$
\Delta S = 4{,}557.6 \times 9.00 = ₹ 41{,}018.40 \; \text{per year}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Setpoint Reset Problem:**
> - 1 TR = **3.517 kW** (not 3.5 kW). Many students write 3.5 and lose a mark.
> - Always explicitly state that this is a **No-Cost ECO** with **SPP = 0**; examiners reward this conclusion.
> - The "6% per °C" rule is an empirical approximation, not a physics law — quote the assumption.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — ECOs in Buildings**

- **Definition Block:**
  - **ECBC** applies to commercial buildings with connected load ≥ **500 kW** or contract demand ≥ **600 kVA**, and ≥ 50% conditioned area.
  - **EPI** = annual kWh / m² — primary metric for BEE star rating of buildings.
  - **LPD** = total lighting wattage / floor area — capped by ECBC.

- **ECBC Numerical Limits (memorize):**
  - Roof: U ≤ **0.33 W/m²·K**
  - Wall: U ≤ **0.44 W/m²·K**
  - Vertical glazing: U ≤ **3.30 W/m²·K**, SHGC ≤ **0.25 / 0.40**
  - Office LPD ≤ **10.76 W/m²**

- **Three ECO Categories (interview & viva-favourite):**
  - **No-Cost** → SPP ≈ 0 → AC setpoint reset, switch-off policy.
  - **Medium-Cost** → SPP 0.25–2 yr → LED, VFD, sensors.
  - **High-Cost** → SPP 2–7 yr → BMS, ECBC façade, rooftop solar.

- **Affinity Laws (must be memorized for problem-solving):**
  - Flow ∝ Speed → $Q_2 / Q_1 = N_2 / N_1$
  - Head ∝ Speed² → $H_2 / H_1 = (N_2 / N_1)^2$
  - Power ∝ Speed³ → $P_2 / P_1 = (N_2 / N_1)^3$

- **Useful Mnemonics:**
  - **HEAL-P**: HVAC, Envelope, Appliances, Lighting, People (5 end-uses).
  - **6%-per-°C**: AC setpoint rule of thumb.
  - **1 TR = 3.517 kW**: not 3.5.

- **Mandatory Compliance Frameworks in India:**
  - **ECBC 2007** (commercial new construction).
  - **ECBC+ 2017** (25% more efficient than ECBC).
  - **SuperECBC 2017** (50% more efficient than ECBC).
  - **BEE Star Rating Programme for Existing Buildings** (1 to 5 stars).
  - **PAT Scheme** designates large buildings as DCs.

- **Common BEE / SDA Reference Standards:**
  - IS 3792 (HVAC insulation), IS 1391 (chillers), IS 10322 (luminaire performance).
  - ECBC User Guide by BEE.

- **Energy Conversions (always keep ready):**
  - 1 TR = 3.517 kW = 12,000 BTU/h
  - 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ = 860 kcal
  - 1 toe = 11,630 kWh

- **Real-World Examples to Cite in Answers:**
  - Infosys Pocharam campus: net-positive energy office, India.
  - CII–Sohrabji Godrej Green Business Centre, Hyderabad: IGBC Platinum.
  - GRIHA and LEED-India certification for high-performance commercial buildings.

- **Viva-Voice Favourite Questions:**
  - Why is the AC setpoint 24–26 °C and not 18 °C? → Human thermal-comfort bands; BEE guideline.
  - Why is the AC star rating different from chiller COP? → Star rating is **ISEER**; chiller is **COP / IPLV**.
  - Why DG sets are part of building energy audit? → They form the **captive power** source, billed at ₹ 18–24/kWh.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Conservation Measures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBBT614 - ENERGY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Energy Conservation Measures # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs):** Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs) are planned, engineered, or behavioural modifications implemented in a facility, process, or system that are designed to **reduce energy consumption** while maintaining or improving the quality of output, service, or production. These measures form the actionable output of an **Energy Audit** and are evaluated against the **triple-bottom-line criteria** of energy savings, cost savings, and implementation feasibility. **Related Core Definitions:** - **Energy Conservation (EC):** The practice of *reducing* the quantity of energy consumed by a system, process, or organization, achieved either through **technical improvements** (efficiency upgrades) or **behavioural changes** (operational discipline). - **Energy Efficiency (η):** The ratio of useful output energy/service to the input energy supplied, expressed as a dimensionless fraction or percentage. - **Energy Audit:** A systematic, documented procedure that quantifies energy usage, identifies wastage, and recommends ECMs with quantified savings. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE), Government of India, classifies industries under the **Perform, Achieve and Trade (PAT)** scheme. The Energy Conservation Act (2001) mandates Designated Consumers (DCs) to conduct mandatory energy audits and implement ECMs. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Overview) Imagine you own a **leaking water tap at home**. The tap represents an industrial process losing energy through: - **Invisible leaks** → Radiation, convection, friction losses (low η) - **Visible splashes** → Wasted heat, exhaust gases, idle running - **Old pipes** → Inefficient motors, outdated boilers, corroded insulation Energy Conservation Measures are like a **plumber's checklist**: 1. **Tighten the tap** (Operational changes — free/low cost) 2. **Replace old washers** (Maintenance upgrades — low cost) 3. **Install a new aerator nozzle** (Technology retrofit — moderate cost) 4. **Switch to a sensor tap** (Advanced automation — high cost, high payback) Each measure has a **cost** (capex) and a **return** (annual savings). The plumber — analogous to a **Certified Energy Auditor** — prioritizes measures that save the most water (energy) for the lowest cost, typically measured by **Simple Payback Period**. --- ## 1.3 Classification of Energy Conservation Measures (ECM Categories) ECMs are broadly categorized under the **"Three Tier ECM Framework"**: | Tier | Category | Capital Investment | Typical Payback | Examples | |:----:|:---------|:------------------:|:----------------:|:---------| | **Tier 1** | **Low-Cost / No-Cost (Operational)** | Negligible / Free | < 1 year | Switching off idle equipment, adjusting setpoints, housekeeping | | **Tier 2** | **Medium-Cost (Retrofit)** | Moderate | 1–3 years | VFD installation, insulation, LED retrofit, capacitor banks | | **Tier 3** | **High-Cost (Capital Intensive)** | High | 3–6 years | Cogeneration, waste heat recovery boiler, boiler replacement | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Energy Savings Equation (Foundation):** > $$\text{Energy Saved} = \text{Energy Before ECM} - \text{Energy After ECM}$$ > $$\text{\% Savings} = \frac{\text{Energy Saved}}{\text{Energy Before ECM}} \times 100$$ --- ## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in ECM Evaluation > [!NOTE] > **Key Engineering Metrics (Bold for emphasis):** > - **Simple Payback Period (SPP):** Time (in years) for cumulative savings to equal the initial investment. > - **Net Present Value (NPV):** Discounted lifetime cash flows minus initial investment. > - **Internal Rate of Return (IRR):** Discount rate at which NPV equals zero. > - **Return on Investment (ROI):** Annual return as a percentage of investment. > - **Cost of Conserved Energy (CCE):** ₹ / kWh saved — directly comparable to tariff. --- ## 1.5 Energy Conservation Measures — Domain Application Map ECMs are implemented across **six major engineering domains**: 1. **Electrical Systems** — Motors, transformers, lighting, HVAC 2. **Thermal Systems** — Boilers, furnaces, steam networks, insulation 3. **Mechanical Systems** — Compressed air, pumps, fans, blowers 4. **Process Systems** — Cogeneration, waste heat recovery, process integration 5. **Building Envelope** — Roof insulation, window glazing, daylight harvesting 6. **Renewable Integration** — Solar PV, solar thermal, biomass gasifiers # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Energy Audit → ECM Implementation Cycle The ECM pipeline follows the **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) cycle**, formalized in **ISO 50001 (Energy Management Systems)**: 1. **Plan (Energy Review):** Baseline establishment, Energy Performance Indicators (EnPIs) defined. 2. **Do (Implementation):** ECM execution, measurement & verification (M&V) infrastructure deployed. 3. **Check (Review):** Compare actual savings against predicted savings using **IPMVP** protocols. 4. **Act (Improvement):** Standardize successful measures, abandon ineffective ones, plan next cycle. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All High-Yield Formulas for Energy Conservation Measures** ### A. Economic & Financial Metrics | Formula | Equation | Variables & Units | |:--------|:---------|:-----------------| | **Simple Payback Period** | $\text{SPP} = \dfrac{I}{S}$ | $I$ = Investment (₹), $S$ = Annual Savings (₹/yr) | | **Return on Investment** | $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{S}{I} \times 100$ | All in ₹ (dimensionless %) | | **Net Present Value** | $\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{S_t}{(1+r)^t} - I$ | $S_t$ = Savings in year $t$, $r$ = Discount rate, $n$ = Life | | **Cost of Conserved Energy** | $\text{CCE} = \dfrac{I \times \text{CRF}}{E_{saved}}$ | CRF = Capital Recovery Factor, $E_{saved}$ = kWh/yr | | **Capital Recovery Factor** | $\text{CRF} = \dfrac{r(1+r)^n}{(1+r)^n - 1}$ | $r$ = discount rate, $n$ = project life | | **Internal Rate of Return** | $\text{NPV}(r = \text{IRR}) = 0$ | Solved iteratively | ### B. Electrical Efficiency Formulas | Formula | Equation | Use Case | |:--------|:---------|:---------| | **Motor Efficiency** | $\eta_m = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + \text{Losses}}$ | Motor sizing | | **Power Factor Correction Savings** | $\Delta P = P \left( \tan \phi_1 - \tan \phi_2 \right)$ | Capacitor bank design | | **Lighting Energy** | $E_L = \dfrac{\text{Area} \times \text{lux} \times \text{hours}}{\text{Lumens per Watt} \times \text{CU} \times \text{LLF}}$ | Lumen method | | **HVAC Load Reduction** | $Q_{saved} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{\text{eff}}$ | Insulation retrofit | | **VFD Energy Savings** | $E_{VFD} = k \cdot \int_{0}^{T} \left( n_1^3 - n_2^3 \right) dt$ | Affinity law for fans/pumps | ### C. Thermal Efficiency Formulas | Formula | Equation | Variables | |:--------|:---------|:----------| | **Boiler Efficiency** | $\eta_b = \dfrac{Q_{steam}}{m_{fuel} \times \text{GCV}}$ | Direct method | | **Heat Loss through Wall** | $Q = \dfrac{A \cdot \Delta T}{\dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{x}{k} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}}$ | Conduction + convection | | **Heat Recovery Savings** | $E_{HR} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T \cdot \eta_{HR}$ | Waste heat boiler | | **Steam Trap Loss** | $L_{steam} = m_{leak} \times h_{fg}$ | Steam audit | > [!WARNING] > **Avoid Pipe Symbol Pitfall:** In markdown tables, always use `\vert` or `\mid` (not `|`) for absolute values. Example: write $\vert V_L \vert$ not $|V_L|$. --- ## 2.3 Affinity Laws for Variable Speed ECMs (Pumps, Fans, Compressors) For centrifugal equipment, when speed changes from $N_1$ to $N_2$: $$\boxed{\dfrac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1}, \quad \dfrac{H_2}{H_1} = \left( \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} \right)^2, \quad \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \left( \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} \right)^3}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** Reducing fan/pump speed by just **20%** saves nearly **49%** of electrical power. This is why **Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs)** are among the most widely recommended ECMs in industrial audits. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of ECMs | Engineering Field | Practical ECM Application | Typical Savings | |:------------------|:--------------------------|:----------------| | **Cement Industry** | Preheater kiln optimization, waste heat recovery (WHR) | 15–25% thermal | | **Steel Plants** | Coke dry quenching, top recovery turbine (TRT) | 10–20% electrical | | **Textile Mills** | Heat recovery from exhaust, motor retrofit | 12–18% electrical | | **Commercial Buildings** | LED + occupancy sensors + star-rated HVAC | 30–40% electrical | | **Pulp & Paper** | Black liquor evaporation, steam network balancing | 20–30% thermal | --- ## 2.5 Decision Hierarchy of ECM Selection The **BEE / ISO 50001 decision tree** for ECM prioritization: 1. **Eliminate** unnecessary energy use (turn off, decommission) 2. **Reduce** demand (right-sizing, setpoint optimization) 3. **Improve** efficiency (retrofit, technology upgrade) 4. **Substitute** energy source (renewable, waste heat recovery) 5. **Generate** efficiently (cogeneration, trigeneration) This hierarchy ensures that **free/low-cost measures** are exhausted before capital-intensive solutions are deployed. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Net Present Value (NPV) for ECM Evaluation The NPV method accounts for the **time value of money**, ensuring future savings are properly discounted to present-day value. ### Step 1: Recognize the Time Value of Money Principle A rupee today is worth more than a rupee next year because today's rupee can be **invested** to earn interest. The **Present Value (PV)** of ₹1 received $t$ years hence at discount rate $r$ is: $$PV = \dfrac{1}{(1+r)^t}$$ > **Conversion Logic:** Future cash flow must be "compressed" back through the discount factor to compare with today's investment. --- ### Step 2: Discount All Future Savings to Present Value Assuming annual savings $S_t$ in year $t$ (often constant $= S$ if no escalation), the present value of total savings over project life $n$ years is: $$\begin{aligned} PV_{\text{savings}} &= \dfrac{S_1}{(1+r)^1} + \dfrac{S_2}{(1+r)^2} + \cdots + \dfrac{S_n}{(1+r)^n} \\ PV_{\text{savings}} &= \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{S_t}{(1+r)^t} \end{aligned}$$ --- ### Step 3: Subtract the Initial Investment The **Net Present Value (NPV)** is the difference between the present value of total savings and the initial capital investment $I$: $$\boxed{\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{S_t}{(1+r)^t} - I}$$ > **Decision Criterion:** Accept the ECM **only if** $\text{NPV} > 0$. --- ### Step 4: Apply Capital Recovery Factor (CRF) — Simplified Form When savings are uniform ($S_t = S$ for all $t$), the geometric series sums to: $$\sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{(1+r)^t} = \dfrac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r(1+r)^n} = \dfrac{1}{\text{CRF}}$$ Therefore: $$\text{NPV} = \dfrac{S}{\text{CRF}} - I$$ where: $$\text{CRF} = \dfrac{r(1+r)^n}{(1+r)^n - 1}$$ --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Complete ECM Evaluation > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A factory is evaluating an ECM to install a **Variable Frequency Drive (VFD)** on a 30 kW induction motor driving a centrifugal pump. Data: > - Initial Investment $I$ = ₹ 2,50,000 > - Estimated Annual Savings $S$ = ₹ 85,000 > - Project Life $n$ = 8 years > - Discount Rate $r$ = 12% (0.12) > - Tax effects ignored > > **Evaluate** Simple Payback, NPV, IRR, and CCE. State if the ECM is financially viable. ### Step A: Simple Payback Period (SPP) $$\text{SPP} = \dfrac{I}{S} = \dfrac{2{,}50{,}000}{85{,}000}$$ $$\boxed{\text{SPP} = 2.94 \text{ years}}$$ > **Interpretation:** The investment is recovered in roughly **3 years**, well within the 8-year equipment life. ✅ --- ### Step B: Capital Recovery Factor (CRF) at $r = 0.12$, $n = 8$ $$\begin{aligned} \text{CRF} &= \dfrac{0.12 (1.12)^8}{(1.12)^8 - 1} \\ (1.12)^8 &= 2.4760 \quad \text{(computed iteratively or by table)} \\ \text{CRF} &= \dfrac{0.12 \times 2.4760}{2.4760 - 1} = \dfrac{0.29712}{1.4760} \\ \boxed{\text{CRF}} &= 0.20130 \end{aligned}$$ --- ### Step C: Net Present Value (NPV) $$\begin{aligned} \text{NPV} &= \dfrac{S}{\text{CRF}} - I \\ \text{NPV} &= \dfrac{85{,}000}{0.20130} - 2{,}50{,}000 \\ \text{NPV} &= 4{,}22{,}254 - 2{,}50{,}000 \\ \boxed{\text{NPV}} &= \text{₹ } 1{,}72{,}254 \end{aligned}$$ > **Interpretation:** Positive NPV ⇒ ECM is **financially viable**. ✅ --- ### Step D: Cost of Conserved Energy (CCE) Assume annual energy savings $E_{saved} = 28{,}000$ kWh/yr (after VFD throttling). $$\begin{aligned} \text{CCE} &= \dfrac{I \times \text{CRF}}{E_{saved}} \\ \text{CCE} &= \dfrac{2{,}50{,}000 \times 0.20130}{28{,}000} \\ \text{CCE} &= \dfrac{50{,}325}{28{,}000} \\ \boxed{\text{CCE}} &= \text{₹ } 1.80 \text{ per kWh} \end{aligned}$$ > **Interpretation:** If grid tariff > ₹ 1.80/kWh, the ECM is **economically sound**. ✅ (Most industrial tariffs in Kerala exceed ₹ 6/kWh.) --- ### Step E: Internal Rate of Return (IRR) — Approximate Using rule of approximation: $\text{IRR} \approx \text{ROI}$ for short payback: $$\text{ROI} = \dfrac{85{,}000}{2{,}50{,}000} \times 100 = 34\%$$ Since IRR (≈34%) > discount rate (12%), the project is **accepted** by IRR criterion as well. ✅ --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Lighting Energy Reduction using Lumen Method The **Lumen Method** computes actual lighting energy required for a given illuminance level: $$E_L = \dfrac{A \times E_{avg} \times t}{\eta_{lum} \times \text{CU} \times \text{LLF}}$$ where: - $A$ = Area of work plane (m²) - $E_{avg}$ = Required illuminance (lux) - $t$ = Operating hours per year - $\eta_{lum}$ = Lamp luminous efficacy (lumens/W) - $\text{CU}$ = Coefficient of Utilization (0 to 1) - $\text{LLF}$ = Light Loss Factor (0 to 1) ### ECM Comparison: Fluorescent (T8, 60 lm/W) vs LED (130 lm/W) $$\begin{aligned} \text{Energy}_{\text{LED}} &= \dfrac{A \times E_{avg} \times t}{130 \times \text{CU} \times \text{LLF}} \\ \text{Energy}_{\text{T8}} &= \dfrac{A \times E_{avg} \times t}{60 \times \text{CU} \times \text{LLF}} \\ \% \text{ Savings} &= \left(1 - \dfrac{60}{130}\right) \times 100 = 53.85\% \end{aligned}$$ > **Engineering Utility:** LED retrofits are **Tier-2 ECMs** with typical payback of 1.5–3 years and lifetime of 50,000 hours. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: ECM Selection Decision Table | Decision Parameter | Weight | Sub-Criteria | Threshold | |:-------------------|:------:|:-------------|:----------| | **SPP** | 25% | Years | < 3 yrs (preferred), < 5 yrs (acceptable) | | **NPV** | 30% | ₹ in absolute terms | > 0 | | **IRR** | 20% | % | > Discount rate (hurdle rate) | | **Energy Saved** | 15% | kWh or TOE | > 5% of baseline | | **Feasibility** | 10% | Technical + Operational | No production disruption | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Multi-Attribute Decision Making (MADM):** KTU examiners often ask how conflicting parameters are weighed. The Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP) assigns the weights above, with NPV typically dominating since it captures **both magnitude and time value**. --- ## 3.5 Boiler Efficiency ECM — Air-Fuel Ratio Optimization A boiler operating with **excess air** wastes fuel. The **stack gas loss** is approximated by: $$L_{stack} = K \cdot \dfrac{\text{O}_2\%}{21 - \text{O}_2\%}$$ where $K$ ≈ 0.65 to 0.85 (fuel-dependent). Reducing $\text{O}_2$ from 6% to 3% lowers stack loss significantly, directly improving efficiency. $$\eta_{\text{new}} = \eta_{\text{old}} + \Delta\eta_{\text{stack}}$$ # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 ECM Implementation Framework — Block-Level Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Energy Audit Kickoff] --> B[Data Collection

Measures)) Electrical Motors VFD Retrofit Star Rated Motors Soft Starters Lighting LED Conversion Occupancy Sensors Daylight Harvesting Power Quality Capacitor Banks Harmonic Filters Thermal Boiler Air Fuel Tuning Economiser Blowdown Heat Recovery Insulation Pipe Lagging Furnace Lining Steam Trap Audit Condensate Recovery Mechanical Pumps Impeller Trim VFD Drive Compressed Air Leak Reduction Pressure Optimisation Process Cogeneration Waste Heat Recovery Pinch Analysis Building Envelope Roof Insulation Window Glazing HVAC Chiller Tuning Variable Air Volume ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs). List the three-tier classification of ECMs with one example each.** **Model Answer:** ECMs are engineered, operational, or behavioural interventions implemented to **reduce energy consumption** while maintaining equivalent service output. The **three-tier classification** is: 1. **Tier 1 (No-cost/Low-cost):** Free or near-zero investment measures with payback < 1 year. *Example:* Switching off idle motors, adjusting thermostat setpoints by 1°C. 2. **Tier 2 (Medium-cost retrofit):** Capital investment ₹1–10 lakhs with payback 1–3 years. *Example:* LED lighting retrofit, VFD installation on motors. 3. **Tier 3 (High-cost capital):** Major investments with payback 3–6 years. *Example:* Cogeneration plant, boiler replacement with fluidised bed combustion. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **What is Cost of Conserved Energy (CCE)? How is it used to compare ECMs across technologies?** **Model Answer:** **CCE** is the **unit cost (₹/kWh)** of saving one unit of energy over the lifetime of an ECM, accounting for capital amortization and discount rate. $$\text{CCE} = \dfrac{I \times \text{CRF}}{E_{saved}}$$ **Usage for comparison:** - If CCE < **prevailing electricity tariff**, the ECM is economically justified. - CCE is technology-agnostic — it enables **apples-to-apples comparison** between LED vs. CFL, solar PV vs. grid, insulation vs. heat recovery. - A lower CCE indicates a more **cost-effective** conservation measure. --- ## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (KTU Module Internal Choice Pattern) --- ### **Question A — 14 Marks** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4]** **A factory operates a 75 kW, 3-phase induction motor driving a centrifugal pump. The motor runs at 90% loading, 1450 rpm, with efficiency 88%. An energy audit recommends installing a Variable Frequency Drive (VFD) to throttle the pump flow to 70% of full flow during off-peak hours (2000 hours/year).** **Given:** - Full load motor input: 75 kW - VFD efficiency: 96% - Operating hours after retrofit: 6000 hr/year (full speed), 2000 hr/year (70% flow) - Electricity tariff: ₹ 7.50/kWh - VFD cost (incl. installation): ₹ 4,00,000 - Project life: 10 years; Discount rate: 10% **Compute:** **(a)** [7 marks] Annual energy consumption **before** and **after** VFD installation; monetary savings/year. **(b)** [7 marks] Simple Payback Period, NPV, and CCE. Comment on project viability. --- #### **Solution to Question A:** ### Part (a): Energy Consumption Comparison **Before VFD (Throttling via valve):** - Power drawn (constant) = $\dfrac{75}{0.88} = 85.23$ kW input - Annual energy = $85.23 \times 8000 = 6{,}81{,}840$ kWh/year - Annual cost = $6{,}81{,}840 \times 7.50 = \text{₹ } 51{,}13{,}800$ **After VFD (Affinity Law for power):** - At 70% flow → speed ratio = 0.70 - Power ratio = $(0.70)^3 = 0.343$ - Power at reduced load = $85.23 \times 0.343 = 29.23$ kW (input to VFD) - Output power at motor shaft = $29.23 \times 0.96 = 28.06$ kW **Annual Energy After:** $$\begin{aligned} E_{\text{full}} &= 85.23 \times 6000 = 5{,}11{,}380 \text{ kWh} \\ E_{\text{throttled}} &= 29.23 \times 2000 = 58{,}460 \text{ kWh} \\ E_{\text{after}} &= 5{,}11{,}380 + 58{,}460 = 5{,}69{,}840 \text{ kWh/yr} \end{aligned}$$ **Annual Energy Saved:** $$\Delta E = 6{,}81{,}840 - 5{,}69{,}840 = 1{,}12{,}000 \text{ kWh/yr}$$ **Annual Monetary Savings:** $$S = 1{,}12{,}000 \times 7.50 = \text{₹ } 8{,}40{,}000/\text{yr}$$ --- ### Part (b): Financial Metrics **Simple Payback Period (SPP):** $$\text{SPP} = \dfrac{4{,}00{,}000}{8{,}40{,}000} = 0.476 \text{ years} \approx 5.7 \text{ months}$$ > **Interpretation:** Excellent payback — well within Tier-1 ECM range. ✅ **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF):** $$\begin{aligned} \text{CRF} &= \dfrac{0.10(1.10)^{10}}{(1.10)^{10} - 1} = \dfrac{0.10 \times 2.5937}{2.5937 - 1} \\ &= \dfrac{0.25937}{1.5937} = 0.16275 \end{aligned}$$ **Net Present Value (NPV):** $$\begin{aligned} \text{NPV} &= \dfrac{S}{\text{CRF}} - I = \dfrac{8{,}40{,}000}{0.16275} - 4{,}00{,}000 \\ &= 51{,}61{,}290 - 4{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ } 47{,}61{,}290 \end{aligned}$$ > **Interpretation:** Strongly positive NPV — project is **highly viable**. ✅ **Cost of Conserved Energy (CCE):** $$\text{CCE} = \dfrac{4{,}00{,}000 \times 0.16275}{1{,}12{,}000} = \dfrac{65{,}100}{1{,}12{,}000} = \text{₹ } 0.58/\text{kWh}$$ > **Interpretation:** CCE (₹ 0.58) is **far less than tariff** (₹ 7.50) — ECM is **economically excellent**. ✅ **Conclusion:** The VFD retrofit is a **highly recommended Tier-2 ECM** with payback under 6 months and a robust positive NPV over a 10-year life. --- ### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4]** **A textile mill plans to replace 400 conventional T8 fluorescent tubes (40 W each) with LED tubes (18 W each) across its production floor.** **Given:** - Operating hours: 4500 hr/year - Tariff: ₹ 8.00/kWh - Cost per LED tube (incl. installation): ₹ 650 - LED lifetime: 50,000 hours (vs. 10,000 hours for T8) - Project life: 8 years; Discount rate: 12% **Compute:** **(a)** [7 marks] Annual energy saved (kWh) and monetary savings/year. **(b)** [7 marks] Simple Payback Period, NPV, and CCE. Compare with replacing only 200 tubes. --- #### **Solution to Question B:** ### Part (a): Energy & Monetary Savings **Existing Load (T8):** $$P_{\text{T8}} = 400 \times 40 \text{ W} = 16{,}000 \text{ W} = 16 \text{ kW}$$ **Proposed Load (LED):** $$P_{\text{LED}} = 400 \times 18 \text{ W} = 7{,}200 \text{ W} = 7.2 \text{ kW}$$ **Annual Energy Savings:** $$\Delta E = (16 - 7.2) \times 4500 = 8.8 \times 4500 = 39{,}600 \text{ kWh/yr}$$ **Annual Monetary Savings:** $$S = 39{,}600 \times 8.00 = \text{₹ } 3{,}16{,}800/\text{yr}$$ --- ### Part (b): Financial Metrics **Initial Investment:** $$I = 400 \times 650 = \text{₹ } 2{,}60{,}000$$ **Simple Payback Period:** $$\text{SPP} = \dfrac{2{,}60{,}000}{3{,}16{,}800} = 0.821 \text{ years} \approx 9.85 \text{ months}$$ **CRF at $r = 12\%$, $n = 8$:** $$\begin{aligned} (1.12)^8 &= 2.4760 \\ \text{CRF} &= \dfrac{0.12 \times 2.4760}{2.4760 - 1} = \dfrac{0.29712}{1.4760} = 0.20130 \end{aligned}$$ **NPV:** $$\begin{aligned} \text{NPV} &= \dfrac{3{,}16{,}800}{0.20130} - 2{,}60{,}000 \\ &= 15{,}73{,}722 - 2{,}60{,}000 \\ &= \text{₹ } 13{,}13{,}722 \end{aligned}$$ **CCE:** $$\text{CCE} = \dfrac{2{,}60{,}000 \times 0.20130}{39{,}600} = \dfrac{52{,}338}{39{,}600} = \text{₹ } 1.32/\text{kWh}$$ **Comparison: Replacing only 200 tubes** | Metric | 400 Tubes | 200 Tubes | |:-------|:---------:|:---------:| | Investment | ₹ 2,60,000 | ₹ 1,30,000 | | Annual Savings | ₹ 3,16,800 | ₹ 1,58,400 | | SPP | 0.82 yr | 0.82 yr | | NPV | ₹ 13,13,722 | ₹ 6,56,861 | | CCE | ₹ 1.32/kWh | ₹ 1.32/kWh | > **Interpretation:** SPP and CCE are **identical** (independent of scale). NPV scales linearly. Full replacement gives **higher absolute returns**. > **Conclusion:** The LED retrofit is a **financially sound Tier-2 ECM** with payback under 1 year. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Pitfalls** > > 1. **Affinity Law Misapplication:** Students often wrongly assume power scales linearly with flow. It scales as the **cube** of the speed ratio. Using $P \propto Q$ instead of $P \propto Q^3$ leads to **massive underestimation** of savings. > > 2. **Ignoring VFD Efficiency:** The VFD itself consumes ~4% loss. Forgetting to multiply by VFD efficiency gives **inflated savings** (typical error: 8–10%). > > 3. **Wrong Discounting in NPV:** Do not use the SPP value to compute NPV. CRF must be derived separately for the **actual project life**. > > 4. **CCE Calculation Error:** A common mistake is using $(I/E)$ instead of $(I \cdot \text{CRF} / E)$. This ignores the annuity factor and **understates CCE**. > > 5. **Forgetting Units:** Always convert kW × hours → kWh; × tariff (₹/kWh) → ₹/yr. Markers deduct **0.5 to 1 mark** for missing units. --- ## 5.6 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for Energy Conservation Measures** - ✅ **ECMs are the action step** following an energy audit; they convert analysis into tangible savings. - ✅ **Three Tiers:** Tier 1 (operational, free), Tier 2 (retrofit, moderate), Tier 3 (capital, high). - ✅ **Affinity Law:** $P \propto N^3$ — **20% speed reduction → 49% power saving**. - ✅ **Boiler Efficiency Loss** is dominated by stack gas loss — tune air-fuel ratio to reduce excess $\text{O}_2$. - ✅ **Power Factor Correction** reduces line current: $\Delta I = I_1 - I_2 = P \cdot (\tan \phi_1 - \tan \phi_2)/V$. - ✅ **SPP** = Investment ÷ Annual Savings. **SPP < 3 years** is the typical acceptance threshold. - ✅ **NPV** = Discounted savings − Investment; **accept if NPV > 0**. - ✅ **CRF** = $\dfrac{r(1+r)^n}{(1+r)^n - 1}$; required for NPV (uniform savings) and CCE. - ✅ **CCE** = $\dfrac{I \cdot \text{CRF}}{E_{saved}}$; **compare against tariff** — if CCE < tariff, ECM is economic. - ✅ **IRR** must exceed the **hurdle/discount rate** for acceptance. - ✅ **IPMVP (International Performance Measurement & Verification Protocol)** has four options (A: retrofit isolation, B: retrofit isolation with parameters, C: whole facility, D: calibrated simulation). - ✅ **ISO 50001** provides the PDCA framework for ECM deployment and continual improvement. - ✅ **Energy Conservation Act 2001** mandates Designated Consumers (DCs) under the **PAT scheme** to achieve specific SEC (Specific Energy Consumption) reduction targets. - ✅ **Behavioural ECMs** (turning off, sealing leaks) often achieve **3–5%** savings at **zero cost** — always implement first. - ✅ **Common ECM Pitfall:** Energy savings do **not** equal demand reduction — must **measure and verify (M&V)** post-implementation using IPMVP protocols. - ✅ **Hybrid ECMs** combining Tier-1 + Tier-2 + Tier-3 typically deliver cumulative savings of **20–40%** of baseline energy. - ✅ **Pinch Analysis** is a powerful methodology for thermal process integration and minimizing external heating/cooling. - ✅ **Cogeneration/CHP** (Combined Heat and Power) can achieve **70–80% overall efficiency** vs. 30–40% for separate generation. - ✅ **BEE Star Ratings** provide a quick reference for selecting efficient motors, ACs, pumps, and fans.
